title: Consumption SLA Mapping Playbook: route-to-fidelity and migration guidance
preview:
Purpose and scope:

This playbook defines how to map per-route customer SLAs (latency targets, cost ceilings, and fidelity budgets) to Redwood consumption primitives (serving tiers, model variants, caching policies, and quantization profiles). It provides a prescriptive migration and rollout plan for customers moving from coarse global tiers to route- or feature-level fidelity controls.

Intended audience:
- Product managers owning consumption tiers and billable features
- Solutions and customer engineering teams executing migrations
- Backend engineers and runtime owners implementing route-level enforcement
- Finance and pricing for estimating revenue/usage impacts

High-level goals:
- Provide a repeatable mapping from customer SLA statements to Redwood feature configuration (tier, model variant, cache policy).
- Minimize customer-perceived regression risk when shifting fidelity at route granularity.
- Ensure billing and telemetry reflect per-route consumption accurately.
- Supply clear rollout and rollback playbooks with ownership and metrics.

Non-goals:
- This playbook does not prescribe low-level kernel selection or GPU placement decisions (runtime owns that).
- It does not replace legal/contract SLO language; it reconciles technical SLOs with billing mechanisms.

Definitions and primitives:
- Route: a named API surface in a customer workspace (e.g., /chat/compose, /search/rerank).
- Fidelity budget: customer-specified quality tier for a route (high/standard/low) that maps to token-quality, model family, and quantization bounds.
- Consumption primitives: {serving_tier, model_variant, quant_profile, cache_policy, batching_profile}.

Mapping matrix (example):
| Customer SLA | Target p95 latency | Cost ceiling / 1k tokens | Fidelity budget | Recommended primitives | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|---|
| Millisecond interactive (search/rerank) | < 150ms | <$0.10 | high-latency-optim | serving_tier: latency-premium, model_variant: rwd-llama-latency-90, quant_profile: none, cache_policy: aggressive-prefix | Prefer shallow decode; prefer local KV cache warmup |
| Conversational assistant (chat) | < 400ms | $0.05 - $0.25 | conversational-high | serving_tier: balanced, model_variant: rwd-chat-1.2, quant_profile: q4_0 or q3_k, cache_policy: prefix-KV | Adjust batching_profile to 8-16 tokens latency target |
| Background ingestion (embedding + store) | < 2s | <$0.02 | fidelity-low | serving_tier: cost-optimized, model_variant: rwd-embed-compact, quant_profile: q4_1, cache_policy: none | Use asynchronous job pool, enable micro-batching |

Technical requirements (must-haves):
1) Route-level config model in control-plane: routes must support JSON config with keys: \"fidelity_budget\", \"latency_target_ms\", \"cost_ceiling_usd_per_k\", \"preferred_variants\".

2) Billing link: every served token must be labeled with route_id and variant_id in downstream metering logs so finance can attribute to bundles.

3) Telemetry and guards: runtime must emit per-route metrics at 10s granularity: token_rate, p50/p95 latency, fallback_rate, cache_hit_ratio, quant_loss_signal. Guard thresholds must trigger automatic canary or fallback policies.

4) Guardrail approval pipeline: changes to fidelity budgets for a customer (especially downgrades) require a documented migration playbook and a customer opt-in unless contracted otherwise.

Design overview:
- Control plane: route mapping service exposes route config API and validation; stores route->primitive mappings in a versioned store.
- Policy engine: resolves the runtime routing decision by matching route config, customer tier, and current capacity state. Evaluates fallbacks (variant downgrade or region fallback).
- Billing metadata enrichment service: attaches billing tags to each response event before it is ingested by usage pipeline.
- Migration coordinator: orchestrates canary changes, tracks QA prompts, and triggers rollbacks.

Sample route config (JSON example):
{
  \"route_id\": \"customer-123:/chat/assist-v2\",
  \"fidelity_budget\": \"conversational-high\",
  \"latency_target_ms\": \"350\",
  \"cost_ceiling_usd_per_k\": \"0.18\",
  \"preferred_variants\": [\"rwd-chat-1.2\", \"rwd-chat-1.1-quantized\"],
  \"migration_policy\": \"canary-5pct-24h\"
}

Migration and rollout playbook (step-by-step):
1) Discovery and baseline measurement:
   - Collect representative prompt-set (50–200 prompts) for each route.
   - Measure current p50/p95 latencies, token costs, and quality baselines (BLEU/ROUGE/embedding-similarity depending on route).
   - Owner: solutions engineering; duration: 3 business days.
2) Create proposed mapping and staging config:
   - Map SLA -> primitives using the matrix above, pick candidate quant_profile and model_variant.
   - Author a short change document with expected cost delta and quality delta.
   - Owner: product + runtime PM; duration: 1–2 days.
3) Smoke canary in staging:
   - Deploy config to staging route. Run the full prompt-set and collect quality/regression metrics.
   - If quality regression > threshold (see QA thresholds below), abort and iterate.
   - Owner: SRE + runtime test harness; duration: 1 day.
4) Controlled production canary:
   - Canary fraction: 3–5% traffic for 24–72 hours. Monitor per-route metrics at 1m granularity.
   - Run automated evals on incoming responses (automated similarity checks & human sampling).
   - Owner: migration coordinator; duration: 1–3 days.
5) Progressive ramp and stabilization:
   - Ramp to 25% for 24h, then 50% for 24h, then 100% if no regressions.
   - Post-ramp, maintain the variant for 7 days and run batched QoS checks.
6) Post-migration billing and reconciliation:
   - Finance to reconcile expected vs actual cost delta and update billing buckets.
   - Owner: finance + product; duration: 3–5 days.

QA thresholds and safety checks:
- Automated similarity threshold: for conversational routes, embedding cosine similarity between baseline replies and new replies should not drop >0.07 average across prompt-set.
- Latency budget breach: fallback trigger if p95 > configured_latency_target * 1.25 for >5 minutes.
- Billing delta alert: >20% deviation from expected cost per 1k tokens triggers review.
- Human sampling: any canary step with automated signal breach requires a 5-sample human review with at least 4/5 samples passing quality criteria.

Rollback policy:
- Immediate rollback triggers: safety guard breach (accuracy/latency/billing) during canary or ramp phases.
- Manual rollback: product or solutions engineering may request rollback after post-mortem.
- Rollback procedure: revert route config to previous variant_id and clear warm caches; queue a re-warm job for 2 hours.

Monitoring dashboard and alerts (owner and critical thresholds):
- Dashboard: per-customer -> per-route stacked view with metrics: p95 latency, token rate, fallback_rate, cache_hit_rate, cost_per_k.
- Pagers: SRE oncall if p95 latency exceeds SLA * 1.5 for >10 minutes OR fallback_rate > 5% for >5 minutes.

Pricing & packaging implications:
- Route-level fidelity introduces finer-grained metering. We propose two billing models to offer customers during migration:
  A) Consolidated bundle: customer commits to overall monthly committed spend; routes consume from the bundle and Redwood performs internal cost allocation. Good for customers who want predictable spend.
  B) Per-route metering: routes billed at realized consumption (variant_id + token count). Useful for customers who want explicit cost allocation per product feature.
- Recommended default migration offer: 30-day trial where route-level fidelity is enabled with simulated billing (report-only) so customers can evaluate cost impact before committing.

Migration CLI snippets (for SEs):
- Validate prompt set and compute baseline metrics (local tool):
  ./redwood-migrate validate --route \"customer-123:/chat/assist-v2\" --prompts ./prompts/assist-v2.json

- Stage candidate config to the tenant's staging workspace:
  rwctl route apply --workspace customer-123 --file staging-config.json --env staging

- Start controlled canary (example):
  rwctl migrations start-canary --route \"customer-123:/chat/assist-v2\" --fraction 0.05 --duration 24h --notes \"quant-profile q4_0 to reduce cost\"

Owner ship and contact points:
- Product lead: Ava Chen (product)
- Runtime owner: Miguel Alvarez (eng-serving-runtime)
- SRE oncall for rollouts: eng-sre rotation (see oncall roster)
- Finance contact: Priya Rao (finance)

Success metrics (post-migration):
- 95% of routes that adopt route-level fidelity see cost per 1k tokens within ±15% of modeled projection after 14 days.
- >90% of migrations complete without human rollback.
- Per-route SLO compliance (p95 latency) maintained at customer contract level during and after migration in 98% of cases.

Open questions and next steps:
- Evaluate whether cache warming jobs should be charged to the customer or Redwood during migration.
- Define standard prompt-sets for common route archetypes (chat, search, summarization) and store them in the canonical test-harness. Owner: runtime QA.
- Automate billing simulation for SEs to reduce manual finance reconciliation work.

Appendices:
- Appendix A: Example evaluation checklist (CSV-friendly):
  prompt_id, route, baseline_similarity, post_similarity, p50_baseline, p50_post, cost_per_k_baseline, cost_per_k_post, pass_fail
- Appendix B: Common fidelity labels and meaning:
  high-latency-optim: minimal quantization, prioritized kernels, target p95 <= 150ms
  conversational-high: higher sample-quality, moderate quantization allowed, target p95 <= 400ms
  fidelity-low: aggressive quantization and batching, target p95 <= 2000ms

Document change log:
- 2029-10-02: Created initial draft and mapping matrix (Ava Chen).
- 2029-10-08: Added migration CLI snippets and QA thresholds (Miguel Alvarez).
- 2029-10-10: Incorporated finance reconciliation steps and reviewers comments (Priya Rao).

Related artifacts and links:
- Runtime policy engine spec: /eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/policy-engine-spec-2028
- Billing enrichment design: /finance-and-legal/revrec-and-billing/billing-enrichment-design-2029

Approved-by: TBD

title: Channel Partner Win Playbook: Technical Value, Pricing & Security Matrix
preview:
Summary:
This playbook is intended for channel and strategic partners (ISVs, systems integrators, and resellers) who are evaluating or co-selling Redwood Inference with their customers. It provides crisp positioning, a compact competitive comparison, a technical integration checklist, partner-friendly pricing scenarios for pilots and proofs-of-concept (PoCs), and a security & compliance FAQ tailored to partner conversations.

Use cases targeted:
- Embedded chat and summarization inside SaaS products (low-latency interactive path)
- High-throughput batch embeddings for search and recommendation systems
- Regulated verticals (finance, healthcare) using Private/VPC deployments

Top-level positioning (elevator + extended):
- Elevator (30s): Redwood Inference delivers predictable, low-latency LLM serving across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments so your product team can embed AI features without trading off cost or reliability.
- Extended (90s): Unlike single-mode LLM vendors or raw infra providers, Redwood unifies runtime optimizations (KV-caching, continuous batching, quantization profiles) with operational controls (canary rollouts, cost breakdowns, per-route SLOs). That means faster time-to-production, lower per-token cost, and clear fallbacks during capacity events—critical for customers who need predictable user experience and controllable economics.

Key messaging pillars (pick 2–3 for each buyer type):
- Platform/CTO: Predictable performance and unit economics — dedicated pools, SLAs, and routed fallbacks reduce incident risk and TCO variance.
- Product/PM: Fast experimentation paths with the same API across hosted/dedicated/private deployments and built-in quality monitoring tied to prompt sets.
- Security/Compliance: Private deployment options (VPC or on-prem), customer-managed KMS, audit logs and RBAC compatibility for enterprise controls.
- Developer/Platform Engineer: SDKs and observability hooks (traces, per-token cost) plus recommended batching/caching profiles for workload-specific wins.

Competitive brief (short):
- Competitor: Managed LLM API X
  - Where Redwood wins: Lower latency variance for interactive chat (smart routing + KV caching), predictable dedicated capacity, and partner-friendly private deployments.
  - Where X wins: Larger model catalog in hosted-only mode; faster single-tenant onboarding for certain legacy clouds.
- Competitor: Self-hosted inference stack Y
  - Where Redwood wins: Integrated operational tooling, rollouts, transparent per-token economics, and reduced engineering overhead for maintenance and scaling.
  - Where Y wins: Absolute control over hardware and customization of kernels for niche models.
- Competitor: Cloud ML endpoints Z
  - Where Redwood wins: Consistent API surface across deployment modes and better controls for model quality regressions and rollback.
  - Where Z wins: Deep cloud integrations (e.g., native cloud billing and certain managed storage options).

Competitive comparison matrix (high level):
Product                | Hosting modes        | Predictable latency | Private deploys | Cost controls | Partner support
-----------------------|----------------------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|----------------
Redwood Inference      | Hosted, Dedicated, Private | High (SLO-backed)    | Yes (VPC/on-prem) | Batch/caching/quantization guidance | Co-sell & integrations
Managed API X          | Hosted only          | Medium              | No               | Limited       | Marketplace partners
Self-hosted Y          | Private only         | Variable            | Yes              | Engineer-managed | Services-led
Cloud Endpoint Z       | Hosted + cloud-native | Medium-high         | Restricted       | Cloud-native   | Cloud GTM

Partner GTM playbook (step-by-step):
1) Qualify and map:
   - Identify partner motion: embed (ISV), integration (SI), reseller (channel).
   - Target customer profile: attachment point (product team, platform team, security).
   - Minimum technical signal: expected QPS, max TTFB target, latency SLO (p50/p95), model size.
2) Pilot design (7–21 days recommended):
   - Start with hosted sandbox keys and up to 100k tokens free. Validate latency and output quality.
   - Parallel dedicated pilot: provision a 1–2 GPU dedicated pool for throughput-sensitive customers.
   - Define success metrics: p95 latency <= target, cost per 1k tokens <= budget, RTT stability, and qualitative output accuracy on 10–20 representative prompts.
3) Security/contract checks:
   - Determine data residency needs, KMS/HSM requirements, and whether Private deployment is needed.
   - Provide partner with SOC2/ISO evidence and support level options.
4) Commercial & packaging:
   - Offer a 60–90 day pilot credit, with option to convert to committed dedicated capacity or Private license.
   - Co-sell compensation: standard margin schedule or referral fee; for strategic deals consider co-investment in POCs.
5) Scale & embed:
   - Migrate from sandbox to dedicated or Private.
   - Implement canary rollouts for model updates and set up Redwood Optimize recommendations for quantization and batching.

Pricing guidance for partner pilots (example scenarios):
- Embedded exploratory pilot (ISV): 60 days hosted sandbox -> free credits up to $10k spend. Conversion: example pricing for hosted tier = $0.0025 per 1k tokens for generation + $0.0006 per embedding.
- Throughput pilot (integration partner): Short dedicated pilot with one reserved A100-equivalent GPU pool for 30 days. Pilot rate = discounted reserved rate (e.g., 30% off list) with conversion to monthly commit.
- Regulated customer (Private): Fixed license + deployment support: one-time platform fee (example: $75k) + annual maintenance (10–15% of license) + per-GPU usage for inference.
Note: These numbers are illustrative. Always coordinate final pricing with Redwood deal desk and finance.

Technical integration checklist (minimum viable integration):
- API & auth: Confirm API key flow, rate limits, regional endpoints. Example curl test:
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_KEY" -d '{"model":"rwd-3b", "input":"hello"}'
- Latency profiling: Capture p50/p95 for representative prompts; capture cold-start and warm-cache cases.
- Batching/caching: Implement prefix caching for repeated context; for embedding workloads use batch sizes 32–128 depending on GPU memory.
- Tracing & observability: Wire traces (OpenTelemetry recommended) and export per-request token cost and latency metrics to partner dashboards.
- Canary & rollback: Configure a canary with 1–5% traffic, automated quality checks against prompt set, and instant fallback policy to previous model variant.
- Compliance: Validate audit logs ingestion, KMS key exchange process, and retention policy alignment.

Security & Compliance FAQ (for partner-led prospect conversations):
Q: Can a customer run Redwood privately inside their VPC or on-prem?
A: Yes. Redwood Private supports VPC deployments and air-gapped on-prem packages. Deployment patterns vary by customer size and regulatory requirements — provide reference architecture and sizing guidance.

Q: How is encryption handled?
A: Customer data in transit is TLS 1.2+; at-rest encryption uses customer-managed KMS keys where requested for Private deployments. We support integration with AWS KMS, GCP KMS, and HSMs for key lifecycle needs.

Q: What audit evidence do you provide?
A: SOC 2 Type II attestation is available for hosted offerings. For Private deployments we provide deployment checklists and can support third-party attestation needs via professional services.

Q: How do you prevent model regressions from reaching production?
A: Redwood Optimize ties quality monitors to prompt-based evals. Use canary rollouts with automated checks (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE, classification accuracy) and automatic rollback rules.

Q: Where does customer data go when using Hosted?
A: Data is processed in the region selected for the tenant and is not used for model training. Logs and telemetry retention is configurable; sensitive content redaction patterns can be applied at the request of the customer.

Q: What isolation guarantees exist for Dedicated deployments?
A: Dedicated capacity runs in logically isolated GPU pools with tenant-level quotas, dedicated networking, and optional private networking endpoints.

Ready-to-use sales assets and playbook attachments:
- One-pager: Partner technical sell sheet (link: internal asset ID: PA-2029-012)
- Demo kit: Prebuilt sandbox with synthetic dataset for latency and cost benchmarking (asset ID: DK-2029-004)
- Customer references: Case study extract for SaaS search embed (CS-2028-009)

Ownership and escalation:
- Primary owner: Partnerships (Ava Chen)
- Technical owner: Platform Solutions (Diego Martinez)
- Security reviewer: Security & Compliance (Priya Nair)
- Escalation path: For pricing escalations contact Deal Desk (deal-desk@redwood.ai). For technical escalations escalate to Platform SRE oncall and CC the partner success manager.

Appendix: sample objection handling lines (short):
- "We already have a cloud endpoint": Redwood reduces operational burden and offers predictable SLOs plus a consistent API across deployment modes — you get the benefits of both managed endpoints and private control.
- "We need lower cost": Start with Redwood Optimize recommendations (quantization + batching) — partners typically see 30–60% per-token cost reductions depending on workload.

Last updated notes:
- 2029-01-10: Added pricing scenario for regulated Private deployments and updated pilot guidance.
- 2028-11-20: Initial draft and competitive matrix added.

Contact for feedback: partnerships@redwood.ai

- Platform/CTO: Predictable performance and unit economics — dedicated pools, SLAs, and routed fallbacks reduce incident risk and TCO variance.
- Product/PM: Fast experimentation paths with the same API across hosted/dedicated/private deployments and built-in quality monitoring tied to prompt sets.
- Security/Compliance: Private deployment options (VPC or on-prem), customer-managed KMS, audit logs and RBAC compatibility for enterprise controls.
- Developer/Platform Engineer: SDKs and observability hooks (traces, per-token cost) plus recommended batching/caching profiles for workload-specific wins.

Competitive brief (short):
- Competitor: Managed LLM API X
  - Where Redwood wins: Lower latency variance for interactive chat (smart routing + KV caching), predictable dedicated capacity, and partner-friendly private deployments.
  - Where X wins: Larger model catalog in hosted-only mode; faster single-tenant onboarding for certain legacy clouds.
- Competitor: Self-hosted inference stack Y
  - Where Redwood wins: Integrated operational tooling, rollouts, transparent per-token economics, and reduced engineering overhead for maintenance and scaling.
  - Where Y wins: Absolute control over hardware and customization of kernels for niche models.
- Competitor: Cloud ML endpoints Z
  - Where Redwood wins: Consistent API surface across deployment modes and better controls for model quality regressions and rollback.
  - Where Z wins: Deep cloud integrations (e.g., native cloud billing and certain managed storage options).

Competitive comparison matrix (high level):
Product                | Hosting modes        | Predictable latency | Private deploys | Cost controls | Partner support
-----------------------|----------------------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|----------------
Redwood Inference      | Hosted, Dedicated, Private | High (SLO-backed)    | Yes (VPC/on-prem) | Batch/caching/quantization guidance | Co-sell & integrations
Managed API X          | Hosted only          | Medium              | No               | Limited       | Marketplace partners
Self-hosted Y          | Private only         | Variable            | Yes              | Engineer-managed | Services-led
Cloud Endpoint Z       | Hosted + cloud-native | Medium-high         | Restricted       | Cloud-native   | Cloud GTM

Partner GTM playbook (step-by-step):
1) Qualify and map:
   - Identify partner motion: embed (ISV), integration (SI), reseller (channel).
   - Target customer profile: attachment point (product team, platform team, security).
   - Minimum technical signal: expected QPS, max TTFB target, latency SLO (p50/p95), model size.
2) Pilot design (7–21 days recommended):
   - Start with hosted sandbox keys and up to 100k tokens free. Validate latency and output quality.
   - Parallel dedicated pilot: provision a 1–2 GPU dedicated pool for throughput-sensitive customers.
   - Define success metrics: p95 latency <= target, cost per 1k tokens <= budget, RTT stability, and qualitative output accuracy on 10–20 representative prompts.
3) Security/contract checks:
   - Determine data residency needs, KMS/HSM requirements, and whether Private deployment is needed.
   - Provide partner with SOC2/ISO evidence and support level options.
4) Commercial & packaging:
   - Offer a 60–90 day pilot credit, with option to convert to committed dedicated capacity or Private license.
   - Co-sell compensation: standard margin schedule or referral fee; for strategic deals consider co-investment in POCs.
5) Scale & embed:
   - Migrate from sandbox to dedicated or Private.
   - Implement canary rollouts for model updates and set up Redwood Optimize recommendations for quantization and batching.

Pricing guidance for partner pilots (example scenarios):
- Embedded exploratory pilot (ISV): 60 days hosted sandbox -> free credits up to $10k spend. Conversion: example pricing for hosted tier = $0.0025 per 1k tokens for generation + $0.0006 per embedding.
- Throughput pilot (integration partner): Short dedicated pilot with one reserved A100-equivalent GPU pool for 30 days. Pilot rate = discounted reserved rate (e.g., 30% off list) with conversion to monthly commit.
- Regulated customer (Private): Fixed license + deployment support: one-time platform fee (example: $75k) + annual maintenance (10–15% of license) + per-GPU usage for inference.
Note: These numbers are illustrative. Always coordinate final pricing with Redwood deal desk and finance.

Technical integration checklist (minimum viable integration):
- API & auth: Confirm API key flow, rate limits, regional endpoints. Example curl test:
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_KEY" -d '{"model":"rwd-3b", "input":"hello"}'
- Latency profiling: Capture p50/p95 for representative prompts; capture cold-start and warm-cache cases.
- Batching/caching: Implement prefix caching for repeated context; for embedding workloads use batch sizes 32–128 depending on GPU memory.
- Tracing & observability: Wire traces (OpenTelemetry recommended) and export per-request token cost and latency metrics to partner dashboards.
- Canary & rollback: Configure a canary with 1–5% traffic, automated quality checks against prompt set, and instant fallback policy to previous model variant.
- Compliance: Validate audit logs ingestion, KMS key exchange process, and retention policy alignment.

Security & Compliance FAQ (for partner-led prospect conversations):
Q: Can a customer run Redwood privately inside their VPC or on-prem?
A: Yes. Redwood Private supports VPC deployments and air-gapped on-prem packages. Deployment patterns vary by customer size and regulatory requirements — provide reference architecture and sizing guidance.

Q: How is encryption handled?
A: Customer data in transit is TLS 1.2+; at-rest encryption uses customer-managed KMS keys where requested for Private deployments. We support integration with AWS KMS, GCP KMS, and HSMs for key lifecycle needs.

Q: What audit evidence do you provide?
A: SOC 2 Type II attestation is available for hosted offerings. For Private deployments we provide deployment checklists and can support third-party attestation needs via professional services.

Q: How do you prevent model regressions from reaching production?
A: Redwood Optimize ties quality monitors to prompt-based evals. Use canary rollouts with automated checks (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE, classification accuracy) and automatic rollback rules.

Q: Where does customer data go when using Hosted?
A: Data is processed in the region selected for the tenant and is not used for model training. Logs and telemetry retention is configurable; sensitive content redaction patterns can be applied at the request of the customer.

Q: What isolation guarantees exist for Dedicated deployments?
A: Dedicated capacity runs in logically isolated GPU pools with tenant-level quotas, dedicated networking, and optional private networking endpoints.

Ready-to-use sales assets and playbook attachments:
- One-pager: Partner technical sell sheet (link: internal asset ID: PA-2029-012)
- Demo kit: Prebuilt sandbox with synthetic dataset for latency and cost benchmarking (asset ID: DK-2029-004)
- Customer references: Case study extract for SaaS search embed (CS-2028-009)

Ownership and escalation:
- Primary owner: Partnerships (Ava Chen)
- Technical owner: Platform Solutions (Diego Martinez)
- Security reviewer: Security & Compliance (Priya Nair)
- Escalation path: For pricing escalations contact Deal Desk (deal-desk@redwood.ai). For technical escalations escalate to Platform SRE oncall and CC the partner success manager.

Appendix: sample objection handling lines (short):
- "We already have a cloud endpoint": Redwood reduces operational burden and offers predictable SLOs plus a consistent API across deployment modes — you get the benefits of both managed endpoints and private control.
- "We need lower cost": Start with Redwood Optimize recommendations (quantization + batching) — partners typically see 30–60% per-token cost reductions depending on workload.

Last updated notes:
- 2029-01-10: Added pricing scenario for regulated Private deployments and updated pilot guidance.
- 2028-11-20: Initial draft and competitive matrix added.

Contact for feedback: partnerships@redwood.ai

title: Corporate Controls and Workflows — Policies Reference
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This handbook centralizes company-wide operational controls and step-by-step workflows for access & permissions, change management, data handling, procurement & expenses, vendor management, travel, and the canonical internal templates. Its goal is to reduce ambiguity, accelerate approvals, and ensure consistent compliance with our internal risk posture and partner requirements.

Audience: All Redwood employees, contractors, and teams owning operational changes or supplier relationships.

Scope: Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployment controls, all production and non-production environments, and all vendors engaged on Redwood contracts.

Owners and escalation contacts (quick reference)
| Area | Primary Owner | Secondary Owner | Escalation | SLA for Triage |
|---|---:|---:|---|---:|
| Access & Permissions | Security (rbac@redwood.ai) | IT Ops | Head of Security | 24 hours |
| Change Management | Eng Platform | Eng SRE | CTO | 48 hours |
| Data Management | Privacy & Legal | Eng Platform | Chief Legal Officer | 72 hours |
| Procurement & Expenses | Finance | Procurement Manager | CFO | 48 hours |
| Vendor Management | Finance-Legal | Security | Head of Partnerships | 5 business days |
| Travel & Expenses | People Ops | Finance | Head of People Ops | 3 business days |

1) Access & Permissions
Principles
- Least privilege by default.
- Role-based assignments with time-limited elevated access.
- All privileged grants require a business justification and an owner.
- Access review cadence: quarterly for production environments; semi-annually for non-production.

RBAC tiers (standardized roles)
- viewer: Read-only to logs, dashboards, and metrics.
- developer: Code and non-prod infra access; no production secrets.
- operator: Production deploy/run privileges without secret-management write.
- admin: Full control; requires 2-person approval and annual attestation.

Request and approval flow (step-by-step)
1. Submit an Access Request using the "Access Request" template (see Templates section). Include scope (systems, duration), justification, and approver (service owner).
2. Automated checks: system enforces MFA, corporate device, and current employment status.
3. Approver reviews within 48 hours.
4. If approved, Access is provisioned with a maximum duration of 30 days for elevated roles; auto-expire.
5. Requester or approver must document business rationale in the request record.

Emergency access
- For urgent operational needs, request a Break Glass session via the on-call runbook channel. A second approver must confirm the session within 8 hours. All Break Glass sessions are logged and subject to post-event review.

Periodic access audit
- Owners must attest to role membership quarterly. Audit artifacts retained for 2 years.

2) Change Management
Change classes
- Minor (low-risk): config tweaks, non-production deploys, documentation updates. No RFC required; notify owner and schedule.
- Standard (medium-risk): production config changes, model toggles, infra scaling. Requires RFC and scheduled maintenance window.
- Major (high-risk): architecture changes, schema migrations, database sharding, or anything requiring rollback coordination. Requires formal RFC, runbook, canary strategy, and SRE sign-off.

RFC template (must include)
- Title, author, date
- Change class and business justification
- Impact matrix (services, customers, data scope)
- Rollout plan (canary %, observability signals, metrics to monitor)
- Backout/rollback criteria and steps
- Validation plan and post-deploy verification checklist
- Owner and approvers

Approval cadence and SLAs
- Standard changes: approvals requested at least 48 hours in advance; approver response SLA 48 hours.
- Major changes: RFC posted and reviewed in the weekly architecture forum; approvals documented before change window.

Deployment windows and blackout periods
- Regular maintenance windows: Saturdays 02:00–06:00 UTC for non-critical infra tasks.
- Blackout: Major product launches or peak traffic periods (documented per release). No non-emergency changes during blackout.

Emergency change process
1. Triage by on-call SRE and owner.
2. Notify stakeholders via the incident channel.
3. Implement hotfix with at least one peer review and record the change per post-mortem.

3) Data Management
Classification and handling
- PUBLIC: Safe to publish.
- INTERNAL: Company information that should not be public.
- CONFIDENTIAL: Customer PII, credentials, financials. Access limited.
- SENSITIVE: Regulated data (HIPAA, GDPR PII where applicable). Additional contractual controls required.

Retention schedule (canonical)
- Application logs (non-PII): 90 days (default).
- Audit logs / access logs: 1 year (security-critical).
- Backups: 90 days snapshot, longer per retention policy for legal holds.
- Customer data: retention per customer contract; default 2 years unless specified.

Encryption and key management
- All CONFIDENTIAL and SENSITIVE data must be encrypted at rest and in transit.
- Keys must be stored in a company-managed KMS; BYOK exceptions require Legal approval.

Data deletion and subject requests
- Data deletion requests initiated via Privacy intake form. Owner: Privacy & Legal. Service owners must honor deletion within 30 days or document exception and notify Privacy.

Data access for third parties
- Only allowed after a Data Processing Agreement and Security Questionnaire are validated. Technical controls (tokenized/limited views) preferred over bulk exports.

4) Procurement & Expenses
Approval thresholds and process
- <= $5,000: Manager-level approval and corporate card use.
- $5,001–$50,000: Department head + Procurement notification; must use standard PO template.
- > $50,000: Formal Procurement RFx and Legal review; Procurement lead coordinates vendor evaluation.

Vendor selection rubric (scorecard highlights)
- Security posture (SOC2 / ISO27001): 35%
- Product fit and roadmap: 25%
- Total cost of ownership: 15%
- Reference customers and support: 15%
- Diversity and supplier risk: 10%

Contracting and payment
- Legal must review all vendor agreements prior to signature.
- Finance issues POs for contract amounts > $5k unless recurring SaaS (then follow SaaS procurement flow).
- Payment terms target: Net 30 for standard vendors; exceptions documented in the contract.

Expense policy highlights
- Receipts required for any single expense > $25.
- Per diems: domestic $75/day, international country-by-country (see Travel Appendix).
- Card misuse escalations: repeated violations may result in revocation of corporate card privileges.

5) Vendor Management
Onboarding checklist (must be completed before production work)
1. Complete Vendor Intake form and submit to Procurement.
2. Security Questionnaire AND evidence (SOC2 report or equivalent).
3. Legal reviews and signs contract template (include data handling clauses as needed).
4. Finance sets up vendor record and payment terms.
5. Technical onboarding: least-privilege credentials, IP allowlist, and expirations.
6. Kickoff meeting with success plan and QBR cadence.

Ongoing oversight
- Annual risk review for vendors with access to CONFIDENTIAL or SENSITIVE data.
- Quarterly QBRs for strategic vendors, including performance KPIs and incident review.
- Termination checklist: revoke credentials, collect data, confirm certificate of destruction when relevant.

6) Travel & Expenses
Pre-approval and booking
- All international travel requires pre-approval from People Ops and manager.
- Preferred booking channels: company travel portal. Use corporate rates where available.

Safety and compliance
- Employees traveling to high-risk countries must complete a travel risk assessment and receive security clearance before booking.
- Travel insurance and emergency contacts are provided through People Ops.

Expense submission rules
- Submit expenses within 15 days of return. Late submissions may be denied unless exception granted.
- Mandatory receipts for airfare, lodging, and meals above per-diem.

Per diem and allowable items
- Meals: up to domestic per diem. Alcohol allowed only when hosted by vendor and must be itemized.
- Ground transportation: ride-share and taxis acceptable; car rental requires justification for >4 passengers or lack of alternatives.

7) Templates and canonical artifacts
- Access Request Template: templates/access-request-2027.md
- RFC Template (Change Management): templates/change-rfc-2027.md
- Vendor Intake Form: templates/vendor-intake-2027.pdf
- Procurement PO Template: templates/po-template-2027.xlsx
- Expense Report Template: templates/expense-report-2027.csv

Example RFC snippet (minimal valid front matter)
```
Title: "Schema migration for user_profiles"
Author: "Samir Patel"
Date: "2028-01-08"
Class: "Major"
Impact: ["auth-service", "billing-service"]
Rollout: "Canary 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 72 hours"
RollbackCriteria: "Error rate > 1% sustained for 5 minutes or >20% increase in tail latency"
```

8) Governance, metrics, and reporting
- Monthly operations review: owners report on outstanding access exceptions, RFC backlog, vendor renewals, and procurement spend vs. forecast.
- KPIs to track: average access request approval time, RFC cycle time, number of vendor incidents, expense report turnaround time.

Change log (recent updates)
- 2027-11-02: Initial baseline document (created).
- 2028-01-15: Added detailed vendor onboarding checklist and scoring rubric.
- 2028-02-14: Updated access approval SLA and added emergency Break Glass procedure.

How to use this page
- Use the templates link above to raise requests and RFCs. For area-specific nuances (e.g., RBAC for Dedicated clusters, private deployment KMS requirements) follow the linked pages in Related Pages.

Questions or exceptions
- Submit exceptions to governance@redwood.ai with business justification; exceptions are time-boxed and require a sponsor and compensating controls.

Acknowledgements
- Contributors: Diego Morales (Eng Platform), Priya Nair (Security), Samir Patel (Finance), Maya Lopez (People Ops).

Appendix: Contact matrix
- Procurement: procurement@redwood.ai
- Finance: finance@redwood.ai
- Security: rbac@redwood.ai
- Privacy & Legal: privacy@redwood.ai
- People Ops: people-ops@redwood.ai

title: Controlled rollout and compatibility gates: private deployment guide
preview:
Overview
========

Purpose: This guide defines a single, repeatable process for performing controlled rollouts of Redwood Private deployments (VPC and on-prem) that enforces compatibility gates across runtime, model artifacts, packaging, and network/KMS dependencies. It collates pre-installation checks, staged rollout steps, day-2 validation, rollback criteria, and customer-facing readiness items so that field installs, upgrades, and hot-swap model rollouts are low-risk and observable.

Scope: applies to: 
- New environment installs (VPC private clusters and on-prem air-gapped installs)
- Versioned upgrades of the control plane and serving runtime
- Model binary swaps that change kernel/quantization profiles or KV cache format

Owners and stakeholders
-----------------------
- Primary owner: eng-private-deployments (Deployment Engineering)
- Runbook owner: on-call Private Deploys SRE rotation
- Security & signing owner: security-and-compliance (image signing and KMS policy)
- Product owner: product (Private deployments roadmap)

Key principles
--------------
- Block unsafe changes early: automated gates should prevent incompatible artifacts from reaching customer infra.
- Incremental blast radius: staged canaries, region-by-region or tenant-by-tenant rollouts.
- Observable safety nets: meaningful telemetry, alerting, and automated rollback when SLO regressions are detected.
- Reproducible preflight: deterministic prechecks customers or field engineers can run prior to any change.

Preflight checklist (run before any install/upgrade)
--------------------------------------------------
1) Customer / environment readiness (owner: field engineer)
   - Hardware: nodes meet minimums in the Customer Requirements table below.
   - Network: egress to Redwood control plane (if hybrid), internal peering, and MTU >= 1500.
   - KMS: key accessible from the control plane subnet; test encrypt/decrypt within maintenance window.
   - Telemetry endpoints: prometheus/OTLP endpoints reachable (or local relay configured).
2) Artifact and package validation (owner: deploy engineering)
   - Image signature verification: image digest matches signatures in signed release manifest.
   - Model artifact compatibility: model .bin and quant profiles validated against the runtime compatibility matrix.
   - Kernel and libc compatibility: verify kernel ABI list in the release notes matches target hosts.
3) Backups and rollback window
   - KV cache snapshots exported (if performing stateful upgrades).
   - Control plane backup stored in customer-approved bucket (retention >= 72 hours).
4) SLO and baseline capture
   - Capture baseline latency percentile (p50/p95/p99), token/sec throughput and error rate for the active model/prefix distribution.

Minimum customer requirements (short table)
------------------------------------------
| Requirement | Minimum | Notes |
|-------------|---------|-------|
| GPU | 1 x A10 or equivalent per 4 vCPU | For small trial clusters; production requires validated GPU pool sizing |
| RAM per host | 64 GB | Host RAM for batching and KV cache |
| Disk | 500 GB NVMe | OS + model shard storage; larger for large models |
| Network | 10 Gbps | Prefer 25 Gbps for high-throughput tenants |
| KMS | AES-256 key with CMK policy | Must allow decrypt from control plane subnet |

Compatibility gates (automated, enforced)
-----------------------------------------
- Signature gate: Docker/OCI images and model bundles must be signed by a trusted Redwood release key.
- Schema gate: model metadata.json must include runtime_version, quant_profile, and kv_format_version fields that match allowed values.
- Kernel gate: upgrade job will compare host kernel ABI list to release-supported kernels and abort if mismatch.
- Config gate: active config flags (batching_enabled, prefix_cache_size_mb) must be present and within allowed ranges.

Staged rollout pattern (recommended)
----------------------------------
1) Canary (internal): deploy to internal staging cluster with production-weight traffic replay for 12h. Verify: no increase in p95 latency > +10% and error rate < +0.1% absolute.
2) Customer shadow (opt-in): select 1–2 low-impact customers for shadowing model or runtime for 24–48h.
3) Gradual tenant ramp: 10% -> 30% -> 60% -> 100% of customer’s tenant capacity over >48h with automated checks at each step.
4) Global rollout: region-by-region with hold windows and capacity gates.

Automated verification checks (run after each stage)
---------------------------------------------------
- Latency regression: compare live p50/p95/p99 to baseline; regression thresholds: p95 > +20% or p99 > +25% triggers immediate pause.
- Error budget burn: if error rate consumes >30% of error budget in a 1h window, pause and rollback.
- Token throughput drop: sustained throughput drop >15% relative to baseline for 30m triggers investigation.
- Model quality check (if applicable): run representative prompt set against canary model; if quality score drops by >3 points, hold.

Deployment commands and samples
------------------------------
- Verify image signature: 
```bash
redwoodctl images verify --manifest signed-release-v2.json --keyring /etc/redwood/keys/release.pub
```
- Run preflight checks (example CLI):
```bash
redwoodctl preflight --env staging --checks network,kms,signature,kernel
```
- Example deployment fragment (YAML):
```yaml
apiVersion: redwood/v1
kind: PrivateDeploy
metadata:
  name: private-deploy-canary
spec:
  controlPlaneVersion: 2027-02-03
  servingRuntimeVersion: v2.8.1
  canaryPercent: 10
  modelBundle: s3://releases/models/chat-q4f/signed-manifest.json
  gates:
    - signature
    - schema
    - kernel
```

Day-2 validation playbook (30–120 minutes after stage)
-----------------------------------------------------
1) Confirm service endpoints: all pods reported Ready and readiness probes passing.
2) Query health endpoints: /health/runtime, /health/kvs, /health/eviction returning 200.
3) Validate telemetry: verify metrics present for token emit, request_count, tail_latency distribution.
4) Run sample prompts against canary tenants and compare structured outputs against golden responses.
5) Verify backups: KV snapshot audit appears in the backup log within the window.

Rollback decision matrix
------------------------
- Immediate rollback triggers (automatic):
  - p99 latency > +30% sustained for 10m
  - error rate spike to >2% absolute for core endpoints for 5m
  - model format mismatch detected (runtime cannot load model)
  - image signature validation failure on any host
- Manual rollback triggers (requires triage):
  - subtle model quality regressions detected in human-in-loop QA
  - intermittent OOMs correlated with config changes

Rollback procedure (stateful and stateless)
------------------------------------------
1) Stateless (serving runtime only):
   a) Scale down new version: redwoodctl deploy promote --to previous --grace-period 120
   b) Monitor p95/p99 for 15 minutes for stabilization
2) Stateful (KV cache or metadata format changes):
   a) Trigger controlled KV restore from snapshot: redwoodctl kv restore --snapshot-id <id> --target <cluster>
   b) Drain write traffic using routing policy and keep read-only for degraded capacity
   c) Reintroduce previous runtime binary with pinned model bundle digest

Packaging and signing notes (on-prem specifics)
---------------------------------------------
- Air-gapped packaging: release tarballs are produced with deterministic hashes and bundled with a release-signature.asc file. Field installers must validate signatures with the offline keyring shipped on the installer USB.
- Container image verification: even when using a private registry, the release manifest (signed) must be mirrored and verified against its digest.
- Firmware/kernel prechecks: the installer will run kernel-compat checks and fail-fast if ABI list is incompatible; provide a "--force" flag only for controlled experiments and require security approval.

Customer-facing readiness items (what to provide to the customer)
----------------------------------------------------------------
- Preflight report: PDF with network, KMS, and telemetry verification results.
- Upgrade window and rollback SLAs: agreed maintenance window, expected downtime (if any), and RTO targets.
- Contact list: field engineer, SRE lead, and security contact with 24/7 escalation phone numbers.

Telemetry and alerting configuration (recommended thresholds)
------------------------------------------------------------
- Alerts to create in customer/central monitoring: 
  - redwood.runtime.latency.p95 > baseline*1.2 for 15m (sev-2)
  - redwood.runtime.error_rate > baseline+0.01 for 5m (sev-2)
  - redwood.kv.restore.failure > 0 (sev-1)
  - image.signature.invalid detected on host (sev-1)

Long-form examples and appendices
--------------------------------
- Appendix A: Example release manifest (JSON snippet)
  {
    "release": "2027-02-03",
    "images": ["sha256:..."],
    "signed_by": "rel-team@redwood.internal"
  }

- Appendix B: Preflight CLI full output example (truncated)
  - NETWORK OK: egress ports 443, 9411 reachable
  - KMS OK: sample encrypt/decrypt succeeded
  - SIGNATURE OK: image digest matches signature in signed-release-v2.json

- Appendix C: Escalation matrix (names and pager IDs) - kept in team wiki for PII reasons.

Related references
------------------
- See: container-image-signing-and-verification-playbook (onprem-packaging)
- See: private-deploy-onboarding-and-failover-runbook (deployment-guides)
- See: kv-cache-migrations-and-runtime-state-migration-playbook (day-2-ops)

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-11-20: created by Ava Chen (initial draft)
- 2026-12-15: added controlled rollout thresholds and packaging notes (review by Diego Martinez)
- 2027-02-10: clarified rollback matrix and preflight automation (approved)
text:
Overview
========

Purpose: This guide defines a single, repeatable process for performing controlled rollouts of Redwood Private deployments (VPC and on-prem) that enforces compatibility gates across runtime, model artifacts, packaging, and network/KMS dependencies. It collates pre-installation checks, staged rollout steps, day-2 validation, rollback criteria, and customer-facing readiness items so that field installs, upgrades, and hot-swap model rollouts are low-risk and observable.

Scope: applies to: 
- New environment installs (VPC private clusters and on-prem air-gapped installs)
- Versioned upgrades of the control plane and serving runtime
- Model binary swaps that change kernel/quantization profiles or KV cache format

Owners and stakeholders
-----------------------
- Primary owner: eng-private-deployments (Deployment Engineering)
- Runbook owner: on-call Private Deploys SRE rotation
- Security & signing owner: security-and-compliance (image signing and KMS policy)
- Product owner: product (Private deployments roadmap)

Key principles
--------------
- Block unsafe changes early: automated gates should prevent incompatible artifacts from reaching customer infra.
- Incremental blast radius: staged canaries, region-by-region or tenant-by-tenant rollouts.
- Observable safety nets: meaningful telemetry, alerting, and automated rollback when SLO regressions are detected.
- Reproducible preflight: deterministic prechecks customers or field engineers can run prior to any change.

Preflight checklist (run before any install/upgrade)
--------------------------------------------------
1) Customer / environment readiness (owner: field engineer)
   - Hardware: nodes meet minimums in the Customer Requirements table below.
   - Network: egress to Redwood control plane (if hybrid), internal peering, and MTU >= 1500.
   - KMS: key accessible from the control plane subnet; test encrypt/decrypt within maintenance window.
   - Telemetry endpoints: prometheus/OTLP endpoints reachable (or local relay configured).
2) Artifact and package validation (owner: deploy engineering)
   - Image signature verification: image digest matches signatures in signed release manifest.
   - Model artifact compatibility: model .bin and quant profiles validated against the runtime compatibility matrix.
   - Kernel and libc compatibility: verify kernel ABI list in the release notes matches target hosts.
3) Backups and rollback window
   - KV cache snapshots exported (if performing stateful upgrades).
   - Control plane backup stored in customer-approved bucket (retention >= 72 hours).
4) SLO and baseline capture
   - Capture baseline latency percentile (p50/p95/p99), token/sec throughput and error rate for the active model/prefix distribution.

Minimum customer requirements (short table)
------------------------------------------
| Requirement | Minimum | Notes |
|-------------|---------|-------|
| GPU | 1 x A10 or equivalent per 4 vCPU | For small trial clusters; production requires validated GPU pool sizing |
| RAM per host | 64 GB | Host RAM for batching and KV cache |
| Disk | 500 GB NVMe | OS + model shard storage; larger for large models |
| Network | 10 Gbps | Prefer 25 Gbps for high-throughput tenants |
| KMS | AES-256 key with CMK policy | Must allow decrypt from control plane subnet |

Compatibility gates (automated, enforced)
-----------------------------------------
- Signature gate: Docker/OCI images and model bundles must be signed by a trusted Redwood release key.
- Schema gate: model metadata.json must include runtime_version, quant_profile, and kv_format_version fields that match allowed values.
- Kernel gate: upgrade job will compare host kernel ABI list to release-supported kernels and abort if mismatch.
- Config gate: active config flags (batching_enabled, prefix_cache_size_mb) must be present and within allowed ranges.

Staged rollout pattern (recommended)
----------------------------------
1) Canary (internal): deploy to internal staging cluster with production-weight traffic replay for 12h. Verify: no increase in p95 latency > +10% and error rate < +0.1% absolute.
2) Customer shadow (opt-in): select 1–2 low-impact customers for shadowing model or runtime for 24–48h.
3) Gradual tenant ramp: 10% -> 30% -> 60% -> 100% of customer’s tenant capacity over >48h with automated checks at each step.
4) Global rollout: region-by-region with hold windows and capacity gates.

Automated verification checks (run after each stage)
---------------------------------------------------
- Latency regression: compare live p50/p95/p99 to baseline; regression thresholds: p95 > +20% or p99 > +25% triggers immediate pause.
- Error budget burn: if error rate consumes >30% of error budget in a 1h window, pause and rollback.
- Token throughput drop: sustained throughput drop >15% relative to baseline for 30m triggers investigation.
- Model quality check (if applicable): run representative prompt set against canary model; if quality score drops by >3 points, hold.

Deployment commands and samples
------------------------------
- Verify image signature: 
```bash
redwoodctl images verify --manifest signed-release-v2.json --keyring /etc/redwood/keys/release.pub
```
- Run preflight checks (example CLI):
```bash
redwoodctl preflight --env staging --checks network,kms,signature,kernel
```
- Example deployment fragment (YAML):
```yaml
apiVersion: redwood/v1
kind: PrivateDeploy
metadata:
  name: private-deploy-canary
spec:
  controlPlaneVersion: 2027-02-03
  servingRuntimeVersion: v2.8.1
  canaryPercent: 10
  modelBundle: s3://releases/models/chat-q4f/signed-manifest.json
  gates:
    - signature
    - schema
    - kernel
```

-----------------------------------------------------

2) Query health endpoints: /health/runtime, /health/kvs, /health/eviction returning 200.
3) Validate telemetry: verify metrics present for token emit, request_count, tail_latency distribution.
4) Run sample prompts against canary tenants and compare structured outputs against golden responses.
5) Verify backups: KV snapshot audit appears in the backup log within the window.

------------------------------------------

   a) Scale down new version: redwoodctl deploy promote --to previous --grace-period 120
   b) Monitor p95/p99 for 15 minutes for stabilization
2) Stateful (KV cache or metadata format changes):

------------------------------------------------------------
- Alerts to create in customer/central monitoring: 
  - redwood.runtime.latency.p95 > baseline*1.2 for 15m (sev-2)
  - redwood.runtime.error_rate > baseline+0.01 for 5m (sev-2)
  - redwood.kv.restore.failure > 0 (sev-1)
  - image.signature.invalid detected on host (sev-1)

Long-form examples and appendices
--------------------------------

title: Causal Latency Attribution and Signal Enrichment Playbook
preview:
Summary and purpose

This playbook defines a repeatable approach for attributing causal sources of request latency in the serving runtime and enriching traces and metrics with synthesized signals that improve root-cause diagnosis. The intended audience is runtime engineers, SREs, and profiling/product teams who investigate tail-latency incidents or validate scheduler/kernel changes against real traffic.

Goals
- Produce an actionable causal hypothesis for >80% of investigated tail-latency incidents within 48 hours.
- Ensure enriched telemetry is available for post-incident analysis and automated detection rules within 6 hours of rollout.
- Keep incremental instrumentation cost increase under 12% of telemetry budget for a standard region.

Scope and non-goals
- Scope: production serving runtime (request surface -> kernel dispatch -> GPU execution -> response), associated request routing, KV cache behavior, and kernel scheduling decisions. Includes streaming, chat, and embedding request shapes.
- Non-goals: model-family quality regressions, client-side application logic, and offline evaluation harnesses. Quality regressions should be investigated using the eval-harness docs.

Signal catalogue (what to collect)
Collect the following signals for each traced request. Where possible, attach a single trace_id and request_id to correlate logs, metrics, and kernel traces.

Primary signals (always-on):
- request_id, trace_id, timestamp_start, timestamp_end
- request_shape: token_count, prompt_length, max_decode_length, batch_size, priority_class
- rpc_spans: inbound_http, auth, router, proxy-layer, runtime_api
- kernel_spans: kernel_start_ns, kernel_queue_ns, kernel_execution_ns, kernel_end_ns, kernel_name
- kvcache_hits: prefix_hits, kvcache_warmup_ms
- gpu_counters: sm_util_pct, mem_alloc_bytes, warp_stall_cycles
- host_metrics: numa_node, cpu_steal_ms, irq_drop_counts

Enrichment signals (high-cardinality; sample and attach heuristics):
- model_variant_hash, quantization_profile_tag, compiled_kernel_version
- scheduling_decision: preempted_by, time_in_priority_queue, admission_reason
- shard_topology: replica_id, numa_zone, local_gpu_id
- batching_context: batch_trigger_reason (timeout/size), constituent_request_ids

Sampling and cost strategy
1) 100% lightweight metrics: counters, duration histograms, basic request metadata. Retain for 14d.
2) 10% deterministic sampling for full tracing of production traffic (uniform across tenants). Retain traces for 30d.
3) Spike sampling: on SLO breach (p95 > configured threshold) automatically increase trace sampling to 50% for impacted routes for the next 1h.
4) High-fidelity kernel traces (GPU perf counters + nvtx) on 0.5% of requests. Escalate to 5% during incident windows.

Instrumentation implementation notes
- Use trace context propagation header X-Redwood-Trace to join router, runtime, and GPU trace collectors. Ensure header is present on internal RPCs and passthrough to private deploys only if configured.
- Kernel instrumentation should use lightweight entry/exit probes with a separate binary that collects NVTX ranges and GPU counters and attaches them by trace_id. Avoid synchronous RPCs from kernel probes; write to local ring buffer and flush asynchronously.
- KV cache metrics should be emitted at two levels: aggregate counter per shard and per-request kvcache_hit fields. Per-request kvcache fields should be added to sampled traces only (see sampling policy).

Causal analysis methodology (step-by-step)
1) Triage: Confirm SLO breach and scope impact (routes, models, customers). Pull p50/p95/p99 histograms and error rates for the last 30m, 2h, 24h.
2) Surface anomalies: Run automated anomaly detectors against enriched signals (kernel queue growth, nvlink error rates, sudden drop in prefix_hits). If detector flags present — mark candidate signals.
3) Correlate with deployment/rollout metadata: map anomalies to rollout windows, kernel rollouts, or canary changes.
4) Build causal graph: for the candidate timeframe, assemble the sequence of dependent spans (router -> admission -> kernel_queue -> kernel_exec -> fetch_kv). Enrich nodes with host and kernel signals.
5) Use counterfactual checks: replay small subset of requests on a mirrored infra (shadow) with alternative scheduler settings or kernel version to test causality.
6) Validate by targeted mitigation: e.g., revert kernel flag, route traffic to fallback kernel, or pin model_variant. Observe expected latency regression rollback.

Investigation runbook (actionable play steps)
- Step A: Gather baseline
  1. Identify impacted route(s) and window: `redwood-observability query \"latency.p95{route=\\\"/v1/generate\\\"}\" --from=-2h --to=now`
  2. Collect traces: filter traces by high-latency (>p99 threshold) and `sample_rate>0`.
  3. Export top 50 kernel_spans ordered by kernel_queue_ns.

- Step B: Quick hypothesis checks (first 30 mins)
  1. Kernel queue growth: check kernel_queue_ns histogram per replica. If > 3x baseline, inspect recent scheduling policy changes.
  2. KV cache coldness: compare prefix_hits ratio for impacted requests vs baseline. If prefix_hits dropped >25%, suspect cache churn.
  3. Host-level contention: check cpu_steal_ms and pci_bandwidth_errors.

- Step C: Escalation and mitigations
  1. If kernel_queue growth is causal, apply emergency mitigation: increase admission_caps for impacted priority_class or route traffic to a less-loaded pool. Use `redwood-admin admission adjust --route /v1/generate --add-cap 20`
  2. For KV cache issues, trigger targeted prefetch warmups for top prompt prefixes or pin sessions to warmed replicas.
  3. For GPU-level stalls, disable the suspect kernel variant and roll back to previously stable kernel: follow runtime-upgrade-validation-and-canary-matrix.

Template investigation checklist (for incident postmortem)
- Incident window and scope
- Evidence of change (deployments, kernel flags, autoscaler events)
- Key enriched signals correlated with latency spike
- Counterfactual replay results and mitigation outcome
- Root cause classification (scheduling, kernel bug, resource contention, routing)
- Estimated user impact and duration
- Follow-up remediation and owners

Signal enrichment pipeline (design)
- Ingress collection: routers attach request metadata and initial trace headers.
- Short-term enrichment: runtime proxies add kernel and kvcache metrics to sampled traces.
- Asynchronous aggregator: writes enriched records to a trace-enrichment topic (Kafka). An enrichment worker joins kernel counters, host metrics, and batch composition and emits a single enriched trace payload.
- Storage and index: enriched traces are stored in the tracing backend with indexed fields for kernel_name, quantization_profile_tag, and batch_trigger_reason.

Configuration table (operational knobs)
| parameter | default | unit | purpose | owner |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
|trace_sampling.rate|0.10|fraction|Deterministic sampling rate for end-to-end traces|observability-team|
|kernel_trace.high_fidelity_rate|0.005|fraction|Proportion of requests with NVTX + perf counters|serving-runtime|
|spike_increase.multiplier|5|x|Multiplier applied to sampling when SLO breached|sre|
|enrichment.flush_interval|15|seconds|How often kernel probe flushes ring buffer|serving-runtime|
|telemetry_budget_pct|12|percent|Max extra telemetry spend from enrichment|finance|

SLOs and alert thresholds (examples)
- Route /v1/generate: p95 < 240ms, p99 < 900ms. Trigger: sustained p95 breach for 10m.
- Kernel queue growth: alert when median kernel_queue_ns increases 2.5x over baseline for 5m.
- KV cache eviction surge: alert when global prefix_miss_rate increases by +15% in 3m.

Common causal patterns and remediation matrix
- Pattern: kernel_queue growth + high batch sizes -> Remediation: lower max_batch_size for latency-sensitive priorities; enable batch_slicer.
- Pattern: sudden nvlink_errors + mem_alloc spikes -> Remediation: failover replica, reduce concurrent_session_limit, schedule maintenance to repro NIC/driver issues.
- Pattern: prefix_hits drop after rollout -> Remediation: rollback model_variant change or pre-warm KV cache using production-sourced prompt prefixes.

Verification and testing
- Always test instrumentation changes in the staging sandbox with production-synth traffic generator. Follow the runtime-scaling-experiment-protocol.
- Use deterministic replay of sampled traces in a shadow infra to validate mitigation effects before wide rollback.

Rollout checklist for enrichment changes
1. Implement feature flag gating for enrichment fields.
2. Canary to 5% of traffic for 24h; validate no tail impact.
3. Evaluate telemetry cost delta and adjust sampling rates.
4. Promote to 50% for 48h; monitor SLOs and kernel queue metrics.
5. Full rollout with staged increases to 100% over 7 days.

Ownership and contacts
- Primary owner: serving-runtime observability subteam (oncall: runtime-profiling).
- Secondary: eng-sre (oncall: runtime-sre).
- Pager escalation: follow oncall runbook and incident-bridge setup.

Appendix: example commands and traces
- Query top kernel queue contributors (CLI):
```
redwood-traces query --filter 'latency>500ms and route==\"/v1/generate\"' --fields trace_id,kernel_name,kernel_queue_ns --sort kernel_queue_ns --limit 50
```
- Minimal enriched trace JSON (sampled):
```
{
  \"trace_id\": \"abc123\",
  \"request_id\": \"r-984\",
  \"route\": \"/v1/generate\",
  \"kernel_spans\": [{\"kernel_name\":\"matmul_v2\",\"kernel_queue_ns\":4200000,\"kernel_execution_ns\":230000}],
  \"kvcache\": {\"prefix_hits\":0,\"prefix_miss_reason\":\"cold-start\"}
}
```

Related artifacts and reading
- See runtime-telemetry-sampling-cost-aware-profiling-2027 for sampling strategies.
- Refer to runtime-upgrade-validation-and-canary-matrix-2026 for safe rollback steps.
- Integration notes for private deployments: ensure X-Redwood-Trace header passthrough is enabled in the VPC control plane.

Change log
- 2026-11-12 (Ava Chen): initial draft and prototype enrichment schema.
- 2027-01-18 (Ethan Park): sampling policy updates and spike sampling automation.
- 2027-02-03 (Miguel Alvarez): added example commands and verification checklist.

Document maintenance
This playbook is maintained by the serving-runtime observability subteam. Review cadence: quarterly or after any major kernel/scheduler rollout.

- Step A: Gather baseline
  1. Identify impacted route(s) and window: `redwood-observability query \"latency.p95{route=\\\"/v1/generate\\\"}\" --from=-2h --to=now`
  2. Collect traces: filter traces by high-latency (>p99 threshold) and `sample_rate>0`.
  3. Export top 50 kernel_spans ordered by kernel_queue_ns.

- Step B: Quick hypothesis checks (first 30 mins)
  1. Kernel queue growth: check kernel_queue_ns histogram per replica. If > 3x baseline, inspect recent scheduling policy changes.
  2. KV cache coldness: compare prefix_hits ratio for impacted requests vs baseline. If prefix_hits dropped >25%, suspect cache churn.
  3. Host-level contention: check cpu_steal_ms and pci_bandwidth_errors.

- Step C: Escalation and mitigations
  1. If kernel_queue growth is causal, apply emergency mitigation: increase admission_caps for impacted priority_class or route traffic to a less-loaded pool. Use `redwood-admin admission adjust --route /v1/generate --add-cap 20`
  2. For KV cache issues, trigger targeted prefetch warmups for top prompt prefixes or pin sessions to warmed replicas.
  3. For GPU-level stalls, disable the suspect kernel variant and roll back to previously stable kernel: follow runtime-upgrade-validation-and-canary-matrix.

Template investigation checklist (for incident postmortem)
- Incident window and scope
- Evidence of change (deployments, kernel flags, autoscaler events)
- Key enriched signals correlated with latency spike
- Counterfactual replay results and mitigation outcome
- Root cause classification (scheduling, kernel bug, resource contention, routing)
- Estimated user impact and duration
- Follow-up remediation and owners

Signal enrichment pipeline (design)
- Ingress collection: routers attach request metadata and initial trace headers.
- Short-term enrichment: runtime proxies add kernel and kvcache metrics to sampled traces.
- Asynchronous aggregator: writes enriched records to a trace-enrichment topic (Kafka). An enrichment worker joins kernel counters, host metrics, and batch composition and emits a single enriched trace payload.
- Storage and index: enriched traces are stored in the tracing backend with indexed fields for kernel_name, quantization_profile_tag, and batch_trigger_reason.

Configuration table (operational knobs)
| parameter | default | unit | purpose | owner |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
|trace_sampling.rate|0.10|fraction|Deterministic sampling rate for end-to-end traces|observability-team|
|kernel_trace.high_fidelity_rate|0.005|fraction|Proportion of requests with NVTX + perf counters|serving-runtime|
|spike_increase.multiplier|5|x|Multiplier applied to sampling when SLO breached|sre|
|enrichment.flush_interval|15|seconds|How often kernel probe flushes ring buffer|serving-runtime|
|telemetry_budget_pct|12|percent|Max extra telemetry spend from enrichment|finance|

SLOs and alert thresholds (examples)
- Route /v1/generate: p95 < 240ms, p99 < 900ms. Trigger: sustained p95 breach for 10m.
- Kernel queue growth: alert when median kernel_queue_ns increases 2.5x over baseline for 5m.
- KV cache eviction surge: alert when global prefix_miss_rate increases by +15% in 3m.

Common causal patterns and remediation matrix
- Pattern: kernel_queue growth + high batch sizes -> Remediation: lower max_batch_size for latency-sensitive priorities; enable batch_slicer.
- Pattern: sudden nvlink_errors + mem_alloc spikes -> Remediation: failover replica, reduce concurrent_session_limit, schedule maintenance to repro NIC/driver issues.
- Pattern: prefix_hits drop after rollout -> Remediation: rollback model_variant change or pre-warm KV cache using production-sourced prompt prefixes.

Verification and testing
- Always test instrumentation changes in the staging sandbox with production-synth traffic generator. Follow the runtime-scaling-experiment-protocol.
- Use deterministic replay of sampled traces in a shadow infra to validate mitigation effects before wide rollback.

Rollout checklist for enrichment changes
1. Implement feature flag gating for enrichment fields.
2. Canary to 5% of traffic for 24h; validate no tail impact.
3. Evaluate telemetry cost delta and adjust sampling rates.
4. Promote to 50% for 48h; monitor SLOs and kernel queue metrics.
5. Full rollout with staged increases to 100% over 7 days.

Ownership and contacts
- Primary owner: serving-runtime observability subteam (oncall: runtime-profiling).
- Secondary: eng-sre (oncall: runtime-sre).
- Pager escalation: follow oncall runbook and incident-bridge setup.

Appendix: example commands and traces
- Query top kernel queue contributors (CLI):
```
redwood-traces query --filter 'latency>500ms and route==\"/v1/generate\"' --fields trace_id,kernel_name,kernel_queue_ns --sort kernel_queue_ns --limit 50
```
- Minimal enriched trace JSON (sampled):
```
{
  \"trace_id\": \"abc123\",
  \"request_id\": \"r-984\",
  \"route\": \"/v1/generate\",
  \"kernel_spans\": [{\"kernel_name\":\"matmul_v2\",\"kernel_queue_ns\":4200000,\"kernel_execution_ns\":230000}],
  \"kvcache\": {\"prefix_hits\":0,\"prefix_miss_reason\":\"cold-start\"}
}
```

Related artifacts and reading
- See runtime-telemetry-sampling-cost-aware-profiling-2027 for sampling strategies.
- Refer to runtime-upgrade-validation-and-canary-matrix-2026 for safe rollback steps.
- Integration notes for private deployments: ensure X-Redwood-Trace header passthrough is enabled in the VPC control plane.

Change log
- 2026-11-12 (Ava Chen): initial draft and prototype enrichment schema.
- 2027-01-18 (Ethan Park): sampling policy updates and spike sampling automation.
- 2027-02-03 (Miguel Alvarez): added example commands and verification checklist.

Document maintenance
This playbook is maintained by the serving-runtime observability subteam. Review cadence: quarterly or after any major kernel/scheduler rollout.

title: Observability cost-sandboxing pilot retrospective
preview:
Summary:

This retrospective covers the Observability Cost-Sandboxing Pilot (Sep 2026 — Nov 2026). The pilot tested a lightweight sandboxing workflow for experimental telemetry consumers to run new metrics/alerts in a cost-isolated environment before promoting them to production. Goals: reduce unreviewed telemetry sprawl, measure actual cost impact of new instrumentation, and shorten feedback loops for SLI owners.

Background:

- Problem statement:
  - Over the past 18 months we onboarded ~120 new telemetry consumers (dashboards, alerts, continuous guards). Many were created without explicit ownership or cost estimates.
  - Result: increasing ingestion bills, alert noise, and SLI signal duplication across teams.

- Pilot hypothesis:
  1) A sandboxed pathway (dev -> sandbox -> prod) with cost accounting and a lightweight review gate will reduce unnecessary production telemetry by >= 30% for a sampled set of changes.
  2) Cost visibility to authors will change instrumentation design choices (sampling, aggregation, retention).

Scope and participants:

- Scope: ingestion-level metrics, histogram series, and derived SLI series generated by platform and model-serving subsystems. Logs and traces were out-of-scope for the initial pilot.
- Participating teams: eng-platform (obs infra), eng-serving-runtime, eng-sre, two product engineering squads (Search + Chat).
- Pilot coordinator: Ava Chen (eng-platform).

Timeline:

1) 2026-09-01: Kickoff, requirements and success metrics defined.
2) 2026-09-15: Sandbox environment provisioned with cost tagging and chargeback wiring.
3) 2026-09-20 — 2026-10-31: Instrumentation and consumer onboarding; weekly sandbox review meetings.
4) 2026-11-01 — 2026-11-14: Promotion decisions and wrap-up measurements.

Design of the sandbox workflow:

- Steps for a telemetry change:
  1. Author creates instrumentation proposal (PR + Telemetry Change Template). Template fields: purpose, expected cardinality, retention needs, estimated cost (monthly), candidate SLI mapping.
  2. Deploy to sandbox prefix (metrics.*.sandbox.<team>) for 2 weeks. Sandbox enforces: reduced retention (default 7d), stricter cardinality caps, and cost tags.
  3. Weekly sandbox review: authors present usage, sample charts, and cost delta. Reviewers: obs-owners + impacted SLI owners.
  4. Promotion checklist: ownership assigned, sampling/aggregation validated, alert thresholds exercised, cost estimate agreed.
  5. Promote to prod with agreed retention and tags.

Telemetry cost model used (summary table):

| Item | Unit price (example) | Notes |
|------|---------------------:|-------|
| Ingested metric series (per million samples) | $0.90 | Synthetic example using our cost model (ingest + storage) |
| Histogram bucket writes (per million) | $1.20 | Higher due to cardinality and bucket overhead |
| Retention per GB/month | $10.00 | Tiered after 30 days |
| Alert evaluation (per 1k evaluations) | $0.05 | Includes rule engine compute |

Key metrics / success criteria and results:

- Reduction in production-series growth for piloted teams: 38% (target >=30%).
- Total monthly cost delta for piloted additions (if promoted without change): $4,900 projected. After sandbox tuning and sampling decisions, promoted cost delta: $1,400 (71% reduction).
- Average time from proposal -> promotion: 16 days (target <= 21 days).
- Number of duplicated SLI series removed prior to promotion: 12.
- Developer sentiment (survey): 86% found the feedback useful; 32% reported extra overhead as a minor pain point.

What worked well:

- Cost visibility changed choices: several teams moved from 1s resolution histograms to 60s aggregated series, saving estimated 45% of the per-series cost.
- The enforced retention cap and cardinality guard prevented runaway series during early testing.
- Cross-team reviews caught three near-duplicate SLI series that would have caused noisy dashboards and alert churn.
- The sandbox review meetings were lean (30–45 minutes) and focused on measurable signals rather than design debates.

What did not work / risks observed:

- Onboarding friction: the initial template was too long and several early proposals missed required fields. We shortened the template after two weeks.
- Engineering velocity concern: some teams felt the 2-week sandbox window delayed urgent instrumentation; we need an expedited path for low-risk changes.
- Tooling gaps: our rollout lacked automated diff-of-costs per proposal — authors manually estimated costs (error-prone).
- Edge cases where sandbox retention/aggregation hid real production problems (e.g., tail-latency percentiles that only appear at 90-day retention).

Decisions and follow-up actions (owner + due date):

1) Telemetry Change Template v2 (shortened, compulsory cost-checkboxes) — Owner: Miguel Torres — Due: 2026-12-10.
2) Fast-track promotion for emergency/bugfix telemetry with 48-hour review SLA — Owner: Priya Desai — Due: 2026-12-05.
3) Implement automated cost delta calculator in the sandbox UI (PoC) — Owner: eng-platform/obs infra — Due: 2027-01-31.
4) Expand pilot to include traces (sampling decisions only) — Owner: Oliver Wu — Due: 2027-02-28.
5) Update SLI runbook: add guidance when long-retention signals are required and define escalation for retention mismatch — Owner: eng-sre — Due: 2026-12-20.
6) Create a tagging standard for sandbox-promoted series: metrics.<team>.<name>.prod / metrics.<team>.<name>.sandbox — Owner: eng-platform — Due: 2026-12-01 (completed).

Playbook: Promoteable telemetry checklist (for reviewers):

- Ownership assigned (team + owner)
- Cost estimate present and approved
- Cardinality tests performed (synthetic load)
- Retention level justified and documented
- Alert rules stress-tested in sandbox (false positive simulation)
- Duplicate/overlapping series check completed

Lessons learned (operational principles):

- Visibility before enforcement: displaying estimated cost early is more effective than arbitrary caps.
- Lightweight governance works better than heavy approval workflows — keep review meetings < 1 hour and focused on metrics.
- Provide an escape hatch for urgent changes to avoid blocking incident work.
- Measurement windows must align with signal characteristics: include a policy for long-retention signals that require staged retention increases with review.

Appendix A — Telemetry Change Template (condensed):

- Title:
- Owner (name + slack):
- Purpose / user story:
- SLI mapping (if applicable):
- Expected cardinality (unique series, labels):
- Resolution (1s / 10s / 60s):
- Retention required (days):
- Estimated monthly cost delta (USD):
- Promotion risk level (low / medium / high):
- Test plan (load profile, synthetic checks):

Appendix B — Sandbox operational knobs:

- Default retention: 7 days (override with reviewer approval)
- Default resolution enforced: 60s for histograms unless explicitly justified
- Cardinality cap: 100k unique series per service prefix in sandbox

Appendix C — Survey summary (n=22 engineers):

- Net positive usefulness: 86%
- Suggested improvements: automated cost previews (70%), shorter template (55%), clearer fast-track criteria (40%).

Conclusion:

The sandboxing pilot met its core hypotheses: it reduced unnecessary production telemetry growth, materially cut projected ingest costs for promoted series, and improved cross-team SLI hygiene. The main trade-off is modest added process overhead; we recommend rolling out a lightweight, automated version of the sandbox workflow across all platform-owned telemetry with the fast-track path enabled for incident work.

Action items are recorded above; eng-platform will coordinate implementation and report progress in the next roadmap checkpoint (2027-01). For questions or to join the pilot expansion, contact Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai) or join #obs-sandbox on Slack.
