title: SRE cascade mitigation and graded fallback runbook
preview:
h1. Purpose
This runbook defines a structured, graded approach for mitigating cascading failures in the inference stack and executing progressive fallback policies while preserving customer-facing SLOs. It unifies SLO/error-budget signals, capacity operations, routing decisions, and communication steps so on-call engineers and SRE playbooks can perform safe, reversible actions under pressure.

h2. Scope
- Applies to: Redwood inference-serving clusters (hosted + dedicated) and the edge routing plane.
- Services covered: model-router, request-queue, batch-worker, kv-cache, gpu-proxy, autoscaler.
- Not covered: control-plane database migrations, onboarding new model artifacts.

h2. Background and motivation
Cascading degradation often begins with a localized capacity pressure or a latency spike that propagates through backpressure to upstream routers and clients. The objective of this runbook is to:
- Contain the blast radius quickly,
- Preserve critical tier SLOs (see SLO table below), and
- Recover to steady state with minimal customer impact.

h2. Ownership and contacts
- Primary owner: eng-sre (oncall-sre rotation)
- Escalation: Runtime team lead — Diego Morales (pager: eng-runtime)
- Pager escalation matrix: follow standard oncall policy (see /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-and-incident-response)

h2. Key Signals (priority order)
1) Platform SLO burn rate > 2x sustained over 5m (primary signal)
2) 95th/99th latency increases > 2x baseline for inference route
3) Error ratio (%) for 500/503 calls > 1% for 3 minutes
4) GPU utilization > 90% and queue depth growth > 1000 requests
5) KV-cache miss rate spike > 30% relative to baseline

h2. SLO / Error budget matrix
| SLO name | Target | Error budget per week | Action threshold | Owner |
| Model-inference-latency-95 | 300ms | 604.8s | burn rate > 3x (5m) | serving-runtime |
| Model-inference-availability | 99.95% | 0.05% | burn rate > 2x (10m) | eng-sre |
| Embedding-throughput | 5k req/s | N/A | sustained drop > 20% | capacity |

h2. Preconditions and safety checks (before any config changes)
- Confirm current runbook version and active author on rotation.
- Notify stakeholders in #sre-incident and create an incident in Incident Manager.
- Ensure at least one rollback path is validated for any configuration change.
- Snapshot autoscaler settings and save to /tmp/autoscale-snapshot-<ts>.yaml

h2. Graded mitigation steps (do them in order until the signal stabilizes)
1) Observe & Triage (0–5 minutes)
   - Confirm the primary signal with Grafana and Prometheus.
   - Queries:
     - PromQL: increase(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route=~"/v1/(generate|chat)"}[5m]))
     - PromQL: sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[1m])) by (route)
   - Attach relevant dashboard links to the incident ticket.

2) Non-disruptive countermeasures (5–10 minutes)
   - Enable per-tenant request shaping on router (soft limit) for the offending tenant(s). CLI:
     - redwoodctl router throttle --tenant $TENANT --rate 100r/s --mode soft
   - Increase batch time budget for low-priority models: adjust batcher config (safe + reversible).
   - Evict large cold model-shards from cache that are > 90th percentile coldness, only if cache eviction is automated and tested.

3) Smart routing and graceful demotion (10–20 minutes)
   - Runbook decision matrix: if SLO burn persists after step 2 and GPU util > 85%, proceed.
   - Action A: Route new incoming low-tier traffic to cheaper quantized variants (q4/q5) using traffic policy:
     - redwoodctl routing add-policy --from-tier standard --to-model model-x-q4 --percent 30
   - Action B: Offload embedding fanouts to the embedding-worker pool if available.

4) Capacity and autoscale intervention (20–40 minutes)
   - If cluster CPU/GPU pressure is sustained: trigger emergency scale (manual) for the affected autoscaling group.
     - CLI: aws autoscaling set-desired-capacity --auto-scaling-group-name $ASG --desired-capacity $NEW
   - If on-prem: follow day-2 ops playbook to spin up additional nodes or enable burst pools.
   - Update tickets with evidence and new desired capacity.

5) Hard degradations and tenant hedging (40–90 minutes)
   - If error budget burn continues and SLOs remain violated: promote graded fallbacks.
   - Steps:
     - Apply per-tenant rate limits (hard) for best-effort classes.
       - redwoodctl router throttle --tenant $TENANT --rate 20r/s --mode hard --reason "emergency-degrade"
     - Temporarily disable expensive features (e.g., tool-calling, heavy-context chains) via feature flags for impacted routes.
     - Enable degraded response template to upstream clients to avoid retries that exacerbate load.

6) Circuit breaker and partial fleet drain (90+ minutes)
   - If degradations persist beyond 90m, execute circuit breaker for misbehaving service nodes and initiate controlled tenant draining:
     - kubectl label node $NODE degraded=true && kubectl cordon $NODE
   - Route traffic to fallback region if cross-region capacity is available.
   - Prepare rollback and recovery checklist for postmortem analysis.

h2. Decision matrix (summary)
| Condition | First response | Escalation | Recovery indicator |
| High 99 latency + GPU high | soft throttles + route to q variant | manual scale + runtime lead | tail latency back within 1.2x baseline for 10m |
| Error spike 5% | immediate hard rate-limits on BE | SRE lead to open capacity incident | 5xx rate < 0.5% for 15m |

h2. Operational tooling & commands
- Dashboards: 'SRE / Inference - Capacity Overview', 'Router - Per-tenant Throttles', 'KV Cache Heatmap'
- Prometheus alerts to check: infra:inference_gpu_saturated, infra:request_queue_growth, sla:inference_slo_burnrate
- Useful CLIs: redwoodctl (routing, throttle, status), redwood-scale (autoscale snapshots), kubectl
- Example queries:
  - Top offenders (by tenant):
    - PromQL: topk(10, sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[1m])) by (tenant))
  - Queue depth trend:
    - PromQL: sum(queue_depth{service="request-queue"}) by (cluster)

h2. Communication checklist
1) Immediately update incident ticket with: root symptoms, first mitigations, and current SLO burn percent.
2) Post summary to #sre-incident and #runtime with tag @oncall.
3) If degradation impacts customer SLA tiers, notify CS/escalations and account owners (include remediation ETA).
4) During major degradations (>60m), publish periodic updates every 30 minutes with: current burn rate, actions taken, next steps.

h2. Safety and rollback notes
- All routing/policy changes must include an explicit revert command (store in the incident ticket).
- Reversion examples:
  - redwoodctl routing remove-policy --id <policy-id>
  - redwoodctl router throttle --tenant $TENANT --mode off
- After any manual scale, validate that node pools are healthy before re-enabling full traffic.

h2. Post-incident tasks and incident review standards
1) Triage window: within 24 hours, assign owners for: capacity root cause, code/regression, and operational gaps.
2) Evidence collection checklist (append to incident):
   - Grafana screenshots for 5m/30m/2h windows
   - PromQL queries and results used during mitigation
   - redwoodctl change log and autoscale snapshots
   - Node-level logs for any cordoned/drained nodes
3) Postmortem template fields (required):
   - TL;DR, timeline, impact (customers/tenants and internal), root cause hypothesis, mitigation actions, follow-ups, detection gaps, and metrics regression.
4) Reliability initiative triggers (if postmortem recommends):
   - Add to SLO backlog if detection or alerting thresholds are incorrect.
   - Initiate capacity experiment to validate autoscaler responsiveness (see capacity-experiment-template).

h2. Metrics for runbook effectiveness
- Mean time to containment (goal < 30m)
- % incidents where graded fallback avoided full region failover (target > 85%)
- Frequency of manual hard-degrades per quarter (target < 3)

h2. Related playbooks and references
- /confluence/eng-sre/operational-tooling/capacity-observability-and-recovery-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/service-priority-fallback-synthesis-runbook-2026
- Capacity experiment templates: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/capacity-experiment-template

h2. Appendix: quick checklist (printable)
1) Verify alert validity and create incident ticket
2) Soft throttle offending tenants and increase batcher time budget
3) Route low-priority traffic to quantized model variants
4) Trigger manual scale if utilization persists
5) Apply hard rate-limits and disable expensive features if required
6) Circuit-breaker and drain nodes as last resort
7) Collect evidence and schedule postmortem within 24h

--
Document version: 1.0. Last author update: Ava Chen (2026-11-03)

This runbook defines a structured, graded approach for mitigating cascading failures in the inference stack and executing progressive fallback policies while preserving customer-facing SLOs. It unifies SLO/error-budget signals, capacity operations, routing decisions, and communication steps so on-call engineers and SRE playbooks can perform safe, reversible actions under pressure.

h2. Scope
- Applies to: Redwood inference-serving clusters (hosted + dedicated) and the edge routing plane.
- Services covered: model-router, request-queue, batch-worker, kv-cache, gpu-proxy, autoscaler.
- Not covered: control-plane database migrations, onboarding new model artifacts.

h2. Background and motivation
Cascading degradation often begins with a localized capacity pressure or a latency spike that propagates through backpressure to upstream routers and clients. The objective of this runbook is to:
- Contain the blast radius quickly,
- Preserve critical tier SLOs (see SLO table below), and
- Recover to steady state with minimal customer impact.

h2. Ownership and contacts
- Primary owner: eng-sre (oncall-sre rotation)
- Escalation: Runtime team lead — Diego Morales (pager: eng-runtime)
- Pager escalation matrix: follow standard oncall policy (see /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-and-incident-response)

h2. Key Signals (priority order)
1) Platform SLO burn rate > 2x sustained over 5m (primary signal)
2) 95th/99th latency increases > 2x baseline for inference route
3) Error ratio (%) for 500/503 calls > 1% for 3 minutes
4) GPU utilization > 90% and queue depth growth > 1000 requests
5) KV-cache miss rate spike > 30% relative to baseline

h2. SLO / Error budget matrix
| SLO name | Target | Error budget per week | Action threshold | Owner |
| Model-inference-latency-95 | 300ms | 604.8s | burn rate > 3x (5m) | serving-runtime |
| Model-inference-availability | 99.95% | 0.05% | burn rate > 2x (10m) | eng-sre |
| Embedding-throughput | 5k req/s | N/A | sustained drop > 20% | capacity |

h2. Preconditions and safety checks (before any config changes)
- Confirm current runbook version and active author on rotation.
- Notify stakeholders in #sre-incident and create an incident in Incident Manager.
- Ensure at least one rollback path is validated for any configuration change.
- Snapshot autoscaler settings and save to /tmp/autoscale-snapshot-<ts>.yaml

h2. Graded mitigation steps (do them in order until the signal stabilizes)
1) Observe & Triage (0–5 minutes)
   - Confirm the primary signal with Grafana and Prometheus.
   - Queries:
     - PromQL: increase(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route=~"/v1/(generate|chat)"}[5m]))
     - PromQL: sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[1m])) by (route)
   - Attach relevant dashboard links to the incident ticket.

2) Non-disruptive countermeasures (5–10 minutes)
   - Enable per-tenant request shaping on router (soft limit) for the offending tenant(s). CLI:
     - redwoodctl router throttle --tenant $TENANT --rate 100r/s --mode soft
   - Increase batch time budget for low-priority models: adjust batcher config (safe + reversible).
   - Evict large cold model-shards from cache that are > 90th percentile coldness, only if cache eviction is automated and tested.

3) Smart routing and graceful demotion (10–20 minutes)
   - Runbook decision matrix: if SLO burn persists after step 2 and GPU util > 85%, proceed.
   - Action A: Route new incoming low-tier traffic to cheaper quantized variants (q4/q5) using traffic policy:
     - redwoodctl routing add-policy --from-tier standard --to-model model-x-q4 --percent 30
   - Action B: Offload embedding fanouts to the embedding-worker pool if available.

4) Capacity and autoscale intervention (20–40 minutes)
   - If cluster CPU/GPU pressure is sustained: trigger emergency scale (manual) for the affected autoscaling group.
     - CLI: aws autoscaling set-desired-capacity --auto-scaling-group-name $ASG --desired-capacity $NEW
   - If on-prem: follow day-2 ops playbook to spin up additional nodes or enable burst pools.
   - Update tickets with evidence and new desired capacity.

5) Hard degradations and tenant hedging (40–90 minutes)
   - If error budget burn continues and SLOs remain violated: promote graded fallbacks.
   - Steps:
     - Apply per-tenant rate limits (hard) for best-effort classes.
       - redwoodctl router throttle --tenant $TENANT --rate 20r/s --mode hard --reason "emergency-degrade"
     - Temporarily disable expensive features (e.g., tool-calling, heavy-context chains) via feature flags for impacted routes.
     - Enable degraded response template to upstream clients to avoid retries that exacerbate load.

6) Circuit breaker and partial fleet drain (90+ minutes)
   - If degradations persist beyond 90m, execute circuit breaker for misbehaving service nodes and initiate controlled tenant draining:
     - kubectl label node $NODE degraded=true && kubectl cordon $NODE
   - Route traffic to fallback region if cross-region capacity is available.
   - Prepare rollback and recovery checklist for postmortem analysis.

h2. Decision matrix (summary)
| Condition | First response | Escalation | Recovery indicator |
| High 99 latency + GPU high | soft throttles + route to q variant | manual scale + runtime lead | tail latency back within 1.2x baseline for 10m |
| Error spike 5% | immediate hard rate-limits on BE | SRE lead to open capacity incident | 5xx rate < 0.5% for 15m |

h2. Operational tooling & commands
- Dashboards: 'SRE / Inference - Capacity Overview', 'Router - Per-tenant Throttles', 'KV Cache Heatmap'
- Prometheus alerts to check: infra:inference_gpu_saturated, infra:request_queue_growth, sla:inference_slo_burnrate
- Useful CLIs: redwoodctl (routing, throttle, status), redwood-scale (autoscale snapshots), kubectl
- Example queries:
  - Top offenders (by tenant):
    - PromQL: topk(10, sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[1m])) by (tenant))
  - Queue depth trend:
    - PromQL: sum(queue_depth{service="request-queue"}) by (cluster)

h2. Communication checklist
1) Immediately update incident ticket with: root symptoms, first mitigations, and current SLO burn percent.
2) Post summary to #sre-incident and #runtime with tag @oncall.
3) If degradation impacts customer SLA tiers, notify CS/escalations and account owners (include remediation ETA).
4) During major degradations (>60m), publish periodic updates every 30 minutes with: current burn rate, actions taken, next steps.

h2. Safety and rollback notes
- All routing/policy changes must include an explicit revert command (store in the incident ticket).
- Reversion examples:
  - redwoodctl routing remove-policy --id <policy-id>
  - redwoodctl router throttle --tenant $TENANT --mode off
- After any manual scale, validate that node pools are healthy before re-enabling full traffic.

h2. Post-incident tasks and incident review standards
1) Triage window: within 24 hours, assign owners for: capacity root cause, code/regression, and operational gaps.
2) Evidence collection checklist (append to incident):
   - Grafana screenshots for 5m/30m/2h windows
   - PromQL queries and results used during mitigation
   - redwoodctl change log and autoscale snapshots
   - Node-level logs for any cordoned/drained nodes
3) Postmortem template fields (required):
   - TL;DR, timeline, impact (customers/tenants and internal), root cause hypothesis, mitigation actions, follow-ups, detection gaps, and metrics regression.
4) Reliability initiative triggers (if postmortem recommends):
   - Add to SLO backlog if detection or alerting thresholds are incorrect.
   - Initiate capacity experiment to validate autoscaler responsiveness (see capacity-experiment-template).

h2. Metrics for runbook effectiveness
- Mean time to containment (goal < 30m)
- % incidents where graded fallback avoided full region failover (target > 85%)
- Frequency of manual hard-degrades per quarter (target < 3)

h2. Related playbooks and references
- /confluence/eng-sre/operational-tooling/capacity-observability-and-recovery-playbook-2026
- /confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/service-priority-fallback-synthesis-runbook-2026
- Capacity experiment templates: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/capacity-experiment-template

h2. Appendix: quick checklist (printable)
1) Verify alert validity and create incident ticket
2) Soft throttle offending tenants and increase batcher time budget
3) Route low-priority traffic to quantized model variants
4) Trigger manual scale if utilization persists
5) Apply hard rate-limits and disable expensive features if required
6) Circuit-breaker and drain nodes as last resort
7) Collect evidence and schedule postmortem within 24h

--
Document version: 1.0. Last author update: Ava Chen (2026-11-03)

title: Developer Fidelity Guardrail Assistant — Product Brief
preview:
Overview

This brief describes the Developer Fidelity Guardrail Assistant (DFGA), a product module in Redwood Optimize intended to help developers detect, understand, and automatically remediate fidelity regressions and cost-risk in early-stage integrations. The DFGA combines lightweight local simulation, telemetry-driven heuristics, and in-console prescriptive actions so teams can move from exploratory API usage to production-safe consumption with minimal manual tuning.

Goals

- Reduce time-to-safe-production for developer integrations by detecting fidelity vs. cost regressions during onboarding and early ramp.
- Provide actionable remediation suggestions (config changes, candidate model variants, quantization presets) scoped to a customer’s observed workload.
- Integrate with Console, SDKs, and CI pipelines: capture preflight checks, post-deploy regressions, and staged rollout gates.

Non-goals

- Replacing full-scale model evaluation labs or human-in-the-loop QA. DFGA provides guardrails and prescriptive templates, not exhaustive evaluations.
- Automatic model retraining or dataset management.

Key user personas

1. Developer (PLG onboarding): wants fast feedback on whether the default consumption profile will meet cost and fidelity expectations.
2. ML/Platform engineer (Customer): needs deterministic preflight checks and a repeatable set of remediation actions for rollouts.
3. Solutions engineer (Redwood): uses DFGA reports to advise customers during Dedicated or Private onboarding.

Core capabilities

1) Preflight simulation sandbox (local & hosted)
- Run a 50–500 sample prompt set in a lightweight local emulator or hosted synthetic runner that mirrors customer traffic shape (length distribution, stop tokens).
- Outputs: per-prompt fidelity delta (against a golden baseline), token-rate cost estimate, latency percentiles.

2) Telemetry-driven anomaly detection
- Live monitoring of promptset drift and sudden fidelity regressions using rolling-window comparisons (p95 of loss-like similarity metrics and Delta-ROUGE/BLEU-style scores for structured outputs).
- Alert triggers: fidelity drop > 5% absolute over baseline for 15m, cost-per-1000-tokens increase > 20% sustained for 30m.

3) Prescriptive remediation and playbooks
- Config suggestions: batch-size, max-new-tokens, prefix-caching TTL, KV prefetch policy.
- Model recommendations: fall back to quantized 4-bit variant for short-form responses, or to a stronger small-parameter variant for high-fidelity long-form content.
- Deployment actions: staged canary rollout with 10/30/60% traffic split, automatic rollback thresholds.

4) CI / Pre-merge checks and SDK hooks
- CLI: redwood dfga run --promptset ./onboard/prompts.json --baseline gptx-base --profile ci-fast
- SDK: dfga.client.preflightCheck(promptSet, {profile: 'interactive', maxLatencyMs:600}) -> returns pass/fail and remediation tokens.

Acceptance criteria (MVP)

- Preflight sandbox executes within 20s for 100 prompts when run in hosted-fast mode.
- DFGA produces a deterministic remediation plan (one of: change-config, switch-variant, canary-plan) with estimated cost delta and expected fidelity delta.
- Console surfaces a clear pass/fail badge and a downloadable single-page PDF report.

SLOs and thresholds

| Metric | Target (MVP) | Alert threshold | Owner |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Preflight run latency (100 prompts, hosted) | < 20s | > 40s | product/infra |
| False-positive remediation suggestions (manual override rate) | < 15% | > 25% | product/research |
| Preflight determinism score (same input -> same plan) | 95% | < 90% | eng-platform |

Telemetry and observability requirements

- Instrument events: preflight.start, preflight.finish, remediation.generated, remediation.applied, regression.alert, canary.rollback.
- Trace context to surface model-variant, quantization profile, and sequence-length bucket.
- Dashboard: per-customer preflight pass-rate, average recommended cost delta, remediation adoption rate.

Data model / Output schema (stringified example for operator teams)

- preflight_id (uuid)
- promptset_name (string)
- baseline_variant (string)
- suggested_actions (list of strings)
- estimated_monthly_delta_usd (string, formatted)
- fidelity_delta_summary (string)

Example remediation payload (text sample)

{
  "preflight_id": "pf_123e4567",
  "baseline_variant": "redwood-neo-embed-1",
  "suggested_actions": ["set_batch_size:8","kv_prefetch:enabled","switch_variant:redwood-neo-embed-1-q4"],
  "estimated_monthly_delta_usd": "-120.00",
  "fidelity_delta_summary": "median similarity drop 2.6% on short prompts; recommended quantized fallback for <256 token responses"
}

Rollout plan (6-week phased)

1. Week 0–1: Internal alpha: feature flags enabled for Sandbox tenants, manual remediation application only. Owners: product, eng-developer-experience.
2. Week 2–3: Beta: invite selected customers; add Console UI for preflight results and remediation preview. Add telemetry dashboards. Owners: product, eng-platform, support.
3. Week 4: Public launch (soft): self-serve in Console for all new accounts, opt-in toggle for existing accounts. Provide migration notes for Dedicated customers. Owners: product, go-to-market.
4. Week 5–6: Stabilization and feedback: iterate on false-positive heuristics, add SDK pre-merge hooks, and integrate with onboarding templates. Owners: product, research.

Security and privacy considerations

- Prompt and sample data stored encrypted at rest when hosted; default retention 7 days for DFGA preflight artifacts.
- Option: tenant-managed S3 sink for audit exports in Private deployments.
- Access controls: RBAC for remediation actions; only users with "deployment-admin" role can apply suggested canary plans.

Operational runbooks (high level)

- If preflight runner latency > 40s consistently: check hosted emulator node pool (dfga-runner), restart pool via infra playbook, and notify eng-infra.
- If remediation adoption rate drops > 20% month-over-month: create a support ticket for top 5 customers and run a walkthrough.

Dependencies and integration matrix

- Console: results UI and PDF export (ticket: EXP-873)
- Optimize telemetry pipeline: ingestion and aggregation for preflight metrics (ticket: OPT-412)
- SDK hooks and CLI distribution (ticket: DX-221)
- Dedicated onboarding flow: mapping remediation actions to customer policies (ticket: DED-109)

Open questions and risks

- How to define a lightweight but meaningful fidelity baseline for multi-turn, stateful chat prompts? Proposal: support two baseline modes (golden-sample and synthetic-aggregated).
- Risk: over-reliance on DFGA recommendations could cause blind rollout; mitigate with explicit canary and rollback thresholds and manual approval by default.

Appendix — example Console UX copy

Title: "Preflight check: fidelity & cost guardrails"
Short: "DFGA analyzed your sample prompts and recommends a low-cost quantized fallback for short responses. Expected monthly savings: $120. Confidence: medium."
Actions: [Preview remediation] [Apply canary (10%)] [Dismiss]

References

- Onboarding and Cost Advisor Playbook (internal) — /product-docs/product-overview/onboarding-and-cost-advisor-playbook-2026-07-28
- Fidelity Staging SDK and Eval Playbook — /product-docs/requirements/prd-fidelity-staging-sdk-and-eval-playbook-2026-12-22
- Telemetry ingestion spec — /eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/telemetry-ingestion-spec-2026-05-12

Owner and contact

- Product owner: Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai)
- Program lead: Marcus Liu
- Slack channel: #product-dfga

title: variant-marketplace-prd-catalog-and-addon-pricing-2026-09-10
preview:
Overview:
This PRD describes the Variant Marketplace: a first-class product surface that lets customers discover, select, and meter curated model variants and optional add-ons (latency tiers, quantization profiles, distillation presets, and managed warm pools). The Marketplace enables price differentiation by variant and supports metered add-ons to improve unit economics without sacrificing discoverability or operational controls.

Context and motivation:
- Customers increasingly want to choose model tradeoffs (cost vs quality vs latency) without building custom model ops.
- Current catalog is binary (model version). We need a marketplace UX + billing contract that encodes variant attributes and add-on metering.
- This unlocks: explicit pricing for lower-cost quantized variants, premium pricing for ultra-low-latency variants on dedicated capacity, and per-customer curated variant bundles.

Goals (what success looks like):
1) Catalog: Surface at least 6 curated variant types (base fp16, int8-quant, int4-quant, distilled-small, latency-optimized, safety-filtered) with clear attributes and expected quality deltas.
2) Pricing: Implement per-variant and per-add-on metering that feeds billing and usage APIs. Clear price delta visibility in Console.
3) UX: Allow customers to pin a default variant and buy managed add-ons (warm-pool, premium routing) via Console and API.
4) Telemetry: Emit variant_id, add_on_ids, price_modifier, and per-request unit_cost for every billing event.
5) Rollout: Pilot with 3 enterprise customers and GA within 12 weeks after pilot completion.

Non-goals:
- This PRD does not include marketplace for third-party models (future phase).
- It does not replace Dedicated capacity reservations; it augments them with variant-level pricing.

Success metrics (KPIs):
- Percent of new hosted requests selecting a non-default variant: target 12% in Q1 post-GA.
- Incremental ARPU from add-ons: +8% for pilot customers.
- Incorrect billing events rate: <0.01% during GA.
- Time-to-serve for premium routing add-on: p95 <= 120ms.

High-level product requirements (epics):
A. Catalog model metadata and discovery
  - Requirement A1: Extend model catalog schema with fields: variant_id, variant_type, expected_quality_delta_pct, cost_index, recommended_workloads, compatibility_matrix.
  - Acceptance criteria: Console list shows variant badges, filter by cost/latency/size, and API returns variant metadata in catalog endpoints.

B. Pricing & billing contract changes
  - Requirement B1: Billing schema supports price_modifier per variant (positive or negative basis points) and per-add-on flat or per-token meter.
  - Requirement B2: Add-ons must be representable as catalog SKUs: warm_pool_hour, premium_routing_hour, reduced_token_cost_percent.
  - Acceptance criteria: Billing pipeline emits line items with sku, unit_price, quantity and matched variant_id. Test billing reconciliation with sandbox invoice.

C. Console & API UX
  - Requirement C1: Console variant selector in Deployment and Route pages; API param variant_selector: {variant_id, add_ons: []}.
  - Requirement C2: Allow tenant admin to set account-wide default variant with override per-route.
  - Acceptance criteria: Console flow completed in 3 clicks; SDK accepts variant_selector and runtime uses variant routing.

D. Runtime routing and SLOs
  - Requirement D1: Runtime must support preference-aware routing: try preferred variant, fallback to compatible variant when capacity constrained.
  - Requirement D2: Enforce SLOs for premium add-ons: routing must meet latency budget or mark request downgraded and bill accordingly.
  - Acceptance criteria: Canary tests show correct fallback behavior and billing label 'downgraded' in telemetry.

E. Observability and telemetry
  - Requirement E1: New telemetry fields: variant_id, add_on_ids, price_modifier_basispts, billed_unit_cost, routing_decision.
  - Acceptance criteria: Dashboards show per-variant token consumption and add-on revenue.

Implementation details and examples:
- Catalog schema (prose):
  - variant_id: string (eg vm-int8-202609)
  - variant_type: one of [base, quantized, distilled, latency-optimized, safety-filtered]
  - cost_index: numeric relative cost factor compared to base (1.0 = base)
  - expected_quality_delta_pct: -2.5 (meaning expected 2.5% quality delta vs base)
  - compatibility_matrix: list of model families and max_seq_length

- Example API usage (Console and SDK):
  - REST body example (represented as text):
    POST /v1/requests
    {
      "model": "redwood-alpha-13b",
      "variant_selector": {"variant_id":"vm-int8-202609","add_ons":["warm_pool_hour","premium_routing"]},
      "input": "summarize: ..."
    }
  - Expected response includes: request_id, chosen_variant_id, billed_unit_cost (per-token)

Pricing matrix (example):
- Variant-level modifiers (relative to base rate):
  | Variant type           | Cost modifier | Typical use case                     |
  |------------------------|---------------|---------------------------------------|
  | base (fp16)            | 0 bps         | Standard production                    |
  | int8-quant             | -30%          | Cost-sensitive batching workloads     |
  | int4-quant             | -45%          | High volume, tolerant quality loss    |
  | distilled-small        | -55%          | UI companion responses, cheap reps    |
  | latency-optimized      | +40%          | Real-time assistants, sub-150ms SLO   |
  | safety-filtered        | +10%          | Regulated outputs with extra checks   |

- Add-on SKUs (examples):
  | SKU                  | Meter type    | Unit price example | Billing rule             |
  |----------------------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
  | warm_pool_hour       | per-hour      | $0.30 / vCPU-hour  | prorated hourly per-tenant|
  | premium_routing_hour | per-hour      | $1.50 / tenant-hr  | only billed if routed to premium pool|
  | reduced_token_cost_pct | percentage  | policy-based       | reduces base token price by X% for route|

Billing behavior rules (examples):
1) If a request is chosen for variant vm-int8-202609 and uses premium_routing, line items: tokens_used @ base_rate * (1-0.30) and add-on: premium_routing_hour prorated.
2) If premium routing fails and fallback occurs, mark request as billed_with_fallback and use fallback variant modifier. Audit trail must show original requested_variant and resolved_variant.

Console mock flow (text):
1) Deployment -> Edit -> Variant selector: choose tab "Marketplace".
2) Filter by Cost or Latency -> pick variant -> optional checkboxes for add-ons.
3) Confirm changes; preview expected per-request unit cost.

Rollout plan and timeline (12 weeks post-approval):
- Week 0-2: Schema, billing pipeline prototyping, and Console mocks. Owner: Product + Billing infra.
- Week 3-5: Backend implementation (catalog, API changes) + runtime routing hooks. Owner: Eng-serving-runtime.
- Week 6-8: End-to-end integration, billing sandbox, and telemetry dashboards. Owner: Eng-platform + Finance.
- Week 9-10: Pilot with 3 customers (account managers + customer success). Collect feedback and reconcile billing.
- Week 11-12: GA rollout, documentation, self-serve enablement, and SDK updates.

Operational playbook (during pilot):
1) Enable feature flag per-tenant. 2) Monitor telemetry dashboard for billed_unit_cost anomalies. 3) Finance runs daily batch invoice reconciliation for pilot tenants. 4) Roll back feature flag if billing mismatch > 0.1% for 48h.

Open questions and trade-offs:
- Price modeling: use percentage modifiers vs absolute per-token delta. Percentages are easier for multi-model families but can be less precise for extremely large models.
- Warm pool allocation: dedicated warm pools per-tenant vs shared premium pools with tagging. Dedicated pools give predictable perf but higher fixed cost.
- Variant discovery: include quality evaluations and client-facing examples in Console or link to separate eval pages?

Risk matrix and mitigations:
- Risk: Incorrect billing mapping between variant and line item -> Mitigation: End-to-end sandbox reconciliation and daily alert on mismatches.
- Risk: Customers accidentally select a high-cost variant -> Mitigation: Console shows warning and needs 2 clicks to confirm for variants that increase cost by >25%.
- Risk: Performance regressions on quantized variants -> Mitigation: model-onboarding must include perf/quality gates in quantization-profiles pipeline.

Ownership and stakeholders:
- Product owner: Ava Chen (product)
- Engineering owners: Runtime (Kiran Patel), Billing infra (Diego Morales), Console (Hannah Park)
- Finance reviewer: Priya Iyer
- Customer success: Maya Singh (pilot coordination)

Related artifacts and references:
- Catalog model-onboarding checklist: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding
- Billing schema spec: /confluence/finance-and-legal/contracts-and-legal-process/billing-schema-spec
- Quantization profiles and test harness: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles

Appendix: Example reconciliation checklist for pilot customers:
- Nightly job compares billed line items to telemetry token counts grouped by variant_id.
- Tolerance threshold: 0.1% token mismatch; if exceeded notify product + finance and pause feature for tenant.

Notes:
- This PRD intentionally separates catalog discovery (product-facing) from long-term marketplace commerce (third-party models). Phase 2 will evaluate an open marketplace after core billing and telemetry are stable.
text:
Overview:
This PRD describes the Variant Marketplace: a first-class product surface that lets customers discover, select, and meter curated model variants and optional add-ons (latency tiers, quantization profiles, distillation presets, and managed warm pools). The Marketplace enables price differentiation by variant and supports metered add-ons to improve unit economics without sacrificing discoverability or operational controls.

Context and motivation:
- Customers increasingly want to choose model tradeoffs (cost vs quality vs latency) without building custom model ops.
- Current catalog is binary (model version). We need a marketplace UX + billing contract that encodes variant attributes and add-on metering.
- This unlocks: explicit pricing for lower-cost quantized variants, premium pricing for ultra-low-latency variants on dedicated capacity, and per-customer curated variant bundles.

Goals (what success looks like):
1) Catalog: Surface at least 6 curated variant types (base fp16, int8-quant, int4-quant, distilled-small, latency-optimized, safety-filtered) with clear attributes and expected quality deltas.
2) Pricing: Implement per-variant and per-add-on metering that feeds billing and usage APIs. Clear price delta visibility in Console.
3) UX: Allow customers to pin a default variant and buy managed add-ons (warm-pool, premium routing) via Console and API.
4) Telemetry: Emit variant_id, add_on_ids, price_modifier, and per-request unit_cost for every billing event.
5) Rollout: Pilot with 3 enterprise customers and GA within 12 weeks after pilot completion.

Non-goals:
- This PRD does not include marketplace for third-party models (future phase).
- It does not replace Dedicated capacity reservations; it augments them with variant-level pricing.

Success metrics (KPIs):
- Percent of new hosted requests selecting a non-default variant: target 12% in Q1 post-GA.
- Incremental ARPU from add-ons: +8% for pilot customers.
- Incorrect billing events rate: <0.01% during GA.
- Time-to-serve for premium routing add-on: p95 <= 120ms.

High-level product requirements (epics):
A. Catalog model metadata and discovery
  - Requirement A1: Extend model catalog schema with fields: variant_id, variant_type, expected_quality_delta_pct, cost_index, recommended_workloads, compatibility_matrix.
  - Acceptance criteria: Console list shows variant badges, filter by cost/latency/size, and API returns variant metadata in catalog endpoints.

B. Pricing & billing contract changes
  - Requirement B1: Billing schema supports price_modifier per variant (positive or negative basis points) and per-add-on flat or per-token meter.
  - Requirement B2: Add-ons must be representable as catalog SKUs: warm_pool_hour, premium_routing_hour, reduced_token_cost_percent.
  - Acceptance criteria: Billing pipeline emits line items with sku, unit_price, quantity and matched variant_id. Test billing reconciliation with sandbox invoice.

C. Console & API UX
  - Requirement C1: Console variant selector in Deployment and Route pages; API param variant_selector: {variant_id, add_ons: []}.
  - Requirement C2: Allow tenant admin to set account-wide default variant with override per-route.
  - Acceptance criteria: Console flow completed in 3 clicks; SDK accepts variant_selector and runtime uses variant routing.

D. Runtime routing and SLOs
  - Requirement D1: Runtime must support preference-aware routing: try preferred variant, fallback to compatible variant when capacity constrained.
  - Requirement D2: Enforce SLOs for premium add-ons: routing must meet latency budget or mark request downgraded and bill accordingly.
  - Acceptance criteria: Canary tests show correct fallback behavior and billing label 'downgraded' in telemetry.

E. Observability and telemetry
  - Requirement E1: New telemetry fields: variant_id, add_on_ids, price_modifier_basispts, billed_unit_cost, routing_decision.
  - Acceptance criteria: Dashboards show per-variant token consumption and add-on revenue.

Implementation details and examples:
- Catalog schema (prose):
  - variant_id: string (eg vm-int8-202609)
  - variant_type: one of [base, quantized, distilled, latency-optimized, safety-filtered]
  - cost_index: numeric relative cost factor compared to base (1.0 = base)
  - expected_quality_delta_pct: -2.5 (meaning expected 2.5% quality delta vs base)
  - compatibility_matrix: list of model families and max_seq_length

- Example API usage (Console and SDK):
  - REST body example (represented as text):
    POST /v1/requests
    {
      "model": "redwood-alpha-13b",
      "variant_selector": {"variant_id":"vm-int8-202609","add_ons":["warm_pool_hour","premium_routing"]},
      "input": "summarize: ..."
    }
  - Expected response includes: request_id, chosen_variant_id, billed_unit_cost (per-token)

Pricing matrix (example):
- Variant-level modifiers (relative to base rate):
  | Variant type           | Cost modifier | Typical use case                     |
  |------------------------|---------------|---------------------------------------|
  | base (fp16)            | 0 bps         | Standard production                    |
  | int8-quant             | -30%          | Cost-sensitive batching workloads     |
  | int4-quant             | -45%          | High volume, tolerant quality loss    |
  | distilled-small        | -55%          | UI companion responses, cheap reps    |
  | latency-optimized      | +40%          | Real-time assistants, sub-150ms SLO   |
  | safety-filtered        | +10%          | Regulated outputs with extra checks   |

- Add-on SKUs (examples):
  | SKU                  | Meter type    | Unit price example | Billing rule             |
  |----------------------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
  | warm_pool_hour       | per-hour      | $0.30 / vCPU-hour  | prorated hourly per-tenant|
  | premium_routing_hour | per-hour      | $1.50 / tenant-hr  | only billed if routed to premium pool|
  | reduced_token_cost_pct | percentage  | policy-based       | reduces base token price by X% for route|

Billing behavior rules (examples):
1) If a request is chosen for variant vm-int8-202609 and uses premium_routing, line items: tokens_used @ base_rate * (1-0.30) and add-on: premium_routing_hour prorated.
2) If premium routing fails and fallback occurs, mark request as billed_with_fallback and use fallback variant modifier. Audit trail must show original requested_variant and resolved_variant.

Console mock flow (text):
1) Deployment -> Edit -> Variant selector: choose tab "Marketplace".
2) Filter by Cost or Latency -> pick variant -> optional checkboxes for add-ons.
3) Confirm changes; preview expected per-request unit cost.

Rollout plan and timeline (12 weeks post-approval):
- Week 0-2: Schema, billing pipeline prototyping, and Console mocks. Owner: Product + Billing infra.
- Week 3-5: Backend implementation (catalog, API changes) + runtime routing hooks. Owner: Eng-serving-runtime.
- Week 6-8: End-to-end integration, billing sandbox, and telemetry dashboards. Owner: Eng-platform + Finance.
- Week 9-10: Pilot with 3 customers (account managers + customer success). Collect feedback and reconcile billing.
- Week 11-12: GA rollout, documentation, self-serve enablement, and SDK updates.

Operational playbook (during pilot):
1) Enable feature flag per-tenant. 2) Monitor telemetry dashboard for billed_unit_cost anomalies. 3) Finance runs daily batch invoice reconciliation for pilot tenants. 4) Roll back feature flag if billing mismatch > 0.1% for 48h.

Open questions and trade-offs:
- Price modeling: use percentage modifiers vs absolute per-token delta. Percentages are easier for multi-model families but can be less precise for extremely large models.
- Warm pool allocation: dedicated warm pools per-tenant vs shared premium pools with tagging. Dedicated pools give predictable perf but higher fixed cost.
- Variant discovery: include quality evaluations and client-facing examples in Console or link to separate eval pages?

Risk matrix and mitigations:
- Risk: Incorrect billing mapping between variant and line item -> Mitigation: End-to-end sandbox reconciliation and daily alert on mismatches.
- Risk: Customers accidentally select a high-cost variant -> Mitigation: Console shows warning and needs 2 clicks to confirm for variants that increase cost by >25%.
- Risk: Performance regressions on quantized variants -> Mitigation: model-onboarding must include perf/quality gates in quantization-profiles pipeline.

Ownership and stakeholders:
- Product owner: Ava Chen (product)
- Engineering owners: Runtime (Kiran Patel), Billing infra (Diego Morales), Console (Hannah Park)
- Finance reviewer: Priya Iyer
- Customer success: Maya Singh (pilot coordination)

Related artifacts and references:
- Catalog model-onboarding checklist: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding
- Billing schema spec: /confluence/finance-and-legal/contracts-and-legal-process/billing-schema-spec
- Quantization profiles and test harness: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles

Appendix: Example reconciliation checklist for pilot customers:
- Nightly job compares billed line items to telemetry token counts grouped by variant_id.
- Tolerance threshold: 0.1% token mismatch; if exceeded notify product + finance and pause feature for tenant.

Notes:
- This PRD intentionally separates catalog discovery (product-facing) from long-term marketplace commerce (third-party models). Phase 2 will evaluate an open marketplace after core billing and telemetry are stable.

title: Automated Degradation Classifier and Customer Failover Runbook
preview:
Overview

This runbook describes the automated degradation classifier (ADC) used by Customer Success and SRE to detect service-quality regressions for enterprise tenants, validate safe failover eligibility, and orchestrate tiered customer notifications and remediation. It documents classification thresholds, telemetry mappings, alert routing, escalation windows, and step-by-step procedures for automated and manual remediation.

Goals

- Detect real customer-impacting degradation (latency, error-rate, model-quality drift) with <80% false-positive rate in steady-state traffic.
- Automate non-destructive mitigations (routing to fallback model variants, traffic shaping) within 5 minutes of classification.
- Provide clear, auditable customer communications and playbook for escalation to engineering/ops.

Scope

- Applies to Dedicated and Private tenants and high-volume Hosted API customers with SLOs.
- Covers: inference latency P95, error rate, model-generation-quality regression (eval score deltas), KV cache hit-rate, and telemetry signal health (ingest gaps).
- Out of scope: customer billing disputes, hardware-only incidents covered by cloud-provider runbooks.

Telemetry and Signals

Key signals the ADC consumes (priority order):
- p95_inference_latency_ms (per-tenant, 1m/5m windows)
- request_error_rate_pct (HTTP 5xx + model-serve-specific rejects)
- model_eval_delta_pct (per-customer prompt-set baseline vs rolling 24h)
- kvcache_hit_rate_pct (prefix/KV reuse)
- telemetry_ingest_lag_sec (agent/sidecar -> collector)
- synthetic_user_synthetic_score (synthetic journeys score: 0-1)

Signal mapping and derived metrics (table):
| Metric | Window | Threshold (class = degraded) | Notes |
| p95_inference_latency_ms | 5m | >= 1200 ms and delta >= 2x baseline | Baselines stored per-tenant in telemetry-store |
| request_error_rate_pct | 5m | >= 1.5% absolute or 3x baseline | Exclude client-side timeouts |
| model_eval_delta_pct | 24h | >= 7% absolute drop on evaluation suite | Use tenant-specific eval prompt-set |
| kvcache_hit_rate_pct | 10m | drop >= 30% from baseline | Indicates cache invalidation/regression |
| telemetry_ingest_lag_sec | 2m | > 60s | May indicate sidecar/drop or pipeline issue |

Classification logic (simplified):
1) Ingest real-time signals into ADC engine (stream).
2) Compute z-score of each metric vs baseline; apply weights: latency 0.35, error-rate 0.3, model-eval 0.25, kvcache 0.05, ingest-lag 0.05.
3) If weighted score >= 0.7 AND at least two signals exceed thresholds, label tenant_state = "degraded".
4) Run fast deterministic checks (synthetic journey drop, region outage markers).

False-positive protection and cooldowns

- Require sustained classification for 2 consecutive 1-minute intervals before any automated customer-impacting action.
- If classification flips back to healthy within 10 minutes, cancel automated mitigation and create an advisory event only.
- Max automated mitigation frequency per tenant: 3/hr.

Automated Mitigation Options (ordered by least-intrusive first)

1) Lightweight routing fallback: switch small percentage (10-25%) of traffic to a lower-capacity but stable model variant for 3–10 minutes.
2) Dynamic batching adjustment: temporarily increase batching window for background, non-low-latency routes.
3) KV cache warmup prefetch for affected model shards (if kvcache hit-rate drop detected).
4) Regional failover: redirect traffic to secondary region if regional capacity or infra flags are present.
5) Tenant soft-throttle: shed best-effort/lowest-priority routes (e.g., embeddings bulk jobs) while preserving interactive tokens.

Each mitigation must be reversible via the ADC control plane and is subject to TTL and canary rollback.

Escalation Matrix and Ownership

- ADC Alert level 1 (detect only): Customer Success on-call paged by PagerDuty; SRE watch notified (Slack #sre-observe) — 15 minute SLAs for initial triage.
- ADC Alert level 2 (automated mitigation triggered): SRE & Serving Runtime on-call paged; CS notified for customer comms — 10 minute SLA for mitigation validation.
- ADC Alert level 3 (automated mitigation failed or model-quality regression persisted >30m): Incident Commander activated, full incident playbook (see related_pages for incident process).

Routing to escalation teams (table):
| Condition | Primary Team | Secondary Team | Escalation Channel | PagerDuty Policy |
| latency-only | eng-serving-runtime | eng-infra | #serving-runtime | PD-policy-serving |
| error-rate | eng-sre | eng-serving-runtime | #sre-oncall | PD-policy-sre |
| model-eval-delta | applied-ml-and-evals | eng-serving-runtime | #applied-ml | PD-policy-ml |
| telemetry-ingest | eng-infra | customer-success | #telemetry | PD-policy-infra |

Customer Communications (templates)

- Advisory (detect only): "We are monitoring an elevated latency signal affecting X% of your requests and are investigating. No action has been taken. We will follow up within 15 minutes."
- Mitigation notice (automated action): "We detected a service degradation and routed a small percentage of traffic to a stable model variant to preserve availability. We will continue to monitor and notify you of material changes."
- Escalation/update: "Mitigation attempted succeeded/rolled back. Next step: engineering investigation underway; ETA 60 minutes for next update."

All outbound customer messages are logged to the customer-notification-audit stream with template_id and PD-incident id.

Operational Runbook: Step-by-step

1) Detection (automatic)
  a) ADC marks tenant_state = degraded.
  b) ADC posts event to incident-bridge and creates internal ticket (prefix ADC-<tenant>-<ts>).
  c) PagerDuty route triggers based on classification.
2) Initial human triage (CS or SRE) — within 15 minutes
  a) Validate telemetry (run provided queries below).
  b) Check for known issues: maintenance, provider alerts, model rollout windows.
  c) If false positive (e.g., client-side spike), tag ticket and resolve; annotate ADC baseline if needed.
3) If mitigation allowed (policy check) and classification sustained -> ADC triggers mitigation.
4) Post-mitigation verification (5–10 minutes)
  a) Verify p95 latency and error-rate returned to within 90% of baseline.
  b) If improved, send Mitigation notice to customer; schedule 1-hour monitoring window.
  c) If not improved, escalate to Level 3.
5) Full incident (if escalated)
  a) Run full incident template (see oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process).
  b) Capture RCA inputs: model binary, kernel config, KV cache eviction logs, batch window changes.

Observability Queries and Debug Helpers

- Example Prometheus-like queries:
  p95_inference_latency_ms{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[5m]
  sum(rate(request_errors_total{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total{tenant="TENANT_ID"}[5m])) * 100
  kvcache_hit_rate_pct{tenant="TENANT_ID"}
- Synthetic journey probe: synthetic_probe_status{tenant="TENANT_ID",journey="interactive-check"}
- Model-eval delta: fetch latest eval-run and compare to tenant-baseline stored in eval-store: eval_store.query(tenant_id=..., window=24h)

Diagnostic artifacts to collect for RCA

- ADC classification payload (raw signals and weights)
- Serving runtime shard logs for affected model version (last 60 minutes)
- Kernel scheduling trace (if latency spike)
- KV cache eviction and rewarm traces
- Customer request-id traces (correlated via request-correlation-id header)

Policy and Access Controls

- Only SRE and CS on-call roles can approve automated mitigations for regulated tenants.
- ADC control plane actions are auditable and logged to the CIAM audit stream for 13 months.
- Make/Model variant list that ADC may route to is controlled by Applied ML (whitelist per-tenant).

Testing and Validation

- Quarterly game-days: induce synthetic degradations for 10 tenants across Dedicated and Private modes to validate ADC detection and mitigation runbook.
- Canary tests: ADC changes must pass a canary window of 48h in staging with monitored false-positive rate < 8%.

Appendix: Example alert payload (JSON)

{
  "tenant_id": "tenant_acme_inc",
  "timestamp": "2026-07-15T10:12:34Z",
  "signals": {
    "p95_inference_latency_ms": 1850,
    "request_error_rate_pct": 2.1,
    "model_eval_delta_pct": 8.4
  },
  "weighted_score": 0.78,
  "classification": "degraded",
  "recommended_action": "lightweight-routing-fallback",
  "ticket_id": "ADC-tenant_acme_inc-20260715T101234Z"
}

Appendix: Quick lookup checklist (one-page)

- Is ADC classification sustained for 2 consecutive minutes?
- Does telemetry show at least two signals above threshold?
- Has the tenant reached automated mitigation cap (3/hr)?
- Is tenant regulated (manual approval required)?
- If mitigation: monitor 5–10 minutes, then notify customer.

Related pages and references

- Incident response: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process
- Telemetry schema and baselines: /confluence/eng-infra/observability-standards/telemetry-schema-v2
- Model rollback playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/model-rollback-guidelines

Owners and contacts

- Document owner: Ava Chen (Customer Success)
- ADC engineering owner: Miguel Sanchez (eng-serving-runtime)
- SRE lead for escalations: Daniel Park (eng-sre)

Change log

- 2026-03-10: Created (Ava Chen).
- 2026-05-02: Added KV cache mitigation and updated thresholds (review: Ravi Menon).
- 2026-07-15: Published stabilization SLA clarifications and updated sample payload example (review: Daniel Park, Sofia Patel).

- Quarterly game-days: induce synthetic degradations for 10 tenants across Dedicated and Private modes to validate ADC detection and mitigation runbook.

Appendix: Example alert payload (JSON)

{
  "tenant_id": "tenant_acme_inc",
  "timestamp": "2026-07-15T10:12:34Z",
  "signals": {
    "p95_inference_latency_ms": 1850,
    "request_error_rate_pct": 2.1,
    "model_eval_delta_pct": 8.4
  },
  "weighted_score": 0.78,
  "classification": "degraded",
  "recommended_action": "lightweight-routing-fallback",
  "ticket_id": "ADC-tenant_acme_inc-20260715T101234Z"
}

Appendix: Quick lookup checklist (one-page)

- Is ADC classification sustained for 2 consecutive minutes?
- Does telemetry show at least two signals above threshold?
- Has the tenant reached automated mitigation cap (3/hr)?
- Is tenant regulated (manual approval required)?
- If mitigation: monitor 5–10 minutes, then notify customer.

Related pages and references

- Incident response: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process
- Telemetry schema and baselines: /confluence/eng-infra/observability-standards/telemetry-schema-v2
- Model rollback playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/model-rollback-guidelines

Owners and contacts

- Document owner: Ava Chen (Customer Success)
- ADC engineering owner: Miguel Sanchez (eng-serving-runtime)
- SRE lead for escalations: Daniel Park (eng-sre)

Change log

- 2026-03-10: Created (Ava Chen).
- 2026-05-02: Added KV cache mitigation and updated thresholds (review: Ravi Menon).
- 2026-07-15: Published stabilization SLA clarifications and updated sample payload example (review: Daniel Park, Sofia Patel).

title: New Engineer Autonomy and Success Blueprint
preview:
Summary
-------
This blueprint defines the end-to-end process to move a newly hired engineer from first-day orientation to independent contributor with measurable autonomy and impact. It covers the hiring handoff, staged onboarding milestones (30/60/90/180 days), manager and buddy responsibilities, performance and feedback cadence, role-readiness rubric, accommodations, and operational templates to standardize successful activation across teams.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all software engineering hires (IC levels L1–L6) across hosted, dedicated, and private product teams.
- Excludes contractor-only engagements where a separate contractor activation process exists (see related contractor playbook).
- Intended for hiring managers, people-ops partners, technical buddies, and platform/infra owners who contribute to onboarding.

Objectives and success criteria
-----------------------------
1. Reduce average time-to-autonomy (TTA) measured as the median days until a new hire independently completes a deliverable without step-by-step assistance. Target: 90 days for mid-level hires, 180 for senior cross-team roles.
2. Increase first-quarter calibration accuracy: managers' readiness assessment aligned with peer-review and code-review signals > 80%.
3. Maintain new hire satisfaction NPS >= +40 at day 30 and day 90 surveys.

How this document is organized
-----------------------------
1. Hiring-to-Day-0 handoff checklist
2. 30/60/90/180 milestone plan (including acceptance criteria)
3. Manager and buddy responsibilities
4. Onboarding task checklist (technical and non-technical)
5. Performance & feedback cadence and templates
6. Role-readiness rubric and evidence guidelines
7. Accommodations, escalations, and remediation paths
8. Metrics, dashboards, and quarterly review triggers
9. Templates and links (code access, security training, payroll, equipment)

1) Hiring-to-Day-0 handoff checklist
-----------------------------------
Manager responsibilities (pre-start, due at least 5 working days before start):
- Confirm offer details, start date, and first-week agenda with candidate.
- Submit equipment and access requests: laptop spec, peripheral needs, VPN, KMS access, cloud billing account assignment.
- Create a 30-day plan draft and share it with people-ops and the assigned buddy.
- Add new-hire to team calendar invites for orientation, team retro, and product demo.
- Assign a technical buddy and schedule a 90-minute Day-1 deep-dive.

People-ops responsibilities (pre-start):
- Send welcome packet (logistics, benefits links, tax forms) and first-week checklist.
- Kick off security/compliance training enrollment (must-complete by day 7).
- Coordinate relocation or remote-stipend logistics if applicable.

Technical buddy responsibilities (pre-start):
- Verify onboarding repo links, runbook access, and recommended reading list are up to date.
- Prepare starter ticket (low-risk bug or doc edit) for day 7–14.

2) 30/60/90/180 milestone plan and acceptance criteria
-----------------------------------------------------
Milestone: Day 0–30 (Orientation & Setup)
- Goals:
  - Full network and repo access, CI credentials, and IDE toolchain configured.
  - Complete security/compliance training and basic infra walkthrough.
  - Submit first non-production PR (doc, small bugfix).
- Acceptance criteria:
  - All required accounts active and verified in onboarding tracker.
  - Buddy confirms first PR merged or constructive feedback given.
  - Day-30 pulse survey returned.

Milestone: Day 31–60 (Contribution & Collaboration)
- Goals:
  - Independently own one small user-facing route or feature bug.
  - Present a short demo at a team sync.
  - First 1:1 reflection with manager resulting in a development plan.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Demonstrated pipeline from local dev to CI to staging.
  - Code review feedback incorporated and peer-reviewed by two teammates.

Milestone: Day 61–90 (Autonomy & Impact)
- Goals:
  - Deliver a scoped feature or optimization from spec to merged PR with ownership of rollout plan.
  - Begin pairing on cross-team work or integrations.
  - Establish recurring 1:1 rhythm and career conversation.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Manager and buddy independently endorse "role-ready" for routine tasks using the role-readiness rubric.
  - Positive signals in telemetry (reduced review turnaround, increased commit independence).

Milestone: Day 91–180 (Cross-team scope & Leadership foundations)
- Goals:
  - Lead a small cross-functional initiative or reduce a documented production pain point by >25% in latency/cost/error rate.
  - Mentor a new onboarder or run a knowledge-sharing session.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Evidence of broader ownership in roadmap tickets and architecture notes.
  - Promotion readiness floor met for senior hires (if applicable).

3) Manager and buddy responsibilities (detailed)
------------------------------------------------
Manager (ongoing):
- Create and update the 30/60/90/180 plan within the team onboarding template.
- Hold weekly 1:1s (day 7–90 recommended cadence) aimed at removing blockers and calibrating expectations.
- Run a formal 90-day readout: document outcomes, learning, and next-phase goals.
- Provide immediate, concrete feedback within 48 hours of any missed acceptance criteria.

Buddy (ongoing):
- Serve as the first escalation for day-to-day technical questions for first 90 days.
- Provide guided code reviews and pair-program weekly for first 30 days.
- Validate completion of checklist items and mark acceptance criteria as met in the onboarding tracker.

4) Onboarding task checklist (to be tracked in the onboarding tracker)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Technical access:
- Cloud console access (AWS/GCP) – IAM role noted
- Repo access (git, monorepo path), commit signing configured
- CI credentials and developer sandbox created
- Local stack and infrastructure automation run (script: scripts/bootstrap.sh)

Security & compliance:
- Complete security training module 1 and 2 (enrollment link)
- Sign data handling and NDA acknowledgements
- Register device with endpoint management (MDM/HSM)

Operational & product:
- Join product channel(s), read product PRD and current roadmap summary
- Shadow customer-support queue for two 2-hour blocks during week 2

People & culture:
- Schedule intro 1:1s with cross-functional partners (SRE, Product, Sales)
- Review company values and code of conduct; complete the culture exercise in the welcome packet

5) Performance & feedback cadence and templates
-----------------------------------------------
Recommended cadence:
- Weekly manager 1:1s (first 90 days direct focus on ramp impediments)
- Bi-weekly buddy syncs (peer feedback and pairing)
- Day-30 and Day-90 structured pulse surveys (people-ops) with automated reminders
- 90-day readout meeting: manager, buddy, people-ops partner present

Feedback templates (short form):
- "Observed behavior": [concrete example]
- "Impact": [user/product/infra/people impact]
- "Suggested next step": [concrete, time-bound suggestion]

Calibrations:
- At day 90, the manager completes a role-readiness rubric and shares it with hiring manager and people-ops for calibration when cross-team work was expected.

6) Role-readiness rubric and evidence guidelines
------------------------------------------------
Rubric dimensions (each rated 1–5):
- Technical competence (coding, architecture patterns)
- Independent ownership (can deliver scoped pieces end-to-end)
- Collaboration (PR reviews, cross-team communication)
- Reliability (on-call readiness, SLA understanding)
- Growth mindset (incorporates feedback, learning cadence)

Evidence examples (accepted artifacts):
- Merged PRs with clear changelogs and test coverage
- Architecture notes or ADR contributions
- Post-release monitoring dashboards showing reduced error trends
- Peer feedback excerpts from 2+ teammates

7) Accommodations, escalations, and remediation paths
-----------------------------------------------------
Reasonable accommodations:
- People-ops coordinates accommodations for disability access, adjusted start dates, or equipment needs. Managers must not ask for medical details; direct to people-ops.

Escalation levels:
- Level 1 (team-level): manager + buddy; target resolution 5 working days.
- Level 2 (people-ops involvement): People-ops partner coordinates and documents adjustments; target 10 working days.
- Level 3 (formal performance remediation): Initiate Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) per performance policy with HR/legal review; see templates in related pages.

8) Metrics, dashboards, and quarterly review triggers
----------------------------------------------------
Primary metrics to track (dashboard maintained by people-ops):
- Time-to-autonomy distribution (median, 75th percentile) broken down by role and seniority
- Day-30 and Day-90 NPS and qualitative themes
- Onboarding checklist completion rate by day 7 and day 30
- New-hire bug/rollback rate for first 90 days (normalized by team size)

Quarterly triggers:
- If median TTA > target for two consecutive quarters for a hiring cohort, trigger a cross-functional onboarding retro and remediation plan.

9) Templates and links (appendix)
----------------------------------
- 30/60/90 plan template: /confluence/templates/30-60-90-onboarding-template
- Onboarding tracker: internal spreadsheet link (private)
- New-hire PR starter ticket template: .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATES/onboarding.md
- Security training enrollment: /learning/security/mandatory
- Equipment request form: /it/equipment-requests

Appendix A: Example 90-day readout agenda
-----------------------------------------
1. 5 min - Manager opening: objectives and current state
2. 10 min - New hire demo: show what was built or improved
3. 10 min - Buddy observations and evidence (PRs, dashboards)
4. 15 min - Manager and new-hire calibration on next-phase goals
5. 10 min - People-ops notes and final acceptance status ("On track", "Needs support", "Remediation")
6. Action items and owners (published to the onboarding tracker)

Appendix B: Quick-start checklist for remote hires (first 72 hours)
------------------------------------------------------------------
- Day 0: Welcome packet, account activation, schedule 1:1 with manager and buddy (30m each)
- Day 1: Orientation session, MDM enrollment, run bootstrap.sh with buddy over Zoom
- Day 2–3: Take starter ticket, join product demo, complete security module 1

Ownership and maintenance
------------------------
- Primary owner: people-ops (onboarding program) – Ava Chen
- Secondary owners: hiring manager and technical buddy assigned per hire
- Review cadence: this blueprint must be reviewed annually or after any major changes to the platform onboarding flow (e.g., new IAM model, VPC onboarding changes).

Feedback on this blueprint
-------------------------
Send suggestions or improvement requests to people-ops-feedback@redwood.ai with subject: "Onboarding blueprint feedback". Significant changes require reviewer sign-off listed at the top of this page.

End of document
