title: Dependency Failure Resilience Playbook (Enterprise Onboarding)
preview:
Purpose

This playbook documents a standardized approach for identifying, preventing, and remediating failures caused by third-party or internal dependency outages during enterprise onboarding and early go-live. It focuses on the technical mitigations, telemetry assertions, and communication patterns Customer Success (CS), SRE, and Platform teams should follow when dependencies degrade or fail.

Scope

- Applies to enterprise onboarding windows, canary rollouts, and first-72-hour post-go-live stability runs.
- Covers external partners (cloud provider APIs, SCM, identity providers, SaaS connectors) and internal services we rely on (ingress-gateway, fanout-proxy, telemetry-gatekeeper, embedding-indexer, kvcache, model-router).
- Does NOT cover purely customer-side outages (e.g., customer DB down) except where we coordinate cutover.

Owners and RACI

- Primary owner: Customer Success (Onboarding) — Ava Chen
- Technical owner (SRE escalation): Eng SRE — Diego Morales
- Platform mitigations and config changes: Eng Platform — Priya Natarajan
- Observability contract owner: Eng DevEx — Samir Gupta

RACI summary:
1) Detection: Eng SRE (R), Eng DevEx (C), CS (I)
2) Triage/Customer comms: CS (R), Eng SRE (A)
3) Mitigation (routing/fallback): Eng Platform (R/A)
4) Postmortem & action items: Eng SRE (R), CS (C)

Pre-Go-Live Dependency Map Checklist (must be completed before go-live gate)

1. Dependency inventory: maintain a single table with service name, owner, SLA (external), API endpoints, auth method, and last verified test run.
2. Telemetry assertions: add at least three assertion checks for each dependency (latency, error rate, presence of expected metrics).
3. Fallback compatibility: confirm an alternate path exists for each critical dependency (e.g., cached read, degraded feature, queued processing).
4. Runbook verification: ensure a runbook paragraph exists for each item with detection signal and mitigation steps.
5. Communication template: verify customer comms and internal incident templates are available and signed-off.

Dependency Resilience Matrix (example table)

| Dependency | Criticality | SLO tie | Failure modes | Primary detection signal | Immediate mitigation | Owner |
|-----------|-------------|---------|---------------|--------------------------|----------------------|-------|
| ingress-gateway | P0 | 99.9% availability | TLS handshake failures, 502 spikes | 5xx rate > 0.5% over 5m, TLS errors spike | Reroute to standby gateway, enable traffic-shaping to slow new sessions | Eng Platform (Priya) |
| telemetry-gatekeeper | P0 | Inference telemetry 1h TTL | Missing spans, dropped metrics | Missing ingestion heartbeat for 2m, sample rate drop | Fallback to passive buffering sidecar; enable synchronous flush | Eng DevEx (Samir) |
| fanout-proxy | P1 | 99% throughput | Queue growth, per-tenant blocking | Queue depth > 80% capacity for 3m | Enable per-tenant throttles, route low-priority tenants to degraded path | Eng Platform |
| external-idp (SCIM) | P2 | External SLA 99.5% | 429/403 from idp | auth failures or 429s > 1% | Switch to cached user mapping; pause provisioning jobs | Customer Success |
| embedding-indexer (internal) | P1 | Indexing SLA 95% | Index lag, OOM | indexing-lag > 10m | Apply incremental backfill window, drop non-critical batches | Eng Serving Runtime |
| model-router | P0 | Serving SLA 99.95% | Model load failures, KV cache misses | model load errors, cache miss rate > 15% | Switch to compatible quantized variant, enable cache warmup | Eng Serving Runtime |

Operational Runbook (detection -> mitigation -> validate)

1. Detection (first 5 minutes)
   - Watchers: SRE alerting channels receive automated signals (PagerDuty, Slack #sre-ops).
   - Initial checks: run ./tools/check-dependency-health --service <name> to confirm alarm.

2. Triage (5–20 minutes)
   - Assign a triage lead (SRE) and a customer liaison (CS).
   - Confirm blast radius: run tenant impact query (example):

     curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $MONITOR_API" "https://telemetry-api.prod/tenant-impact?service=fanout-proxy&window=10m" | jq .

   - If impact > threshold (defined below) escalate to on-call Eng Platform.

3. Immediate mitigations (20–60 minutes)
   - If ingress-gateway is degraded: flip to standby gateway with the CLI:

     rwdctl gateway failover --from ingress-gw-us-west-2 --to ingress-gw-us-east-1 --reason "onboarding surge"

   - If telemetry-gatekeeper ingestion is missing: enable buffering and start a fast-backfill:

     kubectl -n telemetry apply -f buffering-sidecar-override.yaml
     ./tools/backfill-telemetry --start $(date -u -d '15 minutes ago' +%s) --end $(date -u +%s)

   - If model-router fails to load a quantized variant: re-route traffic to fallback model:

     rwdctl model route --from model:prod:v2 --to model:prod:quantized-v1 --percent 100 --force

4. Validate (after 60 minutes)
   - Confirm telemetry asserts: all dependency assertions pass for 10 consecutive minutes.
   - Confirm SLO posture re-evaluated for impacted tenants and document customer-facing impact.

Escalation thresholds (examples)

- Severity P0: Any customer-facing error rate causing >1% 5xx across the tenant fleet or single large tenant with >0.5% 5xx for 10m. PagerDuty P0, incident bridge, executive notify.
- Severity P1: Throughput drops causing queue growth beyond 50% for >15m. PagerDuty P1; CS alerts impacted customers.
- Severity P2: Auxiliary systems (SCIM, billing connector) errors causing background job failures. Email + Slack notify; CS to log and monitor.

Customer Communication templates

- Initial acknowledgement (under 15 minutes):
  "We’ve detected a degradation affecting [feature/service]. Our engineering team is triaging. We will provide an update within 30 minutes and a root-cause timeline within 4 hours.

  Impact: [brief impact].
  Mitigation: [what we’re doing].
  Contact: your CS rep, [name], + link to status page."

- Mitigation update (30–90 minutes):
  "Summary: We identified [dependency] as the primary cause. We’ve applied [mitigation] which restored service for X% of requests. Engineers continue to monitor and will follow up with next steps by [time]."

Telemetry Assertions (examples to add to onboarding checks)

1. Ingestion heartbeat: telemetry-gatekeeper.heartbeat == true in last 1m
2. Model load success rate: model-router.load_success_rate >= 99.9% over 5m
3. KV cache hit ratio: kvcache.hit_ratio >= 85% over 10m

Add these to the onboarding synthetic suite; example JSON snippet for assertion: 

{
  "assertion_name": "kvcache_hit_ratio_onboarding",
  "expr": "avg(kvcache_hit_ratio{env=\"prod\",stage=\"onboarding\"}) > 0.85",
  "window": "10m",
  "owner": "eng-serving-runtime"
}

Canary Failure Simulation (run prior to go-live)

1. Simulate partial dependency failure: throttle upstream responses to 50% for 10 minutes using traffic-shaper job.
2. Verify that fallback paths engage within target time (<= 30s) and degraded responses are metric-tagged.
3. Run customer communication dry-run: ask CS to publish the initial acknowledgement and verify internal observers see it.

Post-Incident Walkthrough and Action Items (within 72h)

- Convene triage + CS + platform; capture timeline and mitigation chronology.
- Generate action items: code fix, observability gaps, config changes, customer compensation if SLA breach. Assign owners and target dates.
- Update dependency map and onboarding checklist with any missed validations.

Common Failure Mode Playbooks (short form)

- Telemetry ingestion drop
  1) Buffer sidecar enablement
  2) Backfill telemetry for the gap window
  3) Postmortem: add assertion and test to synthetic suite

- External SCIM throttling
  1) Pause provisioning
  2) Switch to cached identity mapping for auth flows
  3) Retry queue with exponential backoff and jitter

- KV cache cold start leading to latency spike
  1) Warm cache using sample keyset: ./tools/warm-kvcache --tenant <tenant-id> --keys-file warm-keys.json
  2) If warmfail, route to model variant with smaller memory footprint

Appendix: Useful commands and references

- rwdctl status dependencies --tenant <id> --since 15m\- shows dependency health summary\- 
- ./tools/check-dependency-health --service telemetry-gatekeeper --verbose\- detailed probe for the service\-
- Example buffering-sidecar-override.yaml (snippet):

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: telemetry-buffer-override
data:
  buffer.max_bytes: "50000000"
  flush.interval_s: "10"

Appendix: Postmortem checklist template (to attach to incident)

- Incident title and severity
- Detection time, mitigation applied, time to recovery
- Impacted tenants (list) and customer-facing communication timeline
- Root cause and contributing factors
- Concrete action items (owner, due date, verification test)

FAQ (short)

Q: When should CS ask for credits or SLA remedies?
A: Follow the existing SLA reconciliation playbook — if the incident breaches the contracted uptime or per-tenant SLO defined in the contract, CS should initiate the billing remediation flow after the postmortem is complete.

Q: Can we always failover to another region?
A: Not always. Region-based failover is a last-resort for data residency or private deployment customers. Follow the regional failover checklist and confirm customer consent where required.

Related pages and references

- Onboarding failure mode scenarios (linked internally)
- Canary simulation playbook (Eng SRE)
- SLA remediation and billing process

This playbook is intended to be a living document. Update the dependency matrix and telemetry assertions after every simulation and postmortem.
text:
Purpose

This playbook documents a standardized approach for identifying, preventing, and remediating failures caused by third-party or internal dependency outages during enterprise onboarding and early go-live. It focuses on the technical mitigations, telemetry assertions, and communication patterns Customer Success (CS), SRE, and Platform teams should follow when dependencies degrade or fail.

Scope

- Applies to enterprise onboarding windows, canary rollouts, and first-72-hour post-go-live stability runs.
- Covers external partners (cloud provider APIs, SCM, identity providers, SaaS connectors) and internal services we rely on (ingress-gateway, fanout-proxy, telemetry-gatekeeper, embedding-indexer, kvcache, model-router).
- Does NOT cover purely customer-side outages (e.g., customer DB down) except where we coordinate cutover.

Owners and RACI

- Primary owner: Customer Success (Onboarding) — Ava Chen
- Technical owner (SRE escalation): Eng SRE — Diego Morales
- Platform mitigations and config changes: Eng Platform — Priya Natarajan
- Observability contract owner: Eng DevEx — Samir Gupta

RACI summary:
1) Detection: Eng SRE (R), Eng DevEx (C), CS (I)
2) Triage/Customer comms: CS (R), Eng SRE (A)
3) Mitigation (routing/fallback): Eng Platform (R/A)
4) Postmortem & action items: Eng SRE (R), CS (C)

Pre-Go-Live Dependency Map Checklist (must be completed before go-live gate)

1. Dependency inventory: maintain a single table with service name, owner, SLA (external), API endpoints, auth method, and last verified test run.
2. Telemetry assertions: add at least three assertion checks for each dependency (latency, error rate, presence of expected metrics).
3. Fallback compatibility: confirm an alternate path exists for each critical dependency (e.g., cached read, degraded feature, queued processing).
4. Runbook verification: ensure a runbook paragraph exists for each item with detection signal and mitigation steps.
5. Communication template: verify customer comms and internal incident templates are available and signed-off.

Dependency Resilience Matrix (example table)

| Dependency | Criticality | SLO tie | Failure modes | Primary detection signal | Immediate mitigation | Owner |
|-----------|-------------|---------|---------------|--------------------------|----------------------|-------|
| ingress-gateway | P0 | 99.9% availability | TLS handshake failures, 502 spikes | 5xx rate > 0.5% over 5m, TLS errors spike | Reroute to standby gateway, enable traffic-shaping to slow new sessions | Eng Platform (Priya) |
| telemetry-gatekeeper | P0 | Inference telemetry 1h TTL | Missing spans, dropped metrics | Missing ingestion heartbeat for 2m, sample rate drop | Fallback to passive buffering sidecar; enable synchronous flush | Eng DevEx (Samir) |
| fanout-proxy | P1 | 99% throughput | Queue growth, per-tenant blocking | Queue depth > 80% capacity for 3m | Enable per-tenant throttles, route low-priority tenants to degraded path | Eng Platform |
| external-idp (SCIM) | P2 | External SLA 99.5% | 429/403 from idp | auth failures or 429s > 1% | Switch to cached user mapping; pause provisioning jobs | Customer Success |
| embedding-indexer (internal) | P1 | Indexing SLA 95% | Index lag, OOM | indexing-lag > 10m | Apply incremental backfill window, drop non-critical batches | Eng Serving Runtime |
| model-router | P0 | Serving SLA 99.95% | Model load failures, KV cache misses | model load errors, cache miss rate > 15% | Switch to compatible quantized variant, enable cache warmup | Eng Serving Runtime |

Operational Runbook (detection -> mitigation -> validate)

1. Detection (first 5 minutes)
   - Watchers: SRE alerting channels receive automated signals (PagerDuty, Slack #sre-ops).
   - Initial checks: run ./tools/check-dependency-health --service <name> to confirm alarm.

2. Triage (5–20 minutes)
   - Assign a triage lead (SRE) and a customer liaison (CS).
   - Confirm blast radius: run tenant impact query (example):

     curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $MONITOR_API" "https://telemetry-api.prod/tenant-impact?service=fanout-proxy&window=10m" | jq .

   - If impact > threshold (defined below) escalate to on-call Eng Platform.

3. Immediate mitigations (20–60 minutes)
   - If ingress-gateway is degraded: flip to standby gateway with the CLI:

     rwdctl gateway failover --from ingress-gw-us-west-2 --to ingress-gw-us-east-1 --reason "onboarding surge"

   - If telemetry-gatekeeper ingestion is missing: enable buffering and start a fast-backfill:

     kubectl -n telemetry apply -f buffering-sidecar-override.yaml
     ./tools/backfill-telemetry --start $(date -u -d '15 minutes ago' +%s) --end $(date -u +%s)

   - If model-router fails to load a quantized variant: re-route traffic to fallback model:

     rwdctl model route --from model:prod:v2 --to model:prod:quantized-v1 --percent 100 --force

4. Validate (after 60 minutes)
   - Confirm telemetry asserts: all dependency assertions pass for 10 consecutive minutes.
   - Confirm SLO posture re-evaluated for impacted tenants and document customer-facing impact.

Escalation thresholds (examples)

- Severity P0: Any customer-facing error rate causing >1% 5xx across the tenant fleet or single large tenant with >0.5% 5xx for 10m. PagerDuty P0, incident bridge, executive notify.
- Severity P1: Throughput drops causing queue growth beyond 50% for >15m. PagerDuty P1; CS alerts impacted customers.
- Severity P2: Auxiliary systems (SCIM, billing connector) errors causing background job failures. Email + Slack notify; CS to log and monitor.

Customer Communication templates

- Initial acknowledgement (under 15 minutes):
  "We’ve detected a degradation affecting [feature/service]. Our engineering team is triaging. We will provide an update within 30 minutes and a root-cause timeline within 4 hours.

  Impact: [brief impact].
  Mitigation: [what we’re doing].
  Contact: your CS rep, [name], + link to status page."

- Mitigation update (30–90 minutes):
  "Summary: We identified [dependency] as the primary cause. We’ve applied [mitigation] which restored service for X% of requests. Engineers continue to monitor and will follow up with next steps by [time]."

Telemetry Assertions (examples to add to onboarding checks)

1. Ingestion heartbeat: telemetry-gatekeeper.heartbeat == true in last 1m
2. Model load success rate: model-router.load_success_rate >= 99.9% over 5m
3. KV cache hit ratio: kvcache.hit_ratio >= 85% over 10m

Add these to the onboarding synthetic suite; example JSON snippet for assertion: 

{
  "assertion_name": "kvcache_hit_ratio_onboarding",
  "expr": "avg(kvcache_hit_ratio{env=\"prod\",stage=\"onboarding\"}) > 0.85",
  "window": "10m",
  "owner": "eng-serving-runtime"
}

Canary Failure Simulation (run prior to go-live)

1. Simulate partial dependency failure: throttle upstream responses to 50% for 10 minutes using traffic-shaper job.
2. Verify that fallback paths engage within target time (<= 30s) and degraded responses are metric-tagged.
3. Run customer communication dry-run: ask CS to publish the initial acknowledgement and verify internal observers see it.

Post-Incident Walkthrough and Action Items (within 72h)

- Convene triage + CS + platform; capture timeline and mitigation chronology.
- Generate action items: code fix, observability gaps, config changes, customer compensation if SLA breach. Assign owners and target dates.
- Update dependency map and onboarding checklist with any missed validations.

Common Failure Mode Playbooks (short form)

- Telemetry ingestion drop
  1) Buffer sidecar enablement
  2) Backfill telemetry for the gap window
  3) Postmortem: add assertion and test to synthetic suite

- External SCIM throttling
  1) Pause provisioning
  2) Switch to cached identity mapping for auth flows
  3) Retry queue with exponential backoff and jitter

- KV cache cold start leading to latency spike
  1) Warm cache using sample keyset: ./tools/warm-kvcache --tenant <tenant-id> --keys-file warm-keys.json
  2) If warmfail, route to model variant with smaller memory footprint

Appendix: Useful commands and references

- rwdctl status dependencies --tenant <id> --since 15m\- shows dependency health summary\- 
- ./tools/check-dependency-health --service telemetry-gatekeeper --verbose\- detailed probe for the service\-
- Example buffering-sidecar-override.yaml (snippet):

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: telemetry-buffer-override
data:
  buffer.max_bytes: "50000000"
  flush.interval_s: "10"

Appendix: Postmortem checklist template (to attach to incident)

- Incident title and severity
- Detection time, mitigation applied, time to recovery
- Impacted tenants (list) and customer-facing communication timeline
- Root cause and contributing factors
- Concrete action items (owner, due date, verification test)

FAQ (short)

Q: When should CS ask for credits or SLA remedies?
A: Follow the existing SLA reconciliation playbook — if the incident breaches the contracted uptime or per-tenant SLO defined in the contract, CS should initiate the billing remediation flow after the postmortem is complete.

Q: Can we always failover to another region?
A: Not always. Region-based failover is a last-resort for data residency or private deployment customers. Follow the regional failover checklist and confirm customer consent where required.

Related pages and references

- Onboarding failure mode scenarios (linked internally)
- Canary simulation playbook (Eng SRE)
- SLA remediation and billing process

This playbook is intended to be a living document. Update the dependency matrix and telemetry assertions after every simulation and postmortem.

title: API Telemetry and Cost-SLO Governance Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook defines a company-wide standard for tying API-level telemetry to cost signals and SLO governance. It prescribes the required telemetry envelope, SLI definitions that include cost attributes (token-cost, GPU-seconds, egress bytes), sampling and retention rules, alert gates, and rollout steps for adopting cost-aware SLOs across Redwood services.

Motivation:
- Inference economics are a first-class operational concern at Redwood. Teams must be able to reason about unit cost alongside latency/availability.
- Current SLOs are primarily latency/availability focused; missing cost signals lead to reactive and inconsistent capacity decisions.
- A standardized telemetry + cost tagging model enables automated budget enforcement, chargeback, and smarter runtime routing (e.g., cheaper model variant fallback when cost SLO is breached).

Scope and Applicability:
- Applies to all public-facing and internal inference APIs and routing services that emit request-level telemetry (hosted API, dedicated proxy, private runtime sidecars).
- Non-applicable: internal tooling with no customer impact (self-serve infra scripts) unless they directly influence inference cost.

Required telemetry envelope (minimum fields):
- request_id (string)
- tenant_id (string or null for system requests)
- route (api route name)
- model_id and model_variant
- sequence_length (tokens_in, tokens_out)
- cost_estimate_cents (integer): model token estimate pre-inference
- actual_cost_cents (integer): reconcile post-inference (if available)
- gpu_ms (integer): GPU milliseconds charged to this request or shard
- egress_bytes (integer)
- request_class (low|standard|priority)
- sample_rate (float) - 0.0..1.0 (for sampled payloads)
- trace_id, span_id
- timestamp (RFC3339)

Instrumentation and Ingestion Requirements:
1) Emit the telemetry envelope as structured logs and an event to the primary telemetry pipeline (metrics + traces + raw events).
2) Tag the request with X-Request-Class and X-Estimated-Cost headers at edge proxies.
3) For batched requests, include per-item token counts and per-batch aggregate cost fields.
4) Sampling: Always capture 100% of requests for metrics aggregation of cost buckets (see SLI buckets). Payload-level (prompt/content) sampling may be 0.1–1% but must be correlated by request_id.

SLI & SLO definitions (examples):
- Latency SLI: p95 server-side latency for route X excluding queued time. SLO: p95 < 300ms (monthly).
- Cost SLI: median cost_per_request_cents for route X. SLO: median <= 12 cents (weekly).
- Cost-Capacity SLI: percent of requests routed to fallback-variant when median cost exceeds threshold. SLO: fallback_rate <= 10% under normal load.
- Efficiency SLI: tokens_out per GPU-ms. SLO: improve by 10% quarter-over-quarter for high-throughput batch jobs.

SLO Bucketing and Aggregation:
- Buckets: free/low-tier, standard, enterprise; and by route (inference, embeddings, rerank).
- For each bucket compute: mean, median, p90, p99 of cost_estimate_cents and actual_cost_cents, plus GPU-ms per 1k tokens.
- Retention: aggregated metrics retained 2 years; raw request events retained 90 days (subject to legal/compliance overrides).

Alerting and Escalation Thresholds (recommended):
- Cost shock alert: if 10-minute rolling cost (sum actual_cost_cents) > 3x baseline for the tenant or route -> paging SRE.
- Unit cost drift: if weekly median cost for a route increases > 30% vs previous 4-week baseline -> create ticket and notify product owner.
- SLO burn rate: if error budget burn-rate > 1.0 for 6h -> emergency review.

Enforcement and Preflight Gates:
1) New or modified API routes that can materially change cost must include a Cost Impact Assessment in PR.
2) CI presubmit checks: telemetry schema validation (schema registry), sample telemetry emission test, and cost estimate logic unit tests.
3) Release gating rule: any change increasing median cost_estimate_cents > 20% for a route requires a staged rollout with canary duration 48h and telemetry verification.

Operational Playbook (runbook steps for cost incidents):
1) Identify scope: filter telemetry by request_class/route/model_variant to isolate blast radius.
2) Backstop: temporarily switch route to cheaper model_variant or enable conservative batching/caching knob.
3) Mitigate: apply a dynamic routing policy to route traffic to Dedicated pool if that pool has lower per-token cost under current pricing.
4) Investigate: examine prompt-set drift, model weights changes, quantization rollouts, or SDK/agent changes that increased tokens_out.
5) Post-incident: update the Cost Impact Assessment and add regression tests to presubmit.

Owner and Roles:
- Telemetry steward: Observability team (primary) - owns schema registry and ingestion pipelines.
- SLO owner: Product + Service team for each route - responsible for SLI definitions and corrective action plans.
- Cost modeling steward: Finance + Platform - owns mapping from raw GPU-ms and cloud billing to actual_cost_cents.

Example telemetry JSON (single event):
{
  "request_id": "r-9f1c2a",
  "tenant_id": "t-42",
  "route": "/v1/generate",
  "model_id": "rw-large-v2",
  "model_variant": "int8-fast",
  "tokens_in": "24",
  "tokens_out": "192",
  "cost_estimate_cents": "8",
  "gpu_ms": "45",
  "egress_bytes": "2048",
  "request_class": "standard",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-05T15:22:31Z"
}

Migration and Adoption Plan:
- Phase 0 (30 days): schema registry changes and ingestion pipeline support; small pilot with 2 services.
- Phase 1 (60 days): require telemetry envelope for all inference routes in Platform team.
- Phase 2 (90 days): enforce CI presubmit checks and require Cost Impact Assessment for PRs.
- Phase 3 (6 months): enable automated cost-aware routing policies and surface per-route cost SLAs in Redwood Console.

Decision record note:
This playbook formalizes a cross-team decision to treat cost as a first-class SLO signal and to require structured telemetry at the API boundary. Future ADRs will spell out exact billing-to-telemetry mapping and legal retention constraints.

Open questions and future work:
- How to expose per-request true cost in Private deployments where billing export is delayed? (deferred reconciliation path required)
- Standardizing mapping of GPU-ms to cloud billing for multi-tenant shared GPUs.
- Privacy: hashing rules for tenant-identifiable prompt content in sampled payloads.

References:
- Telemetry ingestion and retention guidelines (linked in related_pages)
- SLO composition guidance for multi-tenant models

Change log:
- 2026-02-15: Created by Ava Chen (observability drafting).
- 2026-04-10: Reviewer feedback incorporated (Diego Morales) - added batch handling rules.
- 2026-06-05: Publication and small wording updates (Priya Rangan).

title: PRD: Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime — API, SLOs, Pricing, and Rollout
preview:
Summary:
This PRD defines the product requirements for a Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime (\"hybrid runtime\") that unifies low-latency streaming and high-throughput batched inference in a single serving surface. The hybrid runtime should allow per-route policies that select streaming, batched, or hybrid execution, provide transparent pricing breakdowns, and preserve existing SLOs for customers during rollout.

Goals (what success looks like):
- Enable a single API surface where developers can request streaming, batched, or hybrid inference with minimal code change.
- Achieve median 50–150ms token latency for streaming routes under light load, and 2–4x cost reduction on high-concurrency transactional workloads via intelligent batching.
- Provide per-request cost and latency attribution in the Console and Billing API to make tradeoffs visible.
- Ship a staged rollout with canary, per-tenant opt-in, and automatic rollback behavior.

Non-goals:
- Replacing the runtime entirely; initial release will be an optional add-on per route.
- Deep automatic re-writing of prompts to improve batching compatibility (future work).

Background and problem statement:
Redwood currently exposes streaming inference (low-latency, token-by-token) and batched endpoints (optimized throughput) as separate serving modes. Customers with mixed traffic patterns must select one mode per product, build separate retry/fanout logic, or accept suboptimal cost/latency tradeoffs. The hybrid runtime will let us route requests dynamically to streaming or batch execution paths or run a hybrid path that streams early tokens and finishes via a batch path for long responses, reducing cost while maintaining interactive feel.

User stories:
1) As a web chat developer, I want streaming responses for short customer interactions but fall back to batched completion for long-form generation to keep cost predictable.
2) As a platform engineer, I want per-route controls for latency and cost so my team can enforce SLAs.
3) As an SRE, I want telemetry to detect when hybrid routing increases error rates or tail latency so we can auto-roll back to streaming.

Functional requirements (high level):
- API: New request parameter \"execution_mode\" with enum values [\"streaming\", \"batch\", \"hybrid\", \"auto\"]. Default is \"auto\" which uses policy routing.
- Per-route config: Routes may specify target SLOs (p50/p90/p99), cost preference (min-latency | min-cost | balanced), and max-fidelity model set.
- Hybrid behavior: For \"hybrid\" mode, initial tokens must be streamed immediately; the runtime may switch to batch execution for subsequent tokens if batching is available. The switch must be transparent to clients: a single streaming connection/stream ID is preserved.
- Backward compatibility: Existing streaming and batched APIs continue to work with no change.

Non-functional requirements (SLOs & scale):
- Streaming routes: median token latency <= 150ms at p50 under base traffic (defined as <20% of instance concurrency).
- Batched routes: throughput per GPU should improve by at least 1.8x for batched workloads vs streaming-only baseline for 512-token avg sequences.
- Hybrid routes: under mixed traffic, overall end-to-end 95th percentile latency should not exceed streaming-only p95 by more than 30% for the same requests.
- Availability: 99.9% for hosted customers (per existing SLA tiers). Hybrid launch must not degrade global availability.

Telemetry and metrics (required):
For each request/route include the following telemetry fields in Console and exported traces:
- execution_mode_selected: {streaming|batch|hybrid|fallback_streaming|fallback_batch}
- batch_id (if batched) and batch_size
- token_latency_ms (per token sampled at p50/p95)
- end_to_end_latency_ms
- cost_estimate_microtokens (pre- and post-execution)
- fallback_reason (if a fallback occurred: capacity|error|timeout|policy)

API design (examples):
- Request JSON example (hybrid):
{
  \"model\": \"rw-gnl-7b\",
  \"prompt\": \"Summarize the following notes...\",
  \"execution_mode\": \"hybrid\",
  \"route_config\": {
    \"slo_ms\": {\"p50\": 120, \"p95\": 600},
    \"cost_preference\": \"balanced\"
  }
}

- SDK/CLI opt-in toggles: developers can override via headers or SDK flags: X-Redwood-Execution-Mode, redwood.execution.mode.

Price & packaging implications:
- Introduce a \"hybrid\" consumption metric tracked at token-level: tokens_streamed and tokens_batched. Billing will apply existing per-token rates but expose a hybrid surcharge (initial beta) to account for routing overhead: hybrid_surcharge = $0.00002 / request (configurable).
- Revenue transparency: Console must show a per-route breakdown: raw_tokens, batched_tokens, streamed_tokens, estimated_savings_vs_streaming.
- Packaged offering: Hybrid runtime will be included in an \"Optimize Hybrid\" add-on for enterprise + Dedicated customers initially; hosted customers can enable via feature flag and pay metered usage.

Design constraints & trade-offs:
- Preserving streaming semantics is paramount: clients expect partial tokens as they are generated. Any switching must maintain ordering and IDs.
- Batching improves GPU utilization but adds head-of-line wait; hybrid requires predictive routing heuristics to avoid increased tail latency.
- Cost attribution must be reliable enough for billing; conservative rounding required until confidence >95%.

Routing & policy engine (requirements):
- Policies: static (route-level), adaptive (runtime load), and tenant-preference.
- Heuristic examples: switch to batch if (queued_same_model_requests >= threshold && estimated_batch_fill_time < slo_window).
- Fallbacks: if batch capacity unavailable or batch fails, auto-fallback to streaming with a recorded fallback_reason.

Rollout plan (phases):
1) Internal Canary (month 0–1): enable hybrid in infra namespaces; run synthetic traffic patterns and measure regressions vs streaming baseline.
2) Beta customer program (month 2–4): invite 10 enterprise customers; per-tenant opt-in with telemetry dashboards and weekly QBR.
3) Public launch opt-in (month 5): available via Console toggle for all hosted customers; add hybrid_surcharge trial.
4) Default routing (month 8): after telemetry shows stable SLOs and savings, enable \"auto\" routing for select low-risk routes with opt-out.

Migration and compatibility guidance for customers:
- Minimal code change: set execution_mode in existing calls or toggle via Console route settings.
- Best practices: mark latency-critical routes explicitly as \"streaming\"; set balanced cost_preference for conversational UIs.
- Mitigations: provide a migration checklist and traffic simulation tooling in Console to preview cost/latency tradeoffs before enabling hybrid.

Testing & QA strategy:
- Synthetic workloads: short-chat, long-form, mixed sessions; measure p50/p95/p99 latency and cost per 10k requests.
- Chaos tests: simulate failed batch workers, delayed kernels, and verify auto-fallback and no data loss.
- A/B testing: use canary routing and evaluate metrics for 2 weeks with statistical significance thresholds (power >= 0.8).

Security & compliance considerations:
- Maintain existing request isolation guarantees for multi-tenant dedicated customers.
- Ensure hybrid routing does not create new data residency crossovers: batch candidates must be selected within the same region and tenant-scoped pools.
- Audit logging: every decision to switch execution paths must be logged for 30 days (configurable per enterprise).

Ownership & timeline:
- Product owner: Ava Chen (product)
- Engineering lead: Miguel Ortega (eng-serving-runtime)
- SRE lead: Samira Khan (eng-sre)
- Designer: Priya Nair (eng-devex)
- Target milestones:
  - Spec freeze: 2026-05-15
  - Canary build complete: 2026-06-15
  - Beta onboarding starts: 2026-07-01
  - Public opt-in launch: 2026-09-01

Open questions (to resolve in design phase):
- Exact hybrid_surcharge amount and whether it should be waived for early adopters.
- Data model for precise per-token crediting across streamed -> batched transitions.
- UI affordances for displaying intermediate token prices in Console.

Appendix A — example telemetry table (human-readable):
| metric_key | description | unit | ingestion frequency | owner |
|-----------|-------------|------|---------------------|-------|
| execution_mode_selected | mode chosen by policy | enum | per-request | serving-runtime |
| batch_size | number of requests aggregated | count | per-batch | serving-runtime |
| token_latency_ms | per-token latency sample | ms | sampled | observability |
| fallback_reason | why fallback occurred | string | per-fallback | sres |

Appendix B — CLI sample to enable hybrid on a route:
$ redwood routes update --route-id r-12345 --set execution_mode=auto --set policy.cost_preference=balanced

Appendix C — minimal YAML for Dedicated config (example):
route:
  id: conversational-primary
  execution_mode: auto
  policy:
    slo_ms:
      p50: 100
      p95: 500
    cost_preference: balanced

References and related docs:
- Observability & cost/latency tradeoffs guide (internal): ../product-overview/observability-cost-latency-tradeoffs-guide-2025-06-09.json
- Local developer runtime sandbox (design): ../design-specs/local-developer-runtime-sandbox-design-spec-2026-02-12.json

Please review the SLOs and pricing sections carefully. Next steps: engineering scoping session (2026-04-20), finalize telemetry contract (2026-04-30).
text:
Summary:
This PRD defines the product requirements for a Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime (\"hybrid runtime\") that unifies low-latency streaming and high-throughput batched inference in a single serving surface. The hybrid runtime should allow per-route policies that select streaming, batched, or hybrid execution, provide transparent pricing breakdowns, and preserve existing SLOs for customers during rollout.

Goals (what success looks like):
- Enable a single API surface where developers can request streaming, batched, or hybrid inference with minimal code change.
- Achieve median 50–150ms token latency for streaming routes under light load, and 2–4x cost reduction on high-concurrency transactional workloads via intelligent batching.
- Provide per-request cost and latency attribution in the Console and Billing API to make tradeoffs visible.
- Ship a staged rollout with canary, per-tenant opt-in, and automatic rollback behavior.

Non-goals:
- Replacing the runtime entirely; initial release will be an optional add-on per route.
- Deep automatic re-writing of prompts to improve batching compatibility (future work).

Background and problem statement:
Redwood currently exposes streaming inference (low-latency, token-by-token) and batched endpoints (optimized throughput) as separate serving modes. Customers with mixed traffic patterns must select one mode per product, build separate retry/fanout logic, or accept suboptimal cost/latency tradeoffs. The hybrid runtime will let us route requests dynamically to streaming or batch execution paths or run a hybrid path that streams early tokens and finishes via a batch path for long responses, reducing cost while maintaining interactive feel.

User stories:
1) As a web chat developer, I want streaming responses for short customer interactions but fall back to batched completion for long-form generation to keep cost predictable.
2) As a platform engineer, I want per-route controls for latency and cost so my team can enforce SLAs.
3) As an SRE, I want telemetry to detect when hybrid routing increases error rates or tail latency so we can auto-roll back to streaming.

Functional requirements (high level):
- API: New request parameter \"execution_mode\" with enum values [\"streaming\", \"batch\", \"hybrid\", \"auto\"]. Default is \"auto\" which uses policy routing.
- Per-route config: Routes may specify target SLOs (p50/p90/p99), cost preference (min-latency | min-cost | balanced), and max-fidelity model set.
- Hybrid behavior: For \"hybrid\" mode, initial tokens must be streamed immediately; the runtime may switch to batch execution for subsequent tokens if batching is available. The switch must be transparent to clients: a single streaming connection/stream ID is preserved.
- Backward compatibility: Existing streaming and batched APIs continue to work with no change.

Non-functional requirements (SLOs & scale):
- Streaming routes: median token latency <= 150ms at p50 under base traffic (defined as <20% of instance concurrency).
- Batched routes: throughput per GPU should improve by at least 1.8x for batched workloads vs streaming-only baseline for 512-token avg sequences.
- Hybrid routes: under mixed traffic, overall end-to-end 95th percentile latency should not exceed streaming-only p95 by more than 30% for the same requests.
- Availability: 99.9% for hosted customers (per existing SLA tiers). Hybrid launch must not degrade global availability.

Telemetry and metrics (required):
For each request/route include the following telemetry fields in Console and exported traces:
- execution_mode_selected: {streaming|batch|hybrid|fallback_streaming|fallback_batch}
- batch_id (if batched) and batch_size
- token_latency_ms (per token sampled at p50/p95)
- end_to_end_latency_ms
- cost_estimate_microtokens (pre- and post-execution)
- fallback_reason (if a fallback occurred: capacity|error|timeout|policy)

API design (examples):
- Request JSON example (hybrid):
{
  \"model\": \"rw-gnl-7b\",
  \"prompt\": \"Summarize the following notes...\",
  \"execution_mode\": \"hybrid\",
  \"route_config\": {
    \"slo_ms\": {\"p50\": 120, \"p95\": 600},
    \"cost_preference\": \"balanced\"
  }
}

- SDK/CLI opt-in toggles: developers can override via headers or SDK flags: X-Redwood-Execution-Mode, redwood.execution.mode.

Price & packaging implications:
- Introduce a \"hybrid\" consumption metric tracked at token-level: tokens_streamed and tokens_batched. Billing will apply existing per-token rates but expose a hybrid surcharge (initial beta) to account for routing overhead: hybrid_surcharge = $0.00002 / request (configurable).
- Revenue transparency: Console must show a per-route breakdown: raw_tokens, batched_tokens, streamed_tokens, estimated_savings_vs_streaming.
- Packaged offering: Hybrid runtime will be included in an \"Optimize Hybrid\" add-on for enterprise + Dedicated customers initially; hosted customers can enable via feature flag and pay metered usage.

Design constraints & trade-offs:
- Preserving streaming semantics is paramount: clients expect partial tokens as they are generated. Any switching must maintain ordering and IDs.
- Batching improves GPU utilization but adds head-of-line wait; hybrid requires predictive routing heuristics to avoid increased tail latency.
- Cost attribution must be reliable enough for billing; conservative rounding required until confidence >95%.

Routing & policy engine (requirements):
- Policies: static (route-level), adaptive (runtime load), and tenant-preference.
- Heuristic examples: switch to batch if (queued_same_model_requests >= threshold && estimated_batch_fill_time < slo_window).
- Fallbacks: if batch capacity unavailable or batch fails, auto-fallback to streaming with a recorded fallback_reason.

Rollout plan (phases):
1) Internal Canary (month 0–1): enable hybrid in infra namespaces; run synthetic traffic patterns and measure regressions vs streaming baseline.
2) Beta customer program (month 2–4): invite 10 enterprise customers; per-tenant opt-in with telemetry dashboards and weekly QBR.
3) Public launch opt-in (month 5): available via Console toggle for all hosted customers; add hybrid_surcharge trial.
4) Default routing (month 8): after telemetry shows stable SLOs and savings, enable \"auto\" routing for select low-risk routes with opt-out.

Migration and compatibility guidance for customers:
- Minimal code change: set execution_mode in existing calls or toggle via Console route settings.
- Best practices: mark latency-critical routes explicitly as \"streaming\"; set balanced cost_preference for conversational UIs.
- Mitigations: provide a migration checklist and traffic simulation tooling in Console to preview cost/latency tradeoffs before enabling hybrid.

Testing & QA strategy:
- Synthetic workloads: short-chat, long-form, mixed sessions; measure p50/p95/p99 latency and cost per 10k requests.
- Chaos tests: simulate failed batch workers, delayed kernels, and verify auto-fallback and no data loss.
- A/B testing: use canary routing and evaluate metrics for 2 weeks with statistical significance thresholds (power >= 0.8).

Security & compliance considerations:
- Maintain existing request isolation guarantees for multi-tenant dedicated customers.
- Ensure hybrid routing does not create new data residency crossovers: batch candidates must be selected within the same region and tenant-scoped pools.
- Audit logging: every decision to switch execution paths must be logged for 30 days (configurable per enterprise).

Ownership & timeline:
- Product owner: Ava Chen (product)
- Engineering lead: Miguel Ortega (eng-serving-runtime)
- SRE lead: Samira Khan (eng-sre)
- Designer: Priya Nair (eng-devex)
- Target milestones:
  - Spec freeze: 2026-05-15
  - Canary build complete: 2026-06-15
  - Beta onboarding starts: 2026-07-01
  - Public opt-in launch: 2026-09-01

Open questions (to resolve in design phase):
- Exact hybrid_surcharge amount and whether it should be waived for early adopters.
- Data model for precise per-token crediting across streamed -> batched transitions.
- UI affordances for displaying intermediate token prices in Console.

Appendix A — example telemetry table (human-readable):
| metric_key | description | unit | ingestion frequency | owner |
|-----------|-------------|------|---------------------|-------|
| execution_mode_selected | mode chosen by policy | enum | per-request | serving-runtime |
| batch_size | number of requests aggregated | count | per-batch | serving-runtime |
| token_latency_ms | per-token latency sample | ms | sampled | observability |
| fallback_reason | why fallback occurred | string | per-fallback | sres |

Appendix B — CLI sample to enable hybrid on a route:
$ redwood routes update --route-id r-12345 --set execution_mode=auto --set policy.cost_preference=balanced

Appendix C — minimal YAML for Dedicated config (example):
route:
  id: conversational-primary
  execution_mode: auto
  policy:
    slo_ms:
      p50: 100
      p95: 500
    cost_preference: balanced

References and related docs:
- Observability & cost/latency tradeoffs guide (internal): ../product-overview/observability-cost-latency-tradeoffs-guide-2025-06-09.json
- Local developer runtime sandbox (design): ../design-specs/local-developer-runtime-sandbox-design-spec-2026-02-12.json

Please review the SLOs and pricing sections carefully. Next steps: engineering scoping session (2026-04-20), finalize telemetry contract (2026-04-30).

title: Field Operator Offline Debugging & Synthetic SLA Replay Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook describes the standardized process for field operators and on-call engineers to perform offline debugging of private-deploy runtime incidents and to run deterministic synthetic SLA replay tests. Use this when a customer site experiences intermittent or reproducible SLA regressions but live traffic replay is not possible or desirable (air-gapped sites, PII constraints, or when reproducing production load would risk further degradation).

Goals:
- Produce a deterministic reproduction of the failing condition with minimal customer impact.
- Validate mitigation and rollout changes by running synthetic SLA replays against a controlled replica of the tenant environment.
- Capture forensics and telemetry artifacts required for post-incident review and compliance evidence.

When to use this playbook:
- Repeated elevated p95/p99 latency for inference endpoints with no clear live-trigger-able pattern.
- Tenant-facing degradation that cannot be safely reproduced with live traffic (regulated customers, air-gapped installs).
- After a model swap, quantization change, or runtime upgrade that correlates with latency or error regression.

Preconditions (must be satisfied before starting):
1) Operator prerequisites:
   - Operator role: tenant-operator or field-operator (RBAC token present).
   - Local toolkit: redwood-agent v2.4+ installed and authenticated (run redwood-agent whoami).
2) Telemetry and snapshot prerequisites:
   - Telemetry sampling level must include full request/response traces for target tenant (eg: sampling=0.1 or full for timestamp window).
   - A recent state snapshot of model artifacts, KV cache metadata, and runtime config exported to the safe bucket (see Snapshot naming convention below).
3) Safety and approvals:
   - For customer-facing private deployments, secure approval from the customer point-of-contact (email or ticket) is required unless the tenant is in a pre-agreed emergency escalation policy.

Key artifacts to collect before offline replay:
- runtime-metrics-export-<tenant>-<ts>.tgz (CPU/GPU utilization, host-level metrics)
- kv-cache-metadata-<tenant>-<ts>.json (KV cache size, hit/miss rates)
- model-artifact-manifest-<tenant>-<version>.yaml (model checksum, quantization profile)
- request-sample-set-<tenant>-<window>.jsonl (sanitized inputs for replay)

Snapshot naming convention (example):
- <tenant>-snapshot-2026-03-14T14-12-00Z

Environment setup (operator steps):
1) Provision an isolated replay environment (operator sandbox) that mirrors the tenant runtime:
   - Create an ephemeral namespace: redwood-<tenant>-replay-<run-id>
   - Clone control-plane config: redwoodctl export-config --tenant <tenant> --to ./config.yaml
   - Ensure model artifacts are mounted read-only from the snapshot bundle.
2) Configure telemetry to capture a full replay trace:
   - redwood-telemetry set sampling --tenant <tenant> --level full --target redis

Synthetic SLA replay specification (recommended defaults):
| Parameter | Default | Purpose |
|---|---:|---:|
| duration | 15m | Validate sustained behavior over steady-state window |
| rps | 50 | Start at low RPS then scale (see ramp schedule) |
| concurrency | 8 | Parallel client threads to stress batching paths |
| payload-distribution | 70% short (<=64 tokens), 30% long (>=512 tokens) | Matches observed production token distribution |
| seed | fixed per run-id | Ensure deterministic model sampling when applicable |

Example synthetic workload YAML (paste into replay controller):
```yaml
run_id: replay-2026-05-02-opsA
tenant: example-corp
duration: 900 # seconds
ramp_schedule:
  - { duration: 120, start_rps: 5, end_rps: 20 }
  - { duration: 300, start_rps: 20, end_rps: 50 }
concurrency: 8
payloads: ./request-sample-set-example.jsonl
token_distribution:
  short: 0.7
  long: 0.3
metrics_export: ./replay-metrics/
```

Replay runbook (step-by-step):
1) Validate preconditions and collect pre-run artifacts (see above).
2) Start telemetry capture: redwood-telemetry start --label replay-<run-id>
3) Boot ephemeral replay environment (operator sandbox). Confirm host and GPU topology match production tenant profile (use redwoodctl topology --tenant <tenant>).
4) Load sanitized request-sample-set and configure the replay controller with the run YAML.
5) Start the run in dry-run mode (no model execution) to verify request timing and sampling: redwood-replay start --config replay.yaml --dry-run.
6) When dry-run succeeds, start an instrumented run: redwood-replay start --config replay.yaml --label replay-<run-id>.
7) Monitor SLOs live: dashboard link: https://console.internal/redwood/replay/<run-id> (CPU/GPU, queue-latency, KV hit-rate, per-model p95/p99).
8) If observed p95/p99 breaches configured acceptance gates (see Safety gates below), abort and capture full artifacts.
9) If run is stable, iterate: adjust model quantization profile or batching window and re-run to validate improvements.

Acceptance gates and abort criteria (safety):
- Abort immediately if:
  - Error rate increases > 5x baseline and absolute error rate > 1%
  - GPU OOM observed on >1 node during run
  - Host-level thermal emergency or fan-throttle event
- Soft gate: flag run if p99 latency increases by >25% vs baseline for more than 2 consecutive sampling windows (1 minute each).

Post-run forensic collection checklist (automated + manual):
1) redwood-telemetry stop --label replay-<run-id> && redwood-telemetry bundle --label replay-<run-id> --out ./telemetry-<run-id>.tgz
2) Export replay metrics: redwood-replay export-metrics --label replay-<run-id> --format prometheus --out ./metrics-<run-id>.prom
3) Capture model runtime profiler snapshot: redwood-profiler snapshot --label replay-<run-id> --out profiler-<run-id>.json
4) Package all artifacts and upload to secure evidence bucket with run metadata: s3://evidence.redwood/replays/<tenant>/<run-id>/

Rollback and mitigation recommendations (if replay confirms regression):
- Short-term (immediate):
  1) Revert model variant to last-known-good model-artifact-manifest (model swap rollback)
  2) Reduce batching window to force lower queueing latency (config: runtime.batch.window_ms -> 0-5ms)
  3) Throttle tenant RPS via edge-router for controlled traffic shed (policy: tenant-traffic-quota)
- Mid-term:
  1) Apply targeted KV cache warm-up script prior to traffic ramp (see Appendix)
  2) Roll forward a quantization fallback variant (configured in runtime catalog with label quant-fallback)

Owner and escalation matrix:
- Primary: Field Operations (field-ops@redwood.com)
- Secondary: Serving Runtime Team (runtime@redwood.com)
- Escalation to Engineering Manager (on-call): esc-runtime@redwood.com if no mitigation within 2 hours.

SLOs and measurement window for replay validation:
- p95 inference latency target: <= 1200ms for long requests; <= 120ms for short requests
- p99 inference latency target: <= 2500ms for long requests; <= 350ms for short requests
- KV cache hit-rate: >= 85% during steady state (otherwise investigate eviction policy)

Operational notes and best practices:
- Always use sanitized request samples for regulated tenants. Strip any PII and validate with the customer data owner before replay.
- Use the same model seed and deterministic sampling options in the runtime to make replays repeatable. Set RNG seed in config: runtime.model.seed: <run-seed>.
- For air-gapped installs, use the offline evidence bundle protocol (see related page: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/offline-artifact-delivery-and-model-refresh-runbook-2026).

Appendix A — Common commands and snippets:
- Verify operator identity:
  redwood-agent whoami
- Export control plane config for replay environment:
  redwoodctl export-config --tenant <tenant> --out config-replay.yaml
- Start telemetry capture:
  redwood-telemetry start --label replay-<run-id> --full
- Start replay controller:
  redwood-replay start --config replay.yaml --label replay-<run-id>
- Bundle artifacts for evidence:
  tar czf replay-<run-id>-evidence.tgz telemetry-<run-id>.tgz profiler-<run-id>.json metrics-<run-id>.prom

Appendix B — KV cache warm-up script (example):
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Warm up top 100 frequently used KV keys for tenant
TENANT=$1
for key in $(cat top-100-keys.txt); do
  curl -sS -X POST "https://local-runtime/kv/warmup" -H "x-tenant: $TENANT" -d "{\"key\": \"$key\"}" >/dev/null
done
```

Change log:
- 2026-03-15: Initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2026-05-02: Expanded acceptance gates and added KV warm-up script; reviewers: Diego Morales, Liam O'Connor

Related pages and references:
- Post-installation telemetry and acceptance checklist: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/post-installation-telemetry-and-acceptance-checklist-2026
- Live traffic simulation and SLA validation guidance: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/live-traffic-simulation-and-sla-validation-playbook-2026

Contact for questions: field-ops@redwood.com

Appendix A — Common commands and snippets:
- Verify operator identity:
  redwood-agent whoami
- Export control plane config for replay environment:
  redwoodctl export-config --tenant <tenant> --out config-replay.yaml
- Start telemetry capture:
  redwood-telemetry start --label replay-<run-id> --full
- Start replay controller:
  redwood-replay start --config replay.yaml --label replay-<run-id>
- Bundle artifacts for evidence:
  tar czf replay-<run-id>-evidence.tgz telemetry-<run-id>.tgz profiler-<run-id>.json metrics-<run-id>.prom

Appendix B — KV cache warm-up script (example):
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Warm up top 100 frequently used KV keys for tenant
TENANT=$1
for key in $(cat top-100-keys.txt); do
  curl -sS -X POST "https://local-runtime/kv/warmup" -H "x-tenant: $TENANT" -d "{\"key\": \"$key\"}" >/dev/null
done
```

- 2026-03-15: Initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2026-05-02: Expanded acceptance gates and added KV warm-up script; reviewers: Diego Morales, Liam O'Connor

title: Heterogeneous Deployment Sales Brief for Technical Buyers
preview:
Summary:
This brief equips sales and solutions engineers with a concise, deployment-focused narrative for technical buyers considering a mixed deployment strategy: combining Redwood Hosted, Redwood Dedicated (reserved capacity), and Redwood Private (VPC/on‑prem). It explains when to recommend each option, how to quantify latency and cost tradeoffs, competitive positioning vs cloud-managed endpoints, and an objection-handling playbook for compliance, reliability, and TCO concerns.

Audience:
- Platform engineers evaluating production constraints
- Security/compliance stakeholders assessing data residency and auditability
- Architects designing multi-region, mixed-capacity inference topologies

1) One-line value proposition for technical buyers:
Redwood enables predictable, low-latency LLM inference at the unit economics and operational maturity required for production — and lets you mix hosted, reserved, and private capacity at route level so your app meets SLAs without rebuilding your stack.

2) When to recommend each deployment mode (quick checklist):
- Hosted API: rapid onboarding, experimental workloads, or low-SLA features where TTV matters. Recommend when: team wants SDK-level speed-to-product (<1 day), no strict data residency, expected steady monthly usage < 200M tokens.
- Dedicated: long-running, high-throughput endpoints with predictable demand and strong isolation. Recommend when: 95th percentile latency targets are strict, predictable sustained traffic > 1M tokens/day, or contract-level SLAs required.
- Private (VPC/on-prem): highly regulated or air-gapped workloads with strict control over keys, audit trails, and network egress. Recommend when: data residency, E2E auditability, or internal governance policies forbid cloud-stored PII.

3) Concrete SLO/operational examples to use in conversations:
- Typical Hosted baseline: median latency for a 512-token generation on a Redwood-curated Llama-variant: 55–80 ms median, 95th percentile 140–220 ms (depending on region & model).
- Dedicated baseline (reserved GPU pool): 95th percentile 60–90 ms for the same workload when steady-state batching and pinned kernels are enabled.
- Private (on-prem with optimized inference stack): achievable 95th percentile 50–80 ms when matching equivalent GPU class and using Redwood’s runtime.
- Availability SLOs: Dedicated customers often purchase 99.9% SLA with automatic failover policies; Hosted is covered by best-effort SLOs and visibility dashboards.

4) Pricing stanzas and example scenarios (talking points; use formal quote for commitments):
- Hosted: list-style per-token pricing (example anchor): $0.012 per 1k prompt tokens + $0.015 per 1k generation tokens for LLM-x family. Example: 1M prompt + 2M gen tokens/month -> ~$45/month.
- Dedicated: base reservation + usage multiplier. Example: 8-GPU reserved cluster $18k/month + usage add-on $0.006 per 1k tokens beyond baseline. For predictable high-volume customers, Dedicated typically reduces per-token unit cost by 40–70% vs Hosted.
- Private: platform licensing + installation and support (one-time setup + annual maintenance). Example: 1x rack on-prem bundle $120k initial + $35k/year support; per-token costs modeled on GPU utilization and ops.
- Hybrid recommendations: show blended TCO for migration phases (pilot on Hosted, steady-state on Dedicated, controlled PII routes on Private). Use a 12-month model: estimate 6 months Hosted pilot then 6 months Dedicated ramp.

5) Competitive brief (neutral language) — common competitors and positioning points:
- Competitor A (large cloud vendor managed endpoints):
  - Strengths: deep cloud integration, single-bill simplicity.
  - Redwood counter: our runtime and routing policies reduce per-token cost under bursty traffic by 20–50% via batching and KV caching; we provide a unified API across deployment modes so migration and governance are simpler.
- Competitor B (self-hosted inference vendors):
  - Strengths: tight on-prem control, appeal to infra teams.
  - Redwood counter: we combine on-prem packages with a managed control plane option, reducing ops burden while preserving audit/logging and KMS integrations.
- Competitor C (LLM API providers focused on simplicity):
  - Strengths: easy developer UX and fast iteration.
  - Redwood counter: we match developer ergonomics while adding route-level policies, cost controls, and performance predictability required for production.

6) Objection handling (scenarios and short scripts):
- "We can just use the cloud provider's model endpoints."
  - Response: "Cloud endpoints are convenient, but for production economics and predictable latency under bursty load, Redwood’s runtime and routing reduce per-token costs and guardrails (batching, prefix cache, kernel tuning) that cloud endpoints do not expose. We’ll run a 2-week pilot and show per-token delta and 95th latency graphs."
- "We need absolute data residency and no control plane exposure."
  - Response: "Redwood Private supports a customer-controlled control plane in a VPC or fully air-gapped on-prem package with KMS/HSM integrations and local audit logging. We will map the data flows, provide architecture diagrams, and run a compliance checklist with your security team."
- "We don’t want vendor lock-in for model formats."
  - Response: "Redwood serves common open checkpoints and supports model version pinning and exports of model artifacts and KV cache snapshots. Migration paths are part of the Dedicated contract and private deployment runbook."

7) Pitch deck outline (single-slide bullets for rapid customization):
- Slide 1: Customer challenge — inconsistent latency, unpredictable token spend, compliance constraints.
- Slide 2: Redwood approach — unified runtime + routing + mixed deployments.
- Slide 3: Economics snapshot — Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private TCO for a sample workload.
- Slide 4: Architecture patterns — pilot, steady-state, PII-only private routes. Diagram: client -> route policy -> hosted/dedicated/private fallback.
- Slide 5: Risk mitigation — canary rollouts, automatic fallback to cheaper variants, audit trails.
- Slide 6: Customer case study (anonymized).
- Slide 7: Ask & next steps — pilot proposal, data-sharing checklist, timelines.

8) Short anonymized case study (1 paragraph & metrics):
- Customer: large B2B SaaS in North America (onboarding by Redwood Solutions Eng).
- Challenge: production chat assistant with 600k requests/day, 95th percentile spikes causing >200 ms latency and rising token bill.
- Solution: mixed deployment — high-frequency stateful routes to Dedicated with prefix caching; analytics and low-volume non-sensitive work on Hosted.
- Outcome after 90 days: 40% reduction in per-token cost, 30% improvement in 95th percentile latency, and stabilized error rate <0.1%.

9) Security & compliance FAQ (short answers for SEs):
- Q: Does Redwood store customer prompts or embeddings in the hosted offering?
  - A: By default, minimal telemetry is stored for billing and analytics. Customers can opt-out of retention or move sensitive routes to Dedicated/Private where logs are retained per customer policy.
- Q: Can we integrate our own KMS/HSM?
  - A: Yes. Dedicated and Private support customer-managed keys; Hosted supports KMS integrations for enterprise contracts.
- Q: How are audit logs exported for SOC2/ISO reviews?
  - A: We provide log export mechanisms (S3, secure syslog) and standardized evidence packages for enterprise audits.
- Q: What network boundaries exist for Private deployments?
  - A: Private can be deployed fully inside a customer VPC or on-prem with no egress to Redwood-hosted control planes if required.

10) Pilot playbook (3-step commercial + technical skeleton):
1. Discovery (1 week): collect traffic patterns, PII mapping, and SLO targets.
2. Pilot (2–6 weeks): deploy targeted routes to Hosted and Dedicated; measure latency, token usage, and failover. Provide weekly dashboards and a final TCO runbook.
3. Scale & contract (4–8 weeks): finalize reservations, security attestation, and production cutover plan.

11) Assets and attachments to include with outreach:
- Sample pilot statement of work (SOW) and pricing worksheet (xls).
- Architecture diagrams (Hosted/Dedicated/Private hybrid).
- Benchmark reports: 95th-latency dashboards, per-token cost breakdowns for sample LLM families.
- Compliance checklist and KB for Private installs.

12) Owner & escalation path:
- Primary owner for this brief: Ava Chen (Solutions Engineering).
- Technical reviewer: Miguel Ortega (Platform Eng).
- Security reviewer: Priya Raman (Security).
- Escalation: sols-eng@redwood.ai -> go-to-market-leads@redwood.ai -> eng-platform-oncall@redwood.ai

13) Recommended next steps for SEs engaging prospects:
- Run the 10-minute economics check: estimate monthly tokens, identify PII surfaces, recommend pilot mode (Hosted pilot vs dedicated pilot).
- Book architecture workshop within 72 hours of signaled interest.
- Prepare the hybrid TCO model and a 2-week performance validation plan.

Document history and references:
- Created: 2026-02-15
- Last updated: 2026-03-20
- Related internal references: eng-private-deployments/deployment-guides, eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture, sales-enablement/pilot-activation-playbook-and-technical-economics-2027

Feedback and updates: please send suggested edits and data points to Ava Chen.

title: Assurance Convergence Protocol: Controls Alignment for Auth, Logging, Residency & SDLC
preview:
Summary
This document defines the Assurance Convergence Protocol (ACP): a single operational rubric that aligns RBAC and authentication controls, audit logging guarantees, data residency and retention schedules, secure SDLC gates, and incident-response evidence workflows. The ACP exists to reduce control gaps, accelerate audit preparation, and make exception and risk decisions traceable and reversible.

Scope
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments (Redwood API, Dedicated capacity, and Private/VPC builds).
- Covers: role/permission lifecycle, authn/authz telemetry, audit-log schema & ingestion, retention/residency mapping for customer and telemetry data, SDLC release gates that require security evidence, and incident evidence capture & handoff.
- Excludes product feature design decisions; those are covered in product PRDs but must reference the ACP when they introduce new control requirements.

Guiding principles
1) Single source of truth for control ownership and artifacts. Every control references: owner team, canonical artifact location, retention policy, and review cadence.
2) Minimum viable evidence for audits. Evidence artifacts are pragmatic: attestations, logs, manifests, and linkable references (no bulky ad-hoc exports).
3) Traceable exceptions. Any deviation uses the exception workflow with explicit mitigation and automatic expiration.
4) Recovery-first: incident evidence collection must preserve forensic fidelity while minimizing service impact.

Controls matrix (operational view)
Control | Owner | Canonical artifact(s) | Retention | Review cadence | Escalation on failure
--- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ---
RBAC (role binding + approval history) | identity-team | s3://redwood-secure-artifacts/rbac/attestations/{YYYY-MM}.json, identity-db change log | 7 years for audit roles; 1 year for ephemeral roles | Quarterly automated attestation; manual if unusual changes | Pager to identity-team; escalation to Security on failed attestation
AuthN (SSO + service tokens) | identity-team | oidc-logs (Kafka topic), service-secret-rotation-manifest | 2 years for auth events; secrets rotated per policy | Monthly rotation verification | Immediate rotate + token invalidation SOP
Audit logging (ingestion + schema) | infra-sre / observability | audit-index (clickhouse/olap), immutable-s3: s3://redwood-audit/immutable/{date}.ndjson | 7 years (prod); 1 year (dev/staging) | Continuous ingestion SLA checks; schema contracts validated on deploy | Fallback to immutable-s3 + backlog reingestion; SEV2 if >5% missing
Data residency mapping (customer data tag & location) | legal-compliance / infra-sre | residency-manifest.csv, residency-mapping-api | 7 years or per contractual obligations | Annual and on-new-region deploy | Block new deployment until mapping verified
Secure SDLC gate evidence (pre-release) | eng-platform / sec-ops | pre-release-evidence bundle (tests, SCA, attestation) | 3 years (release artifacts) | Per-release gating | Release hold and security review board (SRB) for failed gates
Incident evidence capture | sec-ops | incident-evidence-manifest.json + forensic bundles in s3://redwood-forensics/{incident-id}/ | 10 years for high-severity incidents; 2 years for medium | After-action review (14 days) | Immediate forensic preservation + legal notification as required

Operational thresholds and SLA examples
- Audit log ingestion SLA: 99.9% of events indexed within 5 minutes of generation.
- Auth event retention for realtime detection: events must be queryable for 90 days with sub-10s query latency.
- RBAC attestation pass rate: 100% of owners respond to quarterly attestations; exceptions automatically auto-expire after 30 days.

How artifacts are named and stored (convention)
- Naming convention: <control>-<artifact-type>-<YYYYMM>-<short-id>.ext
  - Example: rbac-attestation-202603-identitydb.json
- Canonical storage: secure S3 (KMS-encrypted) buckets with object-lock enabled for immutable audit buckets. Buckets: redwood-secure-artifacts, redwood-audit, redwood-forensics.

Evidence manifest example (artifact-level index)
{
  "incident_id": "IR-2026-0427-01",
  "artifacts": [
    {"path": "s3://redwood-forensics/IR-2026-0427-01/conntrack.ndjson", "type": "network-capture", "hash": "sha256:..."},
    {"path": "s3://redwood-forensics/IR-2026-0427-01/rbac-attestation-202603.json", "type": "attestation"}
  ],
  "collected_by": "sec-ops@redwood.com",
  "collected_at": "2026-03-05T14:22:31Z"
}

Automated checks and CI integration
- Code-level gates (SDLC): every merge to main must pass security-oriented checks documented below. The pre-release-evidence bundle is produced by the CI pipeline (build artifact + SCA report + unit/integration test summary) and uploaded to the canonical artifact location.
- Example pre-release policy (yaml snippet):
policy:
  ssa_threshold: 0.0
  sca_blocking: true
  required_artifacts: ["sast-report.json","dependency-sbom.json","qa-eval-summary.json"]

Exception & risk approval workflow (operational steps)
1) Submit exception ticket in Jira component: SECURITY-EXCEPTIONS with fields: control-id, risk-rating (low/med/high), mitigation, expiration date.
2) Auto-attach required artifacts: short risk memo, compensating controls evidence, mitigation acceptance checklist.
3) Security reviews within 5 business days. For high risk: SRB convenes within 48 hours.
4) If approved, exception is timeboxed and a webhook populates enforcement rules (e.g., rate-limiting, increased monitoring) for its duration.
5) Expired exceptions auto-revoke; owners must re-request or system reverts to default control state.

Incident evidence collection: step-by-step (for sec-ops)
1) Triage: identify incident ID and severity. Assign an evidence custodian (person) in PagerDuty incident.
2) Preserve: enable object-lock and snapshot affected systems. If possible, pause log rotation and create immutable copy:
   - CLI: aws s3 cp s3://service-logs/prod/2026-03-05/ s3://redwood-forensics/IR-2026-0427-01/ --recursive --sse aws:kms
3) Collect: gather RBAC change logs, auth events (time window +/- 30 minutes), deployment events, and model-serving runtime traces.
4) Hash & catalog: compute sha256 for each artifact and add to incident-evidence-manifest.json.
5) Share: handoff to Legal/Compliance via the secure evidence portal; include a minimal chain-of-custody statement.

Audit readiness playbook (quarterly)
1) Run artifact discovery job across canonical buckets; produce a gaps report.
2) Resolve high-priority gaps within 7 business days.
3) Perform sample replays: validate that audit logs can be reconstructed and that telemetry maps to control events.
4) Produce an audit readiness attestation signed by owners; store at s3://redwood-secure-artifacts/audit-attestations/{YYYY-MM}.json

Monitoring and alerting (examples)
- Metric: audit_ingest_lag_p50/p95/p99. Alert if p95 > 300s for more than 5 minutes (SEV2).
- Metric: rbac_attestation_response_rate. Alert if < 80% 7 days after attestation launch (SEV3).
- Metric: exception_count_by_risk. Pager duty if any high-risk exception active longer than 14 days.

Operational responsibilities (owners at a glance)
- identity-team: role lifecycle, SSO, service account policy, attestation orchestration.
- infra-sre: audit log pipeline, immutable storage, region mapping.
- sec-ops: incident evidence collection, chain-of-custody, SRB coordination.
- legal-compliance: residency mapping decisions, regulatory evidence requirements.
- eng-platform: CI gate enforcement and pre-release artifact generation.

Appendix A — Evidence templates and quick CLI snippets
- RBAC attestation retrieval (Athena):
  SELECT * FROM rbac_attestations WHERE month = '2026-03';
- Audit bucket immutable copy (example):
  aws s3api put-object-lock-configuration --bucket redwood-audit --object-lock-configuration '{"ObjectLockEnabled": "Enabled","Rule":{"DefaultRetention":{"Days":3650}}}'
- Minimal chain-of-custody text: Collector: <name> | Collected at: <timestamp> | Method: snapshot/s3-copy | Notes: <short description>

Appendix B — Frequently asked questions
Q: When should a product change trigger residency re-review?
A: Any feature that stores or indexes customer-provided data in a new region or service tier requires a residency-mapping change request and legal signoff prior to rollout.

Q: Who signs the pre-release evidence bundle?
A: The engineering release lead plus the eng-platform and sec-ops approvers must sign; legal only signs when the release affects residency or contractual controls.

Change log
- 2026-02-15: Document created (Ava Chen).
- 2026-03-10: Added CLI snippets and tightened exception webhook behavior (reviewers Miguel Alvarez, Priya Nair).

Related artifacts and links
- Canonical bucket policies: s3://redwood-secure-artifacts/policies.md
- SRB charter and membership: /confluence/security-and-compliance/risk-and-exceptions/srb-charter

AuthN (SSO + service tokens) | identity-team | oidc-logs (Kafka topic), service-secret-rotation-manifest | 2 years for auth events; secrets rotated per policy | Monthly rotation verification | Immediate rotate + token invalidation SOP
