Data residency mapping (customer data tag & location) | legal-compliance / infra-sre | residency-manifest.csv, residency-mapping-api | 7 years or per contractual obligations | Annual and on-new-region deploy | Block new deployment until mapping verified
Secure SDLC gate evidence (pre-release) | eng-platform / sec-ops | pre-release-evidence bundle (tests, SCA, attestation) | 3 years (release artifacts) | Per-release gating | Release hold and security review board (SRB) for failed gates
Incident evidence capture | sec-ops | incident-evidence-manifest.json + forensic bundles in s3://redwood-forensics/{incident-id}/ | 10 years for high-severity incidents; 2 years for medium | After-action review (14 days) | Immediate forensic preservation + legal notification as required

Operational thresholds and SLA examples
- Audit log ingestion SLA: 99.9% of events indexed within 5 minutes of generation.
- Auth event retention for realtime detection: events must be queryable for 90 days with sub-10s query latency.
- RBAC attestation pass rate: 100% of owners respond to quarterly attestations; exceptions automatically auto-expire after 30 days.

How artifacts are named and stored (convention)
- Naming convention: <control>-<artifact-type>-<YYYYMM>-<short-id>.ext
  - Example: rbac-attestation-202603-identitydb.json
- Canonical storage: secure S3 (KMS-encrypted) buckets with object-lock enabled for immutable audit buckets. Buckets: redwood-secure-artifacts, redwood-audit, redwood-forensics.

Evidence manifest example (artifact-level index)
{
  "incident_id": "IR-2026-0427-01",
  "artifacts": [
    {"path": "s3://redwood-forensics/IR-2026-0427-01/conntrack.ndjson", "type": "network-capture", "hash": "sha256:..."},
    {"path": "s3://redwood-forensics/IR-2026-0427-01/rbac-attestation-202603.json", "type": "attestation"}
  ],
  "collected_by": "sec-ops@redwood.com",
  "collected_at": "2026-03-05T14:22:31Z"
}

Automated checks and CI integration
- Code-level gates (SDLC): every merge to main must pass security-oriented checks documented below. The pre-release-evidence bundle is produced by the CI pipeline (build artifact + SCA report + unit/integration test summary) and uploaded to the canonical artifact location.
- Example pre-release policy (yaml snippet):
policy:
  ssa_threshold: 0.0
  sca_blocking: true
  required_artifacts: ["sast-report.json","dependency-sbom.json","qa-eval-summary.json"]

Exception & risk approval workflow (operational steps)
1) Submit exception ticket in Jira component: SECURITY-EXCEPTIONS with fields: control-id, risk-rating (low/med/high), mitigation, expiration date.
2) Auto-attach required artifacts: short risk memo, compensating controls evidence, mitigation acceptance checklist.
3) Security reviews within 5 business days. For high risk: SRB convenes within 48 hours.
4) If approved, exception is timeboxed and a webhook populates enforcement rules (e.g., rate-limiting, increased monitoring) for its duration.
5) Expired exceptions auto-revoke; owners must re-request or system reverts to default control state.

Incident evidence collection: step-by-step (for sec-ops)
1) Triage: identify incident ID and severity. Assign an evidence custodian (person) in PagerDuty incident.
2) Preserve: enable object-lock and snapshot affected systems. If possible, pause log rotation and create immutable copy:
   - CLI: aws s3 cp s3://service-logs/prod/2026-03-05/ s3://redwood-forensics/IR-2026-0427-01/ --recursive --sse aws:kms
3) Collect: gather RBAC change logs, auth events (time window +/- 30 minutes), deployment events, and model-serving runtime traces.
4) Hash & catalog: compute sha256 for each artifact and add to incident-evidence-manifest.json.
5) Share: handoff to Legal/Compliance via the secure evidence portal; include a minimal chain-of-custody statement.

Audit readiness playbook (quarterly)
1) Run artifact discovery job across canonical buckets; produce a gaps report.
2) Resolve high-priority gaps within 7 business days.
3) Perform sample replays: validate that audit logs can be reconstructed and that telemetry maps to control events.
4) Produce an audit readiness attestation signed by owners; store at s3://redwood-secure-artifacts/audit-attestations/{YYYY-MM}.json

Monitoring and alerting (examples)
- Metric: audit_ingest_lag_p50/p95/p99. Alert if p95 > 300s for more than 5 minutes (SEV2).
- Metric: rbac_attestation_response_rate. Alert if < 80% 7 days after attestation launch (SEV3).
- Metric: exception_count_by_risk. Pager duty if any high-risk exception active longer than 14 days.

Operational responsibilities (owners at a glance)
- identity-team: role lifecycle, SSO, service account policy, attestation orchestration.
- infra-sre: audit log pipeline, immutable storage, region mapping.
- sec-ops: incident evidence collection, chain-of-custody, SRB coordination.
- legal-compliance: residency mapping decisions, regulatory evidence requirements.
- eng-platform: CI gate enforcement and pre-release artifact generation.

Appendix A — Evidence templates and quick CLI snippets
- RBAC attestation retrieval (Athena):
  SELECT * FROM rbac_attestations WHERE month = '2026-03';
- Audit bucket immutable copy (example):
  aws s3api put-object-lock-configuration --bucket redwood-audit --object-lock-configuration '{"ObjectLockEnabled": "Enabled","Rule":{"DefaultRetention":{"Days":3650}}}'
- Minimal chain-of-custody text: Collector: <name> | Collected at: <timestamp> | Method: snapshot/s3-copy | Notes: <short description>

Appendix B — Frequently asked questions
Q: When should a product change trigger residency re-review?
A: Any feature that stores or indexes customer-provided data in a new region or service tier requires a residency-mapping change request and legal signoff prior to rollout.

Q: Who signs the pre-release evidence bundle?
A: The engineering release lead plus the eng-platform and sec-ops approvers must sign; legal only signs when the release affects residency or contractual controls.

Change log
- 2026-02-15: Document created (Ava Chen).
- 2026-03-10: Added CLI snippets and tightened exception webhook behavior (reviewers Miguel Alvarez, Priya Nair).

Related artifacts and links
- Canonical bucket policies: s3://redwood-secure-artifacts/policies.md
- SRB charter and membership: /confluence/security-and-compliance/risk-and-exceptions/srb-charter

title: concurrency-primitives-and-affinity-guidelines-2026
preview:
Overview

This document defines recommended concurrency primitives, thread/actor affinity patterns, and safe integration points for Redwood's inference runtime and associated services. The goal is to reduce latency variability, avoid priority inversion on shared GPU/CPU resources, and make component-level behavior predictable under both steady-state and burst loads.

Audience
- Runtime engineers
- Infra and SRE teams
- SDK and client-library maintainers

Goals
- Provide low-friction, testable concurrency patterns that limit tail-latency impact on multi-tenant GPU hosts.
- Standardize affinity approaches across CPU-bound pre/post processing, scheduler threads, and GPU work submission paths.
- Give clear migration guidance away from ad-hoc locking and blocking I/O in hot paths.

Scope
This guideline covers:
- Inference serving processes (request dispatch, KV cache, batching)
- Pre/post-processing workers and tokenization pipelines
- Client SDKs that perform local batching or streaming
Not in scope: language-level runtime internals (language VM implementation details).

Principles
1. Prefer non-blocking coordination in hot paths: use lock-free queues, atomics, and single-producer-single-consumer (SPSC) rings where possible.
2. Isolate blocking work into bounded, observable worker pools with backpressure signals.
3. Bind critical threads to stable CPU sets and avoid OS-level preemption during GPU submission windows.
4. Make affinity and scheduling observable: expose metrics for queue lengths, thread utilization, and preemption events.

Recommended primitives by component
(Use the table below to pick the right primitive for each component)

Component | Recommended Primitive | Rationale | Max concurrency
---|---:|---|---:
Request dispatch / ingress thread | SPSC ring buffer + atomic cursor | Minimizes allocation and lock overhead; predictable latency | 1 per ingress socket
KV cache access (read-dominant) | Seqlock or RCU-style snapshot | Readers fast; writers do rare consistent updates | many readers, few writers
KV cache writes / metadata updates | Mutex + short critical section OR per-shard lock striping | Keep write windows short to avoid contention | #shards == CPU sockets
Batch assembly | Lock-free queue with batch-coalescer thread | Allows continuous enqueuing while a single consumer creates batches | bounded pool of batchers (2–4)
Blocking I/O (logs, metrics flush) | Asynchronous worker pool with bounded queue and QoS | Prevents blocking hot threads; degrade QoS under overload | configurable, default 8
Telemetry enrichment | Per-request pre-allocated buffer + atomic append | Avoid heap allocation in fast path | per-thread buffer

Affinity guidelines
- CPU affinity: bind the following to dedicated core sets where supported by the host OS/hypervisor:
  - GPU submission threads: isolate on a small core set with real-time bias if available.
  - Batch coalescer / KV eviction threads: colocate with NUMA-local cores to reduce remote memory access.
  - Tokenizer and detokenizer pools: keep within the same NUMA domain as the dispatcher when latency is critical.
- GPU affinity: prefer single-GPU submission per process when using model shards that maintain an in-GPU KV cache. For multi-GPU processes, pin submission threads to the GPU's preferred CPU set to reduce PCIe context switch costs.
- Avoid oversubscription: ensure sum(allocated_cpu_shares) <= node_cpu_capacity - reserved_system_cores. We recommend reserving at least 1 full core per GPU for submission and poll loops.

Thread pool sizing heuristics
1. Hot path/poller threads: 1 thread per hardware queue (do not use threadpool for pollers).
2. CPU-bound workers (tokenizers): min(2 * hardware_threads_local, 8) with aggressive cooperative yielding.
3. Blocking work pools: small bounded pools (4–16) with adaptive throttling based on queue latency and SLO signals.

Backpressure and QoS
- All public enqueue points must return an overload signal within 20ms when internal queues are above their configured high-water mark. Implementations MUST provide both:
  1) an explicit API error (e.g., 429 / X-Queue-State header)
  2) a telemetry signal (metered count of rejected requests)
- For SDK-local batching, prefer a hybrid policy: wait up to 15ms or until batch_size threshold, whichever comes first. Expose a hook to bypass batching for latency-sensitive calls.

Interaction patterns and safe APIs
- Provide a single "submission fence" API for GPU work that:
  - returns quickly after queuing to kernel (non-blocking where supported)
  - exposes a future/promise for completion so higher layers can await if necessary
- Expose cancellation tokens for in-flight requests. Cancellation must be cooperative: threads should check token at known yield points (e.g., between tokenizer stages, before batch add).

Testing and validation
1. Microbenchmarks: Every change to a hot-path primitive must include a microbenchmark that measures median and 99.9th percentile latency under realistic contention (>= 8 concurrent clients).
2. Fault-injection: Synthesize preemption and core-steal scenarios in CI (containerized fault injection) to validate graceful degradation.
3. Nightly stress: Run an open-loop stress test that simulates burst patterns and validates queue high-water eviction behaves as expected.

Operational runbook (short)
1. Symptom: rising 99.9th percentile latency with stable median.
  - Check: batcher queue length, KV cache writer queue, CPU steal and RCU retry counters.
  - Mitigation: temporarily increase batcher count (if space allows) or scale horizontally to relieve co-located CPU pressure.
2. Symptom: CPU-level preemption spikes during GPU submission window.
  - Check: kernel-level SCHED_LOG or cgroup throttling metrics.
  - Mitigation: move submission threads to reserved cores; enable prewarm submission path.

Migration checklist (for teams moving from coarse locking)
- Identify hot lock holders via p99 profiling (perf/async-profiler).
- Introduce SPSC ring or atomic fast-path with fallback lock for complex updates.
- Add metrics for fast-path vs fallback-path counts.
- Gradually flip traffic with canary (5%, 25%, 100%) while monitoring tail-latency and rejection rates.

Example patterns
- SPSC ring producer pseudocode:
```
class SpscRing {
  atomic<uint64_t> head;
  atomic<uint64_t> tail;
  Item buffer[N];

  bool push(Item x) {
    auto h = head.load();
    if ((h - tail.load()) >= N) return false; // full
    buffer[h % N] = x;
    head.store(h + 1);
    return true;
  }
}
```
- Bounded async worker pool (CLI): configure via env vars: WORKER_POOL_SIZE=8, WORKER_QUEUE_MAX=1024, WORKER_QOS=low

Ownership and compliance
- Runtime-serving: owning team = eng-serving-runtime
- SDK batching: owning team = eng-developer-experience
- Enforcement: code review checklist item "concurrency primitive justification" must be present for PRs touching hot-paths.

Metrics to emit (minimum)
- queue.length.{component} (gauge)
- queue.rejects.{component} (counter)
- thread.preemptions.{thread_group} (counter)
- cpu.affinity.migrations (counter)
- batch.size.histogram (histogram)

References and related pages
- See: runtime hot-path profiling playbook and kernel scheduling guidance in eng-serving-runtime.

Appendix: Decision rationale
Teams repeatedly observed that coarse-grained mutexes introduced p99 amplification when a single slow writer blocked many readers during model warmup and KV updates. We decided to favor reader-fast patterns and bounded worker pools to create predictable overload behavior and simpler operational signals.

Document history
- 2026-01-10: Drafted by Ava Chen
- 2026-02-08: Reviewed by Diego Morales and Priya Nair; added affinity heuristics
- 2026-03-04: Published (this version)

title: Model Exfiltration: Evidence Capture, RBAC Containment, and Compliance Packaging
preview:
Overview:

This document defines the standard operating procedure for responding to suspected or confirmed model exfiltration incidents involving Redwood-hosted inference systems. It combines RBAC containment controls, audit log evidence capture queries, data-residency and retention considerations, secure-SDLC remediation guidance, and the compliance packaging checklist required for regulatory/enterprise customers.

Scope and intent:
- Applies to all incidents where model internals, embeddings, prompt corpora, or customer-provided data may have been exfiltrated from any Redwood environment (hosted, dedicated, private).
- Focus is on rapid containment, defensible forensic evidence capture, and creating a reproducible compliance evidence package for audits.

Goals:
1. Stop ongoing exfiltration within 15 minutes of detection where possible.
2. Preserve forensic evidence with documented chain-of-custody.
3. Limit blast radius via RBAC and credential containment.
4. Produce a compliance artifact that supports SOC2/ISO/GDPR inquiries.

Threat model (concise):
- Insider misuse of privileged machine/service accounts.
- API key leakage used to drive high-rate queries and streaming token capture.
- Side-channel or model extraction via repeated adaptive queries.
- Compromise of a private deployment (customer-managed) that can be used to move artifacts to unauthorised locations.

Ownership and responders:
- Incident Commander (IC): Security Oncall (primary).
- Forensics Lead: Security Engineering.
- Service Owner(s): Runtime / Serving (if hosted) or Customer Success for private deploys.
- Legal & Privacy: notified immediately for potential data subject impact.
- Communications: Customer Success and PR as required.

Priority classification:
- P0: Confirmed exfiltration of customer PII or model weights/secret prompt corpora.
- P1: Strong indicators of extraction attempts (high similarity outputs, repeated boundary probing).
- P2: Unusual query patterns without clear exfil evidence.

Immediate triage checklist (first 15 minutes):
1. IC logs timeline and assigns Forensics Lead.
2. Snapshot current access tokens and session table. Note: do NOT revoke yet if live forensic capture is required—coordinate with Forensics Lead.
3. Temporarily throttle or rate-limit suspected API keys/routes.
4. Place affected model endpoints into maintenance mode (deny external requests) if P0/P1.
5. Preserve volatile state: export KV caches, current LRU for KV cache, last-10K request traces for the model shard.

Containment actions (RBAC-focused):
- Immediate actions (if P0):
  1. Revoke all session tokens associated with implicated service identities and API keys via central auth service.
  2. Disable short-lived service principals and mark for rotation (mark \"rotating=true\" in identity registry).
  3. Apply a temporary deny policy on the model-serving role for all non-essential principals:
     - Example deny policy (pseudo-YAML):
       deny: true
       principal_tags: [\"external\", \"service-account\", \"developer\"]
       resources: [\"/models/*/serve\", \"/models/*/stream\"]
       timeframe: \"T+0 to T+72h\"
- Isolation for private deployments: instruct customer to disable external egress in VPC and snapshot the deployment. Provide step-by-step commands (see \"Private deploy containment\" below).

Audit log evidence capture (guidance and example queries):
- Preserve immutable audit streams first: forward the affected time window to evidence-bucket with write-once retention (see audit retention policy).
- Minimum evidence fields to capture per request:
  - timestamp, request_id, model_id, route, principal_id, ip_address, user_agent, input_length, output_length, tokens_generated, response_sample_hash, parent_request_id

- Example Splunk/Kusto queries (adapt to platform):
  1) High-rate callers in window:
   index=redwood_audit sourcetype=requests earliest=\"-1h\" | stats count by principal_id | where count > 10000
  2) Streams with large outputs:
   index=redwood_audit route=\"/stream*\" | where tokens_generated > 10000 | table timestamp, principal_id, model_id, tokens_generated
  3) Similarity clustering (post-hoc):
   index=redwood_audit | eval sample_hash=sha256(substr(response,0,4096)) | stats count by sample_hash | where count > 3

- Immutable evidence export example (CLI):
  aws s3 cp s3://audit-stream/2026-02-12/window-0001 nds-case-1234/ --storage-class DEEP_ARCHIVE --metadata chain-of-custody=sealed

Forensic capture checklist (ASCII table):
| Artifact type | Recommended action | Retention label | Notes |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------|-------|
| API request traces | Export full JSON payloads for suspected window | evidence:primary | Include parent_request_id links |
| Streaming chunks | Capture earliest and latest 1MB per stream | evidence:primary | Chunk ordering matters |
| KV cache snapshot | Dump hot KV namespaces | evidence:secondary | May contain derived embeddings |
| Service credentials | Export credential issuance logs | evidence:primary | Include rotation events |
| Network egress logs | Capture flow logs + destination IPs | evidence:primary | Correlate with S3/ftp targets |

Data residency & retention considerations:
- If evidence contains EU personal data, keep evidence within EU-bound storage only unless Legal approves cross-border transfer. Mark exports with residency tag: residency=eu|na|apac.
- Evidence retention for compliance packaging:
  - Primary evidence: retain 7 years encrypted (for high-sensitivity incidents) or at least 1 year for lower severity.
  - Secondary artifacts: retain 1 year unless requested otherwise.
  - All retention decisions must be logged in the incident ticket and approved by Legal.

Private deploy containment (customer instructions snippet):
1) SSH into management node: ssh ops@<customer-vpn-ip>
2) sudo iptables -I OUTPUT -m conntrack --ctstate NEW -j DROP  # blocks new external connections
3) sudo systemctl stop redwood-agent.service
4) Snapshot /var/lib/redwood/models and upload to customer-provided S3 with immutable flags

Secure SDLC & remediation steps after containment:
1. Triage root cause: credential leakage, model attack pattern, or config issue.
2. Create a scoped fix PR with: code changes, unit tests for input sanitization/rate-limits, and regression prompt-set evals (see prompt-sets-and-benchmarks).
3. Require mandatory security review and replay of the incident pattern in non-prod for validation.
4. Backport fixes to any maintained runtime branches.
5. Extend CI to include an automated exfiltration smoke-test (detect high-output response patterns).

Compliance evidence packaging (deliverable checklist):
- Cover letter: incident timeline, summary, impact assessment, decision log.
- Chain-of-custody manifest: who exported which artifact, when, target storage.
- Artifact index: CRC/sha256 checksums and storage URIs for every exported file.
- RBAC audit: list of principals revoked/rotated and justification for each.
- Legal sign-off: redacted data subject notifications and regulator contacts if applicable.
- Technical appendix: set of Splunk/Kusto queries used, sample outputs, and threat-model mapping.

Risk and exceptions governance (temporary elevated access):
- Any emergency elevation for triage must be approved by: IC + Forensics Lead + One Legal approver. Approval must be recorded in the ticket with a start/expiry time (max 72 hours).
- All temporary grants must be short-lived credentials (max TTL 1 hour) and tied to a unique justification tag.
- Post-incident, conduct privileged access attestation and rotate any keys used by elevated users.

Post-incident review (30/60/90-day cadence):
1. 72-hour hot debrief: capture immediate lessons and patch rollout plan.
2. 30-day technical retrospective: attackers path, detection gaps, automation opportunities.
3. 90-day risk verification: confirm all artifacts retained per retention schedule, and that recommended controls are in place and tested.

Detection metrics and KPIs to track post-incident:
- Mean time to contain (goal: < 15m for P0).
- Number of compromised principals rotated.
- Percentage of evidence exports that meet \"immovable\" (immutable, checksummed)标准.
- Number of code/CI changes deployed to reduce extraction risk.

Appendices:
A. Example deny policy JSON (authz service):
{
  \"policy_id\": \"deny-model-serve-temporary-2026-02-12\",
  \"effect\": \"deny\",
  \"resources\": [\"arn:redwood:model:*/serve\"],
  \"principals\": [\"*\"],
  \"conditions\": {\"time_window\": \"2026-02-12T15:00Z/2026-02-15T15:00Z\"}
}

B. Quick forensics command snippets:
- Export last 1000 requests for model m-123:
  curl -H \"Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN\" \"https://audit-api.internal/v1/requests?model_id=m-123&limit=1000\" > m-123-window.json
- Hash a file for chain-of-custody:
  sha256sum m-123-window.json > m-123-window.json.sha256

C. Contacts and escalation matrix:
- Security oncall: security-oncall@redwood.ai (Pager)
- Forensics team: se-forensics@redwood.ai
- Legal counsel (privacy): legal-privacy@redwood.ai
- Customer success escalation lead: cs-escalation@redwood.ai

Document change log:
- 2026-02-12: Created by Ava Chen.
- 2026-03-02: Expanded containment controls and added private deploy instructions (reviewers: Diego Morales, Priya Nair).
text:
Overview:

This document defines the standard operating procedure for responding to suspected or confirmed model exfiltration incidents involving Redwood-hosted inference systems. It combines RBAC containment controls, audit log evidence capture queries, data-residency and retention considerations, secure-SDLC remediation guidance, and the compliance packaging checklist required for regulatory/enterprise customers.

Scope and intent:
- Applies to all incidents where model internals, embeddings, prompt corpora, or customer-provided data may have been exfiltrated from any Redwood environment (hosted, dedicated, private).
- Focus is on rapid containment, defensible forensic evidence capture, and creating a reproducible compliance evidence package for audits.

Goals:
1. Stop ongoing exfiltration within 15 minutes of detection where possible.
2. Preserve forensic evidence with documented chain-of-custody.
3. Limit blast radius via RBAC and credential containment.
4. Produce a compliance artifact that supports SOC2/ISO/GDPR inquiries.

Threat model (concise):
- Insider misuse of privileged machine/service accounts.
- API key leakage used to drive high-rate queries and streaming token capture.
- Side-channel or model extraction via repeated adaptive queries.
- Compromise of a private deployment (customer-managed) that can be used to move artifacts to unauthorised locations.

Ownership and responders:
- Incident Commander (IC): Security Oncall (primary).
- Forensics Lead: Security Engineering.
- Service Owner(s): Runtime / Serving (if hosted) or Customer Success for private deploys.
- Legal & Privacy: notified immediately for potential data subject impact.
- Communications: Customer Success and PR as required.

Priority classification:
- P0: Confirmed exfiltration of customer PII or model weights/secret prompt corpora.
- P1: Strong indicators of extraction attempts (high similarity outputs, repeated boundary probing).
- P2: Unusual query patterns without clear exfil evidence.

Immediate triage checklist (first 15 minutes):
1. IC logs timeline and assigns Forensics Lead.
2. Snapshot current access tokens and session table. Note: do NOT revoke yet if live forensic capture is required—coordinate with Forensics Lead.
3. Temporarily throttle or rate-limit suspected API keys/routes.
4. Place affected model endpoints into maintenance mode (deny external requests) if P0/P1.
5. Preserve volatile state: export KV caches, current LRU for KV cache, last-10K request traces for the model shard.

Containment actions (RBAC-focused):
- Immediate actions (if P0):
  1. Revoke all session tokens associated with implicated service identities and API keys via central auth service.
  2. Disable short-lived service principals and mark for rotation (mark \"rotating=true\" in identity registry).
  3. Apply a temporary deny policy on the model-serving role for all non-essential principals:
     - Example deny policy (pseudo-YAML):
       deny: true
       principal_tags: [\"external\", \"service-account\", \"developer\"]
       resources: [\"/models/*/serve\", \"/models/*/stream\"]
       timeframe: \"T+0 to T+72h\"
- Isolation for private deployments: instruct customer to disable external egress in VPC and snapshot the deployment. Provide step-by-step commands (see \"Private deploy containment\" below).

Audit log evidence capture (guidance and example queries):
- Preserve immutable audit streams first: forward the affected time window to evidence-bucket with write-once retention (see audit retention policy).
- Minimum evidence fields to capture per request:
  - timestamp, request_id, model_id, route, principal_id, ip_address, user_agent, input_length, output_length, tokens_generated, response_sample_hash, parent_request_id

- Example Splunk/Kusto queries (adapt to platform):
  1) High-rate callers in window:
   index=redwood_audit sourcetype=requests earliest=\"-1h\" | stats count by principal_id | where count > 10000
  2) Streams with large outputs:
   index=redwood_audit route=\"/stream*\" | where tokens_generated > 10000 | table timestamp, principal_id, model_id, tokens_generated
  3) Similarity clustering (post-hoc):
   index=redwood_audit | eval sample_hash=sha256(substr(response,0,4096)) | stats count by sample_hash | where count > 3

- Immutable evidence export example (CLI):
  aws s3 cp s3://audit-stream/2026-02-12/window-0001 nds-case-1234/ --storage-class DEEP_ARCHIVE --metadata chain-of-custody=sealed

Forensic capture checklist (ASCII table):
| Artifact type | Recommended action | Retention label | Notes |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------|-------|
| API request traces | Export full JSON payloads for suspected window | evidence:primary | Include parent_request_id links |
| Streaming chunks | Capture earliest and latest 1MB per stream | evidence:primary | Chunk ordering matters |
| KV cache snapshot | Dump hot KV namespaces | evidence:secondary | May contain derived embeddings |
| Service credentials | Export credential issuance logs | evidence:primary | Include rotation events |
| Network egress logs | Capture flow logs + destination IPs | evidence:primary | Correlate with S3/ftp targets |

Data residency & retention considerations:
- If evidence contains EU personal data, keep evidence within EU-bound storage only unless Legal approves cross-border transfer. Mark exports with residency tag: residency=eu|na|apac.
- Evidence retention for compliance packaging:
  - Primary evidence: retain 7 years encrypted (for high-sensitivity incidents) or at least 1 year for lower severity.
  - Secondary artifacts: retain 1 year unless requested otherwise.
  - All retention decisions must be logged in the incident ticket and approved by Legal.

Private deploy containment (customer instructions snippet):
1) SSH into management node: ssh ops@<customer-vpn-ip>
2) sudo iptables -I OUTPUT -m conntrack --ctstate NEW -j DROP  # blocks new external connections
3) sudo systemctl stop redwood-agent.service
4) Snapshot /var/lib/redwood/models and upload to customer-provided S3 with immutable flags

Secure SDLC & remediation steps after containment:
1. Triage root cause: credential leakage, model attack pattern, or config issue.
2. Create a scoped fix PR with: code changes, unit tests for input sanitization/rate-limits, and regression prompt-set evals (see prompt-sets-and-benchmarks).
3. Require mandatory security review and replay of the incident pattern in non-prod for validation.
4. Backport fixes to any maintained runtime branches.
5. Extend CI to include an automated exfiltration smoke-test (detect high-output response patterns).

Compliance evidence packaging (deliverable checklist):
- Cover letter: incident timeline, summary, impact assessment, decision log.
- Chain-of-custody manifest: who exported which artifact, when, target storage.
- Artifact index: CRC/sha256 checksums and storage URIs for every exported file.
- RBAC audit: list of principals revoked/rotated and justification for each.
- Legal sign-off: redacted data subject notifications and regulator contacts if applicable.
- Technical appendix: set of Splunk/Kusto queries used, sample outputs, and threat-model mapping.

Risk and exceptions governance (temporary elevated access):
- Any emergency elevation for triage must be approved by: IC + Forensics Lead + One Legal approver. Approval must be recorded in the ticket with a start/expiry time (max 72 hours).
- All temporary grants must be short-lived credentials (max TTL 1 hour) and tied to a unique justification tag.
- Post-incident, conduct privileged access attestation and rotate any keys used by elevated users.

Post-incident review (30/60/90-day cadence):
1. 72-hour hot debrief: capture immediate lessons and patch rollout plan.
2. 30-day technical retrospective: attackers path, detection gaps, automation opportunities.
3. 90-day risk verification: confirm all artifacts retained per retention schedule, and that recommended controls are in place and tested.

Detection metrics and KPIs to track post-incident:
- Mean time to contain (goal: < 15m for P0).
- Number of compromised principals rotated.
- Percentage of evidence exports that meet \"immovable\" (immutable, checksummed)标准.
- Number of code/CI changes deployed to reduce extraction risk.

Appendices:
A. Example deny policy JSON (authz service):
{
  \"policy_id\": \"deny-model-serve-temporary-2026-02-12\",
  \"effect\": \"deny\",
  \"resources\": [\"arn:redwood:model:*/serve\"],
  \"principals\": [\"*\"],
  \"conditions\": {\"time_window\": \"2026-02-12T15:00Z/2026-02-15T15:00Z\"}
}

B. Quick forensics command snippets:
- Export last 1000 requests for model m-123:
  curl -H \"Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN\" \"https://audit-api.internal/v1/requests?model_id=m-123&limit=1000\" > m-123-window.json
- Hash a file for chain-of-custody:
  sha256sum m-123-window.json > m-123-window.json.sha256

C. Contacts and escalation matrix:
- Security oncall: security-oncall@redwood.ai (Pager)
- Forensics team: se-forensics@redwood.ai
- Legal counsel (privacy): legal-privacy@redwood.ai
- Customer success escalation lead: cs-escalation@redwood.ai

Document change log:
- 2026-02-12: Created by Ava Chen.
- 2026-03-02: Expanded containment controls and added private deploy instructions (reviewers: Diego Morales, Priya Nair).

title: Graceful Restart Latency Contracts and Snapshot Synthesis
preview:
Overview

This document defines latency contracts (SLOs/SLO targets) and the operational protocol for graceful restarts of runtime workers, kernel modules, and model shards. It also specifies the snapshot synthesis format we use for resumable execution: what state is captured, how it is validated, and how resume/rollback works. The goal is to provide a concrete, testable, and observable procedure that keeps customer-facing latency and correctness within predictable bounds during maintenance, upgrades, and transient failures.

Audience

- Runtime engineers implementing restart semantics and kernel handoff.
- SRE and oncall responsible for incident response when restarts impact SLOs.
- Release engineers and QA writing canary tests and fidelity benchmarks.

Definitions

- graceful restart: a coordinated shutdown and resume of a worker (or kernel) that preserves sufficient execution state so in-flight requests can be resumed or rerouted without correctness regressions.
- cold restart: full process restart without preserved execution snapshots; requires warmup/synthetic traffic to reach steady-state.
- snapshot: a serialized representation of runtime-required state for resumability (KV cache segments, session token pointers, decoder cache offsets, plugin state checksums).
- resume window: the time window after restart during which resumed requests must meet latency/fidelity guarantees.

Latency Contracts (SLOs)

These contracts are the SLA-facing expectations that the runtime operator must meet under planned maintenance (rolling upgrades) and common unplanned restarts (OOMs, node reboots, kernel panics). Targets are expressed as 30-day rolling metrics and validated through synthetic traffic during canaries.

Primary SLOs

- Controlled rolling restart (per-pool, single-tenant): resumed-request p95 <= 120 ms, p99 <= 400 ms within 30s of restart completion.
- Kernel module swap (same-node live-replace): resumed-request p95 <= 60 ms, p99 <= 200 ms within 10s.
- Cold restart recovery (node-rejoin): median request latency to baseline <= 2x baseline after warmup period of 120s; p99 should not exceed 4x baseline.
- Snapshot application time: time to apply a snapshot and be ready for resume <= 250 ms for per-session snapshots, <= 2s for full shard snapshots.

Measuring and Attribution

- Signals used: request timeline (trace start -> resume/serve), kernel handoff events, snapshot-save and snapshot-apply events, kvcache warmness score, model load digest.
- Primary metric names: runtime.resume.apply_latency_ms (histogram), runtime.resume.success_rate (ratio), runtime.snapshot.serialize_ms, runtime.snapshot.size_bytes.
- Attribution: resumed vs. newly routed requests are tagged with resume=true/false. SLOs are measured only on resumed=true requests for restart contracts and only on resumed=false requests for cold-start recovery windows.

Snapshot Synthesis: What We Capture

Snapshots must be compact, deterministic, and verifiable. They are intended to let a worker resume decoding/generation for an in-flight request or restore a warm execution state for a session cohort. The synthesis procedure produces a snapshot manifest and a binary blob.

Essential snapshot components (per-entity):

- header: snapshot_version, producer_node_id, sequence_id, timestamp (ISO8601).
- kvcache segments: list of (prefix_hash, ttl_ms, compressed_bytes_base64, checksum_sha256).
- decoder cache: token_index_offset, kv_positions, compressed_kv_blob (lz4), kernel_hint_id.
- active invocation metadata: request_id, model_id, config_hash (quantization/profile), prompt_prefix_fingerprint.
- plugin state checksums: list of plugin_name -> checksum, and optional plugin blob pointer.

Format and versioning

- snapshot manifest is JSON (text) with fixed keys; blobs are base64 strings or object-store pointers.
- versioning: snapshot_version follows semver-like format v<major>.<minor>.<build>. Consumers must reject unknown major versions.

Consistency and Safety Checks

Before accepting a snapshot to resume, the worker applies these gates in order:

1) model_id and config_hash must match the local loaded model. If mismatch, reject (fail-open routing to fallback model).
2) checksum verification for all blobs. Any mismatch -> reject.
3) ttl_ms on kvcache segments must not be expired; expired segments are pruned.

If any gate fails during a planned maintenance canary, the runtime must route the in-flight request to a compatible fallback variant and mark the snapshot as incompatible in telemetry.

Operational Protocols

Planned rolling upgrade checklist (per-pool):

1) Mark pool as draining in control plane for 30s.
2) For each worker being rotated: capture snapshot for active invocations and attempt to handoff to hot neighbor using snapshot RPC.
3) Wait for runtime.resume.apply_latency_ms p95 < 120 ms for 5 consecutive seconds on the neighborhood (canary sample).
4) If passes, decommission old worker; if fails, rollback routing and escalate to oncall.

Emergency restart runbook (short):

1) Detect restart cause and capture node-level signals: OOM, kernel-panic, watchdog.
2) If snapshot exists and passes safety checks, apply snapshot and mark resumed=true for requests attached to snapshot.
3) If snapshot rejected or unavailable, use fallback routing to sibling pool and trigger warmup traffic with weight 0.1 -> 1 over 60s.
4) Post-incident: run snapshot validation test suite (see Testing and Canary section) and file incident with execution fingerprint.

Canary and Synthetic Tests

We validate restart contracts using two classes of synthetic tests executed in CI and production canary environments:

- per-invocation resume test: inject 1–3 active invocations, force a swap of kernel module and assert runtime.resume.apply_latency_ms p95/p99.
- shard snapshot roundtrip: serialize a full shard snapshot, write to object store, read back on a separate node, apply, and run e2e generation verification (exact-match on deterministic seeds).

Testing matrix (example):

| Test type | Snapshot size | Assertion | Run cadence | Owner |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| per-invocation resume | small (<=128KB) | p95<=120ms, success_rate>=99% | CI + nightly canary | runtime-team |
| kernel swap live-replace | n/a | p95<=60ms across node | weekly canary | kernel-team |
| shard roundtrip | large (<=50MB) | deterministic output match | nightly | infra-team |

Implementation notes and sample commands

- Capture snapshot (CLI):

  curl -X POST https://control-plane/_internal/snapshot/capture 
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' 
    -d '{"worker_id":"worker-42","mode":"per-invocation"}'

  Response: {"snapshot_manifest_url":"s3://redwood-snapshots/poolA/worker-42/snap-v1.2.3-1234.json"}

- Apply snapshot (worker):

  rw-runtime apply-snapshot --manifest s3://.../snap.json --strict-checks=true

YAML example: runtime resume gating policy

  resume_policy:
    allow_if:
      - model_match: true
      - checksum_ok: true
      - kvcache_warmness_threshold: 0.6
    fallback_strategy: route_to_fallback_pool
    apply_timeout_ms: 250

Observability and Dashboards

- Dashboard panels to create/update: Resume latency heatmap (by pool, model), Snapshot success rate (by node), Snapshot serialization size histogram, Resume/error timeline waterfall.
- Alerts: snapshot.apply_failure_rate > 1% for 5m; resume.apply_latency.p99 > 500ms for 3m -> page oncall.

Ownership and Roles

- Runtime team: implement snapshot APIs, maintain resume metrics and canary infra.
- Kernel team: ensure live-replace path for kernel modules and provide kernel_hint_id semantics.
- SRE: own alerting thresholds, incident runbooks, and warmup traffic orchestration.

Release and Rollout Guidance

- Feature gate: snapshot major version rollouts must be staged behind a feature flag.
- Canary plan: enable on 5% of traffic in poolA for 24h, monitor resume.apply_latency_ms p95/p99 and snapshot.success_rate.

Appendix: Troubleshooting checklist (fast reference)

1) resume.apply_latency samples high: check snapshot.apply_latency_ms, worker CPU contention, and kvcache hit rates.
2) snapshot checksums failing: verify object-store replication and manifest integrity (run crc32 on blob).
3) incompatible model/config: confirm model version pinning and rollout policy, escalate to release engineering if rollout unintended.

References

- See runbooks: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-deployment-circuit-breakers-and-sla-fallback-playbook-2026
- See observability contract: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/team-wiki/runtime-observability-data-model-and-tracebook-2025

Contact

For questions or extensions to this contract, open an RFC in the runtime-team backlog and cc runtime@redwood.internal.

title: Capability Signal Profiles and Client Negotiation Playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook defines a compact, observable protocol for advertising runtime capabilities and negotiating effective behavior between clients and Redwood inference endpoints. The protocol reduces brittle client checks, enables safe feature rollouts, and provides structured signals for debugging and cost accounting.

Scope: Applies to HTTP and gRPC endpoints that expose evolving inference features (streaming modes, structured outputs, function/tool calling semantics, fidelity/quantization hints, early-exit heuristics). Exempt: purely documentation-only changes and telemetry-only fields.

Background and motivation
- Frequent client breakages occurred when runtime features changed without an explicit compatibility surface.
- Feature guards and ad-hoc query params proved inadequate for multi-variant deployments and private/legacy tenants.
- A negotiation layer allows clients to express intent and ordered fallbacks to avoid failures and provide higher-quality telemetry.

Key concepts and definitions
- Capability profile: a short, self-describing descriptor naming a capability and enumerating minimal contract points (semantic id, implied failure modes, preferred fallback).
- Client intent: the set of capabilities the client wishes to use for a request, with a priority order and whether each is required.
- Negotiation handshake: single-round exchange encoded at the request/response header level (no extra RTT).

Design principles
- Explicit, machine-readable: use compact JSON in headers (base64-encoded where needed) rather than free-form flags. Keep header payloads < 1KB.
- Backwards compatible: servers that ignore the header must return conservative behavior and emit a missing-header metric.
- Observability-first: every negotiation must emit a span event and metrics with standardized tags for aggregation.
- Low latency: negotiation piggybacks on the initial request. No additional handshake RTTs.
- Fail-safe: if a required capability is unavailable, servers should fail fast with a clear status and negotiation explanation.

Wire representation (example patterns)
- Request header name: X-RW-Capability-Intent
- Response header name: X-RW-Capability-Profile

Request header (JSON, recommended base64-encoded for transport):
{
  "intentVersion": "1",
  "capabilities": [
    {"id": "structured-output.v2", "required": false, "fallback": "text-only"},
    {"id": "early-exit.v2", "required": true}
  ],
  "costSensitivity": "low"
}

Response header (JSON):
{
  "agreedVersion": "1",
  "applied": ["structured-output.v2"],
  "unavailable": [{"id":"early-exit.v2","reason":"model-incompatible","fallback":"none"}],
  "notes": "Quantized variant chosen (q4)"
}

Negotiation semantics and server behavior
- Unknown header: server treats as no intent and records metric rw.negotiation.missing_header.
- Best-effort application: server should attempt to apply requested optional capabilities and list applied/unavailable in the response header.
- Required capability failure: if a capability is marked required and cannot be provided, server returns 412 Precondition Failed with X-RW-Capability-Profile describing the mismatch and an explicit \"fallback\" field (\"none\" indicates hard failure).
- Fallback execution: when server applies a fallback, it must include \"fallback_applied\" semantics in traces and provide a human-readable note in response headers for quick diagnosis.

Observability and telemetry
- Mandatory metrics:
  - rw.negotiation.requests: counter (labels: endpoint, intentVersion)
  - rw.negotiation.applied_count: counter (labels: capability, endpoint)
  - rw.negotiation.unavailable_count: counter (labels: capability, reason)
  - rw.negotiation.required_failures: counter (labels: endpoint)
- Tracing tags (span attributes): negotiation.client_intent_version, negotiation.applied_list (comma-separated), negotiation.unavailable_reasons.
- Logs: negotiation decisions must be recorded at INFO level with correlation_id and minimal profile content.

Compatibility, schema evolution, and versioning
- intentVersion bumps: non-breaking additions (new optional capabilities) may be added without a version bump. Removing or reinterpreting a capability requires a new intentVersion and documented migration steps.
- Deprecation window: when deprecating a capability, announce in release notes and emit rw.negotiation.deprecation_warning when clients request a deprecated id. Keep behavior stable for a minimum of 90 days.

Security and privacy considerations
- Capability headers may contain sensitive hints (e.g., private feature flags). Mark headers as non-cacheable and avoid echoing sensitive details into high-cardinality logs.
- For private deployments, negotiation may surface tenant-specific variant IDs; redact tenant identifiers in aggregated metrics.

Rollout and testing guidance
1) Add capability: author schema, document behavior, and add unit/integration tests that simulate degraded runtimes.
2) Canary: enable support on a canary region with augmented sampling of negotiation traces.
3) Metrics gating: require zero required_failures and <0.1% required failure rate in canary before wider rollout.
4) Partner testing: notify SDK teams to exercise new optional capabilities and update client-side hinting helpers.

Operational playbook and runbook entries
- Diagnostic checklist when clients report failures:
  1. Inspect X-RW-Capability-Intent in client request and X-RW-Capability-Profile in response.
  2. Check rw.negotiation.required_failures for spike correlated to deploys.
  3. Look for \"model-incompatible\" or \"resource-constrained\" reasons in unavailable list.
  4. If fallback_applied, verify downstream processing and cost accounting.

Examples
- curl example (CLI, unencoded for readability):
  curl -H 'X-RW-Capability-Intent: {"intentVersion":"1","capabilities":[{"id":"structured-output.v2","required":false}]}' \\  https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate
- Server response header example:
  X-RW-Capability-Profile: {"agreedVersion":"1","applied":["structured-output.v2"],"notes":"applied q4 quantized path"}

Troubleshooting examples
- Symptom: Client got a 412 when requesting early-exit.v2.
  Action: Verify server reported \"reason\":\"model-incompatible\"; if so, check model variant mapping and quantization policy for the tenant.
- Symptom: High rate of fallback_applied.
  Action: Correlate with rw.capacity.preemption and resource metrics; consider enabling fallback caching or adjusting batching profiles.

Ownership and next steps
- Primary owner: eng-platform / runtime-protocols (reach out via #eng-runtime-protocols).
- Implementation trackers: create a feature branch for the header parsing library in sdk-shared/protocols.
- Suggested rollout: start with internal endpoints and SDK test harness before externalizing to hosted API.

References and related documents
- See related standards: service-contract-change-signal, wire-format-negotiation, and runtime-sidecar-contract for private deployments.
- ADRs to consult during design: progressive-degradation-and-quality-fallback-playbook, model-annotation-format, and runtime-cache-invalidation.

Revision history
- 2026-02-10: initial draft by Ava Chen.
- 2026-03-01: added observability metric labels and rollout guidance (reviewers: Marcus, Priya, Ethan).
text:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook defines a compact, observable protocol for advertising runtime capabilities and negotiating effective behavior between clients and Redwood inference endpoints. The protocol reduces brittle client checks, enables safe feature rollouts, and provides structured signals for debugging and cost accounting.

Scope: Applies to HTTP and gRPC endpoints that expose evolving inference features (streaming modes, structured outputs, function/tool calling semantics, fidelity/quantization hints, early-exit heuristics). Exempt: purely documentation-only changes and telemetry-only fields.

Background and motivation
- Frequent client breakages occurred when runtime features changed without an explicit compatibility surface.
- Feature guards and ad-hoc query params proved inadequate for multi-variant deployments and private/legacy tenants.
- A negotiation layer allows clients to express intent and ordered fallbacks to avoid failures and provide higher-quality telemetry.

Key concepts and definitions
- Capability profile: a short, self-describing descriptor naming a capability and enumerating minimal contract points (semantic id, implied failure modes, preferred fallback).
- Client intent: the set of capabilities the client wishes to use for a request, with a priority order and whether each is required.
- Negotiation handshake: single-round exchange encoded at the request/response header level (no extra RTT).

Design principles
- Explicit, machine-readable: use compact JSON in headers (base64-encoded where needed) rather than free-form flags. Keep header payloads < 1KB.
- Backwards compatible: servers that ignore the header must return conservative behavior and emit a missing-header metric.
- Observability-first: every negotiation must emit a span event and metrics with standardized tags for aggregation.
- Low latency: negotiation piggybacks on the initial request. No additional handshake RTTs.
- Fail-safe: if a required capability is unavailable, servers should fail fast with a clear status and negotiation explanation.

Wire representation (example patterns)
- Request header name: X-RW-Capability-Intent
- Response header name: X-RW-Capability-Profile

Request header (JSON, recommended base64-encoded for transport):
{
  "intentVersion": "1",
  "capabilities": [
    {"id": "structured-output.v2", "required": false, "fallback": "text-only"},
    {"id": "early-exit.v2", "required": true}
  ],
  "costSensitivity": "low"
}

Response header (JSON):
{
  "agreedVersion": "1",
  "applied": ["structured-output.v2"],
  "unavailable": [{"id":"early-exit.v2","reason":"model-incompatible","fallback":"none"}],
  "notes": "Quantized variant chosen (q4)"
}

Negotiation semantics and server behavior
- Unknown header: server treats as no intent and records metric rw.negotiation.missing_header.
- Best-effort application: server should attempt to apply requested optional capabilities and list applied/unavailable in the response header.
- Required capability failure: if a capability is marked required and cannot be provided, server returns 412 Precondition Failed with X-RW-Capability-Profile describing the mismatch and an explicit \"fallback\" field (\"none\" indicates hard failure).
- Fallback execution: when server applies a fallback, it must include \"fallback_applied\" semantics in traces and provide a human-readable note in response headers for quick diagnosis.

Observability and telemetry
- Mandatory metrics:
  - rw.negotiation.requests: counter (labels: endpoint, intentVersion)
  - rw.negotiation.applied_count: counter (labels: capability, endpoint)
  - rw.negotiation.unavailable_count: counter (labels: capability, reason)
  - rw.negotiation.required_failures: counter (labels: endpoint)
- Tracing tags (span attributes): negotiation.client_intent_version, negotiation.applied_list (comma-separated), negotiation.unavailable_reasons.
- Logs: negotiation decisions must be recorded at INFO level with correlation_id and minimal profile content.

Compatibility, schema evolution, and versioning
- intentVersion bumps: non-breaking additions (new optional capabilities) may be added without a version bump. Removing or reinterpreting a capability requires a new intentVersion and documented migration steps.
- Deprecation window: when deprecating a capability, announce in release notes and emit rw.negotiation.deprecation_warning when clients request a deprecated id. Keep behavior stable for a minimum of 90 days.

Security and privacy considerations
- Capability headers may contain sensitive hints (e.g., private feature flags). Mark headers as non-cacheable and avoid echoing sensitive details into high-cardinality logs.
- For private deployments, negotiation may surface tenant-specific variant IDs; redact tenant identifiers in aggregated metrics.

Rollout and testing guidance
1) Add capability: author schema, document behavior, and add unit/integration tests that simulate degraded runtimes.
2) Canary: enable support on a canary region with augmented sampling of negotiation traces.
3) Metrics gating: require zero required_failures and <0.1% required failure rate in canary before wider rollout.
4) Partner testing: notify SDK teams to exercise new optional capabilities and update client-side hinting helpers.

Operational playbook and runbook entries
- Diagnostic checklist when clients report failures:
  1. Inspect X-RW-Capability-Intent in client request and X-RW-Capability-Profile in response.
  2. Check rw.negotiation.required_failures for spike correlated to deploys.
  3. Look for \"model-incompatible\" or \"resource-constrained\" reasons in unavailable list.
  4. If fallback_applied, verify downstream processing and cost accounting.

Examples
- curl example (CLI, unencoded for readability):
  curl -H 'X-RW-Capability-Intent: {"intentVersion":"1","capabilities":[{"id":"structured-output.v2","required":false}]}' \\  https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate

  X-RW-Capability-Profile: {"agreedVersion":"1","applied":["structured-output.v2"],"notes":"applied q4 quantized path"}

  Action: Verify server reported \"reason\":\"model-incompatible\"; if so, check model variant mapping and quantization policy for the tenant.
- Symptom: High rate of fallback_applied.
  Action: Correlate with rw.capacity.preemption and resource metrics; consider enabling fallback caching or adjusting batching profiles.

Ownership and next steps

- Implementation trackers: create a feature branch for the header parsing library in sdk-shared/protocols.
- Suggested rollout: start with internal endpoints and SDK test harness before externalizing to hosted API.

title: SDK telemetry mismatch — canonical detection & fixes
preview:
Summary
=======

This document describes the common telemetry-mismatch patterns we see between Redwood client SDKs and the Redwood serving/control plane, concrete detection heuristics, triage steps, canonical fixes (SDK and server-side), and recommended tests to prevent regressions. The audience is SDK engineers, developer-experience support, and oncall SREs.

Why this matters
------------------

Telemetry fidelity is required for accurate cost attribution, latency breakdowns, and automatic routing decisions. Mismatches lead to:
- Incorrect token counts in billing dashboards
- Misattributed latency or error spikes that obscure root cause
- Invalid sampling for analytics or A/B rollouts
- Failed dependability checks in canary deployments

Common symptom categories
-------------------------

1) Token count delta — SDK reports fewer/more tokens than server: typically caused by encoding differences, retry double-counting or missing streaming ack points.
2) Event ordering drift — SDK emits events out of order (e.g., start->complete swapped) due to async middleware.
3) Missing error codes — SDK surface layer catches and transforms internal codes to generic errors, stripping server codes used by routing.
4) Sampling mismatch — SDK sampling config (telemetry.sample_rate) different from platform defaults causing uneven analytics coverage.

Detection heuristics (quick checks)
----------------------------------

- Compare per-route token_count_sdk vs token_count_platform aggregated by 5m windows. Flag routes with absolute delta > 5% and > 1k tokens in window.
- Alert when SDK-side success rate diverges from platform success rate by >2 percentage points for 10m.
- Monitor event latency histogram skew: if client-observed p95 differs from server p95 by >50ms and correlation with 'retry' tag is >10%, inspect retry handling.

Automated diagnostic queries (examples)
---------------------------------------

1) Token delta (SQL-ish pseudocode)

SELECT route, window_start, SUM(token_count_sdk) AS sdk_tokens, SUM(token_count_server) AS server_tokens,
  (SUM(token_count_server)-SUM(token_count_sdk))/NULLIF(SUM(token_count_server),0) AS relative_delta
FROM telemetry_ingest.tokens
WHERE window_start >= now() - interval '1 hour'
GROUP BY route, window_start
HAVING SUM(server_tokens) > 1000 AND ABS(relative_delta) > 0.05;

2) Event ordering mismatches (log query)

index=client-telemetry OR index=platform-telemetry |
  transaction_id | sort timestamp | dedup transaction_id | where first.event != last.event

Canonical root causes and fixes
--------------------------------

A. Encoding/Tokenizer mismatch
- Root cause: SDK and server use different tokenizer versions or normalization rules (e.g., unicode normalization, pre-tokenization truncation).
- Fix: Pin tokenizer version in SDK packages and ensure platform tokenizer metadata is available in model catalog.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Add tokenizer-version metadata to every request header: X-Redwood-Tokenizer: "tkn-v2025-11"
  2. Expose a compatibility check API: redwood.sdk.compatibility.checkTokenizer(model, tokenizerVersion)
  3. Add unit tests asserting token counts for a canonical prompt set (see tests/telemetry/token-compat-sets).
- Server-side mitigation: When mismatch exceeds threshold, annotate request with token_estimate_source="client-est" and route to an investigational queue.

B. Retry double-counting
- Root cause: Retries increment telemetry counters both on the original request and on the retry path; duplicate transaction_id is not deduped.
- Fix: Use idempotency key + platform-level dedup TTL.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Generate idempotency_key per logical request and attach header: Idempotency-Key: <uuid4>
  2. Ensure retries reuse the same key; backoff middleware must not regenerate it.
  3. Document the behavior: retries are counted once on platform if idempotency_key present and dedupe TTL=30s.
- Server-side mitigation: Implement duplicate transaction detection using (idempotency_key, route, model) keys and de-duplicate metrics increments.

C. Streaming vs. Final-event mismatch
- Root cause: SDK emits a "response_complete" event when stream is closed locally, while platform waits for final server ack; missing streaming-ack messages cause underreporting.
- Fix: Align client and server streaming lifecycle and add explicit ack messages.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Implement websocket-level "stream_ack" message after final chunk is processed.
  2. Emit events in order: request.start -> chunk.* -> stream_ack -> request.complete.
  3. Add tests that simulate partial disconnects and ensure stream_ack is retried.
- Server-side mitigation: Accept client-side stream_ack as authoritative for final token count if signed by SDK key.

D. Telemetry sanitization middleware swallowing server codes
- Root cause: Middleware transforms platform error 503 -> SDKError("transient") and drops structured_code. Downstream systems rely on structured_code for routing.
- Fix: Preserve structured_error_code and include a mapping in SDK error objects.
- SDK patch checklist:
  1. Extend Error class: StructuredError { message, structured_code, server_timestamp }
  2. Surface structured_code in telemetry events as metadata.
  3. Do not convert structured_code to free text.
- Server-side mitigation: Add assertion tests in the CI that call each SDK and verify structured_code arrives unmodified.

Operational runbook (high-level)
--------------------------------

When an alert fires for telemetry mismatch:
1) Pager oncall receives alert and checks pre-baked diagnostic queries (Token delta, Event ordering).
2) If delta localized to a specific SDK version -> open a bug in SDK repo and tag "telemetry-high"; escalate to SDK release lead.
3) If delta global across SDK versions -> trace by model/region and check for server deployment or tokenizer change; open incident if >5% customers affected.
4) Apply temporary mitigations: enable server-side token_estimate_source tagging, pause affected routing automations, and reduce sampling of analytics to avoid noise.
5) After root cause fix, run golden prompt set and deploy compatibility test before closing the ticket.

Detailed triage checklist (step-by-step)
-----------------------------------------

1. Collect IDs: transaction_id, request_id, sdk_version, model, route, region.
2. Run token delta query for the transaction_id.
3. Search logs for idempotency_key collisions.
4. Reproduce locally with the same SDK version and tokenizer bundle.
5. If streaming: run controlled disconnect test to see if stream_ack missing.
6. If error codes missing: replay requests with debug-telemetry enabled and capture raw server response.
7. Draft PR with minimal SDK change and smoke tests, request expedited review from reviewer list.

Preventing regressions (tests and release gates)
------------------------------------------------
- Add a required pre-merge check in SDK repositories: "telemetry-integration-check" that runs the token-compat fixture (10 canonical prompts) and fails on >1% token count variance.
- Platform CI must run an SDK compatibility matrix (current SDK stable + previous two versions) on every tokenizer or tokenization-related commit.
- Add e2e canary that compares client-observed vs server-observed telemetry for a synthetic traffic profile every hour and opens a ticket on drift >2%.

Snippets & examples
-------------------

1) Example: attaching headers in Python SDK

```python
from redwood import Client
client = Client(api_key="REDACTED")
resp = client.generate(
  model="redwood-alpha-1",
  prompt="Summarize the Q1 goals.",
  headers={
    "X-Redwood-Tokenizer": "tkn-v2025-11",
    "Idempotency-Key": "6f5f0f6a-1b5c-4caa-b2aa-bb1f0e2d3f4a",
  }
)
```

2) Example server-side de-dup pseudocode (Go)

```go
func recordTelemetry(req TelemetryReq) {
  key := fmt.Sprintf("%s:%s:%s", req.IdempotencyKey, req.Route, req.Model)
  if cache.Exists(key) {
    return // already counted
  }
  cache.Set(key, true, 30*time.Second)
  metrics.Inc("tokens", req.TokenCount)
}
```

Configuration table (recommended defaults)
-----------------------------------------

| Setting | SDK default | Platform default | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| telemetry.sample_rate | 0.05 | 0.05 | Ensure SDK default matches platform to avoid sampling mismatch |
| telemetry.tokenizer_version_header | "X-Redwood-Tokenizer" | required | Set and assert at request time |
| telemetry.idempotency_ttl_seconds | 30 | 30 | Deduplication window for token counting |
| telemetry.stream_ack_enabled | true | true | Required for websocket/stream transports |

Ownership & escalation
-----------------------

- Primary owner: Developer Experience (SDK team) — triage and release SDK patches.
- Secondary owner: Eng Platform — server-side dedup and compatibility checks.
- If customer-visible billing impact > $5k in 24h -> page finance-legal and account managers; start incident response.

Communications templates
------------------------

Short customer notification (billing-impacting):
"We detected a telemetry reporting discrepancy affecting X requests between SDK version Y and the platform during YYYY-MM-DD. We have deployed a mitigation and are rolling an SDK fix. We will provide a follow-up with corrected billing if required."

Internal slack template to #dx-oncall:
"ALERT: telemetry-drift route={route} sdk={sdk_version} delta={percent}% action=triage assigned={owner}"

Appendix: canonical prompt set (for token compatibility tests)
-----------------------------------------------------------
- 10 prompts covering: JSON generation, long legal text, unicode normalization edge cases, heavily tokenized code, multi-lingual inputs (zh, es, ar), and streaming-chunked responses.
- Stored at repo: redwood/telemetry/canonical-prompts/compat-2026.json

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-02-10: Document created by Ava Chen.
- 2026-02-20: Added server de-dup pseudocode and configuration table (reviewer: Diego Ramirez).
- 2026-03-01: Added streaming ack guidance and updated triage thresholds (reviewer: Priya Nandakumar).

- 10 prompts covering: JSON generation, long legal text, unicode normalization edge cases, heavily tokenized code, multi-lingual inputs (zh, es, ar), and streaming-chunked responses.
- Stored at repo: redwood/telemetry/canonical-prompts/compat-2026.json

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-02-10: Document created by Ava Chen.
- 2026-02-20: Added server de-dup pseudocode and configuration table (reviewer: Diego Ramirez).
- 2026-03-01: Added streaming ack guidance and updated triage thresholds (reviewer: Priya Nandakumar).

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook defines the Adaptive Eval Funnel (AEF) — a standardized, cost-aware sampling and prioritization pipeline for running continuous evaluation at scale. AEF is designed to reduce token and compute spend by 40–70% relative to naive full-scan evals while preserving coverage for high-risk slices and regression detection sensitivity.

Goals:
- Maintain high-fidelity regression detection on high-risk slices (precision >= 0.90 on targeted failure classes).
- Reduce end-to-end eval cost per release by 40% (measured in GPU-minutes and token units).
- Provide reproducible, auditable sampling decisions and a transparent budget allocation for product/tenant workloads.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: applies to model onboarding, nightly and pre-release eval runs, and production regression triage workload mirroring. Does NOT replace small-sample human evaluation for subjective quality checks.
- Audience: applied-ML engineers, eval platform owners, product reliability engineers, and model steward teams.

Definitions:
- Funnel stage: a logical step in the AEF (seed, stratified-scan, focused-verify, heavy-sim).
- Slice: a partition of workload (intent category, customer tier, language, prompt-length bin, etc.).
- Budget unit: our internal token+GPU-minute currency; 1 unit = 10k input tokens + 1 GPU-minute at p3 equivalent.

When to use AEF:
1) Pre-release validation for model promotions.
2) Nightly smoke and slice-focused monitoring for production models.
3) Regression triage when a large surface area prevents exhaustive replay.

High-level funnel (four stages):
1. Seed discovery (low cost)
   - Objective: locate candidate failing slices cheaply.
   - Inputs: lightweight classifiers, anomaly scoring over telemetry, recent customer Qs, error logs.
   - Typical compute: <5% of total budget.
2. Stratified scan (medium cost)
   - Objective: broad coverage with stratified sampling across prioritized dimensions.
   - Method: stratify by slice weights; draw proportional samples with minimum cell size.
   - Typical compute: 25–35% of budget.
3. Focused verify (targeted)
   - Objective: validate potential regressions with larger, deterministic sample sets per flagged slice.
   - Method: fixed-seed replays, ensemble-checks, human-in-the-loop for ambiguous cases.
   - Typical compute: 30–40% of budget.
4. Heavy simulation (expensive)
   - Objective: full-scan or synthetic amplification for highest-risk or regulatory slices.
   - Triggered only if FDR/precision thresholds not met or downstream business metrics at risk.
   - Typical compute: 10–30% of budget (on demand).

Sampling configuration (recommended defaults):
| Parameter | Default | Rationale |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| monthly_budget_units | 500 | Enough to run nightly stratified scans + 2 pre-release runs for medium-sized products |
| min_cell_size | 50 | Minimum per-slice sample to estimate proportions with modest variance |
| seed_detector_threshold | 0.7 | Anomaly score threshold to escalate from seed to stratified-scan |
| focused_verify_size | 300 | Per-flagged-slice sample size for deterministic checks |
| heavy_sim_trigger_precision | 0.85 | If focused verify precision < this, run heavy sim |

Priority weighting scheme (example):
1) Safety / content policy risk: weight x3
2) High-revenue customers (top 10% ARR): weight x2
3) New prompt patterns / long context: weight x1.5
4) Low-frequency slices: floor sample for minimum visibility

Operational procedure (step-by-step):
1) Configure budget and slice definitions in eval-harness config (file: eval-configs/aef.yml). Make sure labels map to telemetry fields and tenant IDs.
2) Run Seed discovery job (weekly/nightly). Command: 
```bash
redwood-eval run --pipeline aef --stage seed --config eval-configs/aef.yml --output s3://eval-outputs/aef/seed/$(date +%F)
```
3) Seed job produces ranked slice candidates. Automatically promote top N where anomaly_score >= seed_detector_threshold to Stratified scan. Document promotions in the run artifact.
4) Run Stratified scan with budget clamps. Use stratified-sampling operator with deterministic RNG seed for reproducibility. CLI: 
```bash
redwood-eval run --pipeline aef --stage stratified --config eval-configs/aef.yml --budget-units 150 --seed 20260301
```
5) Post-process stratified results to compute per-slice metrics (accuracy delta, entropy change, top-k hallucination rate). If any slice metric breaches alert thresholds (see "Monitoring and thresholds"), schedule Focused verify.
6) Focused verify is launched with deterministic seeds and ensemble baselines. Results produce a triage report with suggested root-cause tags (prompt-sensitivity, quantization-delta, kv-cache-regression, model-parameter-shift). Human reviewer assigned automatically for ambiguous failures.
7) Heavy simulation is run only with explicit owner approval; include cost estimate and rollback action in the run ticket.

Monitoring and thresholds:
- Metrics collected per slice: accuracy_delta, top3_confidence_drop, hallucination_rate, token_cost_per_input, p95_latency.
- Alert thresholds (examples):
  - accuracy_delta <= -0.06 -> auto-priority alert
  - hallucination_rate increase >= 0.03 absolute -> safety review
  - token_cost_per_input increase >= 20% -> cost-investigation
  - p95_latency increase >= 200ms -> perf triage

Acceptance and escalation rules:
- If a high-weight slice (safety or top-revenue) breaches accuracy_delta <= -0.04 in Focused verify, block promotion and open a model regression incident with incident severity S2.
- If multiple mid-weight slices show correlated failures across 3+ prompts, escalate to Applied ML on-call for bisecting.

Reproducibility and auditability:
- All runs must store: config.yaml, RNG seed, model sha, quant-profile id, inference kernel version, and the full input sample set.
- Use artifact naming convention: aef/{model_sha}/{run-date}/{stage}/{budget-id}.zip

Cost control knobs and tradeoffs:
- min_cell_size: raising reduces false positives but increases cost.
- stratified_coverage_ratio: 0.2–0.6 recommended. Lower values favor targeted checks.
- use_kv_cache_replay: toggle; caches reduce token compute for long context but may obscure prompt-sensitivity failures.

Example evaluation config (eval-configs/aef.yml):
```yaml
pipeline: aef
budget_units: 300
priority_weights:
  safety: 3
  revenue: 2
  long_context: 1.5
slices:
  - name: billing-intent
    selectors: ["intent:billing", "lang:en"]
  - name: long-context-technical
    selectors: ["prompt_len:>1024", "topic:technical"]
```

Triage playbook (on detection of regression):
1) Confirm reproducibility with Focused verify using stored RNG seed.
2) Run bisect suite across model versions and quant-profiles.
3) If quantization implicated, tag with quant-profile id and set rollback to float-weighted baseline.
4) If prompt cluster implicated, add representative prompts to the regression prompt-set and schedule patch evaluation.
5) Publish incident summary to the model pager channel and attach artifact bundle.

Ownership and SLAs:
- Eval platform owner: Applied ML platform team (oncall rotation: applied-ml-platform@redwood.com) — SLA: respond within 2 business hours for pipeline failures.
- Model steward (per model): required to respond to S2 regression incidents within 4 business hours.

Metrics to report weekly:
- total_budget_units_consumed
- number_of_slices_flagged
- avg_time_to_triage (hours)
- precision_of_seed_to_flag pipeline (measured vs focused verify)

Appendix: investigative tools and helpers
- Commands:
  - List past runs: redwood-eval list --pipeline aef --model <sha>
  - Fetch artifacts: redwood-eval fetch --run-id <id> --dest ./artifacts
- JSON example of a slice flag record:
```json
{"slice_name":"billing-intent","anomaly_score":0.82,"flag_reason":"accuracy_delta:-0.07","run_id":"aef-20260301-strat-42"}
```

Related policies and pages:
- See prompt-coverage matrix and drift repair guide for how to define slices and minimum coverage targets.
- See regression triage canonical runbook for incident escalation templates.

Revision history:
- 2026-02-18: initial published by Ava Chen.
- 2026-03-01: clarified heavy-simulation gating and added cost knobs by request of reviewers.

Contact:
- Playbook owner: Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai)
- Platform on-call: applied-ml-platform@redwood.com
