title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Console rollout UI bug triggered rollback loop (2026-02-22)
preview: A Console UI change caused repeated client-side retries on a non-idempotent rollback endpoint, putting rollouts-service into a rollback loop for a subset of deployments.
text: A Console UI change caused repeated client-side retries on a non-idempotent rollback endpoint, putting rollouts-service into a rollback loop for a subset of deployments.

title: Status timeline automation spec and template library
preview:
Overview

This page is the canonical library for: (A) structured incident status timelines used by Redwood Inference responder teams and (B) the automation specification for generating and publishing those timelines to the status page, internal Slack bridge, and customer notification channels. The goal is fast, consistent, auditable customer communications that map to incident phases and operator actions.

Who should use this

- On-call SREs and service owners who publish incident updates
- Incident bridge operators and communications owners
- Automation engineers who implement status page webhooks and templates
- Customer Success for pre-approved messaging when high-impact incidents occur

Principles

1) Single source of truth: the status timeline generator (STG) must accept a single incident payload (incident_id) and emit consistent updates to all channels.
2) Phase-first messaging: timeline entries follow discrete phases (Detect, Ack, Mitigate, Customer Notice, Resolution, Retro).
3) Minimal friction: template variables are short and human-editable; automated drafts must be editable before publish.
4) Auditability: every published update must include evidence fields (owner, time, changelog, source).

Incident phases and expected cadence

- Detection (T0): short summary posted internally within 2 minutes of pager. Fields: incident_id, primary symptom, detected_by, initial_severity.
- Acknowledgement (T0+<5m): on-call acknowledges; bridge created and initial internal note placed.
- Mitigation (T0+5–30m): actions in-progress (scopes, mitigations, traffic fencing). Public-facing message: "We are investigating" with impacted features and preliminary mitigation steps.
- Customer Notice (T0+15–90m): first public update if customers are impacted; subsequent updates every 30–60 minutes while active.
- Resolution (when SLO restored): summary update with resolution steps, time to restore, and next steps for customers.
- Retro (postmortem week): consolidated timeline + action items shared with customers if the incident affected SLAs or sensitive data.

Templates (placeholders are double-curly variables)

1) Internal brief (Slack Bridge)

- Subject: [INCIDENT] {{incident_id}} — {{short_title}}
- Body:
  • Severity: {{severity}}
  • Detected: {{detected_at}} by {{detected_by}}
  • Impact: {{impact_summary}}
  • Current action: {{current_action}}
  • Owners: {{primary_owner}} / {{oncall_backup}}
  • Next update: {{next_update_in}}

2) Public status page short update

- Purpose: single-sentence high-level update for the status page header.
- Template: "{{service_list}}: We are investigating an issue affecting {{user_impact}}. Engineers are actively working on a fix. Next update at {{next_update_at}}."

3) Public status page detailed update

- Purpose: for the status page body with a timeline.
- Template: "Summary: {{short_title}}
Impact: {{detailed_impact}}
What we're doing: {{mitigation_steps}}
When it started: {{detected_at}}
Who’s working on it: {{primary_owner}}
Next update: {{next_update_at}}
"

4) Customer email/SMS template (for customers subscribed to incident alerts)

- Subject: Redwood Inference incident {{incident_id}} — {{short_title}}
- Body: "Hello,

We're contacting you because some of your requests to Redwood Inference ({{service_list}}) may be delayed or failing starting at {{detected_at}}. We are actively investigating.

Impact to you: {{customer_specific_impact}}
Temporary mitigation: {{customer_guidance}}
Expected next update: {{next_update_at}}

We apologize for the disruption. If you require immediate assistance, reply to this message or contact your Customer Success rep: {{cs_contact}}.

—The Redwood Inference Ops Team"

Automation specification (STG v1.2)

Inputs (incident payload schema) — must be provided to the generator as JSON via webhook or internal API:

| field | type | example | notes |
|-------|------|---------|-------|
| incident_id | string | INF-2026-1234 | unique id 
| start_time | iso8601 | 2026-02-14T09:12:03Z | detection time 
| detected_by | string | heartbeat/synthetic/pager | origin of signal 
| severity | enum | critical/high/medium/low | maps to page visibility 
| services | list[string] | [inference-proxy, batching-router] | service slugs 
| impact_summary | string | latency spike > 10s for 10% of requests | human brief 
| customer_impacted | boolean | true | feature gating for emails
| primary_owner | string | eng-sre-inference | oncall handle/team
| mitigation_actions | list[string] | [scale-out pool, disable-optimizer] | ops actions in-flight

Processing rules (server-side)

1) Visibility rules: severity=critical -> publish both status page header and detailed body; severity=high -> header + on-demand body; medium/low -> internal only unless customer_impacted=true.
2) Rate limiting: automated publishes limited to 1 per 10 minutes unless human overrides.
3) Freeze window: non-critical scheduled maintenance windows must not trigger public notices from STG.
4) Editable draft: pipeline creates a draft with filled variables in /status-drafts channel; a human must confirm publish for critical incidents.

Delivery targets and channels

- Status page API (primary): /api/v1/incidents/publish — accepts the resolved template body and evidence fields.
- Slack bridge (internal): /hooks/status-bridge — posts internal brief.
- Email/SMS provider: SendGrid/Twilio integration via provider adapter.
- PagerDuty: correlation is created via incident_id tag for cross-references.

Security and compliance constraints

- No sensitive customer PII in public updates. STG must strip any payload fields flagged as pii=true before template rendering.
- Audit log retention: publish events stored for 18 months with operator id and revision history.

Operational runbook (quick steps to configuring STG for a new service)

1) Add service slug to services catalog (git: infra/status/services.yaml). Required fields: slug, display_name, default_impact_text, cs_contact. Example YAML: 

```yaml
- slug: inference-proxy
  display_name: Inference Proxy
  default_impact_text: "Requests may be delayed or fail"
  cs_contact: cs-inference@redwood.com
```

2) Add templating override if service needs different phrasing: infra/status/templates/inference-proxy.md
3) Configure PagerDuty integration: attach the incident_id tag mapping in pd-rules.json and enable correlation=true
4) Set the publish policy: infra/status/policies/{{service_slug}}.json (fields: auto_publish_allowed, publish_rate_limit_mins, human_confirm_required)

Best-practice examples (realistic scenarios)

- Multi-region failover (partial impact): publish header: "We’re routing traffic to healthy regions; customers may experience brief increases in latency." Include a mitigation step that lists the region and expected time to failover.
- Tokenization service regression (quality-only): internal-only notification first; public notice only after impact degrades >5% of inference calls.
- Cache-eviction storm (thundering): publish immediately with guidance for customers to stagger requests and enable local caching.

Governance and ownership

- Primary owner for STG: eng-platform/status-team (pager: #status-ops).
- Publishing ownership: the on-call rotational engineer for the affected service is responsible for sign-off; Customer Success must review high-impact customer notices for enterprise tenants.

FAQ

Q: Who can edit templates?
A: Only members of eng-platform or triaged service owners. Edits require an approved PR and a short release note.

Q: What if automation fails to publish?
A: STG has a fallback: write draft to internal S3 bucket and ping the #status-ops channel. Human bridge operator must copy/paste to the status page manually.

Appendix: canned status snippets (copy-to-edit)

- Investigating header: "We’re investigating reports of degraded {{service_list}} performance. This may affect some customers using {{feature_list}}. Next update: {{next_update_at}}."
- Mitigating body: "Action in progress: {{mitigation_steps}}. We have isolated the problem to {{fault_domain}} and are applying mitigations to restore normal traffic. Impacted customers: {{customer_segment}}."
- Resolved body: "Root cause: {{root_cause_summary}}. What we did: {{remediation_summary}}. Time to restore: {{duration}}. Action items: {{postmortem_actions}}. If you continue to see issues, contact {{cs_contact}}."

Change log

- 2025-11-05: created by Ava Chen (initial STG design)
- 2026-02-18: added rate-limiting and draft flow (reviewed by Diego Martinez)
- 2026-03-01: updated mail/SMS templates and retention policy

Related artifacts and implementation links

- infra repo: git@github.com:redwood/infra (status subsystem folder)
- STG service: stg.redwood-inference.internal (api docs at /docs/swagger)
- Status page provider: status.redwood.com (api key stored in vault path: secret/status-page)
text:
Overview

This page is the canonical library for: (A) structured incident status timelines used by Redwood Inference responder teams and (B) the automation specification for generating and publishing those timelines to the status page, internal Slack bridge, and customer notification channels. The goal is fast, consistent, auditable customer communications that map to incident phases and operator actions.

Who should use this

- On-call SREs and service owners who publish incident updates
- Incident bridge operators and communications owners
- Automation engineers who implement status page webhooks and templates
- Customer Success for pre-approved messaging when high-impact incidents occur

Principles

1) Single source of truth: the status timeline generator (STG) must accept a single incident payload (incident_id) and emit consistent updates to all channels.
2) Phase-first messaging: timeline entries follow discrete phases (Detect, Ack, Mitigate, Customer Notice, Resolution, Retro).
3) Minimal friction: template variables are short and human-editable; automated drafts must be editable before publish.
4) Auditability: every published update must include evidence fields (owner, time, changelog, source).

Incident phases and expected cadence

- Detection (T0): short summary posted internally within 2 minutes of pager. Fields: incident_id, primary symptom, detected_by, initial_severity.
- Acknowledgement (T0+<5m): on-call acknowledges; bridge created and initial internal note placed.
- Mitigation (T0+5–30m): actions in-progress (scopes, mitigations, traffic fencing). Public-facing message: "We are investigating" with impacted features and preliminary mitigation steps.
- Customer Notice (T0+15–90m): first public update if customers are impacted; subsequent updates every 30–60 minutes while active.
- Resolution (when SLO restored): summary update with resolution steps, time to restore, and next steps for customers.
- Retro (postmortem week): consolidated timeline + action items shared with customers if the incident affected SLAs or sensitive data.

Templates (placeholders are double-curly variables)

1) Internal brief (Slack Bridge)

- Subject: [INCIDENT] {{incident_id}} — {{short_title}}
- Body:
  • Severity: {{severity}}
  • Detected: {{detected_at}} by {{detected_by}}
  • Impact: {{impact_summary}}
  • Current action: {{current_action}}
  • Owners: {{primary_owner}} / {{oncall_backup}}
  • Next update: {{next_update_in}}

2) Public status page short update

- Purpose: single-sentence high-level update for the status page header.
- Template: "{{service_list}}: We are investigating an issue affecting {{user_impact}}. Engineers are actively working on a fix. Next update at {{next_update_at}}."

3) Public status page detailed update

- Purpose: for the status page body with a timeline.
- Template: "Summary: {{short_title}}
Impact: {{detailed_impact}}
What we're doing: {{mitigation_steps}}
When it started: {{detected_at}}
Who’s working on it: {{primary_owner}}
Next update: {{next_update_at}}
"

4) Customer email/SMS template (for customers subscribed to incident alerts)

- Subject: Redwood Inference incident {{incident_id}} — {{short_title}}
- Body: "Hello,

We're contacting you because some of your requests to Redwood Inference ({{service_list}}) may be delayed or failing starting at {{detected_at}}. We are actively investigating.

Impact to you: {{customer_specific_impact}}
Temporary mitigation: {{customer_guidance}}
Expected next update: {{next_update_at}}

We apologize for the disruption. If you require immediate assistance, reply to this message or contact your Customer Success rep: {{cs_contact}}.

—The Redwood Inference Ops Team"

Automation specification (STG v1.2)

Inputs (incident payload schema) — must be provided to the generator as JSON via webhook or internal API:

| field | type | example | notes |
|-------|------|---------|-------|
| incident_id | string | INF-2026-1234 | unique id 
| start_time | iso8601 | 2026-02-14T09:12:03Z | detection time 
| detected_by | string | heartbeat/synthetic/pager | origin of signal 
| severity | enum | critical/high/medium/low | maps to page visibility 
| services | list[string] | [inference-proxy, batching-router] | service slugs 
| impact_summary | string | latency spike > 10s for 10% of requests | human brief 
| customer_impacted | boolean | true | feature gating for emails
| primary_owner | string | eng-sre-inference | oncall handle/team
| mitigation_actions | list[string] | [scale-out pool, disable-optimizer] | ops actions in-flight

Processing rules (server-side)

1) Visibility rules: severity=critical -> publish both status page header and detailed body; severity=high -> header + on-demand body; medium/low -> internal only unless customer_impacted=true.
2) Rate limiting: automated publishes limited to 1 per 10 minutes unless human overrides.
3) Freeze window: non-critical scheduled maintenance windows must not trigger public notices from STG.
4) Editable draft: pipeline creates a draft with filled variables in /status-drafts channel; a human must confirm publish for critical incidents.

Delivery targets and channels

- Status page API (primary): /api/v1/incidents/publish — accepts the resolved template body and evidence fields.
- Slack bridge (internal): /hooks/status-bridge — posts internal brief.
- Email/SMS provider: SendGrid/Twilio integration via provider adapter.
- PagerDuty: correlation is created via incident_id tag for cross-references.

Security and compliance constraints

- No sensitive customer PII in public updates. STG must strip any payload fields flagged as pii=true before template rendering.
- Audit log retention: publish events stored for 18 months with operator id and revision history.

Operational runbook (quick steps to configuring STG for a new service)

1) Add service slug to services catalog (git: infra/status/services.yaml). Required fields: slug, display_name, default_impact_text, cs_contact. Example YAML: 

```yaml
- slug: inference-proxy
  display_name: Inference Proxy
  default_impact_text: "Requests may be delayed or fail"
  cs_contact: cs-inference@redwood.com
```

2) Add templating override if service needs different phrasing: infra/status/templates/inference-proxy.md
3) Configure PagerDuty integration: attach the incident_id tag mapping in pd-rules.json and enable correlation=true
4) Set the publish policy: infra/status/policies/{{service_slug}}.json (fields: auto_publish_allowed, publish_rate_limit_mins, human_confirm_required)

Best-practice examples (realistic scenarios)

- Multi-region failover (partial impact): publish header: "We’re routing traffic to healthy regions; customers may experience brief increases in latency." Include a mitigation step that lists the region and expected time to failover.
- Tokenization service regression (quality-only): internal-only notification first; public notice only after impact degrades >5% of inference calls.
- Cache-eviction storm (thundering): publish immediately with guidance for customers to stagger requests and enable local caching.

Governance and ownership

- Primary owner for STG: eng-platform/status-team (pager: #status-ops).
- Publishing ownership: the on-call rotational engineer for the affected service is responsible for sign-off; Customer Success must review high-impact customer notices for enterprise tenants.

FAQ

Q: Who can edit templates?
A: Only members of eng-platform or triaged service owners. Edits require an approved PR and a short release note.

Q: What if automation fails to publish?
A: STG has a fallback: write draft to internal S3 bucket and ping the #status-ops channel. Human bridge operator must copy/paste to the status page manually.

Appendix: canned status snippets (copy-to-edit)

- Investigating header: "We’re investigating reports of degraded {{service_list}} performance. This may affect some customers using {{feature_list}}. Next update: {{next_update_at}}."
- Mitigating body: "Action in progress: {{mitigation_steps}}. We have isolated the problem to {{fault_domain}} and are applying mitigations to restore normal traffic. Impacted customers: {{customer_segment}}."
- Resolved body: "Root cause: {{root_cause_summary}}. What we did: {{remediation_summary}}. Time to restore: {{duration}}. Action items: {{postmortem_actions}}. If you continue to see issues, contact {{cs_contact}}."

Change log

- 2025-11-05: created by Ava Chen (initial STG design)
- 2026-02-18: added rate-limiting and draft flow (reviewed by Diego Martinez)
- 2026-03-01: updated mail/SMS templates and retention policy

Related artifacts and implementation links

- infra repo: git@github.com:redwood/infra (status subsystem folder)
- STG service: stg.redwood-inference.internal (api docs at /docs/swagger)
- Status page provider: status.redwood.com (api key stored in vault path: secret/status-page)

title: Eval cost slice prioritization and resource budgeting
preview:
Summary

This document defines a prioritization and budgeting framework for running slice-driven evaluations in Redwood’s eval harness. It describes a deterministic scoring function for slice prioritization, recommended resource budgets for common evaluation classes (smoke, micro-canary, full-slice, long-tail scan), integration points with quantization profiles and onboarding, and a triage fast-path for regression detection and mitigation.

Why this exists

- Eval budgets are finite: the eval harness runs thousands of slices across many models and releases. Uncontrolled execution causes high cost and slow feedback.
- We need consistent signal for promotion and regression triage while minimizing GPU/CPU spend.
- Quantization changes and heterogeneous deployments (private/dedicated) require different resource profiles; this doc standardizes budgeting and prioritization so teams follow predictable least-cost-first patterns.

Scope

- Applies to all automated and scheduled evaluation jobs executed via the eval harness (interactive, scheduled, and triggered by model promotion pipelines).
- Does NOT cover human labeling budgets or external A/B experiments (separate playbooks exist).

Definitions

- Slice: atomic workload segment (e.g., subset of prompts with a common characteristic).
- Eval class: smoke, micro-canary, full-slice, long-tail scan.
- Cost unit: GPU-hour equivalent at Redwood internal rate (example: 1 A100-80GB hour = 1.0 cost unit; 1 T4/8GB hour = 0.08 cost units).
- Priority window: timeframe within which a slice should be executed relative to model event (e.g., within 2 hours for micro-canary).

Priority scoring algorithm (deterministic)

We compute a score S for each slice to decide execution order when demand exceeds capacity. Higher S runs first.

Formula: S = W_i * I + W_r * R + W_m * M + W_c * (1 / (1 + C))

Where:
- I (impact) in [0,1]: business/feature impact heuristic (0 low, 1 high). Set by owning team in slice metadata.
- R (regression-signal) in [0,1]: recent historical alert density for this slice (0 none, 1 frequent).
- M (model-change): binary 1 if model architecture/quantization variant changed since last passing run, else 0.
- C (cost-estimate): predicted cost in cost units for full slice run.
- W_i, W_r, W_m, W_c = weights default = [0.45, 0.25, 0.20, 0.10]. Weights are tunable via eval-harness config.

Score interpretation:
- S >= 0.7: high priority (run immediately; consider parallelization).
- 0.4 <= S < 0.7: medium (schedule within the next resource window).
- S < 0.4: low (defer to overnight/backfill).

Priority components table

| Component | Range | Source | Default weight | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---:|---:|
| Impact (I) | 0.0 - 1.0 | Owner metadata | 0.45 | Product/PM sets impact; engineering may override for infra-critical slices |
| Regression (R) | 0.0 - 1.0 | Telemetry (alerts/sec, failed-runs) | 0.25 | Smoothed over last 30 days |
| Model-change (M) | 0 or 1 | CI metadata | 0.20 | Includes quantization and kernel switches |
| Cost (C) | >0 | Eval cost estimator | 0.10 | Normalized to cost units; inverted in score to prefer low-cost slices |

Resource budgeting (default budgets)

These are guidance values. Teams may request overrides for high-value flows; override requests go to eng-applied-ml with cost justification. Budget values assume Redwood internal cost unit; convert per-region via pricing table in runbook.

| Eval class | Target wall-clock | Max concurrent workers | Max GPU-hours per run | Example use-cases |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Smoke | 5m | 1 | 0.02 | Pre-commit/quick sanity checks |
| Micro-canary | 30m | 2 | 0.6 | Canary of new model variant on top 10 slices |
| Full-slice | 2-6h | 10 | 8.0 | Promotion gating for model families |
| Long-tail scan | 24h | 4 | 24.0 | Rare-case stress and drift detection |

Budget enforcement rules

1. Each eval job must declare eval_class and estimator.cost_units in job YAML. The scheduler will reject jobs exceeding per-team daily caps.
2. Peak concurrency throttles: when global cost consumption in last 1 hour > 80% of pool capacity, only high-priority (S >= 0.7) slices are scheduled until consumption drops below 60%.
3. Quantized runs count as a separate cost bucket and may require additional shadow runs; treat them as model-change (M=1) for initial prioritization.

YAML: minimal eval job example

---
# eval-job.yaml
job_name: email-intent-full-slice
eval_class: full-slice
owner: \"nlp-team\"
priority_metadata:
  impact: 0.8
  model_change: true
estimator:
  cost_units: 7.5
  gpu_type: a100-80gb
  expected_wall_minutes: 240
---

Scheduling procedure (step-by-step)

1. When a model-promotion event occurs, the promotion orchestration writes a scheduling request for the required slices. Include eval_class, owner, and priority_metadata.
2. The eval-scheduler computes S for each slice and places slices into priority queues.
3. The scheduler enforces daily budget caps per team; if cap exceeded, scheduler downgrades non-critical slices with an annotation and notifies owners.
4. For micro-canaries, the scheduler ensures runs complete within the priority window (default 2 hours). If a micro-canary fails (see acceptance thresholds below), the pipeline pauses and triggers the regression triage fast-path.

Acceptance thresholds and sample sizes

- Smoke: qualitative checks; failure on runtime error or >5% invalid responses.
- Micro-canary: run N=100 samples; acceptance if metric delta <= 1.5x baseline variance for primary metric OR absolute metric >= baseline - epsilon.
- Full-slice: sample size determined by slice variance profiling; baseline formula: N = min(5000, ceil( (Z^2 * sigma^2) / d^2 )), where Z=1.96 (95% CI), sigma is observed stddev from historical runs, d is acceptable absolute difference. Default N=1000 if no history.

Integration with quantization profiles and model onboarding

- Onboarding pipelines must tag any change to quant_profile_id. That tag sets model_change=M for the first subsequent eval run and prioritizes slices accordingly.
- For quantization-induced runs, run two lanes in parallel (float baseline shadow + quantized variant) for the micro-canary. Cost estimation should account for both lanes.

Regression triage fast-path

When a micro-canary or full-slice run exceeds failure thresholds, the following fast-path is executed:

1. Pause promotion and mark model as BLOCKED.
2. Snapshot telemetry and store a prioritized subset: top 100 failed prompts, token-level traces, KV-cache rollback events, and runtimes.
3. Run automated bisect across variants: kernel selection, quant profile, model weights (if multi-version) — limit to 3 parallel bisect branches.
4. If bisect isolates a single cause with confidence > 90% (see bisect spec), auto-open a regression incident with severity determined by impacted SLOs.
5. If cause not isolated within 60 minutes, run fallback: promote earlier verified model variant and schedule detailed offline triage with ML owners.

Telemetry and observability requirements

Eval runs must emit the following fields at 1-minute granularity:
- eval_job_id, slice_id, model_version, quant_profile_id, worker_id, gpu_type, tokens_generated, latency_p50/p95/p99, error_count, failed_prompts_count, metric_values (primary/secondary), cost_units_consumed.

Dashboard and alerts

- A dedicated dashboard \"Eval Cost & Priority\" lists active jobs, S scores, and cost burn rate.
- Alert: when per-team cost consumption >= 90% of daily cap -> page on-call for that team.
- Alert: micro-canary failure -> slack #model-promotions and create incident-tmp channel via API.

Governance and ownership

- Owning team: declares impact and maintains slice metadata; responsible for justifying budget overrides.
- Platform: eng-applied-ml operates scheduler, maintains cost model, and reviews monthly budget exceptions.
- SRE: enforces pool concurrency caps and adjusts rate-limiting during global incidents.

Operational runbook (quick actions)

- If a slice is consuming unexpected cost (>2x estimate):
  1. Cancel active job via evalctl: evalctl cancel <job_id>.
  2. Inspect estimator outputs: evalctl inspect <job_id> --fields estimator.*.
  3. If estimator was stale, file a ticket to eng-applied-ml with the job snapshot.

- Triaging a micro-canary regression:
  1. Lock promotion and run automated bisect: evalctl bisect --job <job_id> --variants quant,kernel,weights
  2. If bisect identifies variant: rollback quant profile via model-registry UI and rerun micro-canary.

Example CLI commands

# schedule a micro-canary
evalctl schedule --job-file eval-job.yaml --priority now
# cancel a running job
evalctl cancel --job-id 2026-AB12
# fetch diagnostics for a completed run
evalctl fetch --job-id 2026-AB12 --artifact traces/*.json

Security and cost accounting

- All eval artifacts containing customer prompt examples must be labeled PII/TOS compliant at creation. The scheduler will reject runs labeled \"customer-sensitive\" unless confidentiality override is granted.
- Cost attribution is exported daily to finance with the following dimensions: team, owner, model_family, quant_profile_id, region.

Change log

- 2026-02-12 (Ava Chen): Initial publish.
- 2026-03-01 (Ava Chen): Weight defaults tuned from internal pilot; added triage bisect timebox and CLI examples.

Related runbooks and references

- eval-harness/runbooks/threshold-tuning (for how to set sigma and d)
- applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/quant-profile-synthesis-and-safety-playbook-2024 (quant change handling)
- applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/production-model-certification-checklist-2026 (promotion gating)

For questions or budget override requests, email eng-applied-ml@redwood.com or open a PR to the eval-harness scheduler config repository.

For questions or budget override requests, email eng-applied-ml@redwood.com or open a PR to the eval-harness scheduler config repository.

title: Customer Success Observability Onboarding Kit
preview:
Overview:
This kit documents the recommended observability and telemetry configuration, validation tests, and post-cutover monitoring runbook that Customer Success and Platform Engineering use to hand off an enterprise customer at go‑live. The goal is to ensure we can reliably measure the customer’s production health within 10 minutes of cutover for the key inference and cost SLOs, and to provide clear stop/rollback criteria if signals indicate instability.

Scope:
- Applies to enterprise Dedicated and Private deployments where Redwood operates a telemetry gatekeeper or where the customer forwards metrics/traces/logs into our observability pipeline.
- Excludes ad-hoc POCs and single-request evaluation accounts.

Audience:
- Customer Success Onboarding PMs
- Platform/Infra Engineers
- Solutions Engineers and SREs

Key outcomes after following this kit:
1) Instrumentation baseline is validated (metrics, traces, logs).
2) Synthetic and real-traffic smoke tests pass for latency, error rate, and cost-attribution.
3) Dashboards and QBR metric bundles are populated and accessible to the customer and CS.
4) Clear criteria for rollback or quick mitigation are agreed and automated alerts are in place.

Owners & Roles:
- Customer Success (owner): overall coordination, customer comms, QBR handoff.
- Platform SRE (owner): observability integration, alerting, runbook edits.
- Solutions Engineering: agent side configuration, test harness.
- Customer Infra Lead: network/firewall and metric export configuration.

Pre-Cutover Checklist (to be completed in sequence):
1. Confirm network connectivity and firewall rules: dedicated tenant endpoints reachable from customer egress IPs (tests included below).
2. Telemetry pipeline: ensure metrics, traces, and logs have been configured in the customer’s telemetry project and namespaced with tenant_id.
3. Sampling and volume controls: verify request sample rate and trace sampling are set per the table below to avoid ingestion spikes.
4. Dashboard access: create and share the tenant-specific dashboard with read-only links to CS and customer stakeholders.
5. Alerting: enable the standard go‑live alert set and confirm proper escalation targets.
6. Runbook reviewed and assigned to oncall rotation for the first 72 hours post-cutover.

Instrumentation Requirements (reference table):
| Signal | Metric name (examples) | Required labels/tags | Target sampling/retention |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Request counters | inference.requests.total | tenant_id, model, route | 100% counter, 90d retention |
| Latency histograms | inference.latency.ms | tenant_id, model, stage | Full histogram, 30d retention |
| Errors | inference.errors.total | tenant_id, model, code | 100% counter, 90d retention |
| Token cost | inference.tokens.consumed | tenant_id, model, billing_unit | Aggregate per-minute, 365d retention |
| Traces | rpc.trace | tenant_id, request_id, route | 5-10% sample for heavy traffic, 100% for synthetic tests |

Note: If customer is forwarding telemetry to their own project, ensure label mapping includes our tenant_id for cross-correlation.

Configuration examples:
- Prometheus scrape relabel config (snippet):
  - job_name: 'redwood-tenant-<TENANT_ID>'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['redwood-telemetry.gw.internal:9100']
    relabel_configs:
      - source_labels: ['__address__']
        target_label: 'instance'

- Trace sampling policy (example):
  service: inference-gateway
  sampling:
    default: 0.05
    routes:
      /api/v1/stream: 0.10
      /api/v1/generate: 0.05

Validation Test Plan (must pass before signalling go‑live):
1. Connectivity smoke (5 min):
   - From customer IP: curl /health -> 200, TLS cert valid, latency < 250ms to control plane.
2. Synthetic throughput ramp (30 min):
   - Inject synthetic requests that match expected 95th percentile token length. Ramp 0 -> 200 reqs/min.
   - Verify request success >= 99.5% and p95 latency remains under the agreed SLO.
3. Trace correlation test (10 min):
   - Trigger a request with X-Request-ID and verify full trace spans appear in tenant traces within 60s.
4. Cost attribution & token accounting (10 min):
   - Run 100 sample requests and confirm tokens consumed match the accounting logs and billing metrics for that tenant.
5. Dashboard & Alert exercise (15 min):
   - Force a non-prod threshold breach (e.g., a synthetic high-latency spike) and ensure alert fires, route to correct paging targets, and incident playbook opens.

SLOs and Operational Thresholds (recommended defaults to sign off go‑live):
- Availability (5m window): 99.95% (errors < 0.05% of requests)
- Latency (tail): p95 < 800ms for standard routes; p95 < 1200ms for streaming routes
- Cost drift: minute-by-minute token-cost delta < 15% from expected baseline during first 48 hours
- Tracing latency: trace ingestion delay < 60s for synthetic tests

Alerting & Paging matrix (examples):
- Sev 1: tenant-wide error rate > 2% for 2 consecutive 1-minute windows -> Platform SRE paging (pager duty) + notify CS escalations channel.
- Sev 2: p95 latency above SLO for 10 minutes -> SRE async + CS on-call notification.
- Billing anomaly (cost drift > 50% sustained over 10 minutes): CS & Finance alert (email + Slack).

Rollback Criteria & Quick Mitigations:
Execute rollback immediately if any of the following occur within the first 6 hours: 
- Availability drops below 99.5% for > 5 minutes and does not recover after automated scaling attempt.
- Customer experiences sustained token-cost blowouts > 200% of baseline for 15 minutes.
- Trace correlation fails for > 30% of requests and blocks triage.

Rollback steps (ordered):
1. Engage SRE and CS leads and mark customer as degraded in status page.
2. Switch traffic to the pre-cutover stable model variant (routing flag) or to fallback region (automated policy).
3. Disable any new batching/caching optimizations introduced during cutover.
4. If unstable after steps 2–3, perform tenant-level quiesce and failback to prior reserved capacity (see tenant quiesce playbook).
5. Initiate postmortem and customer comms within 2 hours of rollback decision.

Post-Cutover 72-hour Monitoring Runbook:
- First 10 minutes: monitor synthetic smoke dashboards and verify no Sev1.
- 10–60 minutes: validate trace ingestion, cost accounting, and token distribution.
- Hourly checks for first 24 hours: availability, p95 latency, billing delta.
- Daily QBR bundle: aggregate 24h metrics for the first 7 days and present in the initial QBR.

Communication templates (example Slack + Email snippets):
- Slack (to customer): "Cutover start: initiating traffic switch at 2026-02-15T09:00Z. We will monitor latency, errors, and billing closely. Expected window is 60 minutes. Will update every 15 minutes or on deviation."
- Customer-facing postmortem template (summary + impact + mitigation + next steps) is attached to this page as a downloadable doc.

FAQ & Troubleshooting tips:
Q: What if dashboards show no metrics for tenant after cutover?
A: Confirm relabel mapping includes tenant_id. If forwarding, check that exporters are writing labels and that our telemetry gatekeeper is receiving samples (curl /telemetry/health).

Q: Who authorizes rollback?
A: The oncall SRE in coordination with CS lead and customer Infra lead. If disagreement, default to SRE for platform stability decisions.

Appendix A — Quick CLI checks:
- curl -sS https://<control-plane>/health | jq .status
- grpcurl -plaintext <inference-host>:<port> inference.Service/HealthCheck

Appendix B — Dashboard links and saved queries:
- Tenant perf dashboard: /dashboards/tenant/<TENANT_ID>/perf
- Cost attribution query: saved_query:cost_attribution_tenant_<TENANT_ID>

Related pages:
- Telemetry Gatekeeper Onboarding and QBR Integration Guide (see related_pages)
- Tenant quiesce and safe failback playbook

Change log:
- 2025-11-02: Created by Ava Chen as onboarding kit draft.
- 2026-01-10: Added rollback criteria and sampling recommendations (review by Diego Martinez).
- 2026-02-15: Finalized and published after pilot with AcmeCorp (notes appended).

title: VPC Handshake and Operator Blueprint for Private Deploys
preview:
Summary:
This playbook defines the VPC "handshake" process and an operator blueprint to safely install, validate, and operate Redwood Private deployments. It combines deployment handoff steps, on-prem packaging checks, day-2 operational guardrails, and a deterministic upgrade/rollback procedure intended for field operators and delivery engineers.

Goals:
- Standardize handoff between Delivery/Install and Customer Ops.
- Ensure a signed, verifiable package and immutable boot path for air-gapped and VPC installs.
- Provide a concise day-2 operator playbook for common incidents, model swaps, and upgrades.
- Define customer-facing requirements and acceptance tests required to proceed to production.

Owners and contact:
- Primary owner: eng-private-deployments field-integration (pager: @field-oncall)
- Secondary owner: eng-platform - runtime (email: runtime-ops@redwood.ai)
- Delivery contact: Solutions Eng (assigned per customer)

Intended audience:
- Field delivery engineers, SREs operating customer VPCs, customer platform engineers, and account technical leads.

Prerequisites (operator checklist):
1) Network & routing
   - BGP or static routes in place between customer transit and Redwood egress (if hybrid).
   - Required ports open: 443 (control plane), 9000-9010 TCP (metrics/operational tunnels), 22 only for image signing/verification tasks if operator uses SSH bastion.
2) Identity & secrets
   - Customer KMS reachable and configured for envelope encryption OR HSM module present for air-gapped installs.
   - Service account with least privilege for telemetry uploads (scope: telemetry:write).
3) Hardware & storage
   - GPU nodes: min 2x for HA, recommended 3x per tenant for production.
   - Persistent storage: 1.5x model footprint available for staging + snapshots.
4) Timing
   - Scheduled maintenance window: minimum 2 hours for initial provisioning; upgrades require 30–90 minutes depending on model swap complexity.

Quick configuration reference (table):
- Model cache size: recommended 40% of GPU DRAM for L2 caching
- KV cache retention: 3 hours by default, configurable per-tenant
- Batching target latency: 50ms median per-token under normal load

Network topology (text diagram):
- Customer VPC (private subnets) -> NAT/Egress -> Redwood control plane (mutual TLS)
- Optional: Edge gateway (optional for air-gapped) -> Ops bastion -> On-prem runtime cluster

VPC Handshake: step-by-step operator flow
1) Pre-handshake validation (Delivery):
   a) Delivery engineer runs local preflight script: `redwood-ops preflight --manifest manifest.json` to validate network, DNS, and KMS connectivity.
   b) Gather the following artifacts to send to customer: signed-install-manifest (SHA256), operator-checklist, public key for image signatures.
2) Customer acceptance of manifest:
   a) Customer security lead verifies manifest signature against the provided public key.
   b) Both parties sign the Handoff Acceptance Record (HAR) — a short JSON with timestamps and signer CNs.
3) Secure transfer and attestation:
   a) For managed VPC: exchange ephemeral tokens scoped to the install window (TTL 1 hour).
   b) For air-gapped: deliver signed images and a manifest on removable media with hash evidence; operator performs local verification using `sha256sum` and `gpg --verify`.
4) Bootstrap sequence (operator):
   Steps to execute in order:
   1. Import signed image bundle: `redwood-ops image import --signed-bundle bundle.tar.gz`
   2. Verify image signature: failure -> abort and contact Delivery.
   3. Provision control-plane agent: `kubectl apply -f agent-install.yaml` (agent only opens egress to designated endpoints).
   4. Start health-check loop and wait for control-plane registration: `redwood-ops status watch --timeout 600`

Acceptance tests (run by Customer + Delivery):
- Smoke tests (pass/fail):
  1) API connectivity: POST /v1/health -> 200 within 2s
  2) Model load: request cold-load of pinned model -> success within 45s
  3) KV cache warm-path test: repeated prompt sequence returns cached tokens after second call with latency < 30ms
  4) Telemetry & logging: agent logs received by control plane within 60s of event emission

On-prem packaging & verification checklist:
- All images must be signed with a Redwood release key. Operators must verify: `gpg --verify images.sig images.tar.gz` and match SHA256 in manifest.
- Image provenance: include `build-id`, `kgx-version`, `kernel-optim` tag in package metadata.
- Immutable boot: golden-boot image recommended; any divergence requires attestation recorded in ticketing system.

Day-2 operator blueprint (common flows):
- Normal maintenance (daily):
  - Verify agent health: `redwood-ops agent check` (expect status: ready).
  - Confirm telemetry sampling rates: must remain at configured baseline to preserve billing fidelity.
- Model hot-swap (operator-safe path):
  1) Validate new model artifact in staging tenant with 1-2 benchmark traces.
  2) Quiesce traffic for target shard: set routing policy to `drain` for 30s.
  3) Swap model binary with atomic rename + restart worker process.
  4) Health-check and small-traffic canary (5% traffic for 15 minutes).
  5) If regressions observed (quality or latency), perform immediate rollback (see rollback procedure).
- KV cache and shard rebalance: daily low-traffic window preferred; use `cache-evict --grace 60` to avoid cold-start storms.

Upgrade and rollback procedure (deterministic):
1) Canary upgrade (safe path):
   - Create canary group: select 5% of shards, prefer non-critical customer shards.
   - Deploy new runtime binary + model variant.
   - Monitor metrics for 30 minutes against baseline: p99 latency, error rate, answered tokens, and model quality signals (prompt-set eval).
   - Success criteria: p99 latency regression < +10ms, error rate delta < 0.1%, model quality pass rate > 98% for validation set.
2) Gradual rollout: 25% -> 50% -> 100% with 30-minute observation windows.
3) Automated rollback triggers:
   - p99 latency > baseline + 25ms for 3 consecutive observation windows
   - error rate spike > 0.5% absolute
   - Quality regression detected by eval harness (fail fast)
4) Manual rollback steps (operator):
   a) Execute `redwood-ops rollout rollback --deployment <id> --reason \"regression detected\"`
   b) Notify Delivery and open an incident ticket with runbook ID.
   c) Run post-rollback smoke tests and confirm acceptance tests pass.

Incident runbook (fast triage):
1) Detect: alert fires (latency or error rate).
2) Contain: toggle traffic to fallback model variant using `routing policy set --variant fallback`
3) Mitigate: increase autoscale target temporarily, enable prefix caching for affected routes.
4) Remediate: identify root cause, apply hotfix or rollback, schedule postmortem if SLO breach occurred.

Customer requirements & acceptance matrix (short table):
- Customer must provide: VPC contact, KMS endpoint or HSM details, minimal maintenance window, firewall exceptions list, and SLA contact details.
- Acceptance gating: all preflight checks pass, manifest signature validated, acceptance tests pass, and a production runbook handed to customer.

Operational SLOs (recommended default):
- Request success rate >= 99.5% (rolling 30d)
- p99 latency <= 120ms for gen endpoints (local-to-region)
- Error budget: 0.5% per month

Post-deployment evidence to preserve in ticketing system:
- Handoff Acceptance Record (HAR) JSON
- Image manifest with signature and SHA256 hashes
- Preflight output log (`preflight-<date>.log`)
- Acceptance test results (timestamped)

Runbook quick commands (cheat sheet):
- `redwood-ops preflight --manifest manifest.json`
- `redwood-ops image import --signed-bundle bundle.tar.gz`
- `redwood-ops status watch --timeout 600`
- `redwood-ops rollout rollback --deployment <id>`

Related artifacts and references:
- See: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/ephemeral-boot-and-attestation-playbook-2026
- See: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/deployment-guides/private-deploy-onboarding-and-failover-runbook-2026
- Templates: HAR template (attached in page attachments), preflight script, smoke-test collection.

Appendix: example HAR JSON (fillable by Delivery and Customer)
```
{
  "handshake_id": "har-20251103-001",
  "delivery_signer": "CN=delivery-01.example.com",
  "customer_signer": "CN=customer-secld.example.com",
  "manifest_sha256": "<sha256-here>",
  "timestamp": "2025-11-03T14:22:00Z"
}
```
Notes and caveats:
- This playbook is intentionally prescriptive to reduce cross-team variance. Exceptions must be recorded and approved by Security and Delivery leads.
- Maintain updated public keys and rotation cadence: keys rotate annually or after any key compromise.

Change history:
- 2025-11-03: Initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2026-02-15: Minor clarifications and acceptance test additions (reviewers: Miguel, Priya)

  4) Health-check and small-traffic canary (5% traffic for 15 minutes).
  5) If regressions observed (quality or latency), perform immediate rollback (see rollback procedure).
- KV cache and shard rebalance: daily low-traffic window preferred; use `cache-evict --grace 60` to avoid cold-start storms.

Upgrade and rollback procedure (deterministic):
1) Canary upgrade (safe path):
   - Create canary group: select 5% of shards, prefer non-critical customer shards.
   - Deploy new runtime binary + model variant.
   - Monitor metrics for 30 minutes against baseline: p99 latency, error rate, answered tokens, and model quality signals (prompt-set eval).
   - Success criteria: p99 latency regression < +10ms, error rate delta < 0.1%, model quality pass rate > 98% for validation set.
2) Gradual rollout: 25% -> 50% -> 100% with 30-minute observation windows.
3) Automated rollback triggers:
   - p99 latency > baseline + 25ms for 3 consecutive observation windows
   - error rate spike > 0.5% absolute
   - Quality regression detected by eval harness (fail fast)
4) Manual rollback steps (operator):
   a) Execute `redwood-ops rollout rollback --deployment <id> --reason \"regression detected\"`
   b) Notify Delivery and open an incident ticket with runbook ID.
   c) Run post-rollback smoke tests and confirm acceptance tests pass.

Incident runbook (fast triage):
1) Detect: alert fires (latency or error rate).
2) Contain: toggle traffic to fallback model variant using `routing policy set --variant fallback`
3) Mitigate: increase autoscale target temporarily, enable prefix caching for affected routes.
4) Remediate: identify root cause, apply hotfix or rollback, schedule postmortem if SLO breach occurred.

Customer requirements & acceptance matrix (short table):
- Customer must provide: VPC contact, KMS endpoint or HSM details, minimal maintenance window, firewall exceptions list, and SLA contact details.
- Acceptance gating: all preflight checks pass, manifest signature validated, acceptance tests pass, and a production runbook handed to customer.

Operational SLOs (recommended default):
- Request success rate >= 99.5% (rolling 30d)
- p99 latency <= 120ms for gen endpoints (local-to-region)
- Error budget: 0.5% per month

Post-deployment evidence to preserve in ticketing system:
- Handoff Acceptance Record (HAR) JSON
- Image manifest with signature and SHA256 hashes
- Preflight output log (`preflight-<date>.log`)
- Acceptance test results (timestamped)

Runbook quick commands (cheat sheet):
- `redwood-ops preflight --manifest manifest.json`
- `redwood-ops image import --signed-bundle bundle.tar.gz`
- `redwood-ops status watch --timeout 600`
- `redwood-ops rollout rollback --deployment <id>`

Related artifacts and references:
- See: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/onprem-packaging/ephemeral-boot-and-attestation-playbook-2026
- See: /confluence/eng-private-deployments/deployment-guides/private-deploy-onboarding-and-failover-runbook-2026
- Templates: HAR template (attached in page attachments), preflight script, smoke-test collection.

Appendix: example HAR JSON (fillable by Delivery and Customer)
```
{
  "handshake_id": "har-20251103-001",
  "delivery_signer": "CN=delivery-01.example.com",
  "customer_signer": "CN=customer-secld.example.com",
  "manifest_sha256": "<sha256-here>",
  "timestamp": "2025-11-03T14:22:00Z"
}
```
Notes and caveats:
- This playbook is intentionally prescriptive to reduce cross-team variance. Exceptions must be recorded and approved by Security and Delivery leads.
- Maintain updated public keys and rotation cadence: keys rotate annually or after any key compromise.

Change history:
- 2025-11-03: Initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2026-02-15: Minor clarifications and acceptance test additions (reviewers: Miguel, Priya)

title: Customer network, firmware, and KMS readiness checklist for private deployments
preview:
Summary:
This checklist captures the minimum network, firmware/driver, and KMS readiness items we require from customers before we begin an on-prem / VPC private deployment handoff. The goal is to reduce field friction during installation, ensure predictable runtime behavior for the Redwood inference runtime, and limit post-installation remedial work. Use this checklist during pre-sales technical validation and include a signed acceptance by the customer prior to scheduling the delivery window.

Audience:
- Solutions engineers and field systems integrators
- Customer infrastructure and security teams
- Private deployment delivery engineers and SREs

Key outcomes:
- Confirmed network paths and firewall rules for control plane, model serving, and telemetry
- Minimum validated firmware and driver versions across GPU hosts and networking gear
- KMS/HSM integration plan with rotation schedule and test vectors
- Agreed post-installation telemetry sampling and data retention constraints

1) Pre-installation network readiness (owner: customer infra, validated by Redwood SE)
- Objectives:
  - Ensure reachability for control-plane components and outbound telemetry.
  - Establish predictable latency and MTU across the inference fabric.
  - Reduce packet drops for KV cache replication and large model weight pulls.
- Required information (collect these values):
  - VPC/subnet CIDRs for compute, control plane, and management networks
  - Egress NAT IP(s) for appliance(s) and NAT traversal plan
  - BGP peer details (ASN, neighbor IPs) if using cross-region/private interconnect
  - Expected 95th-percentile packet loss and RTT between control plane and private nodes
  - Allowed outbound destinations: api.redwood.ai (hosted control plane), telemetry.redwood.ai, container registries (list), time NTP servers
- Firewall/ACL baseline (apply these exact ports):
  - TCP 443 outbound to api.redwood.ai and telemetry endpoints (TLS required)
  - TCP 9443 inbound from load balancer to runtime control socket (if using local LB)
  - UDP 123 outbound to NTP servers
  - TCP 22 (SSH) limited to Redwood IPs for field support during install (enable only during maintenance windows)
- MTU and jumbo frames:
  - Recommended MTU: 9000 for GPU interconnect segments. Confirm jumbo frame support across leaf-spine and on-prem interconnects.
  - If MTU < 9000, document maximum payload size and enable fragmentation-safe settings.
- QoS and traffic shaping guidance:
  - Prioritize control-plane ACKs and KV cache replication traffic. Example DSCP values: AF31 for control-plane, AF21 for model weight pulls, BE for bulk telemetry.
- Validation steps (numbered):
  1. From candidate host, curl https://api.redwood.ai/health (TLS) and confirm 200 within 300ms median.
  2. Run iperf3 between two compute nodes for 10s at MTU 9000 and record max throughput; expected > 90% of link speed.
  3. Capture a 1-minute tcpdump during a simulated model pull and confirm no fragmentation-related errors.

2) Minimum firmware and driver matrix (owner: customer infra -> provide evidence; validated by Redwood SRE)
- Purpose: avoid runtime stalls caused by NIC/BIOS/GPU incompatibilities, NUMA misconfigurations, and PCIe errata.
- Baseline matrix (table):
  - Host type | BIOS (minimum) | GPU driver (minimum) | NIC driver (minimum) | Recommended action
  - \"Dell R750\" | \"BIOS 2.7.0\" | \"NVIDIA driver 535.XX\" | \"Mellanox ONYX 5.8\" | \"Apply vendor hotfix for PCIe link flapping\"
  - \"HPE ProLiant DL380 Gen11\" | \"iLO 5 firmware 2.80\" | \"NVIDIA driver 535.XX\" | \"Intel XL710 5.10\" | \"Disable C-state deep sleep per guidance\"
  - \"Custom Rack (NVIDIA H100)\" | \"Server BIOS 1.12\" | \"NVIDIA driver 535.XX + CUDA 12.2\" | \"Mellanox OFED 5.7\" | \"Validate SR-IOV enablement if used\"
- Evidence required from customer: signed export of lshw or vendor firmware manifests, GPU driver rpm/deb files or checksums, NIC module versions.
- Automated checks we run during install:
  - nvidia-smi --query-gpu=driver_version --format=csv
  - ethtool -i <iface> to confirm firmware and driver strings
  - dmidecode -s bios-version for BIOS verification

3) Storage and filesystem considerations (owner: customer infra)
- We recommend XFS or ext4 with noatime for model cache directories.
- If using NFS: ensure file-locking is enabled and export options include no_root_squash for the service account used by the runtime if customer policy allows.
- For high-throughput model pulls, use local NVMe as staging; network stores must sustain 1GB/s for large model weights (typical H100 checkpoints).

4) KMS / HSM integration readiness (owner: customer security/KMS team; validated by Redwood security engineer)
- Integration modes supported:
  - Remote KMS (TLS-based API; Redwood stores key reference only)
  - On-site HSM backed KMS (PKCS#11 or KMIP connector)
- Required artifacts and details from customer:
  - KMS endpoint(s) and trust root certs (PEM format)
  - Service principal or API key with least privilege for envelope encryption operations (encrypt/decrypt/sign)
  - Key naming convention and desired rotation schedule (e.g., monthly, quarterly)
  - FIPS/HSM compliance statements if HSM is used
- Test vectors to verify during install:
  1. Redwood agent calls KMS encrypt('redwood-internal-test') -> KMS returns ciphertext (expect < 200ms median)
  2. Redwood agent sends decrypt(ciphertext) -> plaintext should match original
  3. Simulate key rotation: create a new key, rewrap test secret, confirm old ciphertexts decrypt with rewrap policy or that Redwood can fetch prior key via key-id resolution.
- Key rotation and emergency plan:
  - Customer must commit to notifying Redwood 72 hours before scheduled key rotations that impact production keys.
  - For emergency rotations, follow the rollback procedure defined in section 6 and provide a signed emergency rotation contact list.

5) Telemetry, privacy and retention (owner: customer and Redwood product)
- Default telemetry collected (high level): service health (SLOs), per-token latency percentiles, error rates, runtime resource metrics (GPU utilization, memory), agent heartbeats, and anonymized prompt hashes for quality regression detection.
- Sampling and PII controls:
  - Default sampling rate: 10% of requests for payload metadata; customer can specify 0%–100% in the contract.
  - PII filter: Redwood will never collect raw prompt content unless explicit customer opt-in with documented data flow and retention window.
- Retention and export requirements:
  - Default retention: 90 days for raw metrics, 365 days for aggregated cost/latency histograms.
  - For customers requiring shorter retention (30/7 days), include a signed Data Retention Annex.
- Telemetry validation steps during acceptance:
  1. Confirm telemetry egress is reaching telemetry.redwood.ai over TLS and that payload size conforms to agreed schema.
  2. Inject a synthetic load (see \"Run acceptance load\" below) and confirm sampled traces appear within 60s in the telemetry dashboard.

6) Run acceptance load and acceptance criteria (owner: delivery engineering + customer infra)
- Acceptance load profile (example):
  - Phase A (warmup): 5 minutes at 5 concurrent requests, sequence length 512 tokens
  - Phase B (throughput): 20 minutes ramp to sustained 250 qps or contract SLO, sequence length distribution: 60% 256 tokens / 30% 512 tokens / 10% 1024 tokens
  - Phase C (spike): 2-minute 4x traffic burst to validate autoscale and fallback behavior
- Pass/fail criteria:
  - 99th-percentile latency under target SLO (contract default: 300ms for short-form completions, or as specified)
  - No more than 0.5% error rate across the run
  - KV cache hit rate > 70% during Phase B for workloads with warm prefix patterns
  - Telemetry sampling receipts confirmed and available to customer dashboards
- Run acceptance commands (examples):
  - curl -X POST https://<local-proxy>/v1/generate -H \"Authorization: Bearer <token>\" -d @payload.json
  - loadtest: rps-local --target https://<local-proxy>/v1/chat --concurrency 200 --duration 20m --body-stream payloads.txt

7) Day-2 ops and patching windows (owner: SRE + customer ops)
- Firmware/driver patch policy:
  - Quarterly planned maintenance windows recommended for non-critical security patches.
  - Critical security patches (kernel, NIC firmware) require a 48-hour SLA for scheduling; customer to provide escalation path.
- Diagnostic artifacts (collected by field engineer):
  - runtime-diagnostics.tgz (nvidia-smi, dmesg, systemctl logs, agent logs, /var/log/redwood/*)
  - network-capture.pcap (5 minutes during failing scenario)
  - KMS audit extract for the relevant timestamp window
- Hotfix and rollback guidance (summary):
  1. If a firmware update causes degradation, immediately pin nodes out of the pool and use a pre-validated rollback image (provided as part of the install bundle).
  2. Invoke the incident playbook: escalate to Redwood SRE, collect artifacts, notify customer security.
  3. If rollback is not available, route traffic to dedicated fallback model (smaller quantized variant) until remediation.

8) Documentation and sign-off (owner: delivery manager)
- Deliverables from customer prior to sign-off:
  - Completed checklist signed by customer's infra lead (email + ticket)
  - SSH bastion window schedule and one-time access token for field engineers
  - KMS test keys and service account with test permissions
  - Confirmation of telemetry retention and any privacy/DPA annexes
- Sign-off template (one-line summary to be included in acceptance ticket):
  - \"Customer ABC Infra Lead (name) confirms network, firmware, storage, and KMS readiness per Redwood checklist v1.0 and schedules install for <date>\"

Appendix A — Quick CLI commands and expected outputs
- Validate GPU driver:
  - Command: nvidia-smi --query-gpu=driver_version --format=csv
  - Expected: driver_version,535.86
- Validate NIC firmware/driver:
  - Command: ethtool -i ens2f0
  - Expected fields: driver: mlx5_core, firmware-version: 21.15.1000
- KMS smoke test (example curl for a REST KMS):
  - curl -X POST https://kms.example.local/v1/encrypt -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" -d '{"plaintext":"redwood-test","key_id":"projects/prod/keys/rw-test"}' -v
  - Expected: 200 with ciphertext field in JSON

Appendix B — Common failure modes observed in the field and mitigations
- Symptoms: intermittent 5xx errors during model pulls
  - Typical cause: MTU mismatch or NIC offload bug. Mitigation: set MTU to 1500 and disable GRO/TSO for troubleshooting; escalate to vendor.
- Symptoms: slow decrypt operations to KMS
  - Typical cause: KMS network path through proxy or high auth latency. Mitigation: add local KMS proxy or increase concurrency pool for KMS client, and add circuit breaker for slow paths.

Contact and ownership
- Redwood delivery owner: Marcus Holt (marcus.holt@redwood.ai)
- Field engineer lead for installs: Lena Patel (lena.patel@redwood.ai)
- Security/KMS SME: Diego Ramos (diego.ramos@redwood.ai)

Revision history:
- 2026-01-10: Initial draft by Ava Chen
- 2026-01-20: Added KMS test vectors and rotation policy (reviewers: Diego Ramos)
- 2026-02-15: Finalized firmware matrix and telemetry retention defaults (published)

title: Holiday & Maintenance Window On-call Orchestration and Customer Communication Toolkit
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This document standardizes how Redwood operates scheduled maintenance and holiday windows when staffing is reduced. It covers on-call rotation adaptations, a concise operational runbook for scheduled degradations, pre- and post-maintenance game-day exercises, and customer-facing status templates. The goal is to maintain SLOs where possible, minimize customer surprise, and ensure a clear, timeboxed recovery path if regressions occur.

Scope
- Services in-scope: redwood-serving, routing-proxy, kv-cache, console-api, billing-queue, telemetry-ingest.
- Applies to: company-approved maintenance (deploys, infra upgrades, region maintenance) and planned holiday staffing reductions (global public holidays, end-of-year window).

Key principles
- Prioritize availability-critical routes: routing-proxy and redwood-serving production routes must be treated with highest priority.
- Timebox actions: every remediation step must have a max timeout (see runbook).
- Proactive comms: notify customers early with clear impact tiers and expected windows.
- Escalation guardrails: rely on compensating automation (feature flags, auto-fallbacks) before escalations when on-call headcount is limited.

SLOs and thresholds to drive action
- Availability SLO: 99.95% (monthly) for core generation endpoints (redwood-serving).
- Latency SLO: p95 < 120ms, p99 < 500ms (production read-only endpoints).
- Error budget policy during maintenance: allow up to 0.5% extra budget consumption during approved maintenance windows; any breach > 1.5x planned consumption triggers immediate escalation.

Pre-maintenance checklist (72-24 hours)
1. Notify internal stakeholders: product, sales engineering, customer-success, security.
2. Publish customer advisory: use template A (see templates section) at T-minus 72h and T-minus 24h.
3. Verify auto-fallback and read-only modes for console-api and billing-queue.
4. Confirm backup snapshots: kv-cache snapshot and model-serving checkpoint saved to GCS: gs://redwood-backups/<date>.
5. Identify test prompts and smoke-checks (see quick smoke list).
6. Assign holiday on-call roster: ensure primary + 1 backup are reachable.

Staffing matrix example (holiday window)
| Timezone coverage | Primary on-call | Backup | Bridge operator | Customer comms lead | Notes |
| UTC-8 (Americas) | eng-oncall-a | eng-oncall-b | platform-bridge | cs-duty-a | Focus peak PST hours |
| UTC+0 (EMEA) | eng-oncall-c | eng-oncall-d | platform-bridge | cs-duty-b | Overlap with EMEA customers |
| UTC+8 (APAC) | eng-oncall-e (on-call light) | eng-oncall-f | platform-bridge | cs-duty-c | Night watch; escalation path to Americas |

On-call rotation adjustments for holiday periods
- Shorten primary shift length to 6 hours for holiday storm windows to keep cognitive load low.
- Each primary must designate a reachable backup who can take full command in the primary's absence.
- Implement an "escalation blackout" on non-critical noise (low-severity alerts aggregated); critical SLO breaches still page immediately.

Runbook: Scheduled Maintenance with Reduced Staffing
(Use this for planned deploys or infra upgrades occurring in holiday windows)
1. Pre-window (T-24h): confirm checklist done and bridge created, publish advisory.
2. Start window (T0): announce on internal #ops-bridge and post abbreviated status page message (Template B).
3. Smoke tests (T+5m): run 3 core probes: healthcheck /health for redwood-serving, 3 synthetic token generations for a known prompt set, and billing-queue enqueue/dequeue check.
   - Commands:
     - kubectl rollout status deployment/redwood-serving -n prod --timeout=5m
     - curl -sS https://health.prod.redwood.ai/health | jq .status
     - ./tools/smoke-test --suite holiday-smoke --region us-west1
4. If smoke tests pass, continue with stepwise rollout (canary 5%, 25%, 100%) with 15-minute observation windows.
5. If p99 latency increases > 2x baseline or errors spike > 0.5% absolute: trigger canary pause and rollback to last known good.
6. Rollback command (example): kubectl rollout undo deployment/redwood-serving -n prod --to-revision=<rev>
7. If rollback fails or anomalies persist > 30m: escalate to on-call lead and open the incident bridge. Bridge operator follows the bridge script below.
8. Post-window: publish completion note, run extended health checks for 4 hours, and kickoff postmortem if any Service Level Objective was violated.

Bridge operator script (timeboxed actions)
- Within first 10 minutes: collect evidence links (Grafana dashboard, Kibana query, deployment history, GCS backup path) and pin in bridge chat. Evidence quick list:
  - Grafana: dashboards/serving/latency-overview (p95/p99)
  - Kibana query: index=prod-logs service=redwood-serving errors
  - Deployment history from CI: ci/builds/<commit-id>
- 10-30 minutes: run mitigation list: temporary route shift to warm pool, enable model-fallback to smaller quantized variant, increase autoscale target.
- 30-60 minutes: if mitigation unsuccessful, invoke cross-region failover (pre-approved only if customer impact > 1% or major SLO breach).

Escalation matrix (holiday simplified)
- Pager -> primary on-call (15 min) -> backup on-call (10 min) -> on-call lead (5 min) -> SRE manager -> product/CS notified if impact > P2 for > 30 min.
- For security incidents, route directly to security@redwood and the on-call security engineer.

Customer communication templates
- Template A (72h advisory):
Subject: Scheduled maintenance on <YYYY-MM-DD> — limited staffing window
Body: We plan routine maintenance on <service> on <date> between <start> and <end> (UTC). We expect minimal disruption. If you use dedicated/private deployments, your account team will contact you directly. For hosted customers: the platform will remain available; fallback routes may produce slightly higher latency for up to 30 minutes. Contact support@redwood.ai for concerns.

- Template B (In-window status page short message):
Status: Partial degradation — performing scheduled maintenance
Details: We are performing scheduled maintenance affecting generation latency in <region>. We are observing elevated latency; teams are following the maintenance runbook. Expected resolution: T+30–90 minutes.

- Template C (Customer-facing incident update for SLA-impact):
Subject: Incident update — degraded generation on <YYYY-MM-DD>
Body: We identified an issue during scheduled maintenance that impacted generation endpoints for some customers in <region>. Mitigations were applied and service was restored at <timestamp> UTC. We are investigating root cause and will share a detailed postmortem within 72 hours.

Internal Slack/Bridge announcement samples
- Start: "[MAINT] Starting scheduled maintenance for redwood-serving in us-west1. Bridge: <link>. Primary: @eng-oncall-a, Backup: @eng-oncall-b. Smoke tests running T+5m."
- Issue detected: "[ALERT] p99 latency spike to 1.2s for redwood-serving-canary. Pausing rollout. Running rollback (cmd). Bridge: <link>."

Game-day scenarios (recommended exercises)
1. Holiday-light staffing drill (2h exercise): simulate a canary failing during a midnight deploy with only primary + backup available. Objectives: test bridge script, customer comm cadence, and cross-region failover.
2. Fail-and-rollback drill (1h): intentionally introduce a faulty model artifact in a test cluster and practice rollback and postmortem execution under holiday staffing constraints.

Postmortem and evidence checklist (if SLO breached or customer impact > P2)
- Postmortem must be posted within 72 hours and include: timeline (UTC), impact summary (customers affected, routes), root cause hypothesis, evidence list (dashboards, logs, deployment SHAs), mitigations taken, action items with owner and ETA, and follow-up verification plan.
- Evidence items to attach: Grafana panels (PNG link), Kibana query exports (ndjson), deployment manifest (yaml), GCS backup path, smoke-test outputs.

Postmortem template (publish as confluence page)
1. Title: Postmortem — <short description> — <YYYY-MM-DD>
2. Impact summary (bullet points)
3. Timeline (table with UTC timestamps and actor)
4. Root cause analysis (clear causal chain)
5. Immediate mitigations and timeline
6. Long-term fixes and owners (action, owner, due date)
7. Verification plan (how we will measure success)
8. Lessons learned and follow-ups

Operational automation snippets
- Autoscale bump (example):
  gcloud compute --project=redwood-infra autoscaler update ... (internal scripted tool)
- Canary rollback helper (internal CLI):
  rhubarb rollback --service redwood-serving --env prod --to-revision <rev>

Metrics and reporting post-window
- Run 24-hour snapshot of SLO metrics and compare to baseline. If error budget consumed > planned threshold, schedule a dedicated follow-up review with SRE and product.

Governance and approvals
- Any cross-region failover during holiday windows must be approved by SRE manager and on-call lead.
- Emergency exceptions (e.g., critical data loss) allow on-call lead to act immediately and notify execs within 30 minutes.

Appendix: smoke-check quick list
- Generation sanity: 3 synthetic prompts across small/large models, verify tokens and latency < p95 baseline*2.
- KV cache consistency: read a known key pre/post-deploy.
- Billing queue round-trip: enqueue/test consumer ack.

Notes and references
- Related runbooks: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-retention-and-responder-onboarding-handbook-2026
- Deployment policy: /confluence/eng-infra/ci-cd-and-build/deployment-policy

Contact and ownership
- Document owner: Ava Chen (oncall coordinator)
- Primary contacts: eng-oncall-rotations@redwood.ai, platform-bridge@redwood.ai, customer-success@redwood.ai

This toolkit is intended to be a compact operational reference for holiday and reduced-staff maintenance windows. Update this page after each holiday window runbook exercise with concrete lessons and revised templates.
text:
Overview

Purpose: This document standardizes how Redwood operates scheduled maintenance and holiday windows when staffing is reduced. It covers on-call rotation adaptations, a concise operational runbook for scheduled degradations, pre- and post-maintenance game-day exercises, and customer-facing status templates. The goal is to maintain SLOs where possible, minimize customer surprise, and ensure a clear, timeboxed recovery path if regressions occur.

Scope
- Services in-scope: redwood-serving, routing-proxy, kv-cache, console-api, billing-queue, telemetry-ingest.
- Applies to: company-approved maintenance (deploys, infra upgrades, region maintenance) and planned holiday staffing reductions (global public holidays, end-of-year window).

Key principles
- Prioritize availability-critical routes: routing-proxy and redwood-serving production routes must be treated with highest priority.
- Timebox actions: every remediation step must have a max timeout (see runbook).
- Proactive comms: notify customers early with clear impact tiers and expected windows.
- Escalation guardrails: rely on compensating automation (feature flags, auto-fallbacks) before escalations when on-call headcount is limited.

SLOs and thresholds to drive action
- Availability SLO: 99.95% (monthly) for core generation endpoints (redwood-serving).
- Latency SLO: p95 < 120ms, p99 < 500ms (production read-only endpoints).
- Error budget policy during maintenance: allow up to 0.5% extra budget consumption during approved maintenance windows; any breach > 1.5x planned consumption triggers immediate escalation.

Pre-maintenance checklist (72-24 hours)
1. Notify internal stakeholders: product, sales engineering, customer-success, security.
2. Publish customer advisory: use template A (see templates section) at T-minus 72h and T-minus 24h.
3. Verify auto-fallback and read-only modes for console-api and billing-queue.
4. Confirm backup snapshots: kv-cache snapshot and model-serving checkpoint saved to GCS: gs://redwood-backups/<date>.
5. Identify test prompts and smoke-checks (see quick smoke list).
6. Assign holiday on-call roster: ensure primary + 1 backup are reachable.

Staffing matrix example (holiday window)
| Timezone coverage | Primary on-call | Backup | Bridge operator | Customer comms lead | Notes |
| UTC-8 (Americas) | eng-oncall-a | eng-oncall-b | platform-bridge | cs-duty-a | Focus peak PST hours |
| UTC+0 (EMEA) | eng-oncall-c | eng-oncall-d | platform-bridge | cs-duty-b | Overlap with EMEA customers |
| UTC+8 (APAC) | eng-oncall-e (on-call light) | eng-oncall-f | platform-bridge | cs-duty-c | Night watch; escalation path to Americas |

On-call rotation adjustments for holiday periods
- Shorten primary shift length to 6 hours for holiday storm windows to keep cognitive load low.
- Each primary must designate a reachable backup who can take full command in the primary's absence.
- Implement an "escalation blackout" on non-critical noise (low-severity alerts aggregated); critical SLO breaches still page immediately.

Runbook: Scheduled Maintenance with Reduced Staffing
(Use this for planned deploys or infra upgrades occurring in holiday windows)
1. Pre-window (T-24h): confirm checklist done and bridge created, publish advisory.
2. Start window (T0): announce on internal #ops-bridge and post abbreviated status page message (Template B).
3. Smoke tests (T+5m): run 3 core probes: healthcheck /health for redwood-serving, 3 synthetic token generations for a known prompt set, and billing-queue enqueue/dequeue check.
   - Commands:
     - kubectl rollout status deployment/redwood-serving -n prod --timeout=5m
     - curl -sS https://health.prod.redwood.ai/health | jq .status
     - ./tools/smoke-test --suite holiday-smoke --region us-west1
4. If smoke tests pass, continue with stepwise rollout (canary 5%, 25%, 100%) with 15-minute observation windows.
5. If p99 latency increases > 2x baseline or errors spike > 0.5% absolute: trigger canary pause and rollback to last known good.
6. Rollback command (example): kubectl rollout undo deployment/redwood-serving -n prod --to-revision=<rev>
7. If rollback fails or anomalies persist > 30m: escalate to on-call lead and open the incident bridge. Bridge operator follows the bridge script below.
8. Post-window: publish completion note, run extended health checks for 4 hours, and kickoff postmortem if any Service Level Objective was violated.

Bridge operator script (timeboxed actions)
- Within first 10 minutes: collect evidence links (Grafana dashboard, Kibana query, deployment history, GCS backup path) and pin in bridge chat. Evidence quick list:
  - Grafana: dashboards/serving/latency-overview (p95/p99)
  - Kibana query: index=prod-logs service=redwood-serving errors
  - Deployment history from CI: ci/builds/<commit-id>

- 30-60 minutes: if mitigation unsuccessful, invoke cross-region failover (pre-approved only if customer impact > 1% or major SLO breach).

Escalation matrix (holiday simplified)
- Pager -> primary on-call (15 min) -> backup on-call (10 min) -> on-call lead (5 min) -> SRE manager -> product/CS notified if impact > P2 for > 30 min.
- For security incidents, route directly to security@redwood and the on-call security engineer.

Customer communication templates
- Template A (72h advisory):
Subject: Scheduled maintenance on <YYYY-MM-DD> — limited staffing window
Body: We plan routine maintenance on <service> on <date> between <start> and <end> (UTC). We expect minimal disruption. If you use dedicated/private deployments, your account team will contact you directly. For hosted customers: the platform will remain available; fallback routes may produce slightly higher latency for up to 30 minutes. Contact support@redwood.ai for concerns.

- Template B (In-window status page short message):

- Template C (Customer-facing incident update for SLA-impact):

2. Fail-and-rollback drill (1h): intentionally introduce a faulty model artifact in a test cluster and practice rollback and postmortem execution under holiday staffing constraints.

- Evidence items to attach: Grafana panels (PNG link), Kibana query exports (ndjson), deployment manifest (yaml), GCS backup path, smoke-test outputs.

2. Impact summary (bullet points)
3. Timeline (table with UTC timestamps and actor)
4. Root cause analysis (clear causal chain)
5. Immediate mitigations and timeline
6. Long-term fixes and owners (action, owner, due date)
7. Verification plan (how we will measure success)
8. Lessons learned and follow-ups

Operational automation snippets
- Autoscale bump (example):
  gcloud compute --project=redwood-infra autoscaler update ... (internal scripted tool)
- Canary rollback helper (internal CLI):
  rhubarb rollback --service redwood-serving --env prod --to-revision <rev>

Metrics and reporting post-window
