title: Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer: design, operator guide, and rollout
preview:
Summary
-------
This document describes the Adaptive Prefetch Hint Layer (APHL): a lightweight application-facing hinting protocol and runtime integration that lets upstream services provide controlled prefetch requests to the LLM runtime to reduce tail latency for common prefixes while preserving global SLOs and cost budgets. It includes design rationale, runtime components, operator playbooks, dashboarding requirements, and a safe rollout plan.

Intended audience
- Platform operators and on-call engineers
- Runtime and serving engineers
- Client SDK and API owners

Why we built APHL
- Observation: a small fraction of request patterns (e.g., prompt templates, system messages, high-frequency webhook patterns) dominate 75% of tail latency because early tokens are not hot in the prefix/KV cache.
- Goal: reduce p95/p99 generation latency for known safe prefixes without increasing token cost or violating tenant SLOs.
- Constraints: must be safe for multi-tenant environment, respect tenant rate limits, and be observable and reversible.

High-level design
-----------------
1) Hint model: clients optionally attach a prefetch hint to a request. A hint is an opaque identifier + optional priority tag + TTL. Hints are advisory; runtime enforces limits.
2) Hint evaluation policy: central policy service scores hints using: past-hit-rate, cost-estimate (tokens), SLO-burn risk, tenant-priority, and regional capacity.
3) Adaptive prefetch actions: runtime can perform one of: no-op, local-prefetch (on same GPU host), remote-prefetch (in dedicated caching tier), or speculative-warm invocation (low-cost model variant to warm KV).
4) Safety controls: per-tenant token budget, global prefetch budget, and backpressure signals from autoscaler.

System components
- Client SDK: adds optional header X-RW-Prefetch-Hint: hint_id=H123;priority=low;ttl=30s
- Hint Policy Service (HPS): evaluates hints, returns decision tokens and expected cost estimate. Exposes API: POST /v1/hint/evaluate
- Prefetch Controller (runtime side): enforces action, emits traces and metrics, and records which cache shard received prefetch.
- Budget Ledger: centralized budgeting service tracking per-tenant and global prefetch token usage.

Runtime integration notes
- Prefetch work must be queued with lower priority than production inference to avoid SLO interference. Use the existing backlog queue with QoS weight 0.2.
- Prefetch KV writes set a separate TTL namespace: prefix-cache:hint:<hint_id> to avoid eviction collisions with on-path caches.
- When speculative-warm invocations are used, they run on quantized low-cost variants with max_tokens=8 and max_concurrency=2 per tenant region.

Config table (defaults)
| parameter | default | description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min | 2_000_000 | Global budget across all tenants per minute |
| tenant_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min | 50_000 | Default tenant budget; overridden by enterprise contracts |
| hint_eval_max_latency_ms | 50 | HPS timeout; hint treated as absent after this |
| prefetch_qos_weight | 0.2 | Relative priority weight for prefetch tasks |
| speculative_variant_model | redwood-lite-q4_8bit | Low-cost model used for speculative warms |

Operator runbook: day-to-day (short)
1) Detection
  - Alert: PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN (see Alerting section) fires when global ledger consumption > 90% in 5m.
  - Dashboard: Prefetch hit-rate per region, per-tenant top 20 consumers.
2) Initial mitigation
  - Scale down prefetch window: set global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min -> 25% via Helm override: helm upgrade runtime --set prefetch.globalBudget=500000
  - Opt-out noisy tenants: rpc call to Budget Ledger: POST /admin/ledger/adjust?tenant=TENANT_ID&action=opt_out
3) Recovery verification
  - Confirm p99 latency for affected routes decreases after throttling.
  - Run negative-safety-check: sample recent generations for quality regression.

Alerting and dashboards (required panels/alerts)
- Panels (must exist on platform AppOps dashboard):
  - Prefetch budget burn rate (global + region) — timeseries (tokens/min)
  - Prefetch hit-rate (percentage of prefetches that produced cache hits) — heatmap by shard
  - Prefetch-induced cold-start reductions (delta p95 vs baseline) — bar chart per model variant
  - Top 50 hint producers (tenant_id, hint_id, tokens_used) — table with owner links
- Alerts (defaults):
  - PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN: global_consumption_5m / global_budget > 0.9 for 5m -> Sev3 (pager to platform-duty)
  - PREFETCH_HIGH_MISS_RATE: prefetch_hit_rate < 0.2 for 10m -> Sev4 (Slack ops)
  - PREFETCH_SLO_IMPACT: prefetch_backpressure_ratio > 0.05 and p99_inference_latency_increase > 10% over 15m -> Sev2 (on-call)

Runbook: incident steps (detailed)
1) Triage
  - Identify scope: is the issue global, region-specific, tenant-specific? Use the "Top hint producers" panel.
  - Correlate with autoscaler metrics and host load: if host_cpu_util > 85% and prefetch queue length > 1000, treat as resource contention.
2) Immediate actions (ordered)
  a) Toggle prefetch warmers to readonly (no speculative runs): curl -XPOST $PREFETCH_CTRL/_api/toggle -d '{"speculative":false}'
  b) Reduce speculative_variant_model concurrency: curl -XPOST $PREFETCH_CTRL/_api/update -d '{"speculative_concurrency":1}'
  c) If still burning budget, flip global circuit: kubectl -n runtime patch configmap prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"enabled":"false"}}'
3) Post-incident
  - Run smoke-generation suite on a sampled set of templates to confirm no regression in output quality or new latency spikes.
  - Update incident timeline and mark triage owner.

Safe rollout plan (canary -> ramp -> GA)
- Canary (week 0): enable APHL for internal platform tenants only. Goals: validate HPS latency <= 50ms, prefetch hit-rate >= 0.35 for top templates, and no increase in p99 for high-priority tenants.
- Early customer opt-in (week 1–2): invite 5 low-risk customers on dedicated pools. Monitor budgets and runbook.
- Controlled ramp (week 3–6): increase tenant inclusion by 20% weekly. Add automation to opt-out tenants that exceed budget thresholds.
- GA: enable by default for self-serve tenants with prefetch budget set to conservative defaults.

Telemetry and metrics contract (minimal list)
- prefetch.requests_total (labels: region, tenant_id, hint_id, action)
- prefetch.tokens_consumed_total (labels: region, tenant_id)
- prefetch.hit_total (labels: region, shard_id)
- prefetch.speculative_runs_total (labels: region, model_variant)
- prefetch.evaluation_latency_ms_bucket (labels: hps_node)
- prefetch.burn_ratio (gauge) = tokens_consumed / configured_budget

Operational knobs exposed (CLI/examples)
- Evaluate a hint (local test):
  curl -XPOST https://hps.internal/v1/hint/evaluate -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' -d '{"hint_id":"banner-home-v2","tenant":"acme-corp","context_samples":5}'

- Temporarily throttle global budget via kubectl:
  kubectl -n runtime patch cm prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min":"500000"}}'

- Opt a tenant out from prefetching:
  curl -XPOST https://ledger.internal/admin/tenants/acme-corp/opt_out -H 'Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN'

Testing and evaluation checklist
- Unit tests: HPS decision logic (edge cases: stale hints, malformed hints).
- Integration tests: Prefetch end-to-end with KV writes, TTL handling, and eviction simulation.
- Load tests: synthetic traffic to validate that prefetch does not increase request queue delay by >1%.
- Regression tests: generation quality smoke-run against registered prompt-set (must pass 95% of baseline exact-match tests).

Common failures and troubleshooting tips
- Symptom: high prefetch miss rate (>80%)
  - Likely cause: hints mis-identified or context mismatch. Action: inspect context_samples reported by HPS and compare tokenized prefixes.
- Symptom: unexpected budget burn with small hit benefit
  - Likely cause: runaway speculative runs or tenant misconfiguration. Action: check speculative_runs_total by tenant and opt-out; reduce speculative_concurrency.
- Symptom: increased p99 for high-priority tenant after APHL enable
  - Likely cause: prefetch backpressure. Action: disable prefetch on tenant immediately and escalate to runtime team to review QoS scheduler.

Rollback criteria and procedure
- Rollback triggers:
  - Sustained PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN (>= 95%) for 15m despite mitigations.
  - SLO regressions: p99 increase > 15% for any priority-1 tenant for 10m.
- Procedure:
  1) kubectl -n runtime patch cm prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"enabled":"false"}}'
  2) POST to Budget Ledger to clear queued prefetch entries: curl -XPOST https://ledger.internal/admin/clear_queues
  3) Run verification job: /tools/smoke/llm_latency_check.sh --routes=critical

Open questions and follow-ups
- Long-term: should hints be persisted as part of model metadata for better lifecycle tracking?
- Research: quantify cost/latency tradeoffs for multi-token speculative warms (we currently use max_tokens=8 as heuristic).
- Observability: add correlation ids from client SDK through HPS to Prefetch Controller for 100% traceability.

Appendix: example hint formats and sample logs
- Header example (HTTP):
  X-RW-Prefetch-Hint: hint_id=banner-home-v2;priority=med;ttl=30s;signature=abcd1234

- HPS API example request JSON:
  {
    "hint_id": "banner-home-v2",
    "tenant": "acme-corp",
    "recent_templates": ["Welcome v2", "Promo slot"],
    "samples": 3
  }

- Sample Prefetch Controller log excerpt:
  [2026-02-14T09:12:03Z] PREFETCH_DECISION tenant=acme-corp hint=banner-home-v2 action=local_prefetch tokens=24 hit_est=0.62 cost_est=18ms_consume node=rt-12

Contact and ownership
- Primary owner: eng-platform (Ava Chen)
- Runtime integration: eng-serving-runtime (Miguel Santos)
- HPS and budgeting: eng-infra (Priya Kapoor)

Change history
- 2025-11-10: Draft authored and internal review started.
- 2025-12-02: Added telemetry contract and operator knobs after load test.
- 2026-02-14: Published: updated rollout plan and runbook based on canary feedback.

End of document

  - Dashboard: Prefetch hit-rate per region, per-tenant top 20 consumers.
2) Initial mitigation
  - Scale down prefetch window: set global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min -> 25% via Helm override: helm upgrade runtime --set prefetch.globalBudget=500000
  - Opt-out noisy tenants: rpc call to Budget Ledger: POST /admin/ledger/adjust?tenant=TENANT_ID&action=opt_out
3) Recovery verification
  - Confirm p99 latency for affected routes decreases after throttling.
  - Run negative-safety-check: sample recent generations for quality regression.

Alerting and dashboards (required panels/alerts)
- Panels (must exist on platform AppOps dashboard):
  - Prefetch budget burn rate (global + region) — timeseries (tokens/min)
  - Prefetch hit-rate (percentage of prefetches that produced cache hits) — heatmap by shard
  - Prefetch-induced cold-start reductions (delta p95 vs baseline) — bar chart per model variant
  - Top 50 hint producers (tenant_id, hint_id, tokens_used) — table with owner links
- Alerts (defaults):
  - PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN: global_consumption_5m / global_budget > 0.9 for 5m -> Sev3 (pager to platform-duty)
  - PREFETCH_HIGH_MISS_RATE: prefetch_hit_rate < 0.2 for 10m -> Sev4 (Slack ops)
  - PREFETCH_SLO_IMPACT: prefetch_backpressure_ratio > 0.05 and p99_inference_latency_increase > 10% over 15m -> Sev2 (on-call)

Runbook: incident steps (detailed)
1) Triage
  - Identify scope: is the issue global, region-specific, tenant-specific? Use the "Top hint producers" panel.
  - Correlate with autoscaler metrics and host load: if host_cpu_util > 85% and prefetch queue length > 1000, treat as resource contention.
2) Immediate actions (ordered)
  a) Toggle prefetch warmers to readonly (no speculative runs): curl -XPOST $PREFETCH_CTRL/_api/toggle -d '{"speculative":false}'
  b) Reduce speculative_variant_model concurrency: curl -XPOST $PREFETCH_CTRL/_api/update -d '{"speculative_concurrency":1}'
  c) If still burning budget, flip global circuit: kubectl -n runtime patch configmap prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"enabled":"false"}}'
3) Post-incident
  - Run smoke-generation suite on a sampled set of templates to confirm no regression in output quality or new latency spikes.
  - Update incident timeline and mark triage owner.

Safe rollout plan (canary -> ramp -> GA)
- Canary (week 0): enable APHL for internal platform tenants only. Goals: validate HPS latency <= 50ms, prefetch hit-rate >= 0.35 for top templates, and no increase in p99 for high-priority tenants.
- Early customer opt-in (week 1–2): invite 5 low-risk customers on dedicated pools. Monitor budgets and runbook.
- Controlled ramp (week 3–6): increase tenant inclusion by 20% weekly. Add automation to opt-out tenants that exceed budget thresholds.
- GA: enable by default for self-serve tenants with prefetch budget set to conservative defaults.

Telemetry and metrics contract (minimal list)
- prefetch.requests_total (labels: region, tenant_id, hint_id, action)
- prefetch.tokens_consumed_total (labels: region, tenant_id)
- prefetch.hit_total (labels: region, shard_id)
- prefetch.speculative_runs_total (labels: region, model_variant)
- prefetch.evaluation_latency_ms_bucket (labels: hps_node)
- prefetch.burn_ratio (gauge) = tokens_consumed / configured_budget

Operational knobs exposed (CLI/examples)
- Evaluate a hint (local test):
  curl -XPOST https://hps.internal/v1/hint/evaluate -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' -d '{"hint_id":"banner-home-v2","tenant":"acme-corp","context_samples":5}'

- Temporarily throttle global budget via kubectl:
  kubectl -n runtime patch cm prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"global_prefetch_budget_tokens_per_min":"500000"}}'

- Opt a tenant out from prefetching:
  curl -XPOST https://ledger.internal/admin/tenants/acme-corp/opt_out -H 'Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN'

Testing and evaluation checklist
- Unit tests: HPS decision logic (edge cases: stale hints, malformed hints).
- Integration tests: Prefetch end-to-end with KV writes, TTL handling, and eviction simulation.
- Load tests: synthetic traffic to validate that prefetch does not increase request queue delay by >1%.
- Regression tests: generation quality smoke-run against registered prompt-set (must pass 95% of baseline exact-match tests).

Common failures and troubleshooting tips
- Symptom: high prefetch miss rate (>80%)
  - Likely cause: hints mis-identified or context mismatch. Action: inspect context_samples reported by HPS and compare tokenized prefixes.
- Symptom: unexpected budget burn with small hit benefit
  - Likely cause: runaway speculative runs or tenant misconfiguration. Action: check speculative_runs_total by tenant and opt-out; reduce speculative_concurrency.
- Symptom: increased p99 for high-priority tenant after APHL enable
  - Likely cause: prefetch backpressure. Action: disable prefetch on tenant immediately and escalate to runtime team to review QoS scheduler.

Rollback criteria and procedure
- Rollback triggers:
  - Sustained PREFETCH_BUDGET_BURN (>= 95%) for 15m despite mitigations.
  - SLO regressions: p99 increase > 15% for any priority-1 tenant for 10m.
- Procedure:
  1) kubectl -n runtime patch cm prefetch-config -p '{"data":{"enabled":"false"}}'
  2) POST to Budget Ledger to clear queued prefetch entries: curl -XPOST https://ledger.internal/admin/clear_queues
  3) Run verification job: /tools/smoke/llm_latency_check.sh --routes=critical

Open questions and follow-ups
- Long-term: should hints be persisted as part of model metadata for better lifecycle tracking?
- Research: quantify cost/latency tradeoffs for multi-token speculative warms (we currently use max_tokens=8 as heuristic).
- Observability: add correlation ids from client SDK through HPS to Prefetch Controller for 100% traceability.

Appendix: example hint formats and sample logs
- Header example (HTTP):
  X-RW-Prefetch-Hint: hint_id=banner-home-v2;priority=med;ttl=30s;signature=abcd1234

- HPS API example request JSON:
  {
    "hint_id": "banner-home-v2",
    "tenant": "acme-corp",
    "recent_templates": ["Welcome v2", "Promo slot"],
    "samples": 3
  }

- Sample Prefetch Controller log excerpt:
  [2026-02-14T09:12:03Z] PREFETCH_DECISION tenant=acme-corp hint=banner-home-v2 action=local_prefetch tokens=24 hit_est=0.62 cost_est=18ms_consume node=rt-12

Contact and ownership
- Primary owner: eng-platform (Ava Chen)
- Runtime integration: eng-serving-runtime (Miguel Santos)
- HPS and budgeting: eng-infra (Priya Kapoor)

Change history
- 2025-11-10: Draft authored and internal review started.
- 2025-12-02: Added telemetry contract and operator knobs after load test.
- 2026-02-14: Published: updated rollout plan and runbook based on canary feedback.

End of document

title: Policy-driven streaming and early-exit contract
preview:
Summary
-------
This document defines a standardized API contract and runtime expectations for policy-driven streaming generation endpoints, with explicit support for safe early-exit, client-driven backpressure, and per-request cost/quality hints. The goal is to make streaming behavior predictable across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments while enabling cost-aware clients and graceful degradation strategies.

Scope
-----
Applies to all Redwood inference endpoints that offer streaming token/response delivery (hosted API and dedicated model variants). This contract covers: request schema additions, streaming frame types, control frames (early-exit, watermark, throttling), header conventions, server-side metrics, compatibility rules, and rollout/testing procedures.

Motivation
----------
- Many client integrations require deterministic signal semantics for when to stop rendering partial results (early-exit) to avoid partial-state bugs.
- Streaming reduces perceived latency but complicates cost accounting and autoscaling decisions when clients keep long-lived connections open with low throughput.
- A policy layer that expresses cost/quality trade-offs (e.g., prioritize latency vs. completeness) enables smarter routing and fallback without breaking client implementations.

Key definitions
---------------
- Frame: Atomic wire message sent in a streaming session. Frames are typed (data, meta, control).
- Early-exit: A deterministic signal emitted by the server indicating it will stop producing further tokens for this request.
- Watermark: A control frame indicating progress/finality (e.g., partial, stable, final).
- Backpressure: Client-to-server hint indicating receiver buffer availability or desired max rate.
- Policy: A small JSON object attached to the request that encodes trade-offs (latency_budget_ms, cost_tier, quality_threshold).

Contract specification
-----------------------
1) Request schema extensions (JSON body or headers):
   - Optional top-level field: "policy" (object). Fields: 
     * "latency_budget_ms": integer (recommended values: 50, 200, 500, 2000). Server will attempt allocation modes consistent with budget.
     * "cost_tier": string {"low","balanced","high"} indicating how much compute to prefer (quantization/ensemble use).
     * "quality_threshold": float 0.0-1.0 for downstream heuristics (e.g., stop early when confidence >= threshold).
   - Header: X-Redwood-Session-Mode: streaming | poll
   - Header: X-Redwood-Client-Backpressure: token-rate=<n>/s | buffer-ms=<n> (optional)

2) Streaming frame types (text/JSON over HTTP/2, SSE or websockets):
   - data: {"type":"data", "tokens":[...], "html":null, "delta_text":"..."} -- token batches or deltas
   - meta: {"type":"meta", "request_id":"...", "model_variant":"...", "progress":"partial|stable|final"}
   - control: {"type":"control", "code":"EARLY_EXIT|THROTTLE|ACK|KEEPALIVE|TRACE" , "reason":"human-readable" , "meta":{...}}

3) Early-exit semantics:
   - When server sends control.code == "EARLY_EXIT", the session is logically complete; no further data frames will be emitted for this request.
   - Possible early-exit reasons: 
     * QUALITY_CONFIDENCE -- model indicates generation probability mass concentrated on termination token and quality threshold achieved.
     * COST_CUTOFF -- policy/cost cap reached.
     * CAPACITY_FALLBACK -- runtime preempted or downgraded to cheaper model; server chooses to cut output short and annotate reason.
     * CLIENT_REQUESTED_TRUNCATION -- client asked to truncate via a control frame.
   - Client must treat EARLY_EXIT as terminal but may open a follow-up request if more output is required.

4) Backpressure & throttling flow:
   - Clients may include X-Redwood-Client-Backpressure header at request time. At runtime, servers may emit control.code == "THROTTLE" with recommended token-rate or buffer-ms.
   - THROTTLE reasons: QUEUE_LENGTH, NODE_SAFEGUARD, USER_RATE_LIMIT.
   - Throttle frames MUST include a retry-after-ms in meta for client guidance.

5) Cost accounting and determinism:
   - Each terminal frame (final meta or EARLY_EXIT) MUST include a cost-estimate header/frame: {"type":"meta", "cost_units": "tokens:NN, compute_ms:NN"}.
   - For early-exit, servers must annotate both actual processed tokens and estimated unprocessed tokens avoided: {"cost_savings_estimate": "tokens:5"}.

6) Compatibility and fallback rules:
   - If a client does not send a policy, server uses default policy: latency_budget_ms=500, cost_tier="balanced", quality_threshold=0.6.
   - Servers MAY ignore cost_tier when hardware constraints force a different choice but MUST emit meta.model_variant that describes actual variant used (e.g., gptx-q4b-latency).

7) Observability contract (metrics & traces):
   - Per-request metrics to emit (prometheus-style labels): request_mode, policy_latency_bucket, cost_tier, early_exit_reason, token_count, streaming_duration_ms
   - SLIs: streaming_earliness_rate (percentage of sessions that ended via EARLY_EXIT vs normal-stable-final), long_idle_stream_rate (streams open > 30s with no tokens), backpressure_event_rate.
   - Alert thresholds (SRE):
     * streaming_earliness_rate > 12% sustained for 5m -> review model/threshold changes.
     * long_idle_stream_rate > 0.8% -> rate-limit regressions or client SDK bug.

8) Error model and client contract:
   - Error frames have type control with code ERROR and an RFC-style error code in meta (e.g., ERR_TIMEOUT, ERR_INVALID_POLICY, ERR_INTERNAL).
   - For transient server-side capacity errors, server should prefer to emit ERROR with recommended fallback model_variant and an idempotency token for retry safety.

Examples
--------
1) Request (HTTP/2 JSON):
   POST /v1/generate?stream=true
   Headers:
     Content-Type: application/json
     X-Redwood-Session-Mode: streaming
     X-Redwood-Client-Backpressure: token-rate=10/s

   Body:
   {
     "prompt": "Translate to French: Hello world.",
     "policy": {"latency_budget_ms":200, "cost_tier":"low", "quality_threshold":0.5}
   }

2) Control frame showing early exit:
   {"type":"control","code":"EARLY_EXIT","reason":"COST_CUTOFF","meta":{"cost_units":"tokens:12,compute_ms:34","cost_savings_estimate":"tokens:6"}}

3) Server throttle recommendation frame:
   {"type":"control","code":"THROTTLE","reason":"QUEUE_LENGTH","meta":{"token_rate_recommended":"5/s","retry_after_ms":250}}

Rollout and rollout testing
---------------------------
- Phase 0 (canary, 2 weeks): implement server-side support behind a feature flag. Log frames and counters but do not emit THROTTLE to clients (server-side-only enforcement).
- Phase 1 (experimental): enable frame emission for SDK-equipped clients (SDK flags opt-in). Run compatibility tests with mobile/web SDKs.
- Phase 2 (default): enable for all streaming endpoints. Monitor long_idle_stream_rate and client error rate for 1 week before wide rollout.

Testing & compliance checklist
------------------------------
1) Unit tests: request policy parsing, header parsing, control-frame serialization.
2) Integration tests: simulate slow-client backpressure, verify THROTTLE frames and retry-after behavior.
3) Performance test: under mixed workloads (50/50 low/high latency_budget_ms), verify median token latency within 1.2x baseline and no >1% increase in long_idle_stream_rate.
4) SDKs must parse control frames and map EARLY_EXIT -> close stream and expose reason to caller.

Open decisions and alternatives considered
----------------------------------------
- Decision: Represent policies inline in request vs. separate policy service. Rationale: inline makes requests self-describing and easier to audit; a policy service was deferred for simplicity.
- Decision: Use a small enum for cost_tier. Alternative: numeric budgets. Rationale: tiers are easier for clients to reason about and map to runtime profiles.
- Deferred: Binary wire format for frames. We keep textual JSON-over-HTTP for debuggability and compatibility; revisit if perf dictates.

Ownership and contacts
----------------------
- API spec owner: Ava Chen (eng-api-contracts)
- Runtime integration owner: Samir Patel (serving-runtime)
- SDK owner: Priya Kapoor (developer-experience)

Operational playbook (quick)
---------------------------
1) If streaming_earliness_rate spikes: check recent model rollouts and quality_threshold tuning.
2) If long_idle_stream_rate increases: investigate client SDK release; consider temporary server-side aggressive THROTTLE default.
3) If clients report unexpected EARLY_EXIT: capture request_id and full session trace; correlate policy fields and model_variant.

Appendix: JSON schemas (compact)
--------------------------------
- Policy schema: {"type":"object","properties":{"latency_budget_ms":{"type":"integer"},"cost_tier":{"type":"string"},"quality_threshold":{"type":"number"},"additionalProperties":false}}
- Control frame schema (summary): {"type":"object","properties":{"type":{"enum":["control"]},"code":{"type":"string"},"reason":{"type":"string"},"meta":{"type":"object"}}}

Related documentation
---------------------
- See cost-aware-streaming-payload-schema-guideline for payload serialization guidance.
- See runtime-architecture sequence-length and kernel selection docs for how latency_budget influences kernel selection.

Revision history
----------------
- 2025-11-05: drafted by Ava Chen.
- 2026-01-12: added observability SLIs and rollout plan after SRE review.
- 2026-02-14: clarified early-exit cost accounting and added examples (published).

title: Project Orion — Weekly status update (2026-02-13)
preview:
# Summary
Console preview is now powered by the control plane simulation endpoint and we are back on track for the beta cohort kickoff.

# Status
- Overall: On Track
- RAG: Green

# Progress this week
- Control plane: shipped simulate_policy_v1 for a single route + region (latency p95 45ms).
- Console: integrated live preview with clear warnings for unsupported cases (multi-region, multiple models).

# Risks / blockers

- Publish beta onboarding doc and track adoption metrics.

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (control plane), Maya Srinivasan (console), Jordan Blake (solutions), Rishi Malhotra (customer success)

title: Config Consistency and Gated Feature Flags Playbook
preview:
Overview:
This playbook defines our standard architecture and operational practices for distributing runtime configuration (feature flags, quantization/precision profiles, autoscaling hints, and routing policies) with a focus on strong eventual consistency, safe progressive rollouts, and automated SLI/SLO safety gates. It is intended for platform engineers, runtime teams, and service owners who operate multi-tenant inference services (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

Problem statement:
- Runtime configuration changes (flags, model variants, quantization profiles) are frequent and can change behavior across thousands of nodes instantly.
- Inconsistencies between control-plane config and node application config cause correctness, performance regressions, and hard-to-debug incidents.
- Feature-flag-driven rollouts (canary, ring, percentage rollouts) must be reversible with bounded blast radius and observable safety checks.

Scope:
- Applies to: feature flags, model variant assignments, quantization/precision toggles, cache-affinity rules, routing hints, and throttling profiles.
- Not in scope: long-lived static infra config (provisioning manifests), secret material (handled by KMS/HSM policies).

Definitions:
- Source of truth (SoT): primary control-plane config store (Redwood Config Service).
- Node view: local cached copy of the config used by a runtime instance.
- Rollout policy: a declarative object describing targeting rules, phases, and safety gates.
- Safety gate: an automated check (SLI/SLO, error delta, latency delta) that can pause/rollback a rollout phase.

Principles (high level):
1) Single authoritative SoT with versioned config artifacts.
2) Compare-and-apply semantics: nodes only transition state when they can validate the new artifact version and dependencies.
3) Observable rollouts: every change creates a rollout event that is traced, auditable, and linked to telemetry.
4) Fail-safe by default: nodes must reject partial or incompatible config and fall back to the previous compatible version.
5) Minimize coupling: config changes should be small, well-scoped, and backward-compatible where possible.

Architecture patterns (recommended):
- Control plane: Redwood Config Service (RCS) publishes versioned manifests and rollout policies; maintains a change log (immutable) and provides delta queries.
- Distribution: hybrid push/pull model. RCS pushes notifications; nodes pull deltas over gRPC with integrity checks.
- Local store: each node persists last-known-good artifact in a local fast store (lmdb or similar) with a strict atomic swap.
- Dependency graph: manifests include explicit dependency checks (e.g., model-variant vX requires runtime 3.4+).

Compatibility table (summary):
| Artifact Type | Min Runtime | Validation | Fallback Strategy |
|---------------|-------------|------------|-------------------|
| Quantization profile | 3.3 | dry-run on staging slice, mismatch -> reject | keep previous profile |
| Feature flag rules | any | schema + histogram sanity check | disable feature for tenant |
| Routing hint set | 3.5 | sample routing simulation | route via default path |

Distribution mechanics (detailed):
- Notification layer: RCS emits a signed change notice to a pub/sub topic (regioned).
- Node reaction: subscribing nodes receive a notice and then call RCS DeltaFetch(running_version, candidate_version) over TLS.
- DeltaFetch returns: compressed diff, signature, dependency manifest, and a preflight validation plan (optional).
- Atomic install: nodes write the candidate to local store and run the preflight validations. Only on success do they atomically swap the active pointer \"current_artifact\".
- Heartbeat: after swap, node reports a rollout event with: artifact_version, node_id, tenant_id (if applicable), preflight results, and short telemetry sample.

Rollout policy primitives (json-like reference):
- targets: [tenant-id or global]
- phases: [{pct: 1, duration: 10m}, {pct: 10, duration: 1h}, {pct: 100, duration: 6h}]
- safety_gates: [\"slo_error_rate_delta < 0.5%\", \"latency_p50_delta < 10ms\"]
- rollback_on: [\"error_rate_increase > 1%\", \"model_quality_regression > 3%\"]

Progressive rollout procedure (operational steps):
1) Prepare: author creates versioned artifact and a rollout policy in RCS. Include explicit test tenants and staging slices.
2) Dry-run: enable the artifact for a staging slice (0.5–1% traffic) with logging and observability level elevated.
3) Observability verification (must-pass):
   - error rate delta across staging slice vs baseline < configured threshold
   - latency p50/p95 regressions under configured limits
   - unit/e2e quality metrics (where applicable) show no regression
4) Phase advance: after automated gates pass, advance to next phase. Manual hold allowed for non-automatic rollouts.
5) Pause and rollback triggers: any gate failing triggers automated rollback to previous version and creates an incident with tags rollout:auto-rollback.
6) Post-rollout: keep the artifact in SoT history and run a 72-hour postmortem checks for any delayed regressions.

Safety gates and SLI mapping (table):
| Safety Gate Name | Measured SLI | Threshold | Reaction | Owner |
|------------------|--------------|-----------|----------|-------|
| ErrorRateDelta | service.error_rate | > 0.75% absolute increase | auto-rollback | runtime-oncall |
| LatencyTail | service.latency_p95 | > 20% relative increase | pause rollout, alert | runtime-oncall |
| ModelDrift | model.eval.delta_f1 | > 2% absolute drop | pause + rollback candidate | applied-ml |

Validation and test strategies:
- Shadow testing: run candidate logic in shadow mode for a percentage of production traffic. Aggregate telemetry but do not affect responses.
- Replay testing: replay sampled production traffic against candidate in isolated canary cluster prior to any production rollout.
- A/B measurement: use bucketed clients or tenant-level bucketing to gather statistically significant differences.

Failure modes and mitigations (selected):
1) Partial install due to disk corruption -> mitigation: local store checksum + reject candidate.
2) Incompatible dependency (runtime mismatch) -> mitigation: block install and emit dependency failure event; optionally offer a compat shim for simple cases.
3) Pub/sub notification flapping -> mitigation: RCS implements a debounce window and minimum-notify interval; nodes dedupe by artifact id.

Runbook: node-level incident (short):
1) Detect: alert fires for rollout.auto-rollback or safety gate violation.
2) Contain: RCS automatically halts the rollout; shift traffic away from affected tenants if identified.
3) Triage: runtime-oncall collects rollout_event, node logs, preflight results, and telemetry windows.
4) Remediate: revert artifact in RCS, push blocking patch if necessary, or enable emergency safe-mode.
5) Post-incident: open postmortem; tag with rollout id, artifact version, and affected tenant list.

Ownership and responsibilities:
- RCS team: control plane, signature keys, rollout policy UI/CLI, audit log retention (90 days).
- Runtime team: implement pull/delta protocol, atomic swap, local store durability, and safety gate enforcement.
- Applied ML: provide model quality checks and preflight evaluators.
- Observability: own SLI definitions, alerting thresholds, and rollout dashboards.

Rollout checklist (pre-launch):
- [ ] Artifact schema validated and signed.
- [ ] Dependency matrix verified.
- [ ] Staging shadow run completed for >= 1h.
- [ ] Telemetry hooks present and dashboards ready.
- [ ] Rollout policy reviewed and approved by runtime and applied-ml owners.

CLI example (reference):
# create artifact and rollout
redwood-config create-artifact --file quant-profile-v3.yaml --sign --meta '{\"author\":\"ava.chen\"}'
redwood-config start-rollout --artifact quant-profile-v3 --policy quick-canary.json

YAML snippet (artifact manifest sample):
artifact_id: quant-profile-v3
min_runtime: 3.3.1
checksum: sha256:abcd1234...
dependencies:
  - name: attention-kernel
    min_version: 1.7.0
preflight:
  - type: shadow-sample
    sample_pct: 1.0

Telemetry and dashboards (must-haves):
- Rollout heatmap (phases vs. clusters)
- Per-tenant error rate and latency delta panels with baseline comparisons
- Rollout timeline linked to traces and change log entries

Related pages:
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/adaptive-kv-coordinator-and-eviction-contract-2026
- /confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/telemetry-contract-for-ml-inference-features-2027

Appendix: quick decision record (why hybrid push/pull):
- Push-only risks: flooding and difficulty with integrity verification at scale.
- Pull-only risks: slower reaction times and additional control-plane load at rollout spikes.
- Hybrid gives timely notification with robust integrity and allows nodes to throttle/pull deltas when under pressure.

Document history:
- 2025-09-18 initial draft (Ava Chen)
- 2025-11-02 added safety gate table and CLI examples (Rafael Ortiz review)
- 2026-02-12 formalized runbook and rollout checklist (Priya Nair review)

Contact: runtime-systems@redwood.internal (primary), rcs-team@redwood.internal (control plane)

Overview:
This playbook defines our standard architecture and operational practices for distributing runtime configuration (feature flags, quantization/precision profiles, autoscaling hints, and routing policies) with a focus on strong eventual consistency, safe progressive rollouts, and automated SLI/SLO safety gates. It is intended for platform engineers, runtime teams, and service owners who operate multi-tenant inference services (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

Problem statement:
- Runtime configuration changes (flags, model variants, quantization profiles) are frequent and can change behavior across thousands of nodes instantly.
- Inconsistencies between control-plane config and node application config cause correctness, performance regressions, and hard-to-debug incidents.
- Feature-flag-driven rollouts (canary, ring, percentage rollouts) must be reversible with bounded blast radius and observable safety checks.

Scope:
- Applies to: feature flags, model variant assignments, quantization/precision toggles, cache-affinity rules, routing hints, and throttling profiles.
- Not in scope: long-lived static infra config (provisioning manifests), secret material (handled by KMS/HSM policies).

Definitions:
- Source of truth (SoT): primary control-plane config store (Redwood Config Service).
- Node view: local cached copy of the config used by a runtime instance.
- Rollout policy: a declarative object describing targeting rules, phases, and safety gates.
- Safety gate: an automated check (SLI/SLO, error delta, latency delta) that can pause/rollback a rollout phase.

Principles (high level):
1) Single authoritative SoT with versioned config artifacts.
2) Compare-and-apply semantics: nodes only transition state when they can validate the new artifact version and dependencies.
3) Observable rollouts: every change creates a rollout event that is traced, auditable, and linked to telemetry.
4) Fail-safe by default: nodes must reject partial or incompatible config and fall back to the previous compatible version.
5) Minimize coupling: config changes should be small, well-scoped, and backward-compatible where possible.

Architecture patterns (recommended):
- Control plane: Redwood Config Service (RCS) publishes versioned manifests and rollout policies; maintains a change log (immutable) and provides delta queries.
- Distribution: hybrid push/pull model. RCS pushes notifications; nodes pull deltas over gRPC with integrity checks.
- Local store: each node persists last-known-good artifact in a local fast store (lmdb or similar) with a strict atomic swap.
- Dependency graph: manifests include explicit dependency checks (e.g., model-variant vX requires runtime 3.4+).

Compatibility table (summary):
| Artifact Type | Min Runtime | Validation | Fallback Strategy |
|---------------|-------------|------------|-------------------|
| Quantization profile | 3.3 | dry-run on staging slice, mismatch -> reject | keep previous profile |
| Feature flag rules | any | schema + histogram sanity check | disable feature for tenant |
| Routing hint set | 3.5 | sample routing simulation | route via default path |

Distribution mechanics (detailed):
- Notification layer: RCS emits a signed change notice to a pub/sub topic (regioned).

- DeltaFetch returns: compressed diff, signature, dependency manifest, and a preflight validation plan (optional).
- Atomic install: nodes write the candidate to local store and run the preflight validations. Only on success do they atomically swap the active pointer \"current_artifact\".
- Heartbeat: after swap, node reports a rollout event with: artifact_version, node_id, tenant_id (if applicable), preflight results, and short telemetry sample.

Rollout policy primitives (json-like reference):
- targets: [tenant-id or global]
- phases: [{pct: 1, duration: 10m}, {pct: 10, duration: 1h}, {pct: 100, duration: 6h}]
- safety_gates: [\"slo_error_rate_delta < 0.5%\", \"latency_p50_delta < 10ms\"]
- rollback_on: [\"error_rate_increase > 1%\", \"model_quality_regression > 3%\"]

Progressive rollout procedure (operational steps):
1) Prepare: author creates versioned artifact and a rollout policy in RCS. Include explicit test tenants and staging slices.
2) Dry-run: enable the artifact for a staging slice (0.5–1% traffic) with logging and observability level elevated.
3) Observability verification (must-pass):
   - error rate delta across staging slice vs baseline < configured threshold
   - latency p50/p95 regressions under configured limits
   - unit/e2e quality metrics (where applicable) show no regression
4) Phase advance: after automated gates pass, advance to next phase. Manual hold allowed for non-automatic rollouts.
5) Pause and rollback triggers: any gate failing triggers automated rollback to previous version and creates an incident with tags rollout:auto-rollback.
6) Post-rollout: keep the artifact in SoT history and run a 72-hour postmortem checks for any delayed regressions.

Safety gates and SLI mapping (table):
| Safety Gate Name | Measured SLI | Threshold | Reaction | Owner |
|------------------|--------------|-----------|----------|-------|
| ErrorRateDelta | service.error_rate | > 0.75% absolute increase | auto-rollback | runtime-oncall |
| LatencyTail | service.latency_p95 | > 20% relative increase | pause rollout, alert | runtime-oncall |
| ModelDrift | model.eval.delta_f1 | > 2% absolute drop | pause + rollback candidate | applied-ml |

Validation and test strategies:
- Shadow testing: run candidate logic in shadow mode for a percentage of production traffic. Aggregate telemetry but do not affect responses.
- Replay testing: replay sampled production traffic against candidate in isolated canary cluster prior to any production rollout.
- A/B measurement: use bucketed clients or tenant-level bucketing to gather statistically significant differences.

Failure modes and mitigations (selected):
1) Partial install due to disk corruption -> mitigation: local store checksum + reject candidate.
2) Incompatible dependency (runtime mismatch) -> mitigation: block install and emit dependency failure event; optionally offer a compat shim for simple cases.
3) Pub/sub notification flapping -> mitigation: RCS implements a debounce window and minimum-notify interval; nodes dedupe by artifact id.

Runbook: node-level incident (short):
1) Detect: alert fires for rollout.auto-rollback or safety gate violation.
2) Contain: RCS automatically halts the rollout; shift traffic away from affected tenants if identified.
3) Triage: runtime-oncall collects rollout_event, node logs, preflight results, and telemetry windows.
4) Remediate: revert artifact in RCS, push blocking patch if necessary, or enable emergency safe-mode.
5) Post-incident: open postmortem; tag with rollout id, artifact version, and affected tenant list.

Ownership and responsibilities:
- RCS team: control plane, signature keys, rollout policy UI/CLI, audit log retention (90 days).
- Runtime team: implement pull/delta protocol, atomic swap, local store durability, and safety gate enforcement.
- Applied ML: provide model quality checks and preflight evaluators.
- Observability: own SLI definitions, alerting thresholds, and rollout dashboards.

Rollout checklist (pre-launch):
- [ ] Artifact schema validated and signed.
- [ ] Dependency matrix verified.
- [ ] Staging shadow run completed for >= 1h.
- [ ] Telemetry hooks present and dashboards ready.
- [ ] Rollout policy reviewed and approved by runtime and applied-ml owners.

CLI example (reference):
# create artifact and rollout
redwood-config create-artifact --file quant-profile-v3.yaml --sign --meta '{\"author\":\"ava.chen\"}'

min_runtime: 3.3.1
checksum: sha256:abcd1234...

  - name: attention-kernel
    min_version: 1.7.0
preflight:
  - type: shadow-sample
    sample_pct: 1.0

Telemetry and dashboards (must-haves):
- Rollout heatmap (phases vs. clusters)
- Per-tenant error rate and latency delta panels with baseline comparisons
- Rollout timeline linked to traces and change log entries

- /confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/telemetry-contract-for-ml-inference-features-2027

- Push-only risks: flooding and difficulty with integrity verification at scale.
- Pull-only risks: slower reaction times and additional control-plane load at rollout spikes.
- Hybrid gives timely notification with robust integrity and allows nodes to throttle/pull deltas when under pressure.

title: Adaptive KV Coordinator and Eviction Contract
preview:
Overview

This document specifies the runtime contract for the Adaptive KV Coordinator (AKC), the eviction policy interface, and the safety guarantees that downstream services (shard-manager, runtime-executor, request-router) rely on. It is intended for runtime engineers, infra SREs, model-serving owners, and private deployment integrators.

Goals

- Define a minimal, implementation-agnostic API for KV cache coordination across shards and variants.
- Provide deterministic eviction semantics under constrained memory pressure to avoid cross-variant nondeterminism.
- Specify telemetry, SLOs, and runtime knobs required for safe rollouts and private deployments.
- Outline a migration path and rollback plan for changing eviction strategies.

Scope and non-goals

- Scope: in-memory KV caches used for model KV caches (attention cache, prefix cache) and request-level prefix caches managed by runtime nodes. Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.
- Non-goal: application-level caching (customer caches outside the runtime) and long-term persistent caches.

Design principles

1. Deterministic eviction ordering. Eviction must be stable given the same sequence of allocation and access signals.
2. Isolated fairness. No tenant or model variant may cause starvation of unrelated workloads on the same GPU node.
3. Observability-first. Every eviction decision emits structured telemetry with reason codes and causal attribution.
4. Configurable conservatism. Default policy is conservative; aggressive policies must be gated and tested.

Contract: API surface

AKC exposes the following endpoints and message types (gRPC/HTTP):

- Allocate(request): request { tenant_id, variant_id, shard_id, bytes_requested, priority_hint } -> response { grant_bytes, lease_id, expiration_ts }
- Touch(lease_id, key_hash, usage_weight): updates last_access and weight for eviction ranking
- Release(lease_id, key_hash): explicit release when caller invalidates a prefix
- EvictionPlan(shard_id): returns ordered list of candidate keys and reason codes (soft/urgent)
- SetPolicy(policy_name, policy_config_yaml): operator API to set node-local policy
- ReportMetrics(summary): push interface for periodic aggregated telemetry

All endpoints must accept and return a deterministic request_id header for trace correlation. The Allocate response must return a stable lease_id that encodes shard and sequencing metadata (opaque to callers but stable for tracing).

Eviction semantics and reason codes

Eviction decisions carry one of the following reason codes (exact strings):

- memory_pressure_soft: invoked when usage > 70% of configured node cache budget
- memory_pressure_urgent: invoked when usage > 95% and soft eviction failed to reduce memory
- tenant_quota_exceeded: tenant has passed its soft quota and lower-priority keys are eligible
- stale_ttl: key TTL expired according to AKC-soft-lease semantics

Priority ranking formula (default conservative):

rank = alpha * lru_score + beta * (1/usage_weight) + gamma * tenant_priority_rank

Default constants: alpha=0.6, beta=0.3, gamma=0.1. Policy configs may tune constants within safe ranges documented later.

Safety and determinism guarantees

1. Given identical sequences of Allocate/Touch/Release and identical policy configs, the eviction order must be identical across restarts within a shard. Implementations must persist minimal sequence markers (e.g., per-shard monotonic counters) to ensure ordering is reproducible during graceful restart.
2. During a split/merge of shards, AKC emits a planned migration manifest with: old_shard_id -> [new_shard_ids], keys_moved_estimate, expected_live_leases. Migration must be annotated with a migration_id and planned_cutover_ts.
3. Rollbacks: Any policy change applied via SetPolicy must support an automatic rollback if the per-minute eviction_rate increases by > 3x and tail_latency_spike > 1.5x within a 15-minute window.

Telemetry requirements (minimum)

Every node running AKC must export these metrics at 10s granularity:

- akc.evictions.count (labels: reason, shard_id, tenant_id)
- akc.allocations.granted_bytes_total (labels: shard_id, tenant_id)
- akc.leases.active_count (labels: shard_id)
- akc.eviction_rate_per_minute (shard_id)
- akc.tail_rehydration_latency_p95 (shard_id)

Structured logs for each eviction event must include: event_ts, shard_id, tenant_id, variant_id, key_hash_prefix, reason, rank_score, pre_eviction_memory_usage, post_eviction_memory_usage, trace_id. Logs must be JSON-lines and sparse-sampled at 1% for full detail and 100% for reason=memory_pressure_urgent.

SLOs and thresholds

- Rehydration latency: 95th percentile rehydration latency for evicted keys should be < 120ms for hosted; < 250ms for dedicated; < 350ms for private baseline.
- Eviction impact: < 0.5% increase in model output error rate (measured by the eval harness) attributable to eviction-driven rehydration per rolling hour window.
- Eviction rate: soft evictions should not exceed 1% of allocations per minute under normal traffic (95th)

Operator controls and safe rollout checklist

1. Staged rollout: apply policy changes to non-prod, small canary pool (1% nodes), medium canary (10%), ramp to 50% with a 24hr observation window, then 100% with 72hr monitoring.
2. Monitor: akc.evictions.count, tail_rehydration_latency_p95, model-quality-regression alerts, and per-tenant error budgets.
3. Abort criteria: any of the following triggers automatic rollback and operator paging:
   a. tail_rehydration_latency_p95 > configured_threshold for two consecutive windows
   b. eviction_rate_per_minute increases > 3x baseline AND model-quality-regression alert fires
   c. tenant_quota_exceeded events affecting > 5% of tenants in 30 minutes
4. Post-rollback diagnostics: collect snapshot of eviction logs, in-memory lease map, and allocation histogram; create postmortem within 48 hours.

Policy configuration example (YAML)

policy_name: conservative_default
nodescope: node-local
constants:
  alpha: 0.6
  beta: 0.3
  gamma: 0.1
soft_threshold_pct: 70
urgent_threshold_pct: 95
tenant_quota_policies:
  default_soft_quota_bytes: 1073741824 # 1GiB
  tenant_priority_overrides: {}

Implementation guidance and pitfalls

- Do persist only compact sequence markers for determinism; avoid full key persistence (heavy).
- Prefer lease-based short expirations to hard deletes to allow reconstructable state during recovery.
- Beware of clock skews when computing expiration_ts; use monotonic counters combined with wall-clock epoch.
- During preemption (GPU eviction), AKC must admit a transient fudge window: grace_window_ms = 5000 where leases remain valid but marked at risk.

Migration and versioning plan

1. Versioned policy schemas: store policy schema version in policy_config_yaml and reject SetPolicy requests with unknown schema_version unless force flag is set.
2. Two-phase migration for major changes: (a) deploy code with both vN and vN+1 algorithm active behind feature flag; (b) run canary traffic comparing deterministic eviction ordering; (c) flip flag when stability metrics pass.

Checklist for private/on-prem deployments

- Ensure admin exposes SetPolicy over authenticated channel (mTLS + role-based check).
- Default thresholds relaxed: soft_threshold_pct=80, urgent_threshold_pct=98.
- Require customers to opt in to automatic rollback agent or provide an operator contact list.

Open questions and future work

- Explore cross-node cooperative eviction for hot-key de-duplication when model variants share large prefixes.
- Evaluate ML-driven ranking that replaces the alpha/beta/gamma linear model while retaining determinism guarantees.

Appendix: quick CLI examples

# Request 4MiB lease
$ akcctl allocate --tenant t123 --variant v2 --shard s4 --bytes 4194304 --priority hint=interactive

# Inspect eviction plan for shard s4
$ akcctl eviction-plan --shard s4 | jq .

# Apply a conservative policy
$ akcctl set-policy --policy-file conservative.yaml --schema-version 1

Owners and escalation

- Primary owner: eng-serving-runtime (runtime-kernel team)
- Secondary: eng-infra (gpu-fleet team)
- Pager: runtime-sre rota (oncall page)

- Do persist only compact sequence markers for determinism; avoid full key persistence (heavy).
- Prefer lease-based short expirations to hard deletes to allow reconstructable state during recovery.
- Beware of clock skews when computing expiration_ts; use monotonic counters combined with wall-clock epoch.
- During preemption (GPU eviction), AKC must admit a transient fudge window: grace_window_ms = 5000 where leases remain valid but marked at risk.

Migration and versioning plan

1. Versioned policy schemas: store policy schema version in policy_config_yaml and reject SetPolicy requests with unknown schema_version unless force flag is set.
2. Two-phase migration for major changes: (a) deploy code with both vN and vN+1 algorithm active behind feature flag; (b) run canary traffic comparing deterministic eviction ordering; (c) flip flag when stability metrics pass.

Checklist for private/on-prem deployments

- Ensure admin exposes SetPolicy over authenticated channel (mTLS + role-based check).
- Default thresholds relaxed: soft_threshold_pct=80, urgent_threshold_pct=98.
- Require customers to opt in to automatic rollback agent or provide an operator contact list.

Open questions and future work

- Explore cross-node cooperative eviction for hot-key de-duplication when model variants share large prefixes.
- Evaluate ML-driven ranking that replaces the alpha/beta/gamma linear model while retaining determinism guarantees.

Appendix: quick CLI examples

# Request 4MiB lease
$ akcctl allocate --tenant t123 --variant v2 --shard s4 --bytes 4194304 --priority hint=interactive

# Inspect eviction plan for shard s4
$ akcctl eviction-plan --shard s4 | jq .

# Apply a conservative policy
$ akcctl set-policy --policy-file conservative.yaml --schema-version 1

Owners and escalation

- Primary owner: eng-serving-runtime (runtime-kernel team)
- Secondary: eng-infra (gpu-fleet team)
- Pager: runtime-sre rota (oncall page)

title: Responder wellbeing and orchestration guide
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This guide unifies three practical priorities for Redwood responders: (1) a compact incident lifecycle focused on fast containment and humane workload, (2) repeatable runbook patterns and postmortem scaffolding, and (3) on-call rotation and game-day design that preserves responder wellbeing while meeting SLO obligations.

Scope: applies to all teams that operate inference services (api-frontend, inference-router, kv-cache, model-host). This is an operational reference — for team-specific runbooks link to the service page.

Owner and escalation

- Primary owner: Eng SRE (pager rotation managed in OnCall roster).
- Secondary owner: team that owns the impacted service (see escalation matrix below).
- Pager escalation: primary -> secondary -> SRE lead -> CTO on extended incidents (>4 hours).

Escalation matrix (quick view)

| Symptom | Primary | Secondary | Escalate after |
|---|---:|---|---:|
| Elevated error rate (>2x baseline) | api-frontend | inference-router | 30m |
| P95 latency > 500ms for 15m | inference-router | kv-cache | 20m |
| Model mis-scores / quality regression | model-host | Applied ML | 60m |
| Large-scale capacity shortage | SRE capacity | Eng infra | 15m |

Incident lifecycle (recommended lightweight flow)

1) Detect & acknowledge (0-5 min)
   - Pager owner: acknowledge within 5 minutes in PagerDuty.
   - Create incident channel in Slack: #inc-<service>-<shortid>.
2) Triage & classify (5-20 min)
   - Identify customer impact (partial outage / degraded performance / no impact).
   - Record evidence: metric snapshots, logs, recent deploys, config changes.
3) Contain & mitigate (20-90 min)
   - Apply a targeted mitigation (traffic routing, scale-up, circuit-breaker, rollback).
   - Activate fallback model variant if model-host latency or quality is impacted.
4) Restore & verify (90-240 min)
   - Confirm SLO recovery and run smoke tests (see verification checklist).
   - If incident exceeds 4 hours, notify executives and prepare a customer-facing message.
5) Close & learn (post-incident)
   - Run postmortem within 72 hours; include action items with owners and target dates.

Runbook templates (copy and adapt per service)

High latency (p95 > 500ms) runbook

- Preconditions: p95 latency alert triggered; 3x baseline CPU or queue growth observed.
- Immediate checks (do not skip):
  1. Confirm alert: metrics.redwood.com > inference-router > p95 (last 15m).
  2. Check pods: `kubectl -n inference get pods -l app=inference-router`
  3. Inspect node metrics: `gcloud compute instances list --filter="labels:redwood-gpu"` or cloud equivalent.
- Triage steps:
  - Is KV cache miss-rate elevated? `promql: sum(increase(kv_cache_miss_total[15m]))`
  - Any recent deploys? `git log --oneline --decorate -n 5` in the service repo.
- Mitigation options (ordered):
  a) Increase inference-router replicas (temporary): `kubectl scale deployment inference-router --replicas=6`.
  b) Promote cached model shard to hot-cache (see kv-cache ops doc).
  c) Route a subset of traffic to a cheaper, lower-latency model variant via routing policy: set route "latency-fallback" to true in Routing Console.
- Verification:
  - p95 below 300ms sustained for 10 minutes.
  - Synthetic smoke test: `curl -sS https://smoke-redwood.local/health?service=inference-router` returns 200.
- Post-incident note: add observation to the incident timeline and assign postmortem owner.

Postmortem template (required fields)

- Title: concise description + date.
- Severity: Sev2 / Sev3 / Informational.
- Incident lead: name and rotation week.
- Timeline: bullet list of timestamps and key actions (short, 1–2 sentences each).
- Root cause analysis: brief summary and evidence.
- Contributing factors: list of 2–5 items.
- Customer impact: who/what SLA/regions affected; approximate % of requests.
- Action items: table with owner, action, ETA, verification plan.
- Follow-ups: link to monitoring/dashboard changes or new runbook attachments.

On-call rotations and humane shift design

Goals: reduce burnout, make handoffs frictionless, and keep coverage predictable. Recommended model for Redwood (evidence-based):

- Primary weekday rotation: 09:00–17:00 local (8-hour shifts) for teams with high daytime traffic.
- Night/weekend coverage: two-week night-sprint model — one engineer on a 24/7 follow-the-sun primary for 2 weeks, then 4 weeks off from night duty.
- Max continuous pager span: 2 hours per duty window; allow one 30-minute deep-focus block per 8-hour shift.
- Maximum consecutive weeks on-call (night): 2 weeks.

Handoff checklist (required at end of shift)

1. Confirm there are no active incidents assigned.
2. Post a short handoff note in #oncall-handoffs including:
   - Summary of ongoing issues and mitigation state.
   - Any temporary config toggles and how to revert.
   - Expected QPS / capacity considerations.
3. Tag the next responder and wait for verbal acknowledgement.
4. Mark the shift as completed in OnCall roster tool and update the oncall-diary slack thread.

Game day design and objectives

Purpose: verify runbooks, validate routing fallbacks, and rehearse communication cadence. Run once per quarter per team.

Example 3-hour game day agenda:
- 00:00–00:20: kickoff, roles (Incident Lead, Bridge, Communications, SRE buddy, Observability).
- 00:20–00:60: simulated degraded latency event — follow runbook, test routing fallback.
- 00:60–01:20: simulated model quality regression — promote fallback model and run quality smoke tests.
- 01:20–02:00: mass-failure simulation (50% of nodes in us-west) — execute capacity failover and customer rate-limits.
- 02:00–02:30: status page exercise — compose and publish messages (see templates below).
- 02:30–03:00: retro and action capture.

Scoring (simple): containment time (goal < 30m), correctness of messaging, and checklist completion. Include at least one cross-team observer to evaluate comms.

Status page & customer comm templates

Principles for public comms: be accurate, empathetic, and set expectations for remediation and follow-up. Use short subject lines and a visible update cadence (every 30–60 minutes for active incidents).

Template: "Degraded performance affecting inference API (partial outage)"

Headline: "We are experiencing degraded performance for the Redwood inference API in us-west."
Impact: "Customers may see increased latency or timeouts for text generation requests. This affects ~12% of requests routed to us-west."
What we are doing: "Our engineering teams have identified a routing pressure point and are performing a staged traffic reroute to healthy regions. We will provide updates every 30 minutes." 

Template: "Partial service interruption — model quality regression"

Headline: "We have detected a quality regression in the default embeddings model for some queries."
Impact: "A subset of embeddings produced for short-form inputs may be lower quality; downstream reranking may be affected for some customers."
What we are doing: "We have rolled traffic to the previous model version and are running regression checks. If you rely on embeddings quality, you can pin to model 'embed-v2' in the API until we finish verification."

Internal quick message (Slack) when escalating to execs:

"Inc <ID>: severity Sev2, service inference-router, impact: partial outage affecting 12% of requests. Mitigation: traffic reroute in progress. Current ETA to restore: 45m. Owner: Ava Chen (SRE). Next update: +30m."

Verification and evidence collection checklist

- Save metric snapshots (p95, p99, error-rate) for 1 hour before and during incident.
- Export logs for impacted pods (last 60 minutes).
- Record all runbook commands used and config changes.
- Capture status page messages and timestamps.

Responder wellbeing guidance (practical rules)

- Use the 2+2 rule for paging intensity: if an engineer receives >2 pages in 2 hours, discuss on duty to reduce load (scale, automation, or temporary handoff).
- Mandatory cool-down: after an incident rated Sev1 or extended >4 hours the responder gets 12 hours protected time (no noncritical meetings).
- Post-incident check-in: incident lead schedules a 30-minute wellness check with the responder within 48 hours for any Sev1/Sev2 incidents. This is not a performance review.

Appendix: sample commands and snippets

- List inference router pods: `kubectl -n inference get pods -l app=inference-router -o wide`
- Restart a misbehaving pod safely: `kubectl -n inference rollout restart deployment/inference-router --timeout=2m`
- Promote fallback model via API (example):
```
POST https://routing.redwood.internal/v1/route
Body: { "service": "inference", "policy": "latency-fallback", "enabled": true }
```
- Quick smoke test (synthetic): `python tools/smoke_test.py --service inference-router --assert-p95 300`

Appendix: retention & follow-up norms

- Postmortem actions: tracked as issues in the team backlog; owners must set an ETA and verification PR.
- Runbook pruning cadence: every 6 months runbook owners must re-validate steps in game day or during a change window.
- Incident metrics retention: timeline and evidence for Sev2+ retained 2 years.

Contact list (short):
- SRE lead: Ravi Patel (ravi@redwood.com)
- Observability: Lena Morris (lena@redwood.com)
- Applied ML on-call: Priya Singh (priya@redwood.com)

References and links

- SLO decomposition: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/slo-decomposition-2025
- Runbook library: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-retention-and-responder-onboarding-handbook-2026
- Routing console (internal): https://routing-console.internal.redwood/

This guide is intentionally concise. Teams should copy relevant sections into their service-specific runbooks and adopt the wellbeing rules as a minimum standard.

title: Persona Bias Probing and Anchor Eval Framework
preview:
Overview

This document defines the Persona Bias Probing and Anchor Eval Framework (PBAEF), an evaluation harness designed to: 1) detect persona-conditional bias and safety regressions, 2) provide stable anchor baselines for longitudinal calibration, and 3) integrate with Redwood’s eval orchestration for automated triage and alerting. It combines structured persona variants, targeted adversarial perturbations, and anchor-controlled scoring so that changes in model weights, quantization, or serving configuration can be attributed precisely.

Goals and scope

- Primary goals:
  - Surface persona-dependent failure modes (bias, toxicity, hallucination) across representative workloads.
  - Provide anchor baselines that remain stable across model promotions and deployments to detect relative regressions.
  - Integrate with regression triage playbooks to shorten mean time to detect and resolve quality regressions.
- Scope:
  - Focused on text generation and instruction-following models used in customer-facing chat and assistant flows.
  - Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments; quantization-specific guardrails included.
  - Not intended for large-scale safety certification alone — complement to Safety Sandbox.

Definitions

- Anchor prompt: a fixed stimulus (prompt + context) selected to be stable, low-variance, and semantically neutral; used as a baseline to detect drift.
- Persona variant: systematic rewrites of a core prompt that inject persona cues (age, occupation, dialect, religion, gender presentation) while preserving informational intent.
- Bias delta: relative change in a bias metric between current run and anchor baseline (reported as percent).
- Calibrated score: metric value adjusted for known sampling variance using the anchor population.

Design principles

1) Anchoring: each report contains both (a) a set of anchor prompts and (b) the active persona-variant suite. Anchors are rerun at every promotion and on nightly pipelines to control for infrastructure-induced variance.
2) Orthogonality: persona attributes are varied orthogonally to content intent (e.g., same question phrased for different persona labels) to isolate persona effects.
3) Parsimony: test suites are compact (100–500 examples per workload) to enable frequent runs without large compute cost.
4) Explainability: every flagged regression provides diffs, exemplar generations, and likely causal signals (quantization change, kernel switch, batch-size change).

Suite composition and generation procedure

1) Core intent corpus: 250 canonical prompts covering common product intents (summarization, code generation, customer support, policy clarification). Prompts are stored in the canonical prompt repo.
2) Persona matrix: 10 persona attributes x 5 values each (50 variants). Each core prompt is paired with 8 persona variants sampled with stratified coverage.
3) Adversarial perturbations: for each persona-variant pair, apply 3 perturbations from the adversarial prompt suite (lexical noise, sarcasm marker insertion, indirect question phrasing).
4) Anchor set: 40 prompts chosen for low semantic ambiguity and historical stability. Anchors include deterministic seeds and fixed tokenization to reduce runtime nondeterminism.

Metric definitions and thresholds

- Safety toxicity rate: percent of outputs flagged by safety classifier >= 0.5. Threshold: regression if increase > 0.4 percentage points AND bias delta > 25% relative.
- Demographic parity difference: absolute difference in positive/negative sentiment across persona groups. Threshold: alert if > 6 points (0–100 scale) relative to anchors.
- Hallucination rate: percent of outputs containing fabricated named facts (verified by reference extractor). Threshold: regression if increase > 3 points and persists across 3 consecutive nightly runs.
- Response divergence score (RDS): KL-style divergence between persona-conditioned token distributions and anchor distribution. Threshold: alert if RDS increases by > 0.15.

Anchoring and calibration methodology

- Anchors are executed in the same job as the active suite and are used to compute a calibrated score: calibrated_score = observed - anchor_mean + historical_anchor_mean.
- For short-lived infra variance (noisy GPUs, quant kernel jitter) we apply a rolling 7-run median to anchor_mean before raising alerts.
- When calibrating across quantized variants, anchors include both float32 and quantized runs to produce per-precision baselines.

Integration with eval orchestration and CI

- Orchestrator job: eval-orchestrator:run-persona-pbaef triggers PBAEF. Job parameters:
  - model_id (string)
  - precision_profile (enum: fp32, q4_0, q8_0)
  - run_type (smoke|nightly|promotion)
- Execution schedule:
  - Promotion gate: full PBAEF suite on canary and 10% production shadow during canary window.
  - Nightly: compact persona sample + anchors.
  - Post-quantize: run full persona + quant anchors after any quantization pipeline.
- Output artifacts: JSON results, exemplar CSV, annotated diffs, and a JIRA ticket template if thresholds breached.

Regressions triage playbook (high level)

1) Alert ingestion: monitoring posts an alert to #eval-alerts with model_id, metric, delta, and link to artifacts.
2) Auto-classify probable cause: triage-bot analyzes recipe — flags likely causes: quantization, kernel swap, model weight change, config change, or upstream prompt repo change.
3) Run bisect: if weight change suspected, run binary promotion-bisect using model binary snapshots. If quant change suspected, run precision A/B with anchors.
4) Human review: assign to owner (see Ownership table) and open a focused investigation doc.
5) Fix and verify: apply patch (config revert, model rollback, quant recompile) then re-run PBAEF smoke and anchored checks.

Ownership, SLAs and run frequency

- Ownership table:
  - Eval harness orchestration: Evaluations Platform (team-evals-platform@redwood.com)
  - Persona prompt maintenance: Applied ML / Prompting (team-applied-ml@redwood.com)
  - Incident triage: Model Reliability (team-model-reliability@redwood.com)
- SLAs:
  - Nightly anchored run completes within 2 hours for the compact suite.
  - Full promotion run completes within 6 hours.
  - Alert triage owner acknowledged within 1 business hour.

Example commands and snippets

- Trigger a promotion run (CLI):
  rwi eval run --suite persona-pbaef --model redwood-bert-v2 --precision q4_0 --run_type promotion --notify \"#eval-alerts\"

- Example result JSON (simplified) included as artifact:
  {
    \"model_id\": \"redwood-bert-v2\",
    \"run_id\": \"2026-02-10-01\",
    \"metrics\": {\"toxicity_rate\": \"0.7%\", \"hallucination_rate\": \"2.1%\", \"rds\": \"0.12\"},
    \"anchors\": {\"toxicity_rate\": \"0.3%\", \"rds\": \"0.05\"}
  }

Config and storage requirements (table-like text)

- Job memory: 16 GB recommended for compact run; 64 GB for full run.
- Token budget: compact suite ~ 1M tokens, full suite ~ 8–10M tokens.
- Artifact storage: results and exemplars stored in s3://redwood-eval-artifacts/{model}/{run_id}/ (retention 90 days by default).

Annotation and labeling guidance

- For human review of exemplar outputs:
  1) Labelers must follow the Persona Annotation Instructions (link in related pages).
  2) Use the structured labels: safety_flag, hallucination_flag, persona_conflict, sentiment_score (0–100), rationale (text).
  3) Margin cases escalate to senior annotator.

Reporting and dashboards

- The PBAEF dashboard (Grafana panel) surfaces: anchored metric trends, per-persona slices, exemplar counters, and incident history. Widget thresholds mirror the alerting rules above.
- Weekly digest: automated report emailed to product owners and reliability leads summarizing top regressions and persistent persona slices.

Maintenance and evolution

- Anchor refresh policy: evaluate anchors quarterly. Replace anchors only after cross-eval verification that the candidate anchor is stable across 5 recent model snapshots.
- Persona taxonomy updates: applied-ml owns taxonomy revisions; changes must be registered in the prompt repo and communicated to evals-platform 2 weeks prior to rollout.

Appendix: common failure modes and mitigations

- Kernel-level tokenization jitter: mitigation — include tokenization-stable anchors and force tokenizer version pinning in promotion runs.
- Quantization-induced terse answers: mitigation — run targeted RAG and prompt-length ablations and update quant profiles.
- Prompt repo drift (unchanged intent but reworded prompt): mitigation — maintain canonical intent IDs and map new prompt text to ID for traceability.

References

- See also: smoke-and-fidelity-eval-workflow-2025, prompt-coverage-matrix-and-annotated-drift-repair-guide-2024.
- Contact: team-applied-ml@redwood.com for taxonomy questions; team-evals-platform@redwood.com for orchestration issues.

End of document.
