title: Ops conductor patterns and scenario worksheets
preview:
Summary:

This document defines a set of repeatable incident-command patterns (the Ops Conductor patterns) and scenario worksheets for on-call teams owning LLM inference services. It complements line-item runbooks by codifying the orchestration roles, step sequences, and customer communication templates for common and high‑impact events (capacity spikes, KV cache thrashing, model quality regressions, and regional failovers).

Scope:
- Applies to all on-call responders for inference services: inference-gateway, route-router, kv-cache, model-hostpool, console-api, and billing-proxy.
- Intended for use during live incidents, game-day rehearsals, and postmortem evidence gathering.

Intent and benefits:
- Reduce cognitive load on first responders by prescribing clear conductor/bridge patterns.
- Shorten mean time to acknowledgement (MTA) and mean time to repair (MTTR) via pre-baked decision ladders.
- Ensure consistent, legally-safe customer/status messaging.

1) Roles & responsibilities (Ops Conductor pattern)

Primary roles (one person may hold multiple roles during low-severity incidents):
- Conductor (incident commander): owns the incident timeline, decisions, and external status messages. Typically a senior SRE or service owner.
- Bridge operator: runs the incident bridge, collects logs, and posts interim updates.
- Remediation lead: drives hands-on mitigation (scaling, rollbacks).
- Quality lead: assesses model/regression signals and runs prompt-set checks.
- Customer liaison (CSR/Success): crafts customer-facing messages and triages priority accounts.

Role matrix (minimum staffing by severity):
- Sev3: Conductor + Bridge operator (shared)
- Sev2: Conductor + Bridge operator + Remediation lead
- Sev1: Full cadre (Conductor, Bridge, Remediation, Quality, Customer liaison, Exec liaison)

2) Incident classification (quick reference)
- Latency degradation: 95th percentile p95 response time > 300 ms above baseline for > 5 minutes on a single region; impact: p95 API latency SLO 99.9%.
- Availability: error rate (5xx) > 2% sustained for 3 minutes or customer-visible rate > 0.5% across top-10 tenants.
- Cost/Capacity spike: GPU utilization > 90% on a Dedicated pool for 10+ minutes triggering queueing/backpressure.
- Model-quality regression: automated eval-set BLEU/EM drop > 3% or user-reported semantic regressions affecting CSAT.
- Data-leak or security incident: any verified exfiltration or policy breach -> follow security incident policy and escalate to Security.

3) Conductor play patterns (decision ladders)

Pattern A — Rapid containment (for cascading backend errors):
1. Conductor declares containment window (10 min).
2. Bridge operator collects top-5 error traces (service, trace ID, time window).
3. Remediation lead toggles read-only or route to backup model variant (route-router failover).
4. Conductor posts status: \"Containment in progress; routing to fallback model variants.\"
5. If containment fails in 15 minutes, escalate to region failover plan.

Pattern B — Graceful degradation (for sustained capacity pressure):
1. Conductor activates throttling policy for non‑critical routes (billing-proxy + inference-gateway).
2. Remediation lead increases batch sizing suggestions (redwood-optimize hints) and spins up burst pool if allowed.
3. Customer liaison posts staged notice with guidance for retry/backoff windows.

Pattern C — Quality regression investigation:
1. Quality lead runs the automated prompt-set eval (eval-harness) and sanity checks against canary model.
2. If regression confirmed, initiate immediate rollback to previous version and pin model-hostpool.
3. Conductor coordinates a post-rollback validation window (30 minutes).

4) Scenario worksheet (example): KV cache thrash on europe-pool

- Situation snapshot:
  - Time detected: 2026-01-21T10:03Z
  - Service: kv-cache (europe-pool)
  - Trigger: sudden increase in miss rate (cache miss rate rising from 20% -> 78%) and p95 latency increase on inference-gateway.
  - Initial impact: Multi-tenant latency spikes, p95 increased by 760 ms, error rate steady at 0.4%.

- Immediate actions (first 10 minutes):
  1. Conductor: Acknowledge incident and open bridge (link: internal-bridge://incident-xxxx).
  2. Bridge operator: Execute diagnostics:
     - kubectl -n prod logs deploy/kv-cache --since=15m | head -n 200
     - curl -s \"https://metrics.internal/prom/api/v1/query?query=rate(kv_cache_miss_total[5m])\" | jq .
  3. Remediation lead: Temporarily set kv_cache.eviction_policy=LRU-aggressive in config and roll the daemonset to europe nodes.

- Containment goal (target):
  - Reduce miss rate to < 30% and p95 add-on latency < 200 ms within 30 minutes.

- Escalation ladder:
  - If miss rate > 60% after 20 minutes -> enable per-tenant cache quotas and route-heavy tenants to ephemeral model-hostpool.
  - If miss rate remains > 60% after 40 minutes -> initiate regional read-only for europe and trigger Dedicated pool burst allocation.

5) Runbook snippet: rapid rollback + staging validation (commands)

- Rollback command (kubectl example):
  - kubectl -n prod set image deployment/model-hostpool model-hostpool=registry.redwood.ai/model-hostpool:v2025-11-04
- Post-rollback validation:
  1. Run 100 synthetic chat requests through canary route: curl -X POST https://canary.inference.internal/v1/chat -d '{\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"Hello\"}] }' -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TEST_KEY'
  2. Measure p95: curl \"https://metrics.internal/prom/api/v1/query?query=histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route=\\\"canary\\\"}[5m])) by (le))\"

6) Status page and customer comms templates

- Short status line (for status.redwood.ai):
  - Title: \"Serving degradation in EU region — mitigation in progress\"
  - Body: \"We are investigating elevated latency and partial service degradation affecting some EU tenants. Mitigations have been applied and engineered teams are actively restoring normal service. Next update in 30 minutes or earlier if the status changes.\"

- Customer email / CSR template (for impacted customers):
  - Subject: \"Update: Inference service degraded in EU region — actions and recommended mitigations\"
  - Body: \"Hi <CustomerName>,

We detected a service degradation impacting inference latency in the EU region affecting some requests. Our engineers have applied cache tuning and routing mitigations; we recommend throttling non-essential batch jobs and applying exponential retry with jitter for transient failures. We will send another update within 30 minutes or as we have new information. For urgent account-level concerns, please reply to this email or open a support case with priority flag.

Sincerely,
Redwood Support\"

- Exec summary blurb (for execs and legal):
  - \"Root cause suspected to be cascading cache miss amplification due to recent prefix routing change. Containment measures are active; full restoration expected within 2 hours. No customer data exposure is suspected at this time.\"

7) Post-incident evidence checklist (minimum required for Sev1/Sev2)
- Timestamps and timeline (UTC) with actor IDs and decision points.
- Top-10 traces and span IDs for the incident window.
- Metric graphs: p95 latency, error rate, cache miss rate, GPU util by pool.
- Configuration diffs (git commit IDs) for any immediate changes.
- Customer impact list (tenant IDs, endpoints affected) and any credits issued.

8) Postmortem rubric (what a complete incident write-up must include)
- Author and contributors; incident timeline; impact statement (customers, internal SLOs, financial/CSAT impact).
- Root cause analysis with reproducible hypothesis and evidence.
- Action items: owner, due date, and verification steps.
- Retro notes: what worked, what didn't, and a prioritized remediation backlog.

9) Game-day scenarios and scoring worksheet

Recommended quarterly game days by theme:
- Cache failure day: simulate kv-cache thrash with synthetic miss flood; evaluate MTTR and correctness of throttling policies.
- Model rollback day: validate rollback tooling and rollback-to-canary validation windows.
- Cross-region failover: bring down europe control-plane and observe routing fallback and customer notice cadence.

Scoring rubric (pass/fail thresholds):
- Acknowledge within 5 min (pass if <= 5m).
- Containment action chosen and implemented within 20 min.
- Status update posted within 10 min of bridge opening.
- Postmortem draft published within 72 hours.

10) Operational hygiene and runbook ownership
- Each runbook or incident scenario worksheet must have an owner (service steward) with a quarterly verification sign-off.
- Runbook repository: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-index (link)
- Retirement cadence: any runbook not exercised in 18 months must be reviewed and either updated or archived.

Appendices
A. Example mitigation CLI snippets
- Enable burst pool via CLI (internal):
  - redwood infra pool allocate --pool europe-burst --gpu-count 8 --reason \"incident: kv-cache-thrash\"
- Set per-tenant cache quota (templated JSON):
  - curl -X POST https://config.internal/api/v1/tenants/123/cache-quota -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{\"quota_mb\": 256, \"enforce\": true}'

B. Quick link map (internal links)
- Incident runbook index: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-index
- KV cache observability dashboard: https://dashboards.internal/d/kv-cache
- Eval harness and prompt-set runner: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness

C. Diagram (text):
- The orchestration diagram for the Conductor pattern: \"Conductor\" sits at the top coordinating three horizontal lanes: Observability (metrics, traces), Remediation (scaling, rollback, routing), and Communications (status page, csr). Each lane has owned actors (Bridge, Remediation lead, Customer liaison) and arrows describing decision flows (contains -> escalate -> failover).

Contact and ownership
- Document owner: Ava Chen (SRE)
- Contact: #ops-conductors on Slack, pager: oncall@redwood.ai

Change log:
- 2025-11-12: Created (Ava Chen)
- 2026-02-03: Updated with KV cache scenario and revised SLA thresholds (A. Chen)
text:
Summary:

This document defines a set of repeatable incident-command patterns (the Ops Conductor patterns) and scenario worksheets for on-call teams owning LLM inference services. It complements line-item runbooks by codifying the orchestration roles, step sequences, and customer communication templates for common and high‑impact events (capacity spikes, KV cache thrashing, model quality regressions, and regional failovers).

Scope:
- Applies to all on-call responders for inference services: inference-gateway, route-router, kv-cache, model-hostpool, console-api, and billing-proxy.
- Intended for use during live incidents, game-day rehearsals, and postmortem evidence gathering.

Intent and benefits:
- Reduce cognitive load on first responders by prescribing clear conductor/bridge patterns.
- Shorten mean time to acknowledgement (MTA) and mean time to repair (MTTR) via pre-baked decision ladders.
- Ensure consistent, legally-safe customer/status messaging.

1) Roles & responsibilities (Ops Conductor pattern)

Primary roles (one person may hold multiple roles during low-severity incidents):
- Conductor (incident commander): owns the incident timeline, decisions, and external status messages. Typically a senior SRE or service owner.
- Bridge operator: runs the incident bridge, collects logs, and posts interim updates.
- Remediation lead: drives hands-on mitigation (scaling, rollbacks).
- Quality lead: assesses model/regression signals and runs prompt-set checks.
- Customer liaison (CSR/Success): crafts customer-facing messages and triages priority accounts.

Role matrix (minimum staffing by severity):
- Sev3: Conductor + Bridge operator (shared)
- Sev2: Conductor + Bridge operator + Remediation lead
- Sev1: Full cadre (Conductor, Bridge, Remediation, Quality, Customer liaison, Exec liaison)

2) Incident classification (quick reference)
- Latency degradation: 95th percentile p95 response time > 300 ms above baseline for > 5 minutes on a single region; impact: p95 API latency SLO 99.9%.
- Availability: error rate (5xx) > 2% sustained for 3 minutes or customer-visible rate > 0.5% across top-10 tenants.
- Cost/Capacity spike: GPU utilization > 90% on a Dedicated pool for 10+ minutes triggering queueing/backpressure.
- Model-quality regression: automated eval-set BLEU/EM drop > 3% or user-reported semantic regressions affecting CSAT.
- Data-leak or security incident: any verified exfiltration or policy breach -> follow security incident policy and escalate to Security.

3) Conductor play patterns (decision ladders)

Pattern A — Rapid containment (for cascading backend errors):
1. Conductor declares containment window (10 min).
2. Bridge operator collects top-5 error traces (service, trace ID, time window).
3. Remediation lead toggles read-only or route to backup model variant (route-router failover).
4. Conductor posts status: \"Containment in progress; routing to fallback model variants.\"
5. If containment fails in 15 minutes, escalate to region failover plan.

Pattern B — Graceful degradation (for sustained capacity pressure):
1. Conductor activates throttling policy for non‑critical routes (billing-proxy + inference-gateway).
2. Remediation lead increases batch sizing suggestions (redwood-optimize hints) and spins up burst pool if allowed.
3. Customer liaison posts staged notice with guidance for retry/backoff windows.

Pattern C — Quality regression investigation:
1. Quality lead runs the automated prompt-set eval (eval-harness) and sanity checks against canary model.
2. If regression confirmed, initiate immediate rollback to previous version and pin model-hostpool.
3. Conductor coordinates a post-rollback validation window (30 minutes).
